comics.title: Convergent Subsequence
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A minimalist webcomic featuring a simple character (round head with blue cap) experiencing existential thoughts while watching a sunset. The art style is simple line drawings with bold colors—deep blue sky, yellow and orange sunset gradients, and a silhouetted figure. The comic progresses from a single large panel to increasingly smaller panels, visually representing the character's spiraling thoughts becoming more introspective and fragmented.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (large, top-left):**
"SOMETIMES, WHEN I'M ALONE WATCHING A SUNSET..."

**Panel 2 (top-right):**
"...I FEEL LIKE I'M WATCH-ING THE GREAT SEA OF POSSIBILITY..."

**Panel 3 (middle-left):**
"...AND SOMETIMES I BELIEVE THAT I CAN SEE THE WHOLE SEQUENCE OF MY LIFE BEFORE ME..."

**Panel 4 (middle-right, small):**
"...MEANDERING AIMLESSLY LIKE AN ABSTRUSE GOOSE..."

**Panel 5 (small):**
"...SEARCHING FOR FULFILL-MENT IN THIS CORRUPT SUBSET OF EXISTENCE."

**Panel 6 (small):**
"MY LIFE MUST HAVE A CONVERGENT SUBSEQUENCE SOMEWHERE... OUT THERE..."

**Panel 7-8 (increasingly tiny):**
"WHERE THERE'S A HIGHER FUNCTION..." / "WHY CAN'T I PROVE IT?" / [Additional fragmented dialogue becomes illegible]

# Visual Jokes
The progressive shrinking of panels mirrors the character's mental spiral into increasingly abstract and obsessive philosophical questioning, creating a visual representation of overthinking.

comics.title: SETI Finally Receives a Signal
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A black and white stick figure comic depicting two characters at a computer setup in what appears to be a headquarters. One character is seated at a desk with a monitor and keyboard, while the other stands nearby. The art style is simple line drawings with minimal detail.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"MEANWHILE, AT SET 1 HEADQUARTERS..."

**Panel 2:**
"?!?"
"BEEP BEEP BEEP"

**Panel 3:**
"A SIGNAL! WE GOT A SIGNAL!!!"
"BEEP BEEP BEEP"

**Panel 4:**
"IT'S DEFINITELY NONRANDOM. IT APPEARS TO BE COMING FROM THE PLEIADES CLUSTER."
"WOW!"
"BEEP"

**Panel 5:**
"IT LOOKS LIKE A COMPRESSED BINARY ENCODING OF A PRESSURE WAVE."
"YOU'RE RIGHT!"
"BEEP"

**Panel 6:**
"LET'S SEE IF WE CAN DECODE IT WITH ANY OF OUR STANDARD AUDIO CODECS."
"BEEP"

**Panel 7:**
"THERE."

**Panel 8:**
"CRACKLE..."

**Panel 9:**
"THIS HAS GONE TOO FAR."
"♪ NEVER GONNA GIVE YOU UP ♪ NEVER GONNA LET YOU DOWN... ♪"

# Visual Jokes
The comic is a "rickroll" joke—the alien signal decodes to the opening lyrics of Rick Astley's "Never Gonna Give You Up," a famous internet meme used to trick people into listening to the song.

comics.title: LOLCAT Backlash
comics.transcript:
# Comic Transcription

## Scene Description
A nine-panel webcomic in simple black-and-white line art style featuring a person (drawn as a circle head) and a cat. The setting appears to be indoors. The art style is minimalist and humorous, with expressive character reactions.

## Dialogue and Text (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 2:**
- Person: "TACT YOU IZ IT?"

**Panel 3:**
- Person: "I CAN HAS CHEEZBURGER? HEE HEE..."

**Panel 4:**
- Person: "AH TINK AH SMELLS ME A CHEEZBURGER."

**Panel 5:**
- Person: "I HELP WIF UR SHOPPING. OWNA PLEASE. LOLCATS 5000 LAST YEAR."

**Panel 6:**
- Person: "I BIG SCARY MONSTER. RAAAAAH!"
- Cat: "EVERYDAY... THE SAME SHIT."

**Panel 7:**
- Person: "Y DEFI TREEZ ON DAT PLATE? LAUGH WHILE YOU CAN, MONKEY BOY."

**Panel 8:**
- Person: "HAVE YOU NOTICED I DON'T USE THE LITTER BOX ANYMORE?"
- Cat: "U KANT HAVE MY TOEZ."

**Panel 9:**
- Person: "GUESS WHO SLEEPS WITH HIS MOUTH OPEN!"
- Cat: "IZ MAH HOUSE I"

## Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the person's increasingly absurd impersonations and mimicry of internet cat memes (particularly "I Can Has Cheezburger" and lolcat speak), with the cat's mounting frustration and deadpan responses contrasting with the person's enthusiastic but annoying behavior.

comics.title: There is No Spoon
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A simple stick figure comic with two round-headed characters demonstrating a physics thought experiment. The art style is minimalist line drawing in black and white.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"THERE IS NO SPOON.
THERE IS NO SPOON.
THERE IS NO SPOON.
THERE IS NO SPOON."

**Panel 2:**
"I'M TESTING A THEORY ON MIND."

**Panel 3:**
"I BELIEVE THAT I CAN USE MY MIND TO PRODUCE A QUANTUM GRAVITATIONAL LENSING EFFECT TO BEND THE SPOON'S SUPER-POSED QUANTUM STATES TO MY DESIRED SPACE-TIME GEOMETRY CONFIGURATION."

**Panel 4:**
"IN EFFECT, I'M USING MY MIND TO FORCE THE UNIVERSE TO BEND THE SPOON."

**Panel 5:**
"?!"

**Panel 6:**
"SWITCH"

**Panel 7:**
[No dialogue]

**Panel 8:**
"HARRPH!"

**Panel 9:**
"THAT STILL COUNTS!
TOTALLY!"

# Visual Jokes
The humor depends on the visual progression: one character attempts to bend a spoon through willpower/quantum mechanics while the other character physically switches the bent spoon with the original one, making it appear the mind-bending worked.

comics.title: Schrödinger's Infinitesimal Miscalculation
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A six-panel black and white comic in a simple line-art style featuring a stick-figure character with glasses. The setting appears to be indoors with minimal furniture (boxes/tables). The comic depicts a humorous sequence involving the character and what appears to be a cat.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (top left):**
"...1935"
Equation: |ψ⟩ = ½|LIVE⟩ + ½|DEAD⟩ + ½|CAT⟩
(Character holds head)

**Panel 2 (top center):**
"MEOW"
(Character at box, cat visible)

**Panel 3 (top right):**
(No text - character's head shown floating above box)

**Panel 4 (middle left):**
(No text - character appears to fall or move)

**Panel 5 (middle center):**
"!!"
(Character and cat interaction with action lines)

**Panel 6 (right, spanning middle-bottom):**
Equation: ½|LIVE⟩ + ½|DEAD⟩ + |ε⟩|ANGRY⟩|HUMAN⟩
(Character holds head)

Signature mark in bottom right corner

# Visual Jokes
This is a physics humor comic referencing Schrödinger's cat thought experiment (1935). The joke shows the cat is simultaneously alive and dead until observed—but when the character finally opens the box and finds the living cat, the equation updates to include an "angry human" component, implying the character caused harm or the cat is now angry.

comics.title: Schrödinger's Miscalculation - Part 2
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A stick figure comic strip in black and white with a minimalist art style. The setting progresses from a physics equation context in 1935 to a cat hiding in a box. The single character is a simple stick figure with a round head, shown in various positions interacting with a box.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (top left):**
"...1935"
"|ψ⟩ = ½|LIVE⟩ + ½|DEAD⟩ + ½|CAT⟩"

**Panel 2 (top center):**
"MEOW"

**Panel 3 (top right):**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 4 (middle left):**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 5 (middle center):**
"WHAT FOR U HIDE ME IN DARK COLD BOX?"

**Panel 6 (bottom right):**
"½|LIVE⟩/√|CAT⟩ + ½|DEAD⟩/√|CAT⟩ + ½|LOL⟩/√|CAT⟩"

# Visual Jokes
This is a commentary on Schrödinger's cat thought experiment. The joke depicts a cat literally placed in a box as an illustration of the famous quantum mechanics paradox, with the cat complaining about the treatment. The final panel shows the equation has evolved, with the cat adding a "|LOL⟩" (laugh out loud) component, mocking the absurdity of the original thought experiment.

comics.title: Arguing With a String Theorist
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A comic strip with 8 panels featuring two simple round-faced characters in black and white line art. One character appears increasingly frustrated while the other delivers technical explanations about string theory. The comic uses minimal styling with simple circular heads and basic line drawings.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"...FIRST OF ALL, STRING THEORY HAS NOT MADE A SINGLE TESTABLE PREDICTION IN OVER 30 YEARS."

**Panel 2:**
"BY MAKING STRING THEORY RIDICULOUSLY MALLEABLE WITH YOUR 10TH DIFFERENT WAYS TO COMPACTIFY THE EXTRA DIMENSIONS..."

**Panel 3:**
"...YOU ESSENTIALLY PUT THE THEORY BEYOND THE REACH OF ANY CONCEIVABLE EXPERIMENTAL TEST."

**Panel 4:**
"SECOND OF ALL, STRING THEORY IS ONLY FORMULATED PERTURBATIVELY. A FULL NON-PERTURBATIVE DEFINITION OF THE THEORY DOESN'T EXIST."

**Panel 5:**
"THIRD, STRING THEORY DESCRIBES PERTURBATIVE EXPANSIONS ABOUT FIXED SPACETIME BACKGROUNDS."

**Panel 6:**
"ANY RESPECTABLE FUNDAMENTAL THEORY OF QUANTUM GRAVITY WOULD BE BACKGROUND INDEPENDENT."

**Panel 7:**
"FOURTH, IN STRING THEORY, THE DIRAC OPERATOR AND THE GAUGE FIELDS ARE..."

**Panel 8:**
"PAK"

**Panel 9:**
"YOU KEEP TALKING LIKE A BITCH, I'M GONNA SLAP YOU LIKE A BITCH."

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the abrupt tonal shift: after six panels of technical physics criticism delivered calmly, the character snaps and resorts to physical violence, accompanied by the sound effect "PAK" (a slapping sound).

comics.title: Blind Date
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure webcomic with minimalist line art. Two characters are shown: one with long black hair sitting at a table with papers, and another with a round head wearing blue. The comic progresses through 9 panels showing their conversation, with the final panels depicting the character's thoughts illustrated above their head.

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
Character 1: "SO TELL ME ABOUT YOURSELF."

**Panel 2:**
Character 2: "THIS SILVERWARE ARRANGEMENT REMINDS ME OF THE BINARY REPRESENTATION OF 2110 WHICH IS EXACTLY THE NUMBER OF SYMMETRY GROUPS POSSIBLE IN A CRYSTAL LATTICE."

**Panel 3:**
Character 1: "ACTUALLY, THERE ARE 230 SYMMETRY GROUPS IN 3D IF YOU CONSIDER ENANTIOMORPHOUS CHARACTER."

**Panel 4:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 5:**
Character 2: "FWAP!"

**Panel 6-7:**
(Character 2 exits; hearts float away with musical notes)

**Panel 8:**
Character 1: "I ENJOY LONG WALKS IN THE PARK."

**Panel 2 (final):**
Character 2: "ME TOO."

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on a contrast between a mundane "getting to know you" interview and an absurdly technical tangent about crystallography. When the interviewer pedantically corrects the interviewee's already-obscure reference, it causes the interviewee to literally smack themselves and leave, only to return when given a normal conversation topic.

comics.title: The Red Button
comics.transcript:
# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A stick-figure style comic set in heaven, featuring God (larger figure) instructing Gabriel (smaller angelic figure) about a complex control panel with buttons and dials. The art is minimalist black ink on white background with occasional yellow highlights. The humor builds through multiple panels showing the consequences of disobedience.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (top left):**
Caption: "...meanwhile, in heaven..."
God: "GABRIEL!"
Gabriel: "YES, LORD."

**Panel 2 (top center):**
God: "I HAVE BUSINESS IN ANOTHER UNIVERSE. I NEED YOU TO MONITOR THE FINE-TUNING MACHINE."
Gabriel: "YES LORD"

**Panel 3 (top right):**
God: "THESE BUTTONS CONTROL THE FORCE CONSTANTS AND THESE DIALS ADJUST THE PARTICLE MASS RATIOS. IT'S PRETTY SELF-EXPLANATORY."
Gabriel: (implied acknowledgment)

**Panel 4 (middle left):**
God: "BUT WHATEVER YOU DO NEVER PRESS THE RED BUTTON!"
Gabriel: "YES, LORD."

**Panel 5 (middle center):**
God: "I'LL BE BACK IN A FEW EONS."
Gabriel: "YES, LORD."

**Panel 6 (middle right):**
Caption: "...eons later."

**Panels 7-9 (bottom row):**
[Silent panels showing Gabriel staring at the red button, then pressing it, causing an explosion]

## Visual Jokes
The humor depends entirely on the image: Gabriel, explicitly forbidden from pressing the red button, immediately presses it once God leaves, causing a visible explosion. This is a visual gag playing on the irresistible temptation of the forbidden action and satirizes the creation/fine-tuning of the universe.

comics.title: Ubuntu Sucks
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A nine-panel comic strip in black and white line art showing a person sitting at a desk with a computer. The person has short dark hair and appears increasingly distressed throughout the strip. The art style is simple, sketch-like, with minimal details. The setting is a basic office or workspace with a monitor and keyboard visible.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (Top left):**
[Code visible on screen: FFA 33 CO BE DO BC 00 7C / 8B F4 50 07 50 1F FB FC / BF 00 06 B1 00 01 F2 BC J]

**Panel 2 (Top center):**
[Musical notation visible on screen]
★ HELLO ★

**Panel 3 (Top right):**
SOMETHING FEELS DIFFERENT TODAY.
◇
°

**Panel 4 (Middle left):**
MY BED STORE FEELS FUNNY.
◇

**Panel 5 (Middle center):**
JESUS CHRIST! MY MAIN PARTITION HAS BEEN RESIZED! OH NO YOU DIDN'T?!
← STALL →

**Panel 6 (Middle right):**
WHO'S BEING A SLUGGISH LITTLE VISTA MACHINE TODAY? YOU ARE, YES YOU ARE.

**Panel 7 (Bottom left):**
DON'T PATRONIZE ME YOU TWO-TIMING DUAL-BOOTER.
◇

**Panel 8 (Bottom center):**
I CAN SMELL THE HARDY HERON ON MY HARD DRIVE.

**Panel 9 (Bottom right):**
TURN AWAY. I CAN'T BEAR TO LOOK AT YOU RIGHT NOW.

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the person anthropomorphizing their computer and treating it like an unfaithful partner discovering evidence of dual-booting (running multiple operating systems). References to Vista, Hardy Heron (Ubuntu Linux), and partition resizing are technical computer terms used as relationship betrayal metaphors.

comics.title: Math Text
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description

A multi-panel webcomic featuring a character (Mathy) at a computer desk. The art style is simple line drawing with minimal detail. The character has spiky hair and expresses increasing frustration throughout the strip.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (caption):**
"I'VE ALWAYS SUSPECTED THAT THIS IS HOW MATHEMATICS TEXTS ARE WRITTEN."

**Panel 2 (caption):**
"Define a point p in a metric space X to be a *condensation point* of a set E ⊂ X if every neighborhood of p contains uncountably many points of E."

**Panel 3 (caption):**
"Suppose E ⊂ ℝ^k, E is uncountable, and let P be the set of all condensation points of E."

**Panel 4 (caption):**
"THEOREM: P is perfect and P^c ∩ E is at most countable."

**Panel 5 (caption):**
"PROOF OF THEOREM:"

**Panel 6 (dialogue):**
"HONEY, LOOK WHAT I'M WEARING."

**Panel 7 (sound effect):**
"FWAP"

**Panel 8 (dialogue):**
"DAMN YOU, WALTER RUDIN... ...AND GODSPEED."

**Caption (bottom right):**
"Left as exercise."

# Visual Jokes

The joke relies on the contrast between the dry, abstract mathematical content and the character's partner attempting seduction, which goes completely ignored as the character becomes increasingly enraged by Rudin's textbook. The final panel shows the character literally throwing the book, with "Left as exercise" implying that the reader/character must complete the proof themselves—a common frustration with mathematics textbooks.

comics.title: Ask Me Why
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A minimalist image with a plain white background and black text centered near the top of the frame. There are no visible characters, objects, or detailed settings—just typography.

# Text Transcription
"I'm Superstitious"

# Visual Jokes/Puns
This appears to be a setup for a visual or conceptual joke. The humor likely depends on what follows this statement (potentially on subsequent pages or panels of the webcomic), or it may be a meta-joke about the statement itself being incomplete or ironic in some way that would become apparent in context.

comics.title: I Never
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure style webcomic with four characters (one in blue, three in gray) arranged in a group. Each panel shows the blue character making confessions while the others react with increasing exasperation, indicated by "HA" laughter and "SWIG" sounds as they drink.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"I NEVER MEMORIZED PI TO MORE THAN 1000 DIGITS WHILE WAITING FOR MY CODE TO COMPILE."

**Panel 2:**
"SWIG SWIG SWIG"
"HA HA HA HA"

**Panel 3:**
"OK, OK, I'M NEXT!"

**Panel 4:**
"I NEVER IGNORED MY THESIS PROJECT FOR WEEKS WHILE SECRETLY TRYING TO PROVE THE RIEMANN HYPOTHESIS."
"SWIG SWIG SWIG"

**Panel 5:**
"HA HA HA HA"
"MY TURN, MY TURN!"

**Panel 6:**
"I NEVER SNORTED A LINE OF CAKE OFF OF A STRIPPER'S ASS AT CHUCK'S BACHELOR PARTY."

**Panel 7:**
"SWIG"

**Panel 8:**
(Silent panel - characters react)

**Panel 9:**
"WHAT, I THOUGHT WE WERE TELLING STUFF."

# Visual Jokes
The humor escalates from nerdy programmer confessions (Pi memorization, thesis procrastination) to absurdly inappropriate behavior. The final panel's silence and the blue character's confused response creates the joke—the confession crosses from relatable tech culture humor into shocking territory, leaving the others speechless.

comics.title: The Opportunist
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A four-panel stick figure comic depicting two people sharing a pizza. The art style is minimalist with simple black line drawings on white background. One character wears glasses; both are seated at opposite sides of a table with a pizza in the center.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (top left):**
- Caption: "SOME PEOPLE JUST CAN'T RESIST..."

**Panel 2 (top right):**
- Sound effects: "* GCRK *" and "* CHOKE *"
- Character 2: "?"
- Character 1: "!"

**Panel 3 (bottom left):**
- Sound effects: "* ACKK *" and "* CHOKE *"

**Panel 4 (bottom right):**
- Character 2: "I FIND YOUR LACK OF FAITH... DISTURBING."
- Sound effect: "* GAG! *"

# Visual Joke
The comic is a reference to the famous "I find your lack of faith disturbing" scene from *Star Wars* (Darth Vader's Force choke). The joke plays on the double meaning of "choke"—Character 1 is literally choking on pizza while Character 2 responds with the Vader quote about lack of faith, suggesting Character 1 didn't have enough faith/confidence in their eating ability.

comics.title: dckx
comics.transcript:
# How Electric Skateboards Work

**Scene Description:**
A four-panel stick-figure comic in black ink on white background. The first panel shows a character speaking. Panels 2-4 depict a simple narrative about electric skateboards and their effect on attracting people.

**Text Transcription (reading order):**

Panel 1:
"MY HOBBY: READING XKCD COMICS BACKWARDS. SOMETIMES IT'S FUNNIER THAT DAY. BUT SOMETIMES IT JUST MAKES ME SAD."

Panel 2:
"POINT B"
(Shows multiple stick figures labeled "CHICKS" gathered around a figure on an electric skateboard)

Panel 3:
(Shows a stick figure on skateboard labeled "WHIRRRR" with motion lines)

Panel 4:
"POINT A"
(Shows a solitary stick figure on a skateboard)

**Visual Jokes:**
The comic's humor relies on the reversal of causality—when read backwards (as the narrator does), the sequence suggests that electric skateboards somehow *repel* people rather than attract them. The joke plays on the contrast between the expectation that cool technology attracts attention versus the punchline that it actually drives people away.

comics.title: The Birth of ENIAC
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A multi-panel educational comic in simple black-and-white stick figure style with occasional color highlights (yellow header, brown TV set, video game imagery). The comic presents historical facts about ENIAC through a conversational exchange between three stick figures. The final panel shows a modern twist with the figures watching what appears to be a video game on a television.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (Header):**
UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA, 1946...

**Panel 1 (Label):**
ENIAC

**Panel 1 (Caption):**
IT CONTAINED 17,468 VACUUM TUBES, 7,200 CRYSTAL DIODES, 1,500 RELAYS, 70,000 RESISTORS, AND 10,000 CAPACITORS.

**Panel 2 (Label):**
ENIAC

**Panel 2 (Caption):**
ENIAC WAS THE WORLD'S FIRST GENERAL-PURPOSE ELECTRONIC COMPUTER.

**Panel 2 (Dialogue):**
ENIAC WAS PROGRAMMED BY LABORIOUSLY MANIPULATING SWITCHES AND CABLES BY HAND. EVEN SIMPLE CALCULATIONS TOOK DAYS TO PROGRAM.

**Panel 3 (Label):**
ENIAC

**Panel 3 (Caption):**
IT WEIGHED 30 TONS AND TOOK UP 680 SQUARE FEET OF SPACE.

**Panel 3 (Dialogue):**
IT CONSUMED 150W OF POWER AND EVERY TIME IT WAS SWITCHED ON, THE STREET LIGHTS OF PHILADELPHIA FLICKERED.

**Panel 4 (Caption):**
IT TOOK ALMOST 3 YEARS TO BUILD AND IT COST OVER $500,000.

**Panel 5 (Label):**
ENIAC

**Panel 5 (Dialogue):**
THE ENGINEERS WERE LATER QUOTED AS SAYING...

**Panel 6 (Dialogue):**
...TOTALLY WORTH IT, BITCHES!

# Visual Jokes

1. **Anachronistic punchline**: The final panel shows the figures playing what appears to be the video game *Doom* (identifiable by the iconic box art and HUD), a game released in 1993—nearly 50 years after ENIAC. The joke is that despite ENIAC's massive size, cost, and effort, modern computers are infinitely more powerful and can run entertainment software unimaginable to its creators.

2. **The engineers' quote**: The phrase "totally worth it, bitches!" is humorously written in modern slang, creating a tonal clash with the historical 1946 setting and making the engineers sound unexpectedly contemporary.

comics.title: I.I. Rabi's Question Answered?
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A stick-figure comic in simple black and white style depicting a humorous narrative across six panels. The setting transitions from heaven (top panels) to Earth 15 billion years in the future (bottom right panel). Characters include a large figure (God) and three smaller identical figures (angels/creations).

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- Caption: "...in heaven..."
- God: "ARE WE READY TO START CREATING THE UNIVERSE?"
- Angels: "YES, LORD."

**Panel 2:**
- God: "WE'VE FINISHED THE FORCE CARRIER CALCULATIONS. NOW WE NEED TO START ADDING LEPTONS."

**Panel 3:**
- God: "HERE'S THE INVOICE FROM OUR LAST SHIPMENT FROM US."
- Label: "*UNIVERSE PARTICLE SERVICES LTD*"

**Panel 4:**
- God: "ELECTRON, ELECTRON NEUTRINO, TAU, TAU NEUTRINO, MUON, MUON NEUTRINO... HEAVY QUARK ORDER!?"

**Panel 5:**
- God: "HA HA HA HA HA"
- God: "I CAN'T WAIT TO SEE THE LOOK ON THEIR FACES."

**Panel 6:**
- Caption: "...earth, 15 billion years later..."
- Equations: "π⁻ → μ⁻ + ν̄μ" and "π⁻ → μ⁻ + ν̄μ"
- Human 1: "?"
- Human 2: "UTFP?"
- Label: "*INVOICE*"

# Visual Jokes
The primary joke is God finding humor in a billing error (duplicate/wrong items on an invoice), with the punchline being that humans 15 billion years later are confused and frustrated trying to understand particle physics—implying God's "joke" has consequences across cosmic timescales.

comics.title: Life Imitates Art
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description

This is a multi-panel webcomic in black and white line art style featuring a group of scientists/physicists (represented by simple stick figures with different head styles) dealing with a crisis at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC). The setting is a control room with computer monitors. The humor builds as the group realizes a black hole is forming and turns to their unlikely savior, a character named Wil Wheaton, for help.

---

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"DESPITE ASSURANCES FROM PHYSICISTS AROUND THE WORLD, THE UNTHINKABLE HAS HAPPENED. EARLIER TODAY, THE LARGE HADRON COLLIDER IN SWITZERLAND HAS CREATED A STABLE MICRO BLACK HOLE. THE SITUATION IS GROWING. NOW IT'S THREATENED TO DESTROY THE ENTIRE LHC FACILITY... AND THEN...

EARTH!"

**Panel 2:**
"MEANWHILE, THE WORLD'S TOP MINDS GATHERED AT THE LHC IN AN ATTEMPT TO SOLVE THE PROBLEM."

*[Labels on figures: A B C D E F with various face symbols]*

**Panel 3:**
"ACCORDING TO OUR DATA, THE BLACK HOLE WILL BEGIN TO EXPAND EXPONENTIALLY IN 47 SECONDS. THEN NOTHING CAN STOP IT."

*[Figure symbols]*

**Panel 4:**
"I THINK I CAN HELP.

WIL WHEATON?!"

*[Exclamation marks below]*

**Panel 5:**
"IS THIS SOME KIND OF JOKE, SON?

I'M WITH SAG. WE DON'T JOKE."

*[Figure symbols]*

**Panel 6:**
"NOW THE WAY I SEE IT, THE BLACK HOLE IS FEEDING OFF OF A MATTER STREAM FROM A RESIDUAL PROTON CLOUD DRIFT IN THE CHAMBER."

*[Figure symbols]*

**Panel 7:**
"THE FIRST THING YOU NEED TO DO IS FILL THE CAVITY WITH BARIUM. INJECT AN ELECTRON BUNCH AT EXACTLY THE RIGHT VELOCITY TO CREATE A PLASMA LIKE FIELD. ACCELERATION GRADIENT OF 1.2 GV/M. THAT SHOULD BE SUFFICIENT TO DISRUPT THE MATTER STREAM."

*[Figure symbols]*

**Panel 8:**
"NO PROBLEM. I'LL JUST CALCULATE IT IN MY HEAD RIGHT NOW. I'LL ENTER THE DATA HERE... DONE!"

*[Figure symbols]*

**Panel 9:**
"IT'S WORKING! THE BLACK HOLE IS BEGINNING TO EVAPORATE!"

*[Figure symbols]*

**Panel 10:**
"YOU DID IT, WIL WHEATON! YOU SAVED THE PLANET WITH ONLY 3 SECONDS TO SPARE!"

*[Figure symbols]*

**Panel 11:**
"AND HERE'S SOME COMPLIMENTARY COPIES OF MY BESTSELLING BOOK, 'THE HAPPIEST DAYS OF OUR LIVES'."

*[Text trails off as the figures become increasingly scribbled]*

**Panel 12:**
"NOW YOU FINE LADIES AND GENTLEMEN CAN GET BACK TO YOUR HOLE GUEST OF UNLOCKING THE MYSTERIES OF THE UNIVERSE. HAPPY HIGGS BOSON HUNTING!"

*[Figure symbols]*

**Panel 13:**
"AND REMEMBER - NEVER FORGET TO CHECK YOUR REFERENCES.

THANKS, WIL WHEATON!"

*[Heavily scribbled figures]*

**Panel 14:**
"YOU SAVED US! YOU SAVED US ALL!"

*[Exclamation marks and figure symbols]*

**Panel 15:**
"*SIGH*"

*[Figure symbols]*

**Panel 16:**
"THAT KID'S NOT SO BAD AFTER ALL."

*[Speech bubbles with nonsense text: "YEAH YEAH YEAH YEAH"]*

**Panel 17:**
"MAKE NO MISTAKE ABOUT IT. WESLEY CRUSHER MAY HAVE SUCKED, BUT WIL WHEATON RULES!"

---

# Visual Jokes

1. **The irony of Wil Wheaton (an actor, not a scientist) saving the world's leading physicists** by casually solving complex quantum physics in his head
2. **The increasingly scribbled/messy art style** in later panels as the scientists

comics.title: Out of the Closet
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure comic with minimal linework showing two characters in conversation across nine panels. The art style is deliberately crude with basic circle heads and simple body outlines. The narrative progresses through escalating gang confrontation dialogue before deflating into an absurdist punchline.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (caption):**
"...deep inside the secret hideout of the Dragons, the most feared gang in all of East L.A."

**Panel 1 (dialogue):**
"THE CORRA GANG BROKE INTO OUR GARAGE LAST NIGHT AND STOLE OUR BIKES."
"WHAT?!"

**Panel 2:**
"HOW MANY?"
"ALL OF THEM."

**Panel 3:**
"THAT'S THE LAST STRAW! THERE WILL BE BLOOD! I SAY WE POP TIRES IN ALL THEIR ASSES!"
"YEAH!" "YEAH!" "YEAH!"

**Panel 4:**
"NOBODY MESSES WITH THE F*CKING DRAGONS!"
"YEAH" "YEAH" "YEAH" "YEAH"

**Panel 5:**
"NOBODY INVADES OUR G*DDAMN TURF!"
"YEAH" "YEAH" "YEAH" "YEAH"

**Panel 6:**
"NOBODY TOUCHES OUR GORRAM BIKES!"

**Panel 7-8:**
(Silent panels showing the characters)

**Panel 9:**
"OUR WHAT-BIKES?"
"NOTHING."

**Bottom (yellow fact box):**
"FACT: 1 out of every 10 people is a Browncoat"

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the escalating tough-guy rhetoric completely deflating when the narrator realizes they've been ranting about bikes they don't actually have anymore. The "Browncoat" reference is a *Firefly* fan joke—note the use of "gorram" (Firefly's censored "goddamn") in panel 6, foreshadowing the punchline.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic in black ink depicting three simple smiley-faced characters and a monster with a large mouth. The art style is minimalist line drawings. The comic progresses through 9 panels showing the three friends being interrupted by a chaotic monster, with escalating absurdity and violence, ending with a mathematical equation.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (Caption):** "SO THE THREE HAPPY FRIENDS WERE JUST SITTING THERE, MINDING THEIR OWN BUSINESS..."

**Panel 2 (Caption):** "...WHEN SUDDENLY... OH NO... HERE COMES THE ※ MONSTER !"

**Panel 3 (Caption):** "OMG! HE'S EATING MR e³!"

**Panel 4 (Caption):** "BUT WAIT... IT'S OK. HE'S JUST GOING TO POOP HIM RIGHT BACK OUT UNHARMED. THANK GOD."

**Panel 5 (Caption):** "BUT OH NO, HERE HE COMES AGAIN! RUN! RUN AWAY! DAMNIT, IT'S TOO LATE. HE'S GOT ※ e³!"

**Panel 6 (Caption):** "I CAN'T BEAR TO WATCH THIS. HE'S MAULING MR e² TO DEATH! SWEET FANCY MOSES!"

**Panel 7 (Caption):** "THERE'S e³ BLOOD AND GUTS SPELLING ALL OVER THE PAGE! OH, THE HUMANITY!!!"

**Panel 8 (Caption):** "RUN, MR y³, RUN! THERE'S NO TELLIN' WHAT THAT ※ BASTARD IS GONNA DO TO YOUR SORRY ASS..."
**Character dialogue:** "OK, BILLY, THAT'S ENOUGH. SIT DOWN."

**Panel 9 (Caption):** "BUT I'M NOT FINISHED YET."
**Character dialogue:** "YES YOU ARE."
**Equation visible:** "e^(x+e^y+x) = e^x+0+"

# Visual Jokes
- The characters are labeled with mathematical notation (e³, e², y³), and the monster "eats" them literally
- The "blood and guts" is represented as mathematical symbols and expressions
- The final panel breaks the fourth wall with a child being told to stop their chaotic mathematical monster story, with an unfinished equation trailing off

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A six-panel black and white comic strip in the style of XKCD, featuring two stick figures in an NSA office setting. The characters are seated at a desk with a computer monitor visible. The simple minimalist art style uses basic line drawings against a white background with yellow highlighting in the first panel.

# Text Transcription

**Panel 1 (yellow highlighted):**
Caption: "...inside NSA headquarters. Room ___, OPS 1 Building"
Left figure: "WE INTERCEPTED THIS VIDEO WITH OUR KH-27 SATELLITE LAST NIGHT. IT HASN'T BEEN MADE PUBLIC YET."

**Panel 2:**
Right figure: "IF YOU LOOK AT TIME FRAME 23:10 TO 25:47, THE UPPER LEFT PIXELS FORM A PATTERN. IT'S DEFINITELY A SECRET MESSAGE."

**Panel 3:**
Left figure: "FOURIER ANALYSIS ON THE DEINTERLACED FRAMES REVEALED CIPHERTEXT ENCRYPTED WITH PGP 800-WD."

**Panel 4:**
Right figure: "DUAL_EC_DRGB? CAN WE USE THE BACKDOOR?"
Left figure: "NO GOOD. IT'S HOMEBREWED."

**Panel 5:**
Right figure: "HMMMM... DEVIOUS BASTARDS. WE'RE CURRENTLY BRUTE FORCING IT WITH OUR EXAFLOP SUPERCOMPUTER."

**Panel 6:**
Left figure: "IS IT WISE TO ALLOCATE SUCH AN IMPORTANT RESOURCE TO THIS ONE TASK?"

**Panel 7:**
Left figure: "IF THE RUSSIANS OR THE CHINESE DECODE THIS BEFORE WE DO, THE DIRECTOR WILL HAVE OUR ASSES."
Right figure: "GOOD POINT."

**Panel 8:**
Right figure: "YOU. THIS IS THE BEST EPISODE OF 'LOST'"
Left figure: "HELL, YEAH."

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the escalating absurdity: the NSA dedicates massive computational resources and surveillance assets to decoding what turns out to be an encrypted episode of the TV show "LOST"—a show infamous for its convoluted plot that left viewers frustrated and confused.

comics.title: Math vs Physics
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A comic strip featuring three characters in conversation at what appears to be a casual indoor setting. Two characters with simple line-drawn faces (one blonde, one dark-haired) engage in dialogue with a third character depicted as a blue circle with simple features. The art style is minimalist black-and-white line drawing with simple geometric shapes for characters.

# Dialogue Transcript (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- Blonde character: "IF YOU WANT TO UNDERSTAND PHYSICS, YOU HAVE TO UNDERSTAND THE LANGUAGE IN WHICH SHE'S WRITTEN... MATHEMATICS."

**Panel 2:**
- Blue circle character: "MATH IS JUST A TOOL INVENTED TO HELP US PHYSICISTS SOLVE PROBLEMS."

**Panel 3:**
- Blue circle character: "YOU PHYSICISTS RELY TOO MUCH ON HEURISTIC REASONING."
- Dark-haired character: "MATH HAS NO OBJECTIVE EXISTENCE."

**Panel 4:**
- Dark-haired character: "ARE YOU SAYING WE'RE DELUSIONAL? ARE YOU SAYING WE'RE SLOPPY? ARE YOU SAYING WE'RE INFERIOR?"
- Blue circle character: "SLAP"

**Panel 5:**
- Blue circle character: "ARE YOU AS TURNED ON AS I AM?!"
- Blue circle character: "*KISS* MORE!"

**Panel 6:**
- Blonde character: "I GUESS WE CAN AGREE TO DISAGREE, BUT YOU MADE SOME GOOD POINTS."
- Blue circle character: "THANKS."

**Panel 7:**
- Blonde character: "WELL, IT'S GETTING LATE. I HAVE TO GET HOME."
- Dark-haired character: "ME TOO."

**Panel 8:**
- Blue circle character: "SHIT."

# Visual Joke
The comic depicts an absurd reconciliation where intellectual disagreement unexpectedly escalates into physical passion between the blue circle character and the dark-haired character, creating an ironic contrast between the serious academic debate and their sudden romantic engagement. The final panel's "SHIT" reaction from the blue circle suggests the character realizes they've been abandoned once the other characters leave.

comics.title: NSFW
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description

A stick-figure webcomic in black and white (except the first panel which shows color). The comic depicts an office worker at a computer contemplating the consequences of clicking an unfamiliar file. The art style is minimalist with simple line drawings. The final panels show an increasingly chaotic outcome.

---

# Dialogue and Text (in reading order)

**Panel 1:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 2:** 
"HMMM... NSFW. SHOULD I CLICK IT?"

**Panel 3:**
"THE BOSS ISN'T HERE AND MOST OF MY COWORKERS ARE AT LUNCH."

**Panel 4:**
"WHAT'S THE WORST THAT COULD HAPPEN?"

**Panel 5:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 6:**
"*CLICK*"

**Panel 7:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 8:**
"HELLO, NEO. DO YOU KNOW WHO I AM?"
"?!"
"MORPHEUS?"

**Panel 9:**
"YES, THEY'RE COMING FOR YOU, NEO."
"WHO'S COMING FOR ME?"

**Panel 10:**
"SEE FOR YOURSELF."

**Panel 11:**
(No dialogue - character looks confused with a question mark)

**Panel 12:**
(No dialogue - shows multiple Agent Smith figures descending/attacking chaotically around the computer)

---

# Visual Jokes

This comic parodies *The Matrix* film. The "worst that could happen" setup is subverted when clicking the NSFW file triggers the famous "Hello, Neo. Do you know who I am?" scene with Morpheus, followed by an army of Agent Smiths attacking the worker—a literal, absurd interpretation of the Matrix narrative playing out on the office computer.

comics.title: The Hottie
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A XKCD-style webcomic with simple stick figures. The first three panels show a group of people in conversation at what appears to be a social gathering. The final three panels show increasingly empty scenes with a single character remaining, ending with a "Know Stuff" logo featuring a rainbow and star.

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- Paris: "Will you be making the type 1 mistake?"
- Other voices: "Are you preggers?" "Are you still friends?" "Paris" (repeated multiple times)

**Panel 2:**
- Character: "Did you really just slap him?"
- Another character: "Hey, look over there! It's Ed written!"

**Panel 3:**
- Ed: "Will you be attending strings 2008?"
- Ed: "What is the rest velocity of an unladen proton?"
- Another character: "Are you still working on gauge theories in the Langlands?"

**Panels 4-5:**
- Silent panels showing only a single character (represented by yellow A symbol) remaining

**Panel 6:**
- Logo text: "Know Stuff" with a rainbow swoosh and star

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the contrast between Ed's increasingly obscure and technical scientific questions (string theory, gauge theories, Langlands program, proton velocity) compared to the mundane social questions others are asking. The progressive emptying of the room in subsequent panels suggests people are leaving to avoid Ed's incomprehensible questions, leaving only Ed behind—a commentary on how hyper-specialized academic knowledge can isolate someone socially.

# Scene Description
A minimalist stick-figure webcomic in a 3x3 grid format. Two round-faced figures stand outside a door (left side of frame) while another figure peers around the door frame from inside. The art style is deliberately crude and simple, emphasizing the dialogue over visual detail.

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 2:** 
- Sound effect: "KNOCK KNOCK"

**Panel 3:**
- Figure at door: "CAN I HELP YOU GENTLEMEN?"
- Figure outside: "I THINK IT'S WE THAT CAN HELP YOU."

**Panel 4:**
- Figure at door: "HAVE YOU ACCEPTED THE SCIENTIFIC METHOD INTO YOUR HEART AND GIVEN YOUR LIFE OVER TO EMPIRICISM AND REASON?"
- Figure outside: "WHO ARE YOU GUYS?"

**Panel 5:**
- Figure outside: "WE JUST WANT YOU TO STOP LIVING IN THE DARKNESS OF IGNORANCE AND TO LET THE LIGHT OF SCIENCE ILLUMINATE YOUR UNDERSTANDING."

**Panel 6:**
- Figure outside: "LOOK, FELLAS, WE WERE JUST ABOUT TO START DINNER AND... WE ONLY NEED FIFTEEN MINUTES OF YOUR TIME."

**Panel 7:**
- Figure at door: "GET THE HELL OUTTA HERE!"
- Figure outside: "I'M SENSING HOSTILITY. WHY DOES THE TRUTH OFFEND YOU?"

**Panel 8:**
- Figure outside: "PERHAPS WE COULD JUST LEAVE YOU WITH A COPY OF NEWTON'S PRINCIPIA."

**Panel 9:**
- Sound effect: "SLAM"
- Figure outside: "THE TRUTH WILL SET YOU FREE!"
- Figure at door: "AMEN"

# Visual Jokes
The primary joke is a parody of door-to-door religious evangelism, substituting "Science" and "the Scientific Method" for religion. The punchline in the final panel plays on the irony that despite their hostile rejection, the figure inside responds "AMEN" to the departing evangelist's statement—suggesting that even those resistant to scientific proselytizing unconsciously accept its principles.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A six-panel stick figure comic in black and white, depicting a figure at the Swiss Patent Office in 1907, progressing through equations and ending with a humorous revelation about Einstein's thought process. The art style is minimalist with simple line drawings.

# Text Transcription (reading order)

**Panel 1:**
- Caption: "1907, Swiss Patent Office"
- Equation: "F=ma" with summation symbol "Σ"

**Panel 2:**
- Equation: "F=ma" with two summation symbols "Σ₁ Σ₂" and "F = mMG/r²"

**Panel 3:**
- Caption: "IF EINSTEIN'S MIND WORKED ANYTHING LIKE MINE..."
- Equation: "F=ma" with two summation symbols "Σ₁ Σ₂" and "F = mMG/r²"
- Panel text: "?"

**Panel 4:**
- Equation: "Σ₁ Σ₂" with "F=ma" and "F = mMG/r²"
- Visual element: Light bulb (representing an idea)

**Panel 5:**
- Equation: "Σ₁ Σ₂" with "F=ma" and "F = mMG/r²" with a target/bullseye symbol

**Panel 6:**
- Equation: "Σ₁ Σ₂" with "F=ma" and "F = mMG/r²"
- Caption: "...THERE WOULD BE NO GENERAL THEORY OF RELATIVITY."
- Text: "BOOBIES. HEE HEE."

# Visual Joke
The comic's humor derives from the contrast between theoretical physics and the figure's distraction by breasts (represented by the summation symbols Σ₁ Σ₂ being interpreted as "boobies"), suggesting the absence of general relativity would result from Einstein's mind being similarly distracted—a play on the absurdity of such a distraction preventing groundbreaking science.

comics.title: Blind Date - Part 2
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A six-panel webcomic featuring two simple stick-figure characters in conversation at what appears to be a table or counter. One character has long dark hair, the other wears a blue baseball cap. The art style is minimalist with black lines on white background. The humor builds progressively through the panels as the characters reveal shared interests.

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
Character 1: "YOU KNOW, I'M REALLY JUST A GEEK INSIDE."

**Panel 2:**
Character 1: "I'M ALWAYS AFRAID TO MENTION IT ON THE FIRST DATE BECAUSE I DON'T WANT TO SCARE MY DATE AWAY."
Character 2: "REALLY? ME TOO."

**Panel 3:**
Character 1: "I'M ACTUALLY HAPPY WHEN I HAVE HOURS OF FREE TIME TO JUST HACK AWAY ON SOME COMPUTER CODE."
Character 2: "REALLY? ME TOO!"

**Panel 4:**
Character 1: "I'M EVEN HAPPIER WHEN I'M CONTEMPLATING THE MYSTERIES OF THE UNIVERSE WORKING ON UNSOLVED PROBLEMS IN THEORETICAL PHYSICS."
Character 2: "REALLY?! ME TOO!"

**Panel 5:**
Character 1: "AND I'M THE HAPPIEST WHEN I'M TRYING TO FORMULATE AN ELEGANT PROOF FOR SOME OPEN PROBLEM IN ABSTRACT MATHEMATICS."
Character 2: "REALLY? ME TOO!!"

**Panel 6:**
Character 1: "AND I ABSOLUTELY DON'T HAVE SEX UNTIL I'M MARRIED."
Character 2: "REALLY? ME T..."

**Panel 7:**
Character 1: "WAIT, WHAT?..."

**Panel 8:**
Character 2: "WHY NOT?"

**Panel 9:**
Character 1: "BECAUSE LIFE JUST ISN'T THAT GENEROUS YOU GREEDY BASTARD."

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies entirely on dialogue escalation and subversion of expectations. The punchline's impact depends on the reader assuming both characters share identical values throughout, only to have that assumption shattered in the final panels when their moral/romantic philosophies diverge drastically.

# Scene Description
A 3x3 grid comic panel layout featuring a minimalist art style with white backgrounds and black borders. Each panel contains a single black dot positioned slightly left of center. In the final panel (bottom right), a speech bubble appears with dialogue.

# Text Transcription
"HEY, MAN, WHERE ARE ALL THE CHICKS?"

# Visual Joke
The comic is a visual pun on the word "chicks." The eight panels leading up to the final one show only single black dots, which could represent baby chicks (depicted minimally). The punchline occurs when the character asks where all the chicks are, implying they were expecting to see multiple baby chicks represented by the dots in the previous panels, but there's only ever been one dot per panel.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A six-panel black and white stick figure comic featuring two round characters engaged in an escalating argument. The art style is minimalist line drawing with simple circular heads and basic features. The progression shows the characters becoming increasingly angry and physical, culminating in a chess game in the final panel.

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
Character 1: "I'M SICK OF ALL YER TALK. YOU'S FORCED WITH THE WRONG MAN."

**Panel 2:**
Character 1: "I'M GONNA MAKE YOU EAT THOSE WORDS, PUNK. I'M GOIN' GORDAN FREAKIN' ON YOUR ASS."
Character 2: "BRING IT."

**Panel 3:**
Character 2: "OH, I'LL BRING IT ALRIGHT. I'LL BRING THE HURT AND THE PAIN."
Character 1: "YOUR GRANDMA COULD BRING IT BETTER THAN YOU."

**Panel 4:**
Character 1: "YER GONNA LOOK AWFUL FUNNY WITH MY FOOT UP YO ASS. ENOUGH TALK, A**HOLE."

**Panel 5:**
Character 2: "LET'S GET IT ON!!!"

**Panel 6:**
(No dialogue - visual only)

# Visual Joke
The comic's punchline is a subversion of expectations: after five panels of increasingly aggressive threats and trash talk, the final panel reveals the characters have settled their dispute with a game of chess rather than physical combat—a humorous anticlimax that contradicts all the violent posturing.

comics.title: Closed Timelike Curveball
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A minimalist stick-figure webcomic in black and white with simple line art. The comic features two main characters: a bespectacled stick figure (appearing masculine) and a stick figure with shoulder-length hair (appearing feminine, later colored yellow). The setting is sparse, with characters positioned at what appears to be a table or surface. The emotional tone shifts from romantic to humorous and absurd throughout the strip.

# Dialogue Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 2:**
"HEY, WHAT ARE YOU DOING HERE ALL BY YOURSELF?"

**Panel 3:**
"WELL THIS IS GONNA SOUND KINDA SILLY, BUT I MADE A PROMISE TO MYSELF."

**Panel 4:**
"I TOLD MYSELF THAT IF I EVER BUILT A TIME MACHINE, I WOULD GO BACK TO THE PAST AND MEET MYSELF RIGHT HERE... ON THIS DATE."

**Panel 5:**
"IF EVERYTHING WORKS OUT, MY FUTURE SELF SHOULD BE ARRIVING RIGHT ABOUT... NOW."

**Panel 6:**
"THAT'S ABSURD. EVEN IF IT'S THEORETICALLY POSSIBLE FOR CLOSED TIMELIKE CURVES TO EXIST IN L..."

**Panel 7:** (A character with yellow hair appears)
"?"

**Panel 8:**
"YOU'RE GONNA LOVE THE FUTURE."

**Panel 9:** (Silent panel)

**Panel 10:** (Silent panel)

**Panel 11:**
"WOW, TIME HAS BEEN GOOD TO YOU."
"WELL, I DO MOISTURIZE A LOT."

# Visual Jokes
The primary humor derives from the visual gag of the future self arriving as an attractive woman with yellow hair, completely subverting the scientific discussion about timelike curves. The joke's impact relies entirely on the sudden appearance and design change of the character.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure webcomic with two main characters: a woman with orange/red hair and a man with a blue circular head. The setting is minimalist with basic line drawings. A bowl of colorful candies (appearing to be M&Ms or similar) is central to the plot.

# Dialogue Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
Woman: "I HEARD THAT YOU'RE NOT TALKING TO CHUCKY ANYMORE. I THOUGHT YOU GUYS WERE LIKE BEST FRIENDS SINCE FOREVER."

**Panel 2:**
Man: "WHAT HAPPENED?"

**Panel 3:**
(No dialogue; visual action showing the man gesturing at candies)

**Panel 4:**
Man: "HEY, WHATCHA DOIN'?"
Woman: "I'M ARRANGING MY M&MS BY COLOR."

**Panel 5:**
Man: "THEY HAVE TO BE EATEN IN ORDER OF INCREASING FREQUENCY IN THE ELECTROMAGNETIC SPECTRUM."

**Panel 6:**
Man: "FIRST, I EAT THE RED ONES. THEN I... DON'T BE RIDICULOUS. I WANT A BLUE ONE."

**Panel 7:**
Man: "NO!"

**Panel 8:**
(Sound effect) "CHOMP CHOMP CHOMP"

**Panel 9-11:**
(No dialogue; visual sequence showing escalating conflict)

**Panel 12:**
Woman: "NOTHING HAPPENED. I JUST DON'T LIKE HIM ANYMORE."

# Visual Jokes
The humor depends entirely on the image: the absurdity of arranging candies by electromagnetic frequency (visible light spectrum), the man's angry reaction to the woman eating a blue candy out of order, and the implication that this trivial candy dispute destroyed their friendship.

comics.title: The Exception
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A nine-panel webcomic featuring two simple cartoon characters (drawn as circles with minimal features) having a conversation. Each panel contains a graph with colored lines and a mathematical equation (En = -RT ln(x)). The characters appear in silhouette form at the bottom of each panel. The art style is minimalist line drawing.

# Dialogue and Text (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"SO AS YOU CAN SEE, OUR RESEARCH RESULTS ARE VERY CLEAR."

**Panel 2:**
"ONCE AN INTERNET MEME EXCEEDS A CERTAIN THRESHOLD OF CRITICAL MASS, AWARENESS,..."

**Panel 3:**
"...IT PROPAGATES EXPONENTIALLY THROUGHOUT OUR CULTURE AND QUICKLY REACHES PSYCHOLOGICAL SATURATION."

**Panel 4:**
"AFTER THAT POINT, THE LEVEL OF AMUSEMENT DERIVED FROM THAT PARTICULAR MEME IS SIGNIFICANTLY DIMINISHED."

**Panel 5:**
"FOR EXAMPLE, THIS GREEN LINE REPRESENTS THE TRAJECTORY OF THE 'OM NOM NOM NOM' MEME."

**Panel 6:**
"EXCUSE ME, BUT WHAT IS THAT RED LINE?"

**Panel 7:**
(Silent panel showing graph)

**Panel 8:**
"OH, THAT."

**Panel 9:**
"THAT'S CHUCK NORRIS, MOTHERFUCKER! UP HIGH."

# Visual Jokes
The red line on the graph continues rising indefinitely while the green line (representing a normal meme) plateaus and declines—suggesting Chuck Norris memes never follow normal saturation patterns and remain perpetually popular/powerful. This is a joke about Chuck Norris being an exception to all rules.

# Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic in black and white (with blue coloring on one character's hat). The setting is a simple interior with two characters sitting at a table across from each other. The comic progresses through 9 panels showing a conversation between them.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 2:**
- "HEY, AREN'T YOU GOING TO B's PARTY? HE'S THROWING A KEGGER."

**Panel 3:**
- "I CAN'T GO OUT TONIGHT BECAUSE OF MY RULE."
- "HUH?"

**Panel 4:**
- "EVERY NIGHT WHEN I GO TO SLEEP, I HAVE TO BE AT LEAST A LITTLE SMARTER THAN WHEN I WOKE UP. BUT I DIDN'T LEARN ANYTHING NEW YET TODAY."

**Panel 5:**
- "SO I FIGURE NOW IS A GOOD TIME TO TRY TO FINALLY GROW THE RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN DIOTIVIC COHOMOLOGY AND MINOR AT-THEORY. SO FAR, I'M..."

**Panel 6:**
- "DID YOU KNOW THAT THE SMALL PLASTIC SHEATHS ON THE END OF YOUR SHOELACES ARE CALLED AGLETS?"

**Panel 7:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 8:**
- "REALLY?"

**Panel 9:**
- "LOWER STANDARDS = MORE FUN"
- (Shows a crowd of people celebrating with one figure standing alone)

# Visual Jokes
The final panel's joke depends on the visual contrast: the solitary studious figure is shown against a large group happily partying, illustrating that lowering one's standards (accepting trivial facts like "aglets" as learning) enables participation in social fun rather than isolated self-improvement.

comics.title: So Many Questions
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description

A simple line drawing shows a small child sitting in a sandbox, looking contemplative. The child wears a blue cap and is drawn in a minimalist stick-figure style. The comic uses a plain white background with black text and a yellow caption box at the bottom.

# Text Transcription

**Main body text (reading order):**

DOES P=NP? WHAT HAPPENED BEFORE THE BIG BANG? ARE THERE LIMITS TO HUMAN KNOWLEDGE? WHAT IS MATHEMATICS, REALLY? WHAT IS GRAVITY, REALLY? WHAT IS SOY SAUCE, REALLY? IS PHYSICAL ACHIEVABILITY A PROPER SUBSET OF ABSTRACT CONCEIVABILITY? WHO SHOT JFK? WHO IS PIZZLER? WHO KILLED KENNEDY? WHY DID CAP'N CRUNCH TASTE SO MUCH BETTER WHEN I WAS 10? FOR CONVEX SURFACES OF FIXED INTRINSIC DIAMETER, IS THE DOUBLED DISK THE ONE WITH THE GREATEST AREA? DID GALORA THINK THAT THE UVVS WERE TOO UGLY TO SNUFF? IS THERE SUCH A STRONG STATISTICAL CORRELATION BETWEEN THE HOTNESS OF A WOMAN AND THE DOUCHEBAGGERY OF HER BOYFRIEND? IS MATHEMATICS INVENTED OR DISCOVERED? DOES SHE REALLY LIKE IT WHEN I DO THAT? CAN EVERY EVEN INTEGER GREATER THAN 2 BE WRITTEN AS THE SUM OF TWO PRIMES? WHY WON'T HEATHER EVER LET ME LOOK IN HER PURSE?...

**Caption box:**

Just some of the questions I would like answered before I kick

# Visual Jokes

The humor derives from the contrast between the child's innocent sandbox setting and the mixture of profound philosophical questions (P vs NP, the Big Bang, the nature of mathematics), personal grievances (Cap'n Crunch cereal taste, an ex named Heather), and absurdist non-sequiturs (soy sauce, a made-up character named Galora). The escalating randomness creates comedic absurdism, culminating in the mundane threat to "kick"—suggesting a tantrum over unanswered questions.

# Scene Description
A multi-panel webcomic featuring two characters: a simple circular-faced student (Cueball-style) and a professor (also simple circular design) at what appears to be a desk. The art style is minimalist black-and-white line drawing typical of XKCD. The setting is an academic office or classroom with a brown desk/table visible in each panel.

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- Student: "HERE YOU GO, HERR PROFESSOR."
- Professor: "?"

**Panel 2:**
- Professor: "WHAT IS THIS?"
- Student: "IT'S MY EXTRA CREDIT ASSIGNMENT."

**Panel 3:**
- Professor: "THE LOCALLY EUCLIDEAN METRIZATION OF A K-FOLD CONTRAVARIANT RIEMANNIAN TENSOR FIELD BLAH BLAH BLAH..."

**Panel 4:**
- Professor: "LOOK, FIRST OF ALL, I DIDN'T ASSIGN ANY CREDIT. SECOND OF ALL, THIS IS JUST A FRESHMAN CALCULUS COURSE."

**Panel 5:**
- Professor: "THIRD, YOUR PERFECT EXAM SCORES ARE ALREADY SKEWING THE CURVE BEYOND ALL REASON."

**Panel 6:**
- Student: "I DON'T WANT TO JUST SKEW THE CURVE. I WANT TO INCINERATE IT WITH THE WHITE HOT INTENSITY OF A THOUSAND SUNS."

**Panel 7:**
- Professor: "'CRUSH YOUR ENEMIES, SEE THEM DRIVEN BEFORE YOU, HEAR THE LAMENTATIONS OF THEIR WOMEN!'"

**Panel 8:**
- (Silent panel, professor looking contemplative)

**Panel 9:**
- Student: "IT'S LIKE LOOKING IN A MIRROR."
- Professor: "SNIFF"

# Visual Jokes
The final panel's joke relies on the visual similarity between the two characters' simple circular designs—when the student says "it's like looking in a mirror," we see they literally look identical, creating a humorous moment of the professor recognizing himself in the student's ruthless ambition.

comics.title: The Most Popular Girl in School
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A simple black-and-white webcomic featuring two characters: a woman with long dark hair sitting at a desk with a laptop, and a man wearing glasses. The art style is minimalist stick-figure-adjacent. The comic takes place across 12 panels showing a conversation between the two characters, with the woman becoming increasingly flustered while the man remains calm.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (top left):**
Equation on whiteboard: "∂u/∂t + Σ(j=1 to n) u_j ∂u/∂x_j = ∇△u - ∂ε/∂t + f(x)"
Subtext: "div u = Σ(j=1 to n) ∂u_j/∂x_j = 0"
Boundary condition: "u(x,0) = u°(x)"

**Panel 2 (top center):**
Woman: "EUREKA!"

**Panel 3 (top right):**
Man: "THERE'S A RUMOR GOING AROUND THAT YOU SOLVED THE NAVIER-STOKES PROBLEM. IS IT TRUE?"
Woman: "MAYBE. MAYBE NOT."

**Panel 4:**
Man: "IS THAT YOUR PROOF?"
Woman: "MAYBE. MAYBE NOT."

**Panel 5:**
Man: "CAN I SEE?"
Woman: "NO WAY!"

**Panel 6:**
Woman: "I WANT TO MAKE SURE THERE AREN'T ANY EMBARRASSING MISTAKES BEFORE I SHOW IT TO ANYONE."

**Panel 7:**
Man: "WELL, MAYBE WE COULD GO OUT FOR COFFEE OR SOME THING. I'D LOVE TO PICK YOUR BRAIN."

**Panel 8:**
Man: "HA HA. ONE LITTLE RUMOR AND SUDDENLY I'M THE MOST POPULAR GIRL IN GRAD SCHOOL."

**Panel 9:**
Woman: "TRUTH BE TOLD, I HAVEN'T EVEN DISCUSSED IT WITH MY THESIS ADVISOR YET AND..."

**Panel 10 (bottom left):**
[Woman appears to continue speaking, but no dialogue visible]

**Panel 11 (bottom center):**
Man: "HEY, PAL, MY EYES ARE UP HERE. QUIT PEEKIN' AT MY EQUATIONS. I WASN'T PEEKING, I SWEAR."

**Panel 12 (bottom right):**
Woman: "LIAR!"
Man: "I WAS JUST STARING AT YOUR BREASTS."

# Visual Jokes

The primary visual joke is a role reversal: the man is accused of staring at the woman's breasts when he was actually trying to read her mathematical proof. The woman's accusation of "LIAR!" suggests she doesn't believe his denial—but ironically, he was telling the truth about looking at the equations. The comic subverts the typical "caught staring" scenario by making the accusation ironic rather than justified.

# Scene Description

A simple stick figure comic with minimal art style. Two characters are shown: one with a blue circular head (wearing what appears to be a cap) and one with a white/beige circular head. The comic depicts a humorous misunderstanding about a Q-tip left in an ear.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"...AFTER TAKING A SHOWER, SHE LIKES TO SWAB THE WATER OUT OF HER EARS WITH A Q-TIP."

**Panel 2:**
"ONE TIME SHE LEFT THE Q-TIP IN HER EAR AND JUST STARTED WALKING AROUND THE HOUSE."

**Panel 3:**
"SO SHE WAS STANDING IN THE KITCHEN MAKING A SANDWICH WITH THIS HUGE HONKIN' Q-TIP STICKING OUT OF HER EAR."

**Panel 4:**
"BUT SHE FORGOT IT WAS THERE AND LATER SHE FELT IT WIGGLING AROUND IN HER EAR AND SHE THOUGHT IT WAS A BUG."

**Panel 5:**
"SO SHE WENT TO SWAT IT..."

**Panel 6:**
"POW! JAMMED THAT BAD BOY RIGHT THROUGH HER FREAKIN' EARDRUM!"

**Panel 7:**
"OMG! IS SHE OK?"

**Panel 8:**
"OF COURSE SHE IS. I JUST TOTALLY MADE ALL THAT SHIT UP."
"WHAT?"

**Panel 9:**
"WHY THE HELL WOULD Y... TWO FOR CRINGING!"

# Visual Jokes

The humor relies on the escalating absurdity of the story and the final panel's reveal that the entire anecdote was fabricated as a prank to make the listener uncomfortable ("two for cringing").

# Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic showing the progression of human innovation across four time periods (212 BC, 1600 AD, 1895 AD, and 2008 AD). Each panel shows stick figures engaged in different activities related to scientific advancement, with a final panel containing commentary about modern science. The art style is deliberately crude stick-figure drawing with simple black lines on white background, with yellow date labels.

# Text Transcription

**Panel 1 (212 BC):**
- Caption: "212 BC"
- Dialogue: "DO NOT DISTURB MY CIRCLES."
- Sound effect: "SHING" (sword being drawn)

**Panel 2 (1600 AD):**
- Caption: "c. 1600 AD"
- Dialogue: "OK, ABOUT THAT WHOLE EARTH-REVOLVING-AROUND-THE-SUN THING. MAYBE WE COULD JUST NOT TALK ABOUT IT."

**Panel 3 (1859 AD):**
- Caption: "1859 AD"
- Dialogue: "I AM NOT A MONKEY'S COUSIN! GET THEE BEHIND ME, CHARLES! ...UR... I MEAN, SATAN!!!"
- Sound effect: "*SIGH*"

**Panel 4 (1895 AD):**
- Dialogue: "HEY! I COULD TRAVEL ALONG A BEAM OF LIGHT!"
- Response: "ALBERT! STOP DAYDREAMING IN CLASS! YOU'LL NEVER AMOUNT TO ANYTHING!!"

**Panel 5 (2008 AD):**
- Signs held by figures: "SHUT DOWN THE LABS!", "EVOLUTION IS A LIE!", "DOWN WITH THE SCIENTISTS"
- Title heading: "SCIENCE"
- Caption below: "Y'ALL PISSIN' PEOPLE OFF, YOU AIN'T DOIN' IT RIGHT."

# Visual Jokes
The comic's primary humor derives from the visual contrast between the crude stick-figure depiction of major historical scientific moments and the increasingly hostile societal reactions to scientific discovery over time, culminating in modern protests against science itself.

# Scene Description
A black and white comic panel featuring a distressed character submerged in water inside a glass tank or container. On the left side is a control panel labeled "Thomas A. Anderson" with "IN" and "OUT" controls, a cityscape silhouette, and a yellow and gray keyboard grid (resembling a digital interface). The art style is a simple line drawing with a minimalist, comedic approach.

# Text Transcription
"WHY, OH WHY DIDN'T I TAKE THE **BLUE** PILL?"

Label: "Thomas A. Anderson"

Control panel labels: "IN" and "OUT"

# Visual Jokes/Puns
This is a reference to *The Matrix* (1999), where the protagonist Thomas Anderson (Neo) is offered a choice between a red pill (staying in the matrix) and a blue pill (returning to reality). The joke here is that the character appears to have chosen poorly and is now literally drowning—the comic literalizes the existential dread of taking the "wrong" pill by placing the character in a life-threatening situation.

# Scene Description

A four-panel xkcd webcomic in black stick-figure style. The setting is a large hadron collider control room where two scientists are communicating with researchers. A man in a hat appears in the final panels. The art style is minimalist black line drawings on white background with one panel featuring a yellow highlight box with black text.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
- Caption: "MEANWHILE, AT THE LARGE HADRON COLLIDER..."
- Figure 1: "ANY LUCK?"
- Figure 2: "NADA."

**Panel 2:**
- Figure 1: "WE'VE LITERALLY ANALYZED PETABYTES OF DATA. STILL NO SIGN OF THE HIGGS BOSON."

**Panel 3:**
- Figure 2: "WELL... I'VE BEEN SEARCHING FOR THE HIGGS BOSON FOR 20 YEARS. I GUESS I'LL HAVE TO UNIT A LITTLE LONGER."

**Panel 4:**
- Caption: "MR HIGGS SAYS HELLO!"
- Sound effect: "ACKK"

**Panel 5:**
- Sound effect: "GCRK"

**Panel 6:**
- (Silent panel showing figure alone at console)

**Yellow highlight box (bottom right):**
"You don't find the Higgs boson. The Higgs boson finds you."

# Visual Jokes

The humor relies on a "Predator" reference: the punchline mimics the famous movie tagline structure ("You don't find the Predator. The Predator finds you"), subverting the scientists' search for the Higgs boson by having the particle actively hunt them instead.

# Scene Description
A stick-figure comic in simple black and white line art depicting a conversation between two characters at what appears to be a bar or table setting. One character wears glasses; the other does not. The comic unfolds across 8 panels showing an increasingly awkward social interaction.

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"SO HERE'S MY NUMBER, GIVE ME A CALL SOME TIME."

**Panel 2:**
"GREAT, I'LL CALL YOU THIS WEEKEND."
"THIS WEEKEND? HMMMM... THAT'S A LITTLE TOO SOON."

**Panel 3:**
"I WAS THINKING OF... SAY... IN 5 YEARS."
"5 YEARS????"

**Panel 4:**
"LOOK, YOU SEEM LIKE YOU WOULD BE ABLE TO PROVIDE A STABLE NURTURING ENVIRONMENT FOR KIDS, BUT I WON'T NEED THAT UNTIL I'M IN MY 30s."

**Panel 5:**
*PASS SNACK * [sound effect]
"?"

**Panel 6:**
"C'MON, HURRY UP, TOOTS. MY HARLEY'S DOUBLE PARKED IN THE HANDICAPPED ZONE."

**Panel 7:**
"THANKS FOR THE DRINK."
"LATER, KID."
*CLICK * [sound effect]

**Panel 8:**
"OOH... SCORE."

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the unexpected shift in the character's motivation—he's not interested in dating her but rather evaluating her as a potential future mother for his children, creating an absurd and uncomfortable scenario that plays out silently until his true character is revealed in the final panels.

comics.title: 936 Little Blobs
comics.comment: That last line is directed solely at my own damn self.
comics.commentHtml: That last line is directed solely at my own damn self.
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description

A grid of 936 small gray dots representing months in a life, with scattered colored dots marking significant life milestones. The visual fills most of the upper panel. Below is explanatory text describing the chart, a legend identifying each colored dot's meaning, and a motivational closing statement.

---

# Text Transcription

**Main body text:**

LAST MONTH WENT BY PRETTY QUICKLY, DIDN'T IT? ONE MONTH IS NOT A VERY LONG TIME, BUT THE FACT OF THE MATTER IS THAT THE LIFE EXPECTANCY (IN THE UNITED STATES) IS ONLY ABOUT 936 MONTHS. LIFE SURE LOOKS DIFFERENT TO ME WHEN I CAN SEE IT OF MY LIFE DISPLAYED BEFORE ME AS 936 LITTLE BLOBS.

IF EACH BLOB REPRESENTS ONE MONTH OF YOUR LIFE TAKEN SEQUENTIALLY FROM LEFT-TO-RIGHT, TOP-TO-BOTTOM, HERE ARE SOME INTERESTING MILESTONES/STATS:

**Legend (with colored dots):**

- 🔴 MOZART WRITES HIS FIRST SYMPHONY AT THIS AGE.
- 🔴 CONGRATULATIONS! YOU ARE NOW A WOMAN/MAN.
- 🟣 AVERAGE AGE OF WOMEN AT FIRST CHILD BIRTH.
- 🔵 EINSTEIN PUBLISHES PAPER ON SPECIAL RELATIVITY AT THIS AGE.
- 🟢 AVERAGE AGE OF FIRST MARRIAGE
- 🟠 AYN RAND PUBLISHES 'THE FOUNTAINHEAD' AT THIS AGE.
- 🔴 SORRY, YOU ARE NO LONGER ELIGIBLE FOR THE FIELDS MEDAL.
- 🟢 JOHN F. KENNEDY BECOMES 35th PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES AT THIS AGE.
- 🟡 AVERAGE AGE OF FIRST TIME GRANDPARENT
- 🔵 YOU MADE IT, MANY OF YOU ARE NOW ELIGIBLE FOR SOCIAL SECURITY BENEFITS AT THIS AGE.
- ⭕ YOU'RE ON BORROWED TIME NOW.

**Closing text:**

NOTE: WHERE APPROPRIATE, THE STATISTICS PERTAIN TO THE POPULATION OF THE UNITED STATES, BUT THE NUMBERS ARE VERY SIMILAR IN MANY OTHER PARTS OF THE WORLD.

NOW GET OFF YOUR ASS AND DO SOMETHING THIS MONTH!

---

# Visual Jokes/Puns

The primary impact is visual and emotional rather than punning: the grid of 936 dots makes abstract time concrete and finite, creating a sobering perspective on mortality. The scattered colored dots emphasize how few months contain major achievements, making the clustering of milestones visually apparent. The final exhortation gains weight from seeing one's remaining "blobs" visually represented.

# Scene Description
A simple line-drawn comic showing a stick figure sitting at a computer. The figure progresses through an increasingly absurd conversation via speech bubbles, becoming more frustrated and angry, ultimately yelling at the computer while declaring they don't want to play the game anymore.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Title (top):** ARE YOU A KLINGON OR A COMPUTER?

**Speech bubbles, top to bottom:**

1. "A WARRIOR DOES NOT ASK SUCH QUESTIONS."
2. "JUST ANSWER THE QUESTION."
3. "YOU HAVE NO HONOR!"
4. "WHY WON'T YOU ANSWER THE QUESTION?"
5. "IF YOU WERE ANY OTHER MAN, I'D KILL YOU WHERE YOU STAND!"
6. "OK, OK... SO WHAT DO YOU DO FOR FUN?"
7. "I TRAVEL THE RIVER OF BLOOD!"
8. "NO, SERIOUSLY, WHAT ARE YOUR HOBBIES?"
9. "I CRAVE ONLY THE BLOOD OF THE ENEMY!"
10. "DUDE, WHY ARE YOU SO ANGRY?"
11. "RAAAWR!!!!"
12. "WHY ARE YOU YELLING AT ME?"
13. "RAAAWR!!!!"
14. "I DON'T WANT TO PLAY THIS GAME ANY MORE."
15. "RAAAWR!!!!"

**Caption (bottom):** THE FIRST SUCCESSFUL KLINGON TURING TEST

# Visual Joke
The comic plays on the Turing Test concept—a test of machine intelligence where a human judge tries to determine if they're conversing with a computer or another person. Here, the "test" is reversed: the human is trying to determine if they're talking to a Klingon (from Star Trek) or a computer program, but the responses are so absurdly Klingon-like and unchanging that the human gives up, making it a "successful" test despite their frustration.

comics.title: Fun with Statistics
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A minimalist stick-figure comic with simple black line drawings on white backgrounds. A small stick figure stands on the ground below a trampoline suspended from above. The art style is deliberately crude and humorous.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:** (No text)

**Panel 2:** (No text)

**Panel 3:** 
"I SPIT IN THE FACE OF DEATH!"

**Panel 4:** 
"ZAP" (sound effect)

**Panel 5:** (No text)

**Panel 6:**
"FACT: YOU HAVE A 39% GREATER CHANCE OF BEING STRUCK BY LIGHTNING AND THEN BEING EATEN BY A SHARK THAN YOU DO OF BEING KILLED BY A FALLING PIANO..."

# Visual Jokes
The humor depends entirely on the image: The stick figure defiantly declares they spit in the face of death while bouncing on a trampoline, is immediately struck by lightning, and then gets crushed by a falling piano anyway—the very thing the "fact" claims is statistically unlikely. The juxtaposition of the reassuring statistic with the instant, comedic subversion of it creates the joke.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A 9-panel webcomic in simple black and white line art featuring three characters sitting at a table with plates of food. The characters are simplified figures: one with a circular head (striped pattern), one with a blue circular head, and one with black hair. The comic uses minimal detail and dry humor to depict a meal prayer that becomes increasingly absurd.

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"DEAR LORD, WE THANK YOU THAT WE CAN COME TOGETHER AS A FAMILY EVERY YEAR FOR FOOD, FELLOWSHIP, AND FUN."

**Panel 2:**
"AND WE THANK YOU FOR GIVING US HUMANS DOMINION OVER THE ANIMALS."

**Panel 3:**
"THIS ALLOWS US TO RAISE COWS IN SQUALID LIVING CONDITIONS UNTIL WE SLAUGHTER THEM."

**Panel 4:**
"WE DO THIS BY APPLYING A 300 VOLT 2 AMP ELECTRIC SHOCK TO THE BACK OF THE COW'S HEAD TO KNOCK IT OUT."

**Panel 5:**
"AND THEN WE HANG IT FROM ITS HIND LEG WHILE ITS MAIN ARTERIES ARE SEVERED, ALLOWING ITS BLOOD TO DRAIN."

**Panel 6:**
"BUT I HEARD THAT ABOUT 30% OF THE COWS ARE NOT RENDERED FULLY UNCONSCIOUS BEFORE BEING STRUNG UP AND SO UNCEREMONIOUSLY EXSANGUINATED."

**Panel 7:**
"CONSIDERING THAT COWS HAVE A FAIRLY HIGH DEVELOPED NERVOUS SYSTEM, I CAN'T HELP BUT CONCLUDE THAT THAT SORT OF WRYTHING IN AGONY WOULD BE TOTALLY FUCKED UP IF YOU DID SOMETHING LIKE THAT TO CONSIDERING WHAT WE DO TO THEM."

**Panel 8:**
"SO THANK YOU, LORD, FOR NOT GIVING COWS ANY SOULS... CUZ SHIT, MAN... THAT WOULD BE TOTALLY FUCKED UP IF YOU DID."
"IN JESUS' NAME, AMEN."

**Panel 9:**
"AND I WAS NEVER ASKED TO SAY GRACE AT FAMILY REUNIONS AGAIN."
"... EVER."

# Visual Jokes
The primary humor derives from the contrast between the formal, reverent tone of a traditional meal prayer and the increasingly graphic, detailed description of industrial cattle slaughter and animal suffering. The final panel's visual shows the blue-hatted character's expression becoming progressively more uncomfortable, culminating in a silent, awkward conclusion that explains the punchline.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A simple line-drawn comic strip featuring a man in a blue shirt sitting on a stool speaking with three other figures (drawn as basic circles). The art style is minimalist stick-figure humor typical of webcomics. The comic is arranged in a 3x3 grid of panels with yellow highlight boxes around certain panels.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (yellow box, top-left):**
"This is what I heard."
"GOOD SIR, I FIND IT QUITE INEXPLICABLE."

**Panel 2:**
"WHY DO YOU INSIST ON VIEWING TV DRAMAS INVOLVING CEREBRAL PLOT LINES INVOLVING THE IMAGINING OF PROBABLE FUTURE WORLDS?..."

**Panel 3:**
"WORLDS BASED ON LOGICAL EXTRAPOLATIONS OF CURRENT SCIENTIFIC AND CULTURAL TRENDS? I BELIEVE THIS TO BE INDICATIVE OF LATENT HOMOSEXUAL TENDENCIES..."

**Panel 4:**
"...WHICH, BY IMPLICATION OF MY VOCAL INTONATION, I DEEM TO BE A SOCIALLY UNACCEPTABLE LIFE-STYLE CHOICE IF ACTED UPON."

**Panel 5:**
"ME? I MUCH PREFER WATCHING A GROUP OF BUFF MEN IN TIGHTS ENGAGING IN A MASSIVE HOMOEROTIC DOGGIE PILE"

**Panel 6:**
"...ALL WHILE IN THE COMPANY OF MY SLIGHTLY-INEBRIATED, MALE-ONLY FRIENDS."

**Panel 7:**
"IN FACT, I HAVE AN INSATIABLE URGE TO DO SO RIGHT NOW..."

**Panel 8:**
"SO, WITHOUT FURTHER ADO, I TAKE MY LEAVE OF YOU, GOOD SIR."

**Panel 9:**
"HA HA HA HA" (repeated by three figures)

**Panel 10 (yellow box, bottom-left):**
"But I'm pretty sure *this* is what he said."
"DUDE, WHY ARE YOU WATCHING STAR TREK? THAT'S SO GAY!"

**Panel 11:**
"C'MON, GUYS. LET'S GO WATCH SOME FOOTBALL."

**Panel 12:**
"HA HA HA HA" (repeated by three figures)

# Visual Jokes
The comic's humor relies on the contrast between what someone *actually said* (Panel 10: crude mockery about watching Star Trek) versus what the man *heard it as* (Panels 1-9): an elaborate, formal critique using pseudo-intellectual language. The punchline exposes the irony that watching football (traditionally masculine) is equally homoerotic as watching sci-fi, yet socially acceptable—a satire of arbitrary masculine social codes.

comics.title: All Good Things...
comics.transcript:
# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A stick-figure style webcomic depicting the life trajectory of two characters (one in blue, one in red) from childhood through adulthood. The comic uses simple line drawings with minimalist style. The narrative follows parallel paths shown with blue arrows (for the blue character) and red arrows (for the red character) that eventually converge at the bottom. Key scenes include classroom settings, outdoor play, adolescence, education, career, relationships, and finally a hospital bed scene.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Top panel (two scenes):**
- Scene 1: "GOO." / "GAH."
- Scene 2: "GAH." / "GOO."

**Middle sections (following the blue arrow path):**
- "COLLEGE"
- "DUDE, THAT SUCKED."

**Bottom panel (final scene):**
- Left character: "DUDE, THAT SUCKED."
- Right character: "YEAH, TOTALLY BLEW. I"

## Visual Jokes
The primary visual joke is the cyclical nature of the narrative: it begins with two babies making reciprocal sounds ("GOO" and "GAH"), and concludes with two elderly people in hospital beds expressing the same sentiment about life ("DUDE, THAT SUCKED" / "YEAH, TOTALLY BLEW"). The joke is a darkly humorous commentary on the futility of life's journey—despite decades of experiences (education, careers, relationships), the characters end up back where they started, communicating in simple terms and expressing regret.

# Scene Description
A simple black and white stick figure comic with two characters: a woman on the left with long hair and a man on the right with a round head. They are having a conversation across six panels, with the woman appearing increasingly amused throughout the exchange.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"ANGELINA, CHARLIZE, AND JESSICA."

**Panel 2:**
"JESSICA SIMPSON?"
"JESSICA BIEL."

**Panel 3:**
"OK, HONEY, I PROMISE... IF YOU EVER HAVE A CHANCE TO SLEEP WITH ANY OF THOSE CELEBRITIES, I'LL GIVE YOU A FREE PASS."

**Panel 4:**
"HA HA HA HA"

**Panel 5:**
"OK, LET'S TAKE A LOOK AT YOUR LIST."

**Panel 6:**
"BRAD, LEONARDO, AND GEORGE."

**Panel 7:**
"GEORGE CLOONEY?"
"GEORGE THE POOL BOY."

**Panel 8:**
(Silent panel - man looks uncomfortable)

**Panel 9:**
"YEAAH,... AND I'M GOING TO HAVE TO ASK YOU TO GO AHEAD AND MAKE THAT ONE RETROACTIVE."

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the wife's expectations being subverted—she assumed her husband would list conventionally attractive female celebrities, but his list includes a pool boy (presumably their employee), making the wife's generous "free pass" offer embarrassingly backfire.

comics.title: stop me if you've heard this one
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A six-panel stick figure comic in black and white. The art style is minimalist line drawings. The setting appears to be a bar. Three characters are present throughout: a bald man, another man with simple features, and a woman with long hair.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (Caption):**
"SO AN ENGINEER, A PHYSICIST, AND A MATHEMATICIAN WALK INTO A BAR..."

**Panel 3:**
QUAFF
QUAFF
QUAFF

**Panel 4:**
(No text)

**Panel 5:**
(No text)

**Panel 6:**
BRAINS!
BRAIINNS
BRAIINNS
BRAIINNS

**Bottom right:**
That is all.

# Visual Jokes
The comic subverts the setup of a classic joke format. Instead of delivering a punchline based on wordplay or professional stereotypes, the three professionals simply drink and then transform into zombies chanting "BRAINS!" — a literal, physical interpretation that mocks both the joke format itself and zombie tropes. The humor derives from the unexpected tonal shift and the absurdist subversion of audience expectations.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A webcomic in simple black-and-white stick figure style. The scene is set on stairs where a stick figure (scientist) stands facing a group of superheroes (Batman, Superman, The Flash, and Wonder Woman) sitting on the steps. The scientist wears yellow, while the heroes are depicted in their characteristic colors. The comic shows a conversation that gradually reveals an ironic contrast between the heroes' impressive powers and the scientist's actual contributions to humanity.

# Dialogue Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
- Scientist: "THANK YOU BOTH FOR APPLYING TO THE LEAGUE OF SUPERCOOL HEROES."
- Batman: "UNFORTUNATELY, WE ONLY HAVE ROOM FOR ONE MORE MEMBER. SO LET'S START WITH YOU, SCIENTIST! DO YOU HAVE ANY COOL POWERS?"

**Panel 2:**
- Scientist: "WELL, I'M A SCIENTIST. SCIENCE IS ALL ABOUT INCREASING HUMAN KNOWLEDGE... AND KNOWLEDGE IS POWER."
- Batman: "YEAH, BUT, LIKE, ...CAN YOU FLY OR ANYTHING?"

**Panel 3:**
- Scientist: "I HELPED DEVELOP THE PRINCIPLES OF AERODYNAMICS WHICH ALLOWED US TO MASTER WINGED FLIGHT."
- Superman: "WE DISCOVERED THE PRINCIPLES OF GRAVITY AND MOTION THAT ALLOWED US TO LAND ON THE MOON."

**Panel 4:**
- Batman: "BUT HAVE YOU SAVED ANY DAMSELS IN DISTRESS LATELY?"
- Superman: "IT WAS THROUGH SCIENCE THAT WE DISCOVERED PENICILLIN, THE POLIO AND SMALLPOX VACCINES, AND MANY OTHER MEDICAL ADVANCES."

**Panel 5:**
- Batman: "AND WITH THE ADVENT OF NEW TECHNOLOGIES SUCH AS GENE THERAPY, RNAI THERAPY, AND SYNTHETIC BIOLOGY..."
- Superman: "...WE MAY SOON ERADICATE MANY OF THE MOST COMMON DISEASES KNOWN TODAY."

**Panel 6:**
- Scientist: "SO, YES, IN FACT I HAVE SAVED MILLIONS OF LIVES."
- Batman: "IN FACT, I BELIEVE THAT THROUGH THE POWER OF SCIENCE WE WILL ONE DAY ELIMINATE POVERTY, DISEASE AND POSSIBLY EVEN WAR."

**Panel 7:**
- Scientist: "SO YES, THROUGH SCIENCE, WE WILL ALLEVIATE MUCH OF THE SUFFERING IN THE WORLD AND SAVE BILLIONS OF LIVES."
- [Empty panel on right]

**Panel 8:**
- Scientist: "OK,... WHAT ABOUT YOU, AQUAMAN? DO YOU HAVE ANY COOL POWERS?"
- Aquaman: "I CAN TALK TO FISH."

**Panel 9:**
- [Silent panel showing all superheroes looking embarrassed, with Aquaman looking dejected]

# Visual Jokes/Puns
- **Ironic contrast**: The scientist, despite having no superhuman powers, has demonstrably saved far more lives and benefited humanity more than the superheroes through scientific achievements (vaccines, flight, medical advances). Yet the superheroes initially dismiss the scientist for lacking flashy powers.
- **Aquaman's inadequacy**: The punchline reveals that Aquaman's superpower (talking to fish) is absurdly useless compared to the scientist's contributions, making the heroes' earlier dismissal of the scientist even more ridiculous.
- **Knowledge is power**: The scientist's opening statement about knowledge being power becomes literally proven true through the rest of the comic.

# Scene Description
A two-panel comic strip featuring two stick-figure characters (one with a blue circular head, one with a black circular head) discussing a mathematical discovery. The art style is simple black and white line drawings with minimal detail. The characters are drawn as basic circles with limbs, shown in conversation across nine panels that progressively reveal an equation.

# Dialogue and Text (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
- Blue character: "HEY, I JUST FOUND A WAY TO EXPRESS CALABI-YAU MANIFOLDS IN A WAY THAT GOES BEYOND A NON-VANISHING HARMONIC SPINOR."

**Panel 2:**
- Black character: "CHECK IT. BE PREPARED TO BE AMAZED. THIS IS GONNA BLOW YOUR MIND."

**Panel 3:**
- Black character: "THIS IS GONNA TOTALLY BAKE YOUR NOODLE. THIS IS GONNA..."

**Panels 4-9:**
The equation progressively appears:
- Panel 4: "10² + 11²"
- Panels 5-9: "10² + 11² + 12² = 13² + 14²"

**Panel 9 (final):**
- Blue character: "HOLY SHIT."

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the buildup of grandiose mathematical language about complex geometry, only to reveal a simple integer equation—an anticlimactic subversion of expectations. The repeated "this is gonna..." construction emphasizes the false promise of mind-blowing complexity.

# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure webcomic in black and white, depicting two characters in conversation while a third character lies on the ground. The art style is minimalist with basic line drawings. The setting appears to be indoors with plain backgrounds.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- Character 1: "mmmm"
- Character 1: "$"

**Panel 2:**
- Character 1: "Oh Infinity... thou art a heartless bitch"
- Character 2: "$"

**Panel 3:**
- Character 1: "WHAT'S THE MATTER WITH HIM?"
- Character 2: "There is no Axiom of Choice"
- Character 3: "$"

**Panel 4:**
- Character 2: "HE SPENT THE LAST FIVE WEEKS TRYING TO PROVE THE CONTINUUM HYPOTHESIS."
- Character 1: "urm..."
- Character 3: "$"

**Panel 5:**
- Character 1: "AND THEN HIS MIND JUST SNAPPED."
- Character 2: "Hilbert Space is so pretty"
- Character 3: "$"

**Panel 6:**
- Character 1: "NOW HE'S JUST BABBLING INCOHERENTLY."
- Character 2: "the integers are so lonely"
- Character 3: "$"

**Panel 7:**
- Character 1: "I GUESS THE MYSTERIES OF INFINITY JUST AREN'T MEANT TO BE COMPREHENDED BY FINITE MINDS."
- Character 2: "take me to the Aleph Garden"
- Character 3: "$"

**Panel 8:**
- Character 1: "I'M STARTING TO GET WORRIED ABOUT HIM."
- Character 2: (silent)

**Panel 9:**
- Character 1: "POOR BASTARD BIT OFF MORE THAN HE COULD CHEW."
- Character 2: "I can see Russia"

# Visual Jokes
The visual joke depends on the prone character's distressed state ($) being presented as a humorous consequence of mathematical obsession. The comedy derives from the escalating absurdity of his muttered statements as his mental state deteriorates, treating abstract mathematical concepts with emotional weight ("Hilbert Space is so pretty," "the integers are so lonely").
