# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure comic in black ink showing a character discovering cupcakes on a table in what appears to be an empty room. The art style is minimalist with simple circular heads, basic body lines, and small triangular cupcake shapes. The character progresses from confusion to eating the cupcakes to discovering ghost-like figures among them.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
(Question mark above character's head)

**Panel 2:**
"HMM... WHO PUT ALL THESE CUPCAKES HERE? THERE MUST BE A SEMINAR SCHEDULED IN THIS ROOM."

**Panel 3:**
"I GUESS IT CAN'T HURT TO HAVE JUST ONE."

**Panel 4:**
(Exclamation mark above character's head)

**Panel 5:**
(No dialogue)

# Visual Joke
The humor relies on the visual revelation in the final panel: the "cupcakes" are actually ghost-like figures (depicted with simple rounded tops resembling the cupcake shapes). The character has been eating ghosts instead of cupcakes, which is why the figure looks shocked in panel 4, and why they appear distressed in panel 5 while surrounded by the ghost-cupcake hybrids.

# Scene Description
A two-part comic strip in black and white line art. The first part features three anthropomorphic cheetahs or leopards in grassland discussing hunting gazelles. The second part shows a woman doing laundry at a washing machine, with a man giving her advice.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

"YO, CHECK OUT THOSE GAZELLES."
"FRESH MEAT."

**Panel 2:**

# Scene Description

A stick-figure webcomic with 10 panels showing two round-headed characters in various situations. The art style is minimalist black line drawings on white backgrounds, with occasional red accents for blood or injury. The final panel is a parody video game advertisement in the style of Grand Theft Auto.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"DARMOK AND JALAD... AT TANAGRA."

**Panel 2:**
"DARMOK AND JALAD... ON THE OCEAN."

**Panel 3:**
"WHAT? SPEAK IN PROPER LANGUAGE, MAN."

**Panel 4:**
"BAL AND JIRI... AT LUNGHA."

**Panel 5:**
"MAYBE YOU'LL UNDERSTAND THIS."
"KITEO, HIS EYES CLOSED!"

**Panel 6:**
"SAY HELLO TO MY LITTLE FRIEND."
"MIRAB, HIS SAILS UNFURLED!"

**Panel 7:**
"UHUMP UHUMP UHUMP"
"SHAKA, WHEN THE WALLS FELL."

**Panel 8:**
"THAT'LL LEARN YA."

**Panel 9:**
"CAPTAIN, WE'VE ARRIVED AT BAJOR."
"ON MY WAY, NUMBER ONE."

**Panel 10:**
"COMPUTER, END PROGRAM."

**Bottom Advertisement:**
"GTA CXLIII
COMING SUMMER 2369"

# Visual Jokes

The comic is a reference to the *Star Trek: The Next Generation* episode "Darmok," where an alien species communicates only through metaphorical phrases. The joke escalates from peaceful cultural exchange to violence, ending with a parody suggesting a violent Grand Theft Auto game set in the Star Trek universe during the year 2369 (the TNG era).

# Scene Description
A simple line-drawn comic in a minimalist style showing two figures lying in bed together. The figure on the left wears a blue baseball cap and has a bandaged arm. The figure on the right has long black hair. Both have simple dot eyes and smile lines. On the right side of the image, there are sketched lines suggesting movement or activity happening outside the bed/window.

# Text Transcription
"SIKE."

# Visual Joke
The humor relies on the visual setup: the comic initially appears to depict an intimate moment between two people in bed, but the word "SIKE" (slang for "psych" or "just kidding") subverts this expectation. The sketchy lines on the right side suggest something else is actually happening—implying the scene is not what it initially appeared to be, creating a bait-and-switch joke.

A nine-panel black and white comic strip featuring a simple stick-figure art style with a desktop computer (appears to be from the 1980s-90s era) as the central character. The computer has a simple face depicted through its monitor. Human characters appear in later panels as simple line drawings.

# Webcomic Transcription: "Things I Know Are True (But Don't Really Believe)"

## Scene Description
A single-panel educational webcomic in black ink on white background, featuring a stick-figure character in blue presenting various scientific and mathematical concepts. The character appears throughout in different scenarios illustrating each concept. The art style is minimalist with simple line drawings, diagrams, and mathematical notations.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Header:**
"There are many things that I know are true ..."

---

**QUANTUM ENTANGLEMENT:**

[Diagram showing two stick figures with particles]

"IF A PAIR CREATES TWO ENTANGLED PHOTONS AND I MEASURE ONE..."

[Second panel shows figures separated]
"...INSTANTANEOUSLY THE OTHER 'KNOWS' ABOUT MY MEASUREMENT ACROSS ANY DISTANCE"

[Character response, looking uncertain]
"EINSTEIN 'DIDN'T LIKE IT' EITHER"

"BUT THE MATH WORKS OUT"

---

**PACKET SWITCHING:**

"The internet is largely a packet switched network. The contents of this web page you are viewing was transported through the tubes as separate little packets (possibly) out of sequence and then reassembled before being displayed."

[Diagram showing packets traveling through network tubes from computer]

"INCONCEIVABLE!"

---

**(EXTREME) INTERSPECIES VARIATION:**

[Diagram comparing human figures]
"Drosophila Olfactory, Yao Ming, and I are the same species"

"NOT DRAWN TO SCALE BUT A ROUGH APPROXIMATION"

[Two figures of different heights]

"Gisele Bündchen and I are the same species."

[Two figures shown together]
"YOU KNOW, TECHNICALLY, YOU AND I COULD MAKE A FERTILE OFFSPRING"

"NO."

---

**THE WELL-ORDERING THEOREM:**

[Two stick figures in conversation]
"WELL THEN, EVERY SET CAN BE WELL-ORDERED ON SET?"

"WHY NOT?"

[Response]
"NO FUCK YOU"

---

**NONEXISTENCE:**

"There once was a time when I did not exist... and there will be a time when I will not exist again."

[Timeline diagram showing:]
"~13.7 BILLION YEARS AGO (BIG BANG)"
"[gap]"
"PRESENT DAY"
"[gap]"
"~10^100 YEARS FROM NOW (HEAT DEATH OF UNIVERSE)"

---

**AERODYNAMICS:**

"Every 300 million people sit in a small metal tube 35,000 feet in the air traveling at 500 MPH eating honey roasted peanuts."

[Diagram showing aircraft at high altitude with annotations]
"P = ρ (1/2) v² ρ A + ..."

[Cloud formation shown below]

---

**EULER'S IDENTITY:**

[Mathematical notation box]
"e^(iπ) + 1 = 0"

[Stick figure speaking]
"LIGHT THE HELL UP THIS IS BASICALLY SAYING THE FOUNDATION OF THE NATURAL WORLD IS BASED ON THE NATURAL LOGARITHM AND π"

---

**RELATIVE SIZES:**

"Andromeda galaxy says the size of a grain of salt is visible the closest galaxy to us."

"If our solar system were the size of a grain of salt, the Milky Way would be 2 miles in diameter."

"If Earth were the size of a grain of salt, our solar system would be 2 football fields across."

"If a hydrogen atom was the size of a football field, its nucleus would be the size of a grain of salt."

"If the nucleus of a hydrogen atom was the size of a football field a neutron would be about the size of a grain of salt."

"If a hydrogen atom was the size of our solar system, a neutron would be about the size of an average star."

[Mathematical notation and equation]
"... ≈ 3 × 10^(-7) × 10^(-42) × ..."

"INCONCEIVABLE! = E = √-1"

---

**Footer:**
"... but I don't really believe."

## Visual Jokes/Puns Dependent on Image

1. **Quantum Entanglement**: The character's skepticism mirrors Einstein's famous quote, "spooky action at a

# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure comic in black and white with minimal detail, depicting a person discovering a cat in a box. The art style is minimalist line drawing typical of internet webcomics.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (top left):**
- Label: "...1935"
- Equation: |ψ⟩ = ½|LIVE⟩ + ½|DEAD⟩/|CAT⟩

**Panel 2 (top center):**
- Dialogue: "MEOW"

**Panel 3 (top right):**
- [No text]

**Panel 4 (middle left):**
- [No text]

**Panel 5 (middle center):**
- Dialogue: "MEOW"

**Panel 6 (right side):**
- Dialogue: "I'LL NEVER PUT YOU IN DANGER AGAIN."
- Sound effect: "*SNIFF*"

**Bottom banner (yellow):**
- Caption: "Happiness is a live cat."

# Visual Jokes
This is a reference to Schrödinger's cat, the famous 1935 thought experiment about quantum superposition. The comic plays on the philosophical paradox by showing the person's emotional relief at discovering the cat is alive, with the punchline being a pun on the saying "Happiness is a warm puppy" (Charles M. Schulz).

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A black and white comic strip with 6 panels showing a humorous take on the Big Bang and the origin of the universe. The art style is simple line drawings. The comic features stick figures representing early subatomic particles or matter, depicted as cartoon characters with hats performing in a musical chorus line. The setting progresses from scientific concepts to a theatrical performance metaphor.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (top left):**
"Physicists believe that they know what happened 10^-43 seconds after the big bang. But what happened before that?"

**Panel 2 (top middle):**
[Silent panel with black background]

**Panel 3 (top right):**
"AZIZ, LIGHT!"

**Panel 4 (middle left):**
"BAME"
[with a bright spotlight effect]

**Panel 5 (middle center):**
"OH, WE'RE THE BOYS OF THE CHORUS"

**Panel 6 (middle right):**
"WE HOPE YOU LIKE OUR SHOW"

**Panel 7 (bottom left):**
"WE KNOW YOU'RE ROOTING FOR US"

**Panel 8 (bottom center):**
"BUT NOW WE HAVE TO GO!"

**Panel 9 (bottom right):**
"We may never know."

## Visual Jokes
- References *The Fifth Element* ("Aziz, light!") to humorously suggest the Big Bang was a theatrical performance
- The subatomic particles are depicted as a vaudeville chorus line, turning fundamental physics into entertainment
- The punchline conflates unknowable cosmic origins with a broadway show that leaves before the audience learns what it was

# Scene Description
A three-panel webcomic in simple black-and-white stick figure style. The first two panels show a stick figure at a desk with a time machine. The third panel is a large circular diagram representing time periods, with multiple stick figures positioned around it as if experiencing different eras simultaneously.

# Text Transcription

**Panel 1:**
"I FINALLY FINISHED MY TIME MACHINE."

**Panel 2:**
"WHERE SHOULD I VISIT FIRST? MAYBE I'LL GO SEE SOME DINOSAURS."

**Panel 3 (Circular diagram, reading clockwise from top):**
- "DON'T DO IT!"
- "JUST COME BACK IN THE FUTURE TO WARN YOU."
- "THE FUTURE ISN'T WHAT YOU WARNED ME ABOUT."
- "WHY DIDN'T YOU WARN ME?"
- "I TRIED TO WARN YOU ABOUT THIS."
- "THERE IS NO PROBLEM!"
- "KA-WHOOSH"
- "GO BACK AND TELL YOURSELF NOT TO MAKE THE MACHINE."
- "I BUILT IT BECAUSE OF YOUR WARNING."
- "THE ONLY SOLUTION VISIT ANCIENT ROME TO SEE IF I DISSIPATE THERE."
- "HUH?"
- "NO PROBLEM."
- "MANY I" (partially visible/unclear)

# Visual Jokes
The main joke is a temporal paradox loop: the figure's attempt to prevent a problem with the time machine by warning their past self actually creates the problem, trapping them in an endless causal loop depicted by the circular arrangement of figures and dialogue.

# Scene Description
A three-panel webcomic in simple stick-figure style with a yellow header box. The comic shows a character at a computer in a minimalist black-and-white drawing style with occasional color accents (red bacon strips in panel 3).

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (Header):** "What's new in Python 3.0?"

**Panel 1 (Main):** ">>>"

**Panel 2:** ">>> import bacon"

**Panel 3 (Footer):** "...awesomeness."

# Visual Joke
The humor is a visual pun combining two elements: 
1. The Python programming language's convention of "importing" modules/libraries with the `import` command
2. The literal visual appearance of bacon strips appearing on screen after executing `import bacon`

The joke plays on the double meaning of "import" — the programming instruction versus the literal importation of bacon. The character's delighted expression in panel 3 reinforces the absurdist humor of a bacon module existing in Python, making programming "awesome."

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A worksheet-style image featuring 16 hand turkey drawings (A through P) in various artistic styles and with different decorative elements. Each represents a different person's interpretation of a hand turkey drawing. The drawings range from simple and crude to elaborate and artistic, presented in a grid layout.

# Text Transcription
**Title (top):** Can you guess who drew each of the following hand turkeys?

**Label L:** assume a turkey

# Visual Elements & Jokes

The humor relies entirely on visual interpretation:

- **A, D, F, G:** Simple, basic hand turkey outlines with minimal detail
- **B:** Elaborate hand turkey with detailed feather segments and decorative circles
- **C:** Minimalist hand turkey with simple bird sketch
- **E:** Hand turkey drawn as a prone/horizontal figure
- **H:** Hand turkey with exaggerated pointed feathers
- **I:** Extremely minimalist—just radiating lines with a small circle
- **J:** Blank (no drawing visible)
- **K:** Hand turkey with detailed decorative pattern/shading inside
- **L:** Labeled "assume a turkey"—just a plain hand outline
- **M:** Hand turkey drawn from an unusual perspective/angle
- **N:** Hand turkey with red splatter/paint effect
- **O:** Crescent moon shape with minimal turkey features
- **P:** Chaotic multicolored scribbled lines

**Visual Joke:** The humor derives from the wide variation in artistic skill, style, and interpretation—suggesting these were drawn by different people (possibly children vs. adults, or people with different artistic abilities).

The art style is minimalist line drawings in black and white, with color used sparingly for emphasis (blue water, green land, red tree, yellow fireworks/light effects). The first two panels show a figure on a hillside at night asking existential questions. Subsequent panels transition to educational/scientific imagery (mathematical equations, pyramids, telescopes, DNA helixes, circuit boards, space imagery) before returning to the original scene. The narrative progresses from personal inquiry to cosmic speculation to technological advancement and back to humility.

# Scene Description
A three-panel comic strip in black and white (except for the third panel, which is colored in blue). The art style is simple, sketch-like, resembling early Peanuts comics. A round-headed child character is shown in a grass field setting.

# Text Transcription
**Panel 1:** (No text)

**Panel 2:** "PFF" (sound effect from the child's mouth)

**Panel 3:** Japanese characters in top right corner (artist signature/date)

# Visual Jokes
The comic's humor relies on the visual progression: In panel 1, the child stands normally. In panel 2, the child makes a "PFF" sound effect while blowing, shown with motion lines radiating outward. In panel 3, the child has transformed into a blue figure—the visual gag suggests the child has literally "puffed up" or inflated themselves, creating a comedic before-and-after transformation based entirely on the imagery rather than dialogue.

# Scene Description

A vertical flowchart-style comic in simple black and white line art depicting the journey of oil from extraction to modern life. The art style is minimalist and humorous, showing the geological and industrial process of fossil fuels, culminating in two small dinosaurs at the bottom observing the consequences.

# Text Transcription

**Top panel:** "WEEEEEEE!!!"

**Bottom panels (reading order):**
- "GAS" (label on gas pump)
- "I DUNNO, CARL. I JUST THOUGHT IT WAS SOME KINDA NEAT GLOWING POWDER IN THE SKY." (dialogue from one dinosaur to another)

# Visual Jokes

1. **The opening scene** shows a car driving away making a "WEEE" sound, establishing a joyful tone that contrasts with what follows

2. **The ironic juxtaposition** - The comic traces oil from modern car fuel back through gas stations, oil rigs, underground geological layers, fossilized remains of dinosaurs, and living dinosaurs. The final panel reveals the two conversing dinosaurs are the actual ancestors of the fuel being used, completely unaware of their ultimate fate and current use

3. **Carl's oblivious comment** serves as dark comedy - the dinosaurs are literally discussing what will become of them, with no understanding of the environmental and extinction implications of the process shown above

# Scene Description
A simple line-drawn comic showing two people in bed. One person with dark hair is speaking to another person wearing a blue baseball cap. The art style is minimalist black and white sketch.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Person with dark hair:**
"I'M SORRY, I THOUGHT GUYS LIKED IT WHEN WE DID... THAT."

**Person in blue cap:**
"WE DO... BUT..."

**Person in blue cap (continued, in thought bubble):**
"...BUT WHERE DID SHE LEARN HOW TO DO THAT AND HOW DID SHE GET SO GOOD AT IT? WHICH ONE OF HER DOUCHEBAG EX-BOYFRIENDS TAUGHT HER THAT? WAS IT CHIP, THAT GUY WITH THE BARBED WIRE TATTOO?...OR WAS IT JAKE, THE SEMI-PRO FOOTBALL PLAYER WITH THE MOHAWK? MAYBE SHE JUST WATCHES A LOT OF PORN; OR MAYBE..."

# Visual Jokes
The humor derives from the contrast between the man's polite external response ("we do... but") and his spiraling internal monologue of jealous speculation about his partner's sexual history and skills, which becomes increasingly absurd and detailed.

# Scene Description
A multi-panel comic in simple stick-figure style depicting an escalating disaster scenario. The setting progresses from a power station control room to a map of the United States to a global view, showing the cascading consequences of a decision made by workers.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (Top):**
- "POWER STATION" (label on building)
- Person 1: "OVERLOAD! OVERLOAD! SHUT IT DOWN!"
- Person 2: "BUT THAT WILL BLACK OUT THE ENTIRE EASTERN SEABOARD."
- Person 3: "DO IT!"

**Panel 2 (Middle):**
- Arrow pointing downward
- Map of USA with eastern region colored black
- Caption: "PROBABLE EMP ATTACK. LAUNCHING COUNTERATTACK."
- Label on equipment: "DEFENSE DEFENSE WOPR" (on computer)

**Panel 3 (Bottom):**
- Arrow pointing downward
- Image of Earth with multiple explosion clouds surrounding it

# Visual Jokes
The comic is a satirical chain-reaction sequence: shutting down the power station to prevent overload inadvertently triggers a military defense system's automated response, which launches a counterattack against the perceived threat, resulting in global nuclear warfare. The humor derives from the escalating absurdity and the ironic consequences of trying to prevent one disaster.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A simple stick figure comic with two characters - one wearing a blue helmet/cap and one with black hair - repeatedly passing through each other. The art style is minimalist line drawing on white background. The comic is arranged in a 3x3 grid of panels.

## Text Transcription

**Panel 1 (top left):**
"This happens to me about once a week."

**Panel 7 (bottom left):**
"OMG, I THINK I FINALLY UNDERSTAND QUANTUM MECHANICS."
"REALLY?"

**Panel 8 (bottom center):**
"NO, WAIT. IT PASSED."

## Visual Joke
The humor relies entirely on the visual repetition: two figures keep passing through each other's bodies in identical positions across multiple panels. The joke is that the character finally grasps quantum mechanics (the concept of particles occupying the same space/superposition) while experiencing this phenomenon, but loses understanding almost immediately once it stops happening. The joke depends on the image showing the surreal act of two solid objects interpenetrating repeatedly.

# Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic in black, white, and yellow depicting a lonely character's Christmas Eve experience. The art style is minimalist line drawings with simple geometric shapes. The narrative follows the protagonist's emotional journey from isolation to defiant self-assertion.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"I have an annual holiday tradition."

**Panel 2:**
"Every Christmas, at night..."

**Panel 3:**
"...I take a walk around my neighborhood alone."

**Panel 4:**
"It's the loneliest night of the year."

**Panel 5:**
"The streets are never emptier than on Christmas night."

**Panel 6:**
"Sometimes, I peer into the windows of the houses I pass."

**Panel 7:**
"Everybody seems so happy on this special night, surrounded by the warmth of family and friends."

**Panel 8:**
"...as if the universe refuses to acknowledge my existence."

**Panel 9:**
"One time, I saw a young couple walking the streets, too,..."

**Panel 10:**
"...lost in their own cocoon of happiness and joy, completely oblivious to my presence."

**Panel 11:**
"It makes me feel... insignificant."

**Panel 12:**
"ACKNOWLEDGE MY EXISTENCE! / ACKNOWLEDGE MY EXISTENCE!"

**Panel 13:**
"I EXIST, DAD GAMMI!!!"

**Panel 14:**
"I may be insignificant..."

**Panel 15:**
"MERRY F*CKING CHRISTMAS!"

**Panel 16:**
"...but the universe will NOT ignore me."

# Visual Jokes
The humor escalates from quiet desperation to absurd, shouting defiance. The final panels show the character's emotional catharsis through increasingly frantic gestures and profanity, contrasting sharply with the melancholic isolation of earlier panels.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A three-panel black and white stick figure comic in the style of XKCD. Each panel shows two simplified characters in conversation, with the left character presenting calculations and formulas on a whiteboard or paper while the right character listens. The art style is minimalist with simple circles for heads and basic line drawings.

# Text Transcription

**Panel 1 (Left side):**
- Label: "EFFECT ON EVIL PLAN"
- Equations:
  - KE = ½(6.16×10^24 kg)(1.21×10^5 m/s)²
  - = 3.7×10^34 J
  - P = 1.2 × 10^25 W

**Panel 2 (Middle):**
- Label: "DARK FORCE (ENERGY)"
- Equations:
  - S = ∫d⁴x√-g[¹⁄₁₆πR - ½gᵘᵛ∂ᵤφ∂ᵥφ - V(φ)]
  - ∂ + ½∂̄∂ + ³⁄₈ = 0, V= g²/φ
  - P = 4π·7×10^25 W
- Label: "EFFECT ON EVIL PLAN" with "= ∞"

**Panel 3 (Right):**
- Dialogue: "YOU'RE RIGHT, LORD VADER, THE ABILITY TO DESTROY A PLANET IS NOT STATISTICALLY SIGNIFICANT CAN'T NEXT TO THE POWER OF THE FORCE."

# Visual Jokes
The comic's humor derives from applying rigorous physics calculations to Star Wars lore, juxtaposing the Death Star's destructive power with theoretical dark force energy. The punchline suggests that in terms of actual energy output, the Death Star is negligible compared to "the Force," making its planet-destroying capability statistically insignificant.

# Scene Description
A stick figure webcomic in black and white using simple line drawings. The comic depicts a robbery gone wrong that escalates into an absurd mathematical lesson. Two main characters: a stick figure robber (with a gun) and another stick figure (representing an authority figure or teacher).

---

# Dialogue and Text (in reading order)

**Panel 1:** (Silent - robber stands alone)

**Panel 2:** 
- Robber: "STICK 'EM UP!"

**Panel 3:**
- Victim: "PLEASE DON'T SHOOT, JUST TAKE MY WALLET!"
- Robber: "RELAX. I DON'T WANT YOUR MONEY."

**Panel 4:**
- Robber: "I WANT INFORMATION."

**Panel 5:**
- Robber: "WHAT DOES THE FISCHER-GRIESS MONSTER LOOK LIKE?"
- Victim: "WHAT?"

**Panel 6:** (Silent)

**Panel 7:**
- Victim: "WHAT COUNTRY ARE YOU FROM?"
- Robber: "WHAT?"

**Panel 8:**
- Victim: "'WHAT' AIN'T NO COUNTRY I EVER HEARD OF. THEY SPEAK ENGLISH IN 'WHAT'?"
- Robber: "WHAT?"

**Panel 9:**
- Robber: "ENGLISH, MOTHERFUCKER! DO YOU SPEAK IT?!"
- Victim: "YES!"

**Panel 10:**
- Robber: "THEN YOU KNOW WHAT I'M SAYING. DESCRIBE THE FISCHER-GRIESS MONSTER!"
- Victim: "WHAT?"

**Panel 11:**
- Robber: "SAY 'WHAT' AGAIN! I DARE YOU! I DOUBLE-DARE YOU, MOTHERFUCKER I SAY 'WHAT' ONE MORE GODDAMN TIME!"
- Victim: (appears frightened)

**Panel 12:**
- Victim: "I-I-IT'S A FINITE GROUP."
- Robber: "GO ON!"

**Panel 13:**
- Victim: "IT'S THE LARGEST OF THE SPORADIC GROUPS."
- Robber: "DOES IT LOOK LIKE A BITCH?"

**Panel 14:**
- Victim: "WHAT?"
- (Robber shoots victim with "BANG")

**Panel 15:**
- Victim (dying): "OOOOH"
- Robber walks away

**Panel 16:**
- Victim (on phone): "911 EMERGENCY. HELP, I'VE BEEN SHOT."

**Panel 17:**
- Dispatcher: "WE'LL SEND HELP RIGHT AWAY, BUT FIRST, WHAT'S THE ORDER OF THE MONSTER GROUP?"
- Victim: "WHAT? HOW SHOULD I KNOW?"

**Panel 18:**
- Dispatcher: "I'M SORRY, SIR. THIS LINE IS RESERVED FOR PEOPLE WHO KNOW ELEMENTARY GROUP THEORY."
- Victim: "WHAT?"
- (*CLICK*)

**Panel 19:**
- Dispatcher (to young child): "AND THAT IS THE REASON, SON."

**Panel 20:**
- Parent: "THAT'S WHY YOU NEED TO LEARN MATH."

**Panel 21:**
- Parent: "NOW GO TO YOUR ROOM AND DO YOUR HOMEWORK."

---

# Visual Jokes and Puns

1. **Tarantino Homage**: The entire opening sequence parodies Quentin Tarantino's dialogue style, particularly the "What" scene from *Pulp Fiction*, but applied absurdly to a mathematical robbery.

2. **Mathematical Escalation**: A robber demands information about the Fischer-Griess Monster (a legitimate mathematical object—the largest sporadic finite simple group) instead of money, creating cognitive dissonance.

3. **Semantic Loop**: The victim repeatedly says "What?" which escalates the robber's violence in a dark comedic cycle that mirrors the famous Tarantino scene structure.

4. **Morbid Punchline**: The dispatcher refusing to help because the victim doesn't know group theory, treating mathematical literacy as a matter of life and death.

5. **Parental Moral**: The comic concludes with a parent using the shooting as

# Scene Description
A horizontal comic strip in simple stick-figure style showing a New Year's countdown sequence. The first panel shows three stick figures counting down (3, 2, 1), followed by successive panels showing increasingly compressed/distorted figures, progressing to vertical lines, then ending with a colorful celebration scene featuring the number 2009 and party elements. Below is a caption in handwritten-style text.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)
- "... 3, 2, 1, ..."
- "... 2 ..."
- "1"
- "2009" (in colored numerals)
- "MAY YOUR NEW YEAR CONVERGE TO INTERESTING TIMES."

# Visual Jokes/Puns
The primary visual pun is the **"convergence"** concept: As time counts down to midnight, the stick figures become increasingly compressed and distorted, appearing to "converge" toward a single point. The caption plays on this mathematical/physical meaning of "converge" while wishing convergence toward "interesting times"—a reference to the apocryphal curse "May you live in interesting times." The visual metaphor treats the New Year's moment as a mathematical limit point where multiple entities collapse into celebration.

# Scene Description
A six-panel webcomic in black and white stick-figure style showing two characters in conversation. One character (round head) asks questions while the other (oval head) provides explanations. Yellow caption boxes mark time passages. The comic illustrates a recursive loop of increasingly fundamental computing concepts.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"...THEN YOU NEED TO INVOKE THE COMPILER AND..."
"WHAT'S A COMPILER?"

**Panel 2:**
"WELL,... IN THIS CASE, A COMPILER TRANSLATES SOURCE CODE INTO OBJECT CODE..."

**Panel 3:**
"...WHICH IS THEN PASSED TO A LINKER WHICH TURNS IT INTO MACHINE CODE."
"WAIT... WHAT'S MACHINE CODE?"

**Panel 4:**
"MACHINE CODE IS THE CODE THAT CAN BE DIRECTLY EXECUTED BY THE CPU OF THE..."
"WHAT'S A CPU?"

**Panel 5:**
"THE CPU IS A MICROPROCESSOR THAT..."
"WHAT'S A MICROPROCESSOR?"

**Panel 6 (Yellow Caption):**
"10 minutes later..."
"...SO THE POSITIVE CHARGE ATTRACTS ELECTRONS FROM P-TYPE SILICON THAT SEPARATES THE..."
"WHAT'S AN ELECTRON?"

**Panel 7 (Yellow Caption):**
"30 minutes later..."
"...SO ACCORDING TO THIS THEORY, SYMMETRY BREAKING OCCURS AT ABOUT 100 GeV..."
"WHAT'S A GeV?"

# Visual Jokes
The comic depicts an infinite recursion of "What is X?" questions, where each explanation spawns a new fundamental question, drilling down from high-level programming concepts through hardware to physics. The joke is that answering "what is a compiler" ultimately requires understanding quantum mechanics and particle physics.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic depicting a couple in bed having a conversation. The male character wears a blue cap; the female character has black hair. The setting is primarily their bedroom, with occasional scenes showing the girlfriend leaving or the couple in different positions. The art style is minimalist black and white line drawings.

# Dialogue Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"HEY, WHAT'S ALL THIS?"
"I BRING PIZZA AND BEER. HAPPY 5 MONTH ANNIVERSARY."

**Panel 2:**
"AND THESE ARE MY SORORITY SISTERS FROM COLLEGE AND MY OTHER LEADER GIRLFRIENDS FROM HIGH SCHOOL."

**Panel 3:**
"I TOLD THEM HOW SPECTACULAR YOU WERE IN BED AND I SHARE EVERYTHING WITH MY GIRLFRIENDS."

**Panel 4:**
"SO THEY'RE HERE TO RE-ENACT ALL OF YOUR ROLE-PLAYING SEXUAL FANTASIES."

**Panel 5:**
"WOW, YOU SHOULDN'T HAVE."
[Sound effects: "WHISPERING", "TORI!", "LELIA"]

**Panel 6:**
"GO GET HIM, GIRLS!"

**Panel 7:**
[Sound effects: "MULTIPLE SEX LIGANDS WOOO HOOD!..."]

**Panel 8:**
[Wordless scene showing chaotic activity]

**Panel 9:**
[Wordless scene in bed]

**Panel 10:**
[Wordless scene in bed, couple lying down]

**Panel 11:**
"HEY, WHATCHA THINKIN' ABOUT?"

**Panel 12:**
"I WAS JUST WONDERING WHAT IT WOULD BE LIKE IF ALL YOUR GIRLFRIENDS CAME OVER TO FULFILL ALL MY SEXUAL FANTASIES."

**Panel 13:**
[Wordless bed scene]

**Panel 14:**
"GET OUT, GET OUT OF THE HOUSE."
"WHAT?"

**Panel 15:**
"BUT THIS IS MY HOUSE."
"GO TO A HOTEL."

**Panel 16:**
"BUT..."
"GTFO!"

**Panel 17:**
[Wordless scene]

**Panel 18:**
"HEY, WHATCHA THINKIN' ABOUT?"
"YOU."

**Panel 19:**
[Wordless bed scene]

**Panel 20:**
"AWWW... HOW SWEET."

**Panel 21:**
"YOU JUST EARNED YOURSELF A CUDDLE, MISTER."

**Panel 22:**
"LET'S FORGET THE REST OF THE WORLD. NO TV, NO INTERNET, NO VIDEO GAMES."

**Panel 23:**
"LET'S NEVER GET OUT OF BED AGAIN. NOT EVEN FOR THE EAGLES' POST SEASON GAMES."

**Panel 24:**
[Wordless scene showing the girlfriend sleeping peacefully]

**Panel 25:**
[Wordless bed scene]

**Panel 26:**
"HEY, WHATCHA THINKIN' ABOUT?"

**Panel 27:**
[Wordless bed scene]

**Panel 28:**
"FOOTBALL."
"HA, MEN ARE SO SIMPLE MINDED."

# Visual Jokes
The primary visual humor derives from the repeated "Hey, whatcha thinkin' about?" question format. The boyfriend's answers escalate in selfishness/absurdity: first the fantasy scenario (which backfires spectacularly), then flattery (which works and earns cuddles and promises of bed-bound togetherness), and finally football—revealing that despite the romantic moment, his mind has already moved on to sports, confirming the girlfriend's observation about male simplicity. The joke relies on the contrast between what the girlfriend expects him to be thinking about and the mundane reality.

# Scene Description
A multi-panel webcomic in simple black and white stick figure style. Two characters with long curly hair (representing historical figures) are in conversation, joined by a third character wearing a blue hat. The final panel shows three portrait frames containing historical photographs and a cartoon figure.

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"GRÜB GOTT, ISAAC. WHAT IS THIS PLACE?"

**Panel 2:**
"MY DEAR ALBERT, I CAN ONLY ASSUME THAT WE ARE IN THE PANTHEON OF GREAT SCIENTISTS."

**Panel 3:**
"INDEED, WE ARE TWO OF THE GREATEST MINDS IN HISTORY."

**Panel 4:**
"IT IS DOUBTFUL THAT ANY OTHER MIND WILL EVER JOIN US IN THIS GREAT PANTHEON."

**Panel 5:**
"HEY, GUYS! WHATCHA DOIN'?"

**Panel 6:**
"URR... SAY, CHAP... ARE YOU CERTAIN YOU'RE IN THE RIGHT..."
"SHHH... SHUT UP, ISAAC."

**Panel 7:**
"LET'S JUST HUMOR HIM."

# Visual Jokes
The humor depends on the contrast between the serious, self-congratulatory conversation between the two historical figures (represented as stick figures) and the sudden arrival of an obviously unqualified third character in a blue hat, who is then silently admitted to their exclusive "pantheon." The final panel's portraits mock the pretension by showing the cartoon character has been hung alongside legitimate historical scientists.

# Scene Description
A stick-figure comic strip in simple black and white line art showing a conversation between two characters at what appears to be a fast-food counter. One character (blue head) is a customer, the other (black head) is a fast-food worker. The comic escalates from a simple burger order to an absurd situation involving a viral marketing phenomenon.

---

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
Customer: "...AND A BIG MAC AND A SMALL COKE, PLEASE."

**Panel 2:**
Worker: "WHY?"
Customer: "HUH?"

**Panel 3:**
Worker: "WHY BUY A BURGER WHEN YOU CAN MAKE IT AT HOME?"

**Panel 4:**
Worker: "ALL YOU NEED IS..."

**Panel 5:**
Worker: "...THE MIGHTY BURGATRON 3000!"

**Panel 6:**
Customer: "HI, BILLY MAYS HERE!"

**Panel 7:**
Customer: "NO MORE SQUISHIN' AND SQUASHIN' THOSE BURGERS!... FORGET IT. I'M LEAVING."
Worker: "STOP FOLLOWING ME."

**Panel 8:**
Worker: "...OR FLIPPIN' AND FLOPPIN'!..."
Customer: "LEAVE ME ALONE."

**Panel 9:**
Worker: "JUST SCOOP, PRESS, AND COOK IN JUST 2 MINUTES!..."

**Panel 10:**
Worker: "IT'S FAST! IT'S EASY!"
Customer: "STOP YELLING AT ME."

**Panel 11:**
Worker: "HI, BILLY MAYS HERE FOR MIGHTY MENDIT!"

**Panel 12:**
Customer: "BEFORE YOU THROW IT AWAY, LET MIGHTY MEND IT SAVE THE DAY!"
Worker: "WTF?"

**Panel 13:**
Worker: "ORDER NOW AND WE'LL TRIPLE THE OFFER!!!"
Customer: "GO AWAY!"

**Panel 14:**
Worker: "HI, BILLY MAYS HERE!"
(Multiple instances of the worker character repeated)

**Panel 15:**
Worker: (Multiple repeated voices) "HI, BILLY MAYS HERE! BILLY MAYS HERE! BILLY MAYS HERE!"

**Panel 16:**
Worker: (Chaotic overlapping speech) "HEY! OFFER YET—IT'S NOT QUANTITY & !!!...[unintelligible shouting]"

**Panel 17:**
Caption: "Never fall asleep with the television on."
(Customer sleeping in bed with TV on)

---

# Visual Jokes
1. **Billy Mays persona hijacking**: The worker increasingly adopts the manic infomercial style of real-world TV personality Billy Mays, creating an escalating parody of aggressive marketing.
2. **Exponential multiplication**: The repeated "Billy Mays" appearances visually multiply across panels, symbolizing an uncontrollable viral/repetitive phenomenon.
3. **Non-sequiturs**: Product switches from "Burgatron 3000" to "Mighty Mend It" (another Billy Mays product), showing random infomercial logic.
4. **Final punchline**: The conclusion reveals this absurdity was a dream caused by watching infomercials before sleep—a commentary on how Billy Mays' aggressive advertising style permeates popular culture.

## Scene Description
A three-panel black and white comic in a simple line-drawing style. The first two panels show text on various surfaces (walls, papers, clothing). The third panel depicts a figure with a raised fist appearing to forcefully apply a t-shirt to a smaller character's face.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"THERE ARE 10 KINDS OF PEOPLE IN THIS WORLD THOSE WHO KNOW BINARY AND THOSE WHO DON'T"

**Panel 2:**
"THERE ARE 10 KINDS OF PEOPLE IN THIS WORLD THOSE WHO KNOW BINARY AND THOSE WHO DON'T"

**Panel 3:**
(No dialogue)

**Caption (below all panels):**
"...AND THE PEOPLE IN GROUP 10 DON'T LIKE IT WHEN YOU RUB IT IN THEIR FACES WITH A T-SHIRT."

## Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the classic "10 kinds of people" binary joke being literally rubbed in someone's face via a t-shirt printed with the same joke, illustrating the caption's complaint about people in "group 10" (those who don't understand binary/the joke).

comics.title: Father Knows Best
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A simple black and white stick figure comic depicting a husband and wife sitting at a table. The art style is minimalist line drawing. The scene shows an escalating marital argument about compliments and attention.

# Dialogue Transcript (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"OK, LET'S TRY THIS AGAIN, SON."

**Panel 2:**
"SAY YOU'RE JUST SITTING THERE WATCHING TV MINDING YOUR OWN BUSINESS..."

**Panel 3:**
"WHEN ALLUVA SUDDEN, UH OH, HERE COMES YOUR WIFE..."

**Panel 4:**
"...AND SHE ASKS, 'DOES THIS DRESS MAKE ME LOOK FAT?'"

**Panel 5:**
"WHAT DO YOU SAY?"

**Panel 6:**
(Silent panel)

**Panel 7:**
"'WHY NO, DEAR, YOU LOOK BEAUTIFUL IN ANYTHING.'"

**Panel 8:**
"GOOD. THAT'S GOOD, SON."

**Panel 9:**
"BUT THEN SHE SAYS, 'YOU'RE NOT EVEN LOOKING AT ME. LOOK AT ME.'"

**Panel 10:**
"'I AM LOOKING.'"

**Panel 11:**
"'NO YOU'RE NOT; STOP LYING TO ME, LOOK AT ME, DAMMIT.'"
"'BUT...'"

**Panel 12:**
"'LOOK IT ME, YOU WORTHLESS COUCH POTATO!'"
"I'M LOOKING. I'M LOOKING."

**Panel 13:**
"'HOW DID YOU EVER TRICK ME INTO THIS LOVELESS SHAM OF A MARRIAGE?!'"
"ANSWER ME! I DON'T WANNA PLAY ANYMORE..."

# Visual Humor
The joke relies on the escalation of an absurd domestic argument, culminating in the husband's defeated surrender, suggesting the impossibility of winning such a marital scenario through a teaching/practice dialogue format.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure webcomic in black ink showing two characters connected by a vertical string across multiple cloud layers. The top figure sits on an upper cloud, and the bottom figure hangs below on a lower level. The art style is minimalist line drawing with minimal shading (hatched lines for ground).

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Title:** Do you believe in God?

**Dialogue (top to bottom):**
- HERE YA GO, TAKE IT.
- SIKE!
- AU C'MON, I'M JUST PLAYIN'. HERE, GRAB IT.
- OOPS! YA MISSED AGAIN!
- OK, NO, SERIOUSLY. YOU CAN HAVE IT THIS TIME FOR REAL.
- DOUBLE SIKE!!
- HA HA. I LOVE THIS JOB.

**Label (center):** HAPPINESS

**Caption (bottom):** Well, *someone's* gotta be on the other end of the string.

# Visual Jokes
The comic is a visual pun on the concept of "God" — depicted as a mischievous figure dangling happiness just out of reach of the person below, repeatedly playing tricks. The joke culminates in the caption's dark implication: that someone must play the role of God (the trickster), and that person is the one holding the string.

comics.title: Pure Mathematics
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic set in tall grass with two characters: a professor figure with a rifle and a student. The art style is minimalist black-and-white line drawings. The humor revolves around the contrast between hunting "applied mathematicians" and the abstract nature of pure mathematics.

---

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- Student: "WHAT BIG GAME ARE WE STALKING TODAY, PROFESSOR?"
- Professor: "APPLIED MATHEMATICIANS."

**Panel 2:**
- Professor: "THOSE WRETCHED CREATURES HAVE INVADED EVERY BRANCH OF PURE MATH AND TURNED THEM INTO USEFUL REAL WORLD APPLICATIONS."

**Panel 3:**
- Professor: "THEY'VE CORRUPTED THE BEAUTIFUL PURITY OF THE ENTIRE FIELD."

**Panel 4:**
- Professor: "OBSERVE."

**Panel 5:**
(No dialogue; visual action of hunting)

**Panel 6:**
- Sound effect: "RATTLE RATTLE"

**Panel 7:**
- Student: "HEY, WHAT'S THAT?"
- Professor: "IS THAT 'THE JOURNAL OF REALLY PURE MATHEMATICS'?"

**Panel 8:**
- Professor: "HMMM... INTERESTING ARTICLE."
- Other character: "INDEED."

**Panel 9:**
- Professor: "IF WE CONSTRUCT HOPE FIBRATIONS ON THIS STRUCTURE,... WE COULD APPLY THIS TO GAUGE THEORY. BRILLIANT!"

**Panel 10:**
- Other character: "YOU DAMN DIRTY APPLIED MATHEMATUCKERS!"
- Professor: "?"
- Other character: "!"

**Panel 11:**
- Professor: "GET THE HELL OUTTA MY FIELD!"
- Sound effect: "BLAM! X... X... X..."

**Panel 12:**
- Professor: "WE'RE NOT IN BLOOM COUNTY ANYMORE."
- Other character: "PESKY VARMINTS!"
- Equation on board: "(∇x Z) + (∇x Y) = 0"

---

# Visual Jokes
1. The professor becomes exactly what he hunts—an applied mathematician—the moment he sees a practical application
2. The equation visible in the final panel represents applied mathematics invading pure mathematics
3. The reference to "Bloom County" is ironic given the grass setting

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A simple black and white line drawing webcomic depicting a couple in bed. The male character wears a blue baseball cap throughout. The art style is minimalist with basic line work. The comic shows a conversation between two characters lying in bed together, progressing through multiple panels showing their interaction and emotional beats.

## Dialogue Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
- Male: "SO, HOW WAS I?"
- Female: "EHH... I'VE HAD BETTER."

**Panel 2:**
- Female: "WHAT?"

**Panel 3:**
- Female: "I'M SORRY, BUT WHATYA, WANT ME TO LIE?"

**Panel 4:**
- Male: "NO, I WANT NAMES. NAMES! WHO'S BETTER THAN ME?!"

**Panel 5:**
- Female: "IS SOMETHING WRONG WITH MY TECHNIQUE?!"

**Panel 6:**
(Silent panel - female appears frustrated)

**Panel 7:**
- Male: "SO, HOW WAS I?"
- Female: "THAT WAS THE BEST I EVER HAD."

**Panel 8:**
- Male: "I KNOW, BABE. I KNOW."

**Panel 9:**
- Male: "I WAS EVEN HOLDING BACK A LITTLE FOR YOUR SAKE, KNOV WHAT I'M SAYIN'?"

**Panel 10:**
- Female: "HOW'S IT FEEL TO BE WITH THE KING?"

**Panel 11:**
(Silent panel)

**Panel 12:**
- Male: "SO, HOW WAS I?"
- Female: "HEY, LOOK. CONAN'S ON."

## Visual Jokes
The primary joke is the cyclical nature of the relationship: the male character receives a poor review, demands names, then the female character lies to appease him. When he's satisfied with the compliment, he becomes arrogant. The final panel shows the female character deflecting the question entirely by redirecting attention to the television, suggesting this is a recurring, exhausting pattern in their relationship.

comics.title: Practical Zen
comics.comment: One windy day two monks were arguing about a flapping flag. The first said, "I say the flag is moving, not the wind." The second said, "I say the wind is moving, not the flag." A third monk passed by and said, "The wind is not moving. The flag is not moving. Your minds are moving." --- Zen koan
comics.commentHtml: One windy day two monks were arguing about a flapping flag. The first said, "I say the flag is moving, not the wind." The second said, "I say the wind is moving, not the flag." A third monk passed by and said, "The wind is not moving. The flag is not moving. Your minds are moving." --- Zen koan
comics.transcript:
# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A simple line-drawn comic strip set outdoors with grass and sky. A figure in traditional dress holds a flag on a pole while two seated figures observe. The art style is minimalist black ink on white background with a zen/meditation aesthetic. The final panel shows the flag-holder knocked backward by wind.

## Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 2:** "IS THE FLAG MOVING?... OR IS THE WIND MOVING?"

**Panel 3:** "I SAY THE FLAG IS MOVING; NOT THE WIND."

**Panel 4:** "I SAY THE WIND IS MOVING; NOT THE FLAG."

**Panel 5:** "NO. IT IS NEITHER THE WIND NOR THE FLAG THAT IS MOVING."

**Panel 6:** "IT IS YOUR MINDS THAT..."

**Panel 7:** (No dialogue)

## Visual Jokes
The comic parodies the famous "flag waving" scene from *The Matrix* where a Zen master delivers a cryptic philosophical lesson about perception. The joke's payoff occurs in the final panel: while the master explains that it's neither the wind nor the flag but the observers' minds that move, the character is literally blown over by wind, undercutting the philosophical wisdom with physical comedy. The wind and flag are indeed moving—literally.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A stick-figure comic in the classic XKCD style showing a conversation between a simple stick figure character and a woman. The setting appears to be abstract/cosmic, with the final panel showing the character in what looks like a bucket or container. The art style is minimalist black line drawings on white background.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 2:** 
"HEY, PHOTON! CATCH ME IF YOU CAN, SWEETIE."

**Panel 3:**
"HOLY SH— CHECK OUT THE ASS ON THAT TACHYON. I'D TOTALLY HIT THAT."

**Panel 4:**
"I'M COMING, BABY!"

**Panel 5:**
"KA-BLAM!"

**Panel 6:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 7:**
"WHAT HAPPENED?"

**Panel 8:**
"MOTHER NATURE? WHAT ARE Y..."

**Panel 9:**
"YOU BROKE THE RULES. YOU EXCEEDED THE SPEED LIMIT, C."

**Panel 10:**
"BECAUSE OF YOUR RECKLESS BEHAVIOR, THE FINE-STRUCTURE CONSTANT STARTED TO VACILLATE WILDLY."

**Panel 11:**
"SOON ALL THE OTHER PARTICLES STARTED TO GET CONFUSED..."

**Panel 12:**
"AND ALL OF THEIR MASSES AND FORCE STRENGTHS GOT THROWN ALL OUTTA WHACK."

**Panel 13:**
"PLANETS AND STARS WERE TORN ASUNDER AND ALL ATOMIC NUCLEI LOST COHESION."

**Panel 14:**
"BASICALLY, YOU DESTROYED THE ENTIRE UNIVERSE AND ALL LIFE IN IT. ... AND ALL JUST SO YOU COULD GIT SOME TACHYON TAIL."

**Panel 15:**
"NOW GOD HAS TO START ALL OVER AGAIN."

**Panel 16:**
"OH..."

**Panel 17:**
"I'M VERY DISAPPOINTED IN YOU."

**Panel 18:**
"THIS NEVER HAPPENS TO THE OTHER PARTICLES."

# Visual Jokes
- The humor relies on physics concepts: tachyons (hypothetical faster-than-light particles), the fine-structure constant, and the speed of light as an absolute limit in physics
- The punchline plays on the stick figure's sexual pursuit of a tachyon causing a universal catastrophe, with the disappointed "mother nature" figure providing moral judgment
- The final panel shows the character sitting in a bucket, a visual representation of being in "time out"

# Scene Description
A minimalist webcomic in simple black and white line art featuring a character with a blue circular head writing in a diary. The comic follows the character's account of a romantic encounter, progressing through multiple panels showing the character writing at a desk, then a final scene showing the character in bed with another person.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
DEAR DIARY,

**Panel 2:**
TODAY I RAN INTO ALICE, THE SNOBBY TEES GIRL, AT STARBUCKS.

**Panel 3:**
I INTRODUCED MYSELF AND WE TALKED AND LAUGHED FOR HOURS.

**Panel 4:**
SHE INVITED ME BACK TO HER PLACE FOR "A DRINK—A NIGHT OF REALLY DIRTY SEX."

**Panel 5:**
SHE UNDRESSED ME—TORE OFF MY CLOTHES WITH HER TEETH...

**Panel 6:**
...AND WE MADE LOVE—WENT AT IT LIKE WILD ANIMALS IN HEAT.

**Panel 7:**
WE DID IT BY A ROARING FIREPLACE ON A RUG MADE OF BEAR SKIN...

**Panel 8:**
[blank]

**Panel 9:**
...7 TIMES!

**Panel 10:**
I KEPT TELLING HER THAT I'M NOT A MACHINE...

**Panel 11:**
...BUT SHE WAS ALL LIKE, "C'MON, DO ME ONE MORE TIME, YOU MAGNIFICENT BASTARD!"

**Panel 12:**
[blank]

**Panel 13:**
BY THE WAY,...

**Panel 14:**
IF YOU'RE WONDERING WHY I'M WRITING TOMORROW'S ENTRY TODAY,...

**Panel 15:**
...IT'S BECAUSE I BELIEVE THE FUTURE IS UNWRITTEN AND THAT WE CREATE OUR OWN DESTINY.

**Panel 16:**
[blank]

**Panel 17:**
[blank]

**Panel 18:**
NOT TONIGHT. I'VE GOT A BIG DAY TOMORROW.

# Visual Jokes
The primary joke relies on the contrast between the narrator's elaborate, boastful fantasy account of a sexual encounter and the final panel's reality: he's alone in bed, going to sleep early because he has "a big day tomorrow." The entire diary entry is revealed to be fictional wishful thinking written in advance.

# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure webcomic in black and white with minimal art style. A character with a blue circular head sits at a computer throughout most panels, with another stick figure appearing in the lower panels. The comic uses a 3x4 grid layout.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"LAST MONTH MY LITTLE WEBCOMIC HAD A RECORD 500,000,000* VISITS."

**Panel 2:**
"NOW THAT MY READER BASE IS LARGE ENOUGH, I THINK I SHOULD EXPLAIN WHY I REALLY DRAW COMICS."

**Panel 3:**
"I FEEL I MUST DISCLOSE SOME IMPORTANT INFORMATION TO THE PUBLIC, AND A WEBCOMIC SEEMED LIKE A GOOD WAY TO GAIN AN AUDIENCE."

**Panel 4:**
"FOR YEARS, I HAVE BEEN PART OF A SHADOWY UNDERWORLD OF BLACK OPS AND GOVERNMENT COVERUPS."

**Panel 5:**
"THE WORLD IS ACTUALLY CONTROLLED BY AN ELITE GROUP OF EVIL MEN WORKING IN UNDERGROUND BASES OWNED BY GOOGLE."

**Panel 6:**
"THESE EVIL MEN, IN TURN, ARE BEING TELEPATHICALLY CONTROLLED BY A GROUP OF EVIL REPTILIAN ALIENS FROM THE ALPHA DRACONIS SYSTEM."

**Panel 7:**
"THESE ALIENS CARE NOTHING FOR HUMAN LIFE; BUT ONLY WANT TO CONTROL OUR VAST SUPPLY OF COWS."

**Panel 8:**
"THEY ARE SYSTEMATICALLY TRYING TO REDUCE THE HUMAN POPULATION BY SPRAYING POISONOUS CHEMTRAILS, FLUORIDATING OUR WATER, AND..."

**Panel 9:**
"THERE HE IS! GET HIM!"

**Panel 10:**
"YOU BLABBED, QUAID! YOU BLABBED ABOUT GOOGLE!"

**Panel 11:**
"YOUR ASS IS OFFICIALLY ERA—"

**Panel 12:**
[Silent panel - text cut off]

# Visual Jokes
- The asterisk on "500,000,000*" with footnote "*I USE BASE 5" reveals the inflated visitor count is misleading through a different numerical base.
- The final panel's text cutting off mid-word creates an abrupt comedic ending, mimicking the character being stopped/eliminated before finishing their sentence.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure comic depicting a person in confession with a priest (represented by a cross in a confessional booth). The art style is minimalist black line drawings on white background. The same two characters appear in each panel, with the person's emotional state escalating from remorseful to increasingly distressed.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- Person: "BLESS ME FATHER, FOR I HAVE SINNED."
- Priest: "YES, MY CHILD."

**Panel 2:**
- Person: "IT'S BEEN FOREVER SINCE MY LAST CONFESSION AND... AND..."
- Priest: "GO ON."

**Panel 3:**
- Person: "I'VE BEEN HAVING IMPURE THOUGHTS... *SOB*"
- Priest: (silent)

**Panel 4:**
- Priest: "WHAT KIND OF IMPURE THOUGHTS?"
- Person: "CLASSICAL."

**Panel 5:**
- Person: "I'VE BEEN A QUANTUM PHYSICIST FOR YEARS NOW,..."

**Panel 6:**
- Person: "...AND I STILL FIND MYSELF LAPSING BACK INTO THE DARK ABYSS OF CLASSICAL THINKING."

**Panel 7:**
- Priest: "UTF?"
- Person: "I'M ASHAMED."

**Panel 8:**
- Priest: "CLASSICAL THINKING IS SO SEDUCTIVE AND I CAN'T RESIST ITS COMMON SENSUAL TEMPTATIONS ANY MORE."

**Panel 9:**
- Person: "*SOB*"
- Text: "JESUS CHRIST"

**Panel 10:**
- Person: "*SIGH* OK."

**Panel 11:**
- Priest: "JUST DO SOME FREE PARTICLE PROPAGATOR DERIVATIONS AND TEN HAIL MARYS. I ABSOLVE YOU."

**Panel 12:**
- Priest: "THANK YOU, FATHER."
- Priest: "GO WITH GOD, MY CHILD."

# Visual Jokes
The primary humor is the subversion of expectations: the confession begins as a standard religious guilt scenario about "impure thoughts," but escalates to reveal the "sin" is actually intellectual—the physicist's struggle between classical and quantum mechanics. The absurdist punchline involves the priest prescribing quantum physics homework ("free particle propagator derivations") as penance, conflating religious and academic frameworks.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A six-panel webcomic drawn in simple stick-figure style featuring a group of monkey characters and a human character. The monkeys are drawn as simple curved lines with round heads and tails. The setting appears to be an indoor space with minimal background detail. The comic depicts a darkly humorous scenario about an alien invasion plan involving monkey infiltrators.

# Dialogue Transcript (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"OK, SO YOU ALL KNOW YOUR ASSIGNMENTS?"

**Panel 2:**
"BARRY, YOU TAKE OVER THEIR COMPUTER NETWORKS AND ELECTRICAL GRIDS."

**Panel 3:**
"CARL, YOUR JOB IS TO INFILTRATE THEIR POLITICAL CENTERS OF POWER."

**Panel 4:**
"LEWIS, YOU'RE RESPONSIBLE FOR LIQUIDATING THEIR MILITARY CAPACITY AND..."

**Panel 5:**
"SHHH... HUMAN."
*[The human character appears, holding a banana]*
"HEY MONKEY, WHAT'S THIS IN MY HAND? IS THIS A BANANA?"

**Panel 6:**
"HERE, WANT MY DELICIOUS BANANA?"

**Panel 7:**
"OH, TOO BAD, YOUR DUMB MONKEY ASS IS ALL LOCKED UP IN A CAGE. HAHA."

**Panel 8:**
"COMETH THE LOOK, I'M A STUPID LOWER PRIMATE. OOGH, OOOH..."

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the contrast between the monkeys' sophisticated invasion plan and their inability to resist the simple distraction of a banana, which undermines their entire operation. The final panel plays on the stereotype that monkeys are easily distracted and less intelligent than humans.

comics.title: Alice and Bob
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic in black and white depicting a romantic relationship between Alice and Bob, with a narrative about encryption, public keys, and a marriage proposal. The comic uses simple line drawings against white backgrounds with minimal details.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"One day, Alice and Bob started dating."

**Panel 2:**
"After their first date, Bob sent Alice a message encrypted with his public key."
"?"

**Panel 3:**
"HOW AM I SUPPOSED TO DECRYPT IT IF I DON'T HAVE YOUR PRIVATE KEY?"
"THAT'S FOR YOU TO FIND OUT."

**Panel 4:**
"Bob and Alice dated for years."
[Heart symbol shown]

**Panel 5:**
"...but Bob never told her what the encrypted message said."

**Panel 6:**
"C'MON, JUST TELL ME ALREADY..."
"SORRY, GARR..."

**Panel 7:**
"...and every night, Alice tried to decrypt the message."
"DAMN."
[Asterisks and symbols indicating frustrated attempts]

**Panel 8:**
"...until, one night, Alice made a breakthrough."
"EUREKA!"

**Panel 9:**
"Bob was sure that by the time she cracked the message..."

**Panel 10:**
"--- Begin Message ---
WILL YOU MARRY ME?
--- End Message ---"

**Panel 11:**
"...he will have really meant it."

# Visual Jokes
The primary joke is a metaphorical use of public-key cryptography: Bob's encrypted message represents his romantic feelings, which Alice cannot access without his "private key" (commitment/vulnerability). The punchline subverts expectations—the message's true emotional weight comes not from the words themselves, but from the time that has passed, proving his sincerity through years of patience.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A parody of the Google search homepage styled to look like a search engine called "God." The design mimics Google's classic interface with the colorful "God" logo at the top, a search bar, and two action buttons below it.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)
1. Logo: "God"
2. Search query box: "is the universe a computer?"
3. Left button: "Ask God"
4. Right button: "I'm Feeling Lucky"
5. Right sidebar links:
   - "Advanced Search"
   - "Preferences"
   - "Language Tools"

# Visual Jokes
The primary humor derives from treating God as a search engine with the same interface and functionality as Google, literalizing the idea of asking God philosophical questions. The query "is the universe a computer?" is a philosophical/theological question that takes on comedic absurdity when formatted as a Google-style search. The "I'm Feeling Lucky" button (Google's actual feature) becomes darkly humorous in this context, as asking God for a "lucky" answer to existential questions is inherently absurd.

# Comic Transcription

## Scene Description
A six-panel webcomic in simple black and white line art with blue accents. Two characters—one with dark hair and one with a blue hat—are shown with a computer. The sequential file numbers (1234567885-1234567890) suggest this is documenting a screenshot or file save sequence. The art style is minimalist stick-figure-like characters with simple expressions.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:** 1234567885

**Panel 2:** 1234567886

**Panel 3:** 1234567887

**Panel 4:** 1234567888

**Panel 5:** 1234567889

**Panel 6:** 1234567890
- Character 1: "NOW!"
- Caption: "*SCREENSHOT*"

**Panel 7:** 1234567890
- Character 1: "WHEW~"

**Panel 8:** 1234567890
- Character 2: "THAT WAS ALMOST AS EXCITING AS ||||||||||."
- Character 1: "ALMOST."

## Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the absurdity of treating a screenshot as an exciting, coordinated event requiring the countdown "NOW!" The punchline contrasts this buildup with the mundane reality that taking a screenshot is barely interesting at all—"almost as exciting" as a line of identical symbols (representing nothing, or the ultimate in boredom).

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description

A simple, comic-style chart with a white background showing a grid of red hearts with arrows piercing through them. Each heart contains a fraction. The art style is minimalist and hand-drawn, appearing to be a humorous infographic about love.

# Text Transcription

**Title (top):** HOW DO I LOVE THEE?

**Hearts and fractions in reading order (left to right, top to bottom):**

Row 1:
- 1/1
- 1/2
- 1/3
- 1/4
- 1/5

Row 2:
- 2/2
- 2/3
- 2/4
- 2/5

Row 3:
- 3/3
- 3/4
- 3/5

Row 4:
- 4/2
- 4/3
- 4/4
- 4/5

Row 5:
- 5/2
- 5/3
- 5/4
- 5/5

# Visual Jokes

The visual joke is a pun on the famous opening line of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's poem "How Do I Love Thee?" The comic subverts this romantic question by presenting it as a mathematical chart of fractions. Each heart is pierced by an arrow (Cupid's arrow), but instead of expressing romantic sentiment, the image quantifies love through increasingly complex fractional expressions, creating an absurdist humor through the contrast between romance and mathematics.

comics.title: Dear Higgs Boson
comics.comment: It seemed only fair .
comics.commentHtml: It seemed only fair.
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A single-panel webcomic featuring a letter format. The background is plain white with a black border. At the bottom right is a simple stick figure drawing of a character with a round head, appearing worried or distressed, with small lines suggesting motion or emotion. The art style is minimalist and humorous.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

Dear Higgs Boson,

We know you're out there. We can feel you now. We know that you're afraid. You're afraid of us; you're afraid of change. We don't know the future. We didn't write this to tell you how this is going to end. We wrote this to tell you how it's going to begin.

As you know, our Large Hadron Collider has had some setbacks due to a.... uh... "transformer malfunction" but we know it was you. You sabotaged our machine. We hope you've been enjoying your vacation because we're scheduled to restart in September 2009 and we pissed.

...so run and hide, asshole. Run and hide. If you should get careless and allow yourself to get detected by the Tevatron, we are going to be supremely disappointed; because we want to find you first, and when we do, rest assured we are not going to publish right away. We're going to teach you some manners first.

Love,

CERN

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on personifying the Higgs Boson as a sentient being that CERN is threatening like a character in an action film, combined with the absurd premise that a particle could deliberately sabotage equipment. The stick figure's worried expression reinforces the comedic threat.

# Scene Description
A simple webcomic with a minimalist art style featuring two characters: a person with a blue cap/head on the left and a mirror or reflective surface on the right showing their reflection. The setting appears to be a bathroom or bedroom with basic line-drawn furniture. The comic progresses through 9 panels in a 3x3 grid, showing an escalating conversation between the character and their reflection.

# Dialogue Transcript (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 2:**
Character: "YOU'RE THE ONLY ONE WHO UNDERSTANDS ME."
Reflection: "I KNOW WHAT YOU DID LAST NIGHT."

**Panel 3:**
Character: "NOBODY KNOWS ME AS WELL AS YOU DO."
Reflection: "I KNOW WHAT YOU DID LAST NIGHT."

**Panel 4:**
Character: "YOU KNOW MY EVERY THOUGHT."
Reflection: "I KNOW WHAT YOU DID LAST NIGHT."

**Panel 5:**
Character: "YOU KNOW ALL MY HOPES AND DREAMS."
Reflection: "I KNOW WHAT YOU DID LAST NIGHT."

**Panel 6:**
Character: "...AND YOU NEVER JUDGE ME UNFAIRLY."
Reflection: "I KNOW WHAT YOU DID LAST NIGHT."

**Panel 7:**
Character: "...NEVER CRITICIZE ME UNJUSTLY."
Reflection: "I KNOW WHAT YOU DID LAST NIGHT."

**Panel 8:**
Character: "THAT'S WHY YOU, SIR, ARE AWESOME."
Reflection: "I KNOW WHAT YOU DID LAST NIGHT."

**Panel 9:**
Character: "WHO LOVES YA, BABE?!"
Reflection: "YOU'RE ONE SICK BASTARD."

# Visual Jokes
The primary joke is the escalating emotional monologue contrasted with the mirror's repetitive, ominous response ("I KNOW WHAT YOU DID LAST NIGHT"). The punchline subverts the character's affection by having the reflection finally break its pattern with a judgment-filled insult, suggesting the mirror has indeed been judging them the whole time—ironically proving the character wrong about never being judged.

# Scene Description
A four-panel comic in simple black and white line drawing style. The comic presents a humorous mathematical "proof" about underwear degradation over time, featuring simple sketches of underwear in various states of disrepair and a stick figure at the end.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Top Left Panel:**
"Assume that your new underwear is a multiply connected space."

**Top Right Panel:**
"Over time, it will become "even more" multiply connected.
THIS FACT HAS BEEN VERIFIED TO A HIGH DEGREE OF ACCURACY BY YEARS OF EXPERIMENTAL EVIDENCE BY THE AUTHOR."

**Bottom Left Panel:**
"Eventually, the holes begin to merge and it becomes doubly connected.
I WOULD STILL TOTALLY WEAR THIS."

**Bottom Right Panel:**
"In the end, the waistband will snap (trust me)."

**Below all panels:**
"THEOREM: Simply connected underwear is simply unwearable.

PROOF: Left as exercise for the reader."

**Bottom right corner:** A simple stick figure shrugging

# Visual Jokes
- The progression shows underwear becoming increasingly tattered with holes
- The mathematician's dry assertion "I would still totally wear this" contrasts with the obviously deteriorating garment
- The final panel shows a waistband that has torn into a jagged, ribbon-like shape
- The "proof left as exercise for the reader" is a classic mathematical trope being applied absurdly to worn-out underwear

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A nine-panel comic strip featuring a simple, minimalist art style with identical alien heads (large, oval-shaped with black almond eyes) against white backgrounds. The panels depict a conversation between an alien representative and humanity regarding Earth's application to the Galactic Federation of Planets.

# Dialogue Transcript (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"Greetings, people of Earth."

**Panel 2:**
"Thank you for your application to the Galactic Federation of Planets."

**Panel 3:**
"We regret to inform you that after centuries of observing your 'civilization', ......."

**Panel 4:**
"...we are unable to offer you entrance into our organization at this time."

**Panel 5:**
"While we were very impressed with your qualifications and your potential to... to..."

**Panel 6:**
"Aah, screw it! Let's just get down to it."

**Panel 7:**
"What the hell's the matter with you people?"

**Panel 8:**
[Silent panel with alien head]

**Panel 9:**
"Seriously, man, what the f*ck?"

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the alien's initial attempt at diplomatic formality (panels 2-5) progressively breaking down into exasperation and profanity (panels 6-9). The identical, unchanging alien face juxtaposed with increasingly frustrated dialogue creates comedic contrast. The joke critiques humanity's flaws as so obvious that even polite alien bureaucrats abandon diplomacy.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
This is a nine-panel webcomic in stick figure style depicting a scientific research procedure. The setting is a laboratory with basic equipment (flasks, beakers, computers). A single stick figure scientist appears throughout, showing progression through various emotional states from focused work to exhaustion and frustration.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (top left):**
"The sequence of cDNAs was confirmed by the dideoxy chain-termination method."

**Panel 2 (top center):**
"The cDNAs were then subcloned into the shuttle plasmid vector pADCMV-Link."

**Panel 3 (top right):**
"The recombinant plasmids were cotransfected into 297 cells with DNA of H5.110CMVIacZ digested with ClaI."

**Panel 4 (middle left):**
"Transfected cells were overlaid with agar and kept at 33 degrees C for 12 days."

**Panel 5 (middle center):**
"MUAHAHAHA!"

**Panel 6 (middle right):**
[Empty, showing scientist at rest]

**Panel 7 (bottom left):**
[Silent panel showing scientist]

**Panel 8 (bottom center):**
"A second round of plaque purification was performed on positive plaques."
"* SIGH *"

**Panel 9 (bottom right):**
"Recombinant viruses were expanded, purified by CsCl ultracentrifugation, and stored in..."

**Caption (bottom, highlighted in yellow):**
"Being a mad scientist in real life isn't as fun as in the movies."

# Visual Jokes
The scientist's expression shifts from confident (Panel 5's "MUAHAHAHA!") through exhaustion to resignation, culminating in the ironic caption. The humor contrasts the tedious, repetitive nature of actual scientific work with the dramatic excitement stereotypically associated with "mad scientists."

# Scene Description

A simple stick figure comic with two characters: one studying at a desk with an open book, another character who enters later. The art style is minimalist black and white line drawings with simple circular faces. The setting is consistently a desk with an open textbook throughout all panels.

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 2:** "WOW, I NEVER NOTICED BEFORE HOW BEAUTIFUL THE HAHN-BANACH THEOREM IS."

**Panel 3:** "IT MAKES ME FEEL SO... HMM... WHAT'S THE RIGHT WORD?"

**Panel 4:** "...PRETTY?"

**Panel 5:** "YES! IT MAKES ME FEEL... PRETTY."

**Panel 6:** "I FEEL PRETTY OH SO PRETTY."

**Panel 7:** "I FEEL PRETTY AND WITTY AND BRIGHT!"

**Panel 8:** "AND I PITY ANY GIRL WHO ISN'T ME TONIGHT." (with musical notes)

**Panel 9:** "I FEEL CHARMING, OH SO CHARMING." (with musical notes)

**Panel 10:** "WHAT ARE YOU DOING?" / "HUH?!"

**Panel 11:** "WHAT THE HELL ARE YOU DOING?"

**Panel 12:** "NOTHING."

**Panel 13:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 14:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 15:** "OK, IN 24 HOURS I'M GOING TO PRETEND THAT I NEVER SAW THIS."

**Panel 16:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 17:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 18:** "YOU'RE WELCOME."

# Visual Jokes

The primary joke relies on the character singing lyrics from "I Feel Pretty" from *West Side Story* after discovering the Hahn-Banach theorem (a significant mathematical theorem in functional analysis). The humor comes from the absurdist juxtaposition of rigorous mathematics inspiring romantic/musical theater sentimentality, and the partner's bewildered reaction to the sudden singing and dancing.

A six-panel educational comic in simple black-and-white line art style explaining how the Gymnarchus (a type of fish) uses bioelectricity. "The *Gymnarchus niloticus* fish can make its tail negatively charged with respect to its head."
[Label on fish: ⊖ on tail, ⊕ on head]

"This produces a symmetrical electric field around its body."

"Nearby objects distort this field."

**Panel 4 (middle left):**
"...and the Gymnarchus can sense the distortion on its skin."
[Speech bubble from fish: "FOOD."]

**Panel 5 (center-right):**
[Empty panel with fish and smaller fish silhouettes]

**Panel 6 (bottom right):**
"Gymnarchus are fucking awesome!"

## Visual Jokes
The progression humorously builds from scientific explanation to the fish discovering prey and ending with an enthusiastic endorsement of the species. The final panel's emphatic statement is the comedic payoff to the educational setup.

# Scene Description
A simple, minimalist comic with a white background and black text/borders. The image shows two text elements arranged horizontally: a URL on the left and a bracketed description on the right, with a connecting line between them suggesting a relationship.

# Text Transcription
**Left side:**
http://abstrusegoose.com/secretarchives/a

**Right side:**
first 10-digit prime found in consecutive digits of e

# Visual Joke
This is a meta-joke about Easter eggs and hidden content. The comic implies that the URL leads to a secret archive, but the punchline reveals it's actually just the digits of Euler's number (e ≈ 2.718281828...). The "first 10-digit prime found in consecutive digits of e" would be the password or secret code to access that archive—a humorous reference to mathematical Easter eggs and the absurdist humor typical of the Abstruse Goose webcomic.

# Scene Description
A single-panel comic with a hand-drawn, informal style. The image shows a letter written in casual handwriting against a white background with a black border. No characters or setting are depicted visually—the humor comes entirely from the text.

# Text Transcription
DEAR PHYSICISTS,
    YOU KNOW HOW YOU, LIKE, ALWAYS STEAL ALL OUR IDEAS AND THEN YOU, LIKE, MAKE UP YOUR OWN CRAZY NOTATIONS AND SHIT?
    YEAH... CUT THAT OUT.
                LOVE,
                MATHEMATICIANS
                <X,0>

# Visual Jokes
The signature "<X,0>" is a coordinate pair or vector notation—a humorous example of the "crazy notations" mathematicians are ironically complaining about. This self-aware joke undercuts the complaint, as mathematicians themselves use such notation.

# Scene Description
A simple line-drawn letter on a white background with a black border. The art style is minimalist, hand-written text. No characters or setting are depicted—this is purely text-based.

# Text Transcription
DEAR MATHEMATICIANS,

PISS OFF.

LOVE,
PHYSICISTS
×ᵣ 0ʳ

# Visual Jokes
The signature "×ᵣ 0ʳ" is a mathematical/physics pun. It appears to reference cross products (×) and could be read as "cross zero" or interpreted as a playful misuse of mathematical notation, combining mathematical symbols in a deliberately nonsensical way—fitting for a message telling mathematicians to "piss off." The subscript and superscript formatting adds to the pseudo-mathematical appearance of the signature.

The joke relies on the casual, profanity-laced tone of the message attributed to renowned mathematical physicist Roger Penrose, presented as if he's reassuring the scientific community about some unsolved problem or crisis. comics.title: War Stories
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A simple black-and-white stick figure comic in the style of XKCD. An adult figure wearing a blue cap sits at a computer, speaking to a child sitting on the ground facing them. The adult is leaning back from the computer.

# Text Transcription

**Speech bubble (top):**
THE BATTLE LINES WERE DRAWN, THE TWO ARMIES FACED EACH OTHER ACROSS CYBERSPACE. THERE I WAS... SITTING AT MY COMPUTER. I ENTERED A USENET NEWSGROUP AND TYPED THE WORDS "EIGHT MEGABYTES AND CONSTANTLY SWAPPING".

**Caption (bottom):**
Years from now, I will regale my son with tales of the Editor Wars.

# Visual Joke
The humor relies on the contrast between the grandiose, battle-like language used to describe a trivial online argument (posting a single line in a Usenet newsgroup) and the mundane reality of sitting at a computer typing. The joke mocks the tendency of early internet users to treat minor online disputes with exaggerated dramatic importance—framing a simple forum post as an epic military engagement that will become a legendary tale.

"IF J IS NOT A PROPER IDEAL, THEN 1 ∈ J. BY DEFINITION OF J, WE HAVE 1 ∈ A FOR SOME A ∈ C."

"...BUT A ∈ C.ES. HENCE EACH CHAIN HAS AN UPPER BOUND IN S."

**Panel 3:**
"SO BY ZORN'S L..."

**Panel 4:**
"SMACK"
(Character slaps forehead)

**Panel 5:**
(Character hunched over desk in apparent frustration/exhaustion)

**Panel 6:**
Counter in top right corner: "30 DAYS SINCE USING ZORN'S LEMMA"
(Character back at desk)

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the mathematician repeatedly invoking "Zorn's Lemma" in their proof, leading to self-inflicted frustration (the forehead slap). The counter jokes that they've failed to go a full month without relying on this particular theorem, suggesting an over-dependence on it as a proof technique.

# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure webcomic in black ink on white background, depicting a person stranded on a small island with a palm tree over the course of several years. The comic uses a 3x3 grid layout with sequential panels showing the passage of time.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"HELP!
STRANDED ON
ISLAND IN
SOUTH PACIFIC."

**Panel 4:**
"One year later."

**Panel 5:**
"Two years later."

**Panel 6:**
"Three years later."

**Panel 7:**
"Four years later."

**Panel 9:**
"Request timed out.
Request timed out.
Request timed out.
Request timed out."

# Visual Jokes
The primary joke is that the stranded person's rescue message (shown as a paper airplane in panels 1-3) eventually times out after four years, depicted through repeated "Request timed out" text in the final panel. This is a tech/internet humor reference—the person's SOS metaphorically becomes a failed HTTP request, suggesting the absurdity of waiting for help that never arrives, presented in the format of a broken internet connection rather than actual rescue.

# Scene Description

A simple line drawing of a thick book shown in three-quarter view against a white background. The art style is minimalist, hand-drawn with black ink lines. The book is drawn with simple linear perspective showing its depth and pages.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Cover text (largest, top):**
HOW TO GET RICH
BY WRITING A BOOK

**Subtitle text (medium, center):**
ABOUT HOW TO GET RICH
BY WRITING A BOOK

**Small text (tiny, center-right):**
ABOUT HOW TO GET RICH
BY WRITING A BOOK
(A BOOK)

**Author byline (bottom):**
BY ABSTRUSE GOOSE

# Visual Joke

The humor is a recursive loop: the book's subject is "how to get rich by writing a book about how to get rich by writing a book," creating an infinite regress. The joke suggests that the only way to get rich writing such a book is to write a meta-book about the very concept itself, which is exactly what this book is doing—making it self-referential and absurd.

# Scene Description
A nine-panel webcomic featuring a single character—a person wearing a blue cap—shown from roughly shoulder-up in each panel. The character is drawn in a simple, minimalist line-art style with basic facial features. The character progresses through various states of frustration and confusion, with their expressions becoming increasingly exasperated. The setting is minimal, with white backgrounds and black borders separating each panel.

# Dialogue Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:** "HI, MY INTERNET CONNECTION SEEMS TO BE DOWN."

**Panel 2:** "DID YOU CHECK TO SEE IF YOUR MODEM IS PLUGGED IN?"

**Panel 3:** (silent)

**Panel 4:** (silent)

**Panel 5:** "HELLO? SIR?"

**Panel 6:** "WAIT A MINUTE. I'M THINKING. MAYBE THAT'S WHY MY CAR WON'T START."

**Panel 7:** "MAYBE I NEED TO TURN THE IGNITION FIRST. THAT'S GOTTA BE THE PROBLEM."

**Panel 8:** "Y'KNOW, MY SHOWER DOESN'T WORK EITHER."

**Panel 9:** "WHAT THE HELL ARE THOSE LITTLE KNOBS THAT SAY 'H' AND 'C'?" / "NO NEED TO GET SNIPPY, SIR."

# Visual Jokes
The humor depends on the character's dawning realization that they haven't been doing basic troubleshooting steps (plugging things in, turning on ignition, adjusting shower knobs), while becoming increasingly irritable at the tech support suggestion—and the final panel's irony where the character becomes snippy while literally not understanding basic appliance controls.

comics.title: The String Theory Landscape
comics.comment: Dontcha know? April is national poetry month in the U.S. and Canada.
comics.commentHtml: Dontcha know?  April is national poetry month in the U.S. and Canada.
comics.transcript:
## Scene Description

A webcomic in the style of SMBC (Saturday Morning Breakfast Cereal) or similar stick-figure comics. The upper portion is typeset poem text on a white background in a casual handwritten-style font. The lower portion shows a stick-figure illustration: three angry stick figures with pitchforks chasing a fleeing, more detailed figure wearing glasses. Small artist initials appear in the bottom-right corner.

---

## Full Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Title:**

> OR, A VISION IN A DREAM
> A FRAGMENT

**Poem body:**

> In Academia did theoretical physicists
> A stately string theory decree:
> Where Branes, the sacred rivers, ran
> Through compactified dimensions measureless to man
>             Down to a stable vacuum.
> So twice 5 x 10⁴⁹⁹ configurations of fertile ground
> With walls and towers were girdled round:
> And there were minima bright with sinuous rills,
> Where blossomed many a solution-bearing tree;
> And here were Calabi-Yau manifolds ancient as the hills,
> Enfolding sunny spots of positive vacuum energy.

> But oh! That deep romantic chasm which slanted
> Down the [Greene] hill of refutability!
> A savage place! as holy and enchanted
> As e'er beneath experimental result was haunted
> By the specter of falsifiability!

> And from this chasm, with ceaseless turmoil seething,
> As if theoretical physics in fast thick pants were breathing,
> A mighty fountain momently was forced:
> Amid whose swift half-intermitted burst

> And thesis advisors everywhere did live in mortal fear,
> Since angry grad students by the hundreds did throng:
> As mathematical evidence came e'er so clear,
> That string theory indeed is wrong.

# Scene Description
A plain white background displaying a mathematical problem. The image shows a matrix equation and a question, rendered in black handwritten-style text.

# Text Transcription

**Matrix Equation:**
$$e^{At}=\begin{bmatrix} e^{6t} & 0 & 2e^{10t}-2e^{6t} \\ e^{6t}-e^{7t} & e^{7t} & 2e^{7t}-2e^{6t} \\ 0 & 0 & e^{10t} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Question:**
$$\det A = ?$$

# Visual Elements
- A small marking "4" appears in the bottom right corner, likely indicating a problem number or page reference.

# Notes
This is a linear algebra problem asking students to find the determinant of matrix $A$ given its matrix exponential $e^{At}$. There are no visual jokes; this is a straightforward mathematical problem.

comics.title: Friends
comics.transcript:
# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A three-panel vertical comic in simple black line-art style featuring two stick figures. Each panel shows the two figures with a couch between them, with red splatter marks appearing progressively in panels 2 and 3. The minimalist art style relies on visual progression and the visual joke rather than detailed backgrounds.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)
1. "FRIEND"
2. "BEST FRIEND"
3. "BESTEST FRIEND"

## Visual Joke
The comic uses a humorous visual progression to define friendship levels:
- **FRIEND**: Two people simply lifting/holding a couch together
- **BEST FRIEND**: Two people holding a person suspended between them (depicted as a ball/sphere) with red splatter marks, suggesting rough/chaotic play
- **BESTEST FRIEND**: Two people with the couch, with a person lying on the couch, surrounded by red splatter marks

The joke is that "bestest friend" status involves increasingly reckless, messy, or violent behavior being depicted as signs of closeness, with the red splatters implying blood or injury that escalates with each tier of friendship.
