**Panel 9:**
"...but they're never very helpful."
"THAT IS LIKE THE GAYEST THING YOU'VE EVER SAID."

**Panel 10:**
"I try to call her to make up."

**Panel 11:**
"...but somehow 'Sheila' always seems to answer the phone. And she's all like:"
"SHE DOESN'T WANT TO TALK TO YOU, JERK."

**Panel 12:**
"...as if it was any of her damn business."
"*CLICK*"

**Panel 13:**
"So I stop by my girlfriend's apartment."
"KNOCK KNOCK"

**Panel 14:**
"...but somehow 'Sheila' always seems to answer the door. And she's all like:"
"GO AWAY, DOUCHEBAG!"
"*CLICK*"

**Panel 15:**
"...as if it was any of her damn business."
"SLAM"

**Panel 16:**
"I just KNOW that 'Sheila' is there doing some talkin'."
"JUST DUMP HIM. HE'S ALL WRONG FOR YOU."

**Panel 17:**
"...and talkin'."
"HE'S THE DEVIL IN A BLUE BASEBALL CAP."
"BLAH BLAH BLAH"

**Panel 18:**
"...and talkin'."
"BLAH BLAH BLAH BLAH BLAH EVIL INCARNATE BLAH BLAH BLAH"

**Panel 19:**
"Of course, my girlfriend and I always make up in the end."
"I'M SORRY. IT'S ALL MY FAULT."
"YES, IT IS."
"* KISS *"

**Panel 20:**
"...and then alluvasudden 'Sheila' is all buddy-buddy with me again. And she's all like:"
"HEY, LET'S GO GET A BEER."

**Panel 21:**
"Dear 'Sheila',"
"I detest you with the white-hot intensity of a thousand suns."
[Visual: Multiple suns of varying sizes drawn around the text]

**THE END**

## Visual Jokes/Puns
- The contrast between Sheila's aggressive behavior (ass smack, door slamming, blocking contact) and her sudden friendliness after reconciliation creates the core comedic irony
- The protagonist's realization about Barry Manilow songs while depressed is a self-aware humor moment
- The final panel's "thousand suns" is visualized with literal drawings of multiple suns, a visual exaggeration of the hyperbolic statement

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A stick-figure comic set in heaven, featuring God (large figure with a halo) speaking with angels (smaller stick figures). The art style is minimalist black line drawings on white background with blue highlights on one angel character. The setting is indicated by a yellow banner labeled "...in heaven" in the top left corner.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (caption):** "...in heaven"
**Panel 1 (dialogue):** "HERE ARE THE FINAL BLUEPRINTS FOR CREATING THE UNIVERSE."

**Panel 2:**
- God: "GET STARTED IMMEDIATELY."
- Angel: "YES, LORD. WE'LL GET ... WAIT,"

**Panel 3:**
- Angel: "YOU WANT THE UNIVERSE TO BE 10-DIMENSIONAL?"

**Panel 4:**
- God: "YEAH, THAT'S RIGHT. NO, WAIT. MAKE THAT ELEVEN DIMENSIONS."
- Angel: "ELEVEN?"

**Panel 5:**
- God: "BUT NO MORE THAN ELEVEN, 'CUZ TWELVE WOULD JUST BE SILLY, RIGHT?"
- Angel: "..."

**Panel 6:**
- God: "AND MAKE SURE THAT ALL BUT FOUR DIMENSIONS ARE ALL SCRUNCHED UP REAL TINY SO NOBODY CAN SEE THEM."

**Panel 7:**
- God: "GET TO WORK. CHOP CHOP!"
- Angel: "YES, LORD."

**Panel 8:**
- Angel: "*chop chop*"

**Panel 9:**
- Angel: "YOU'RE RIGHT. HE'S NUTS."

## Visual Jokes
The humor relies on God's arbitrary and contradictory specifications for the universe (the absurdity of declaring 12 dimensions "silly" while accepting 11), and the angels' deadpan acceptance before the final panel reveals their private skepticism about God's sanity.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A two-panel comic in simple stick-figure style. The top panel shows a family of five standing together. The bottom panel depicts an office workplace with multiple people at desks, charts, and corporate settings. The character with the orange/red head from the family appears in both panels.

## Text Transcription

**Top Panel (Caption):**
"When you were growing up, you thought that your family was annoying, stupid, and weird."

**Top Panel (Speech Bubble):**
"JESUS CHRIST! I WAS BORN INTO A FREAKIN' ZOO."

**Bottom Panel (Caption):**
"...but they were simply preparing you for the real world."

**Bottom Panel (Speech Bubble):**
"JESUS CHRIST! I'M STUCK IN A FREAKIN' ZOO."

## Visual Jokes
The primary joke depends on the visual parallel: the childhood family scene mirrors the adult workplace environment. The character's identical exclamation in both panels emphasizes that adult professional life is just as chaotic and dysfunctional as their family dynamics were, suggesting that family life actually serves as training for navigating workplace absurdity. The "zoo" metaphor applies equally to both situations.

# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure webcomic in black and white. The top panel is almost entirely black with minimal visible detail. The bottom panel shows four stick figures standing in a row against a cloudy sky background. Each figure wears large, rectangular sunglasses that cover most of their face. The art style is minimalist line-drawing.

# Text Transcription
**Caption (top of lower panel):** "On second thought..."

**Caption (bottom of lower panel):** "...Avatar 1-D wasn't such a great idea."

# Visual Joke
The humor relies on the visual appearance of the characters wearing the rectangular sunglasses, which make them appear extremely flat and one-dimensional—literally "1-D" (one-dimensional). This is a play on "Avatar," the film known for its 3-D visual technology, contrasted with these deliberately flat-looking characters that appear to be only one dimension.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A stick-figure comic in the style of "Cyanide & Happiness" or similar webcomics. The protagonist is a simple blue-headed stick figure narrating a story to the "Penthouse Forum" (an adult magazine known for reader-submitted sexual stories). The comic depicts a humorous encounter with a woman with wild black hair, featuring crude drawings of intimate situations, mathematical equations, and scientific terminology used ironically in a sexual context.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"Dear Penthouse Forum,
I never thought this would happen to me."

**Panel 2:**
"I was at the Van Pelt Library just minding my own business."

**Panel 3:**
"Suddenly, this mysterious woman with wild unkempt hair approached me."

**Panel 4:**
"I had no idea who she was but we ended up in bed together.
I CALL THIS THE VENUS BUTTERFLY."

**Panel 5:**
"She was like a ferocious animal ... completely uninhibited."

**Panel 6:**
"In fact, I'm pretty sure that some of the things we did are still illegal in 49 states.
SAY MY NAME, BITCH!"

**Panel 7:**
"Afterwards, we just started speaking casually about grand unification using string-network condensates."

**Panel 8:**
"Soon we had a unified string-net picture of fermions and gauge bosons.
⊕(▢—▢—) = ⊕(▢▢▢)
⊕(▢—▢—) = ⊕(▢—▢)"

**Panel 9:**
"We worked furiously through the night working out the masses of all the fermions."

**Panel 10:**
"This indirectly allowed us to reconcile the black hole information paradox with our theory of string-network condensates!"

**Panel 11:**
"Completely exhausted, we both collapsed like wave functions into a state of deep slumber.
Z Z Z
Z     Z Z"

**Panel 12:**
"When I awoke, she was gone - like a phantom that disappeared into the luminiferous aether.
I never even learned her name."

**Panel 13:**
"All I had left to remember the previous night's erotic outburst was the paradigm-altering theory that we formulated together."

**Panel 14:**
"Please see the attachment to view the final theoretical results."

**Panel 15:**
"One way or another, I always get my results published."

## Visual Jokes/Puns

1. **The juxtaposition of sexual narrative with physics**: The comic mocks Penthouse Forum's formulaic storytelling by abruptly shifting from erotic content to string theory and quantum physics discussions.

2. **"String-network"**: A pun on "string theory" in physics, used in a sexual context.

3. **Wave function collapse**: The phrase "collapsed like wave functions" puns on quantum mechanics terminology while describing exhaustion.

4. **The disappearance reference**: "Disappeared into the luminiferous aether" is a physics reference (outdated ether theory) used metaphorically for the woman vanishing.

5. **Academic publication as motivation**: The final panel subverts the Penthouse Forum format by revealing the true goal was publishing scientific results, not sharing the sexual encounter itself.

# Scene Description
A 9-panel comic strip featuring a silhouetted figure (appearing to be a stock/investor) against a cityscape skyline. The character is shown in profile, communicating with an unseen entity. The art style is minimalist black silhouettes with simple line drawings. The tone shifts from corporate cheerfulness to emotional vulnerability.

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"MY EARNINGS WERE RELEASED TODAY."

**Panel 2:**
"SOLID BALANCE SHEET. STRONG CASH FLOW."

**Panel 3:**
"YET MY STOCK IS TRADING SHARPLY LOWER TODAY."

**Panel 4:**
"WHY DON'T YOU LIKE ME? WHY ARE YOU SELLING OFF MY STOCK?"

**Panel 5:**
"YOU DON'T THINK I'M AN ATTRACTIVE INVESTMENT ANYMORE, DO YOU?"

**Panel 6:**
"YOU JUST USED ME FOR SOME QUICK CAPITAL GAINS AND DITCHED ME LIKE A CHEAP WHORE."

**Panel 7:**
"IS THERE SOMEONE ELSE? YOU'RE INVESTING IN ANOTHER COMPANY, AREN'T YOU?"

**Panel 8:**
"* SOB *"

**Panel 9:**
"BE HONEST. MY P/E RATIO MAKES ME FAT, DOESN'T IT?"

**Bottom Caption:**
"Remember... corporations are people too."

# Visual Joke
The comic anthropomorphizes a corporation, depicting it experiencing romantic rejection and emotional distress over stock performance, culminating in body-image insecurity about its P/E ratio—a humorous commentary on corporate personhood and the emotional language often used in financial markets.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A Cyanide & Happiness style stick-figure comic in black ink on white background. The narrative follows two characters (one with long hair, one circular-headed) through a sequence of increasingly absurd events triggered by a simple greeting. The comic progresses from intimate conversation to marriage, family life, and ends with physical separation.

## Dialogue and Text (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (top):**
"C'MON, STOP BEING SUCH A CHICKEN. JUST WALK UP TO HER AND SAY 'HI'."

**Panel 2 (right side, after arrows):**
"HI."

**Panel 3 (right side):**
"OU OU OOUU OU OOUU OU"
"OU OOU."

**Panel 4 (left side):**
"OUUU'O OUO OOUUOU."
"OUOU OUUOU OU."

**Panel 8 (right side):**
"JUST MARRIED" (on banner/arch)

**Panel 11 (right side):**
"MWAAAAH" (sound effect, crow/bird on house roof)

**Panel 15 (right side):**
(Shows two children and two adults standing together - no dialogue)

## Visual Jokes

The primary visual joke is the **phonetic progression**: A simple "HI" greeting triggers an escalating sequence where the characters' dialogue becomes increasingly garbled ("OU OU OOUU"), suggesting miscommunication that somehow leads to marriage, a house, children, and ultimately separation (the man walking away alone as the crow calls). The absurdist humor relies on the contradiction between the mundane opening advice and the surreal life consequences that follow from one simple greeting.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A humorous relationship comic in simple black and white line art. The top panel shows an open Valentine's Day card with mathematical equations and hearts. The bottom nine panels depict a couple in bed having a conversation, with the man wearing a blue hat and the woman with black hair. The art style is minimalist stick-figure-like drawing.

## Text Transcription

**Valentine's Card (Top):**
- Two heart equations with mathematical symbols
- "YOU ARE THE CURVATURE OF MY HEART."
- "HAPPY VALENTINE'S DAY"

**Comic Dialogue (Reading Order):**

Panel 1:
- Woman: "I DON'T GET IT. ARE YOU SAYING THAT THE STRESS-ENERGY TENSOR REPRESENTS ME?"

Panel 2:
- Man: "YES. I MEAN NO. WELL, IF MY HEART IS, YOU KNOW, LIKE A 4-MANIFOLD WITH A LORENTZIAN METRIC... NO, WAIT, WHAT IT IS..."

Panel 3:
- Man: "YOU'RE LIKE THE PRESENCE OF SOME MASSIVE BODY, AND THE RICCI CURVATURE OF MY HEART..."

Panel 4:
- Woman: "SOME MASSIVE BODY?"

Panel 5:
- Man: "WELL, YEAH... I MEAN... NO."

Panel 6:
- Woman: "WHEN I SAY 'MASSIVE,' I DON'T MEAN 'MASSIVE'; I MEAN 'MASSIVE'."

Panel 7:
- Man: "I MEAN, I'M CERTAINLY NOT CALLING YOU A MASSIVE BODY. HA HA HA HA"

Panel 8:
- (Silent panel - both in bed)

Panel 9:
- (Man alone on couch, looking dejected)

## Visual Jokes
The humor relies on a physics/mathematics pun using Einstein's general relativity equations (stress-energy tensor and Ricci curvature), which the man attempts to use as a romantic metaphor but spectacularly fails to explain, leading to an unintended insult about the woman's body size. The final panel's couch punishment is the physical comedic payoff.

# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure comic featuring two aliens (with characteristic oval heads and dots for eyes) encountering a human character. The art style is minimalist line-drawing. The setting appears to be indoors or in a spacecraft, depicted with basic rectangular frames. The human character becomes increasingly animated throughout the comic, with expressive body language.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
Alien 1: "4 ∆ > ⋈ ↓"
Alien 2: "6 ∇ ⊂ ⋈"
(Aliens speaking in symbols/alien language)

**Panel 2:**
Alien 1: "WHAZAAP, MY EXTRA-TERRESTRIAL BIOLOGICAL BITCHES !!!"

**Panel 3:**
Human: "CHECK IT. CHECK IT. YO. WHAT'S THAT LOOK LIKE?"

**Panel 4:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 5:**
Alien: "IT APPEARS TO BE A TATTOO OF A COMPLETELY RANDOM WORD WRITTEN IN OUR LANGUAGE."

**Panel 6:**
Human: "YEAH, BUT WHAT DOES IT MEAN IN ENGLISH?"

**Panel 7:**
Alien: "THERE IS NO ACCURATE TRANSLATION. IT IS MEANINGLESS IN YOUR LANGUAGE."

**Panel 8:**
Alien: "HOWEVER, I BELIEVE THE CLOSEST TRANSLATION WOULD BE... 'SUBATOMIC ENERGY PARTICLES'."

**Panel 9:**
Human: "THAT'S RIGHT. BADASS !!!"

**Panel 10:**
(Silent panel - human celebrating)

**Panel 11:**
(Silent panel - aliens observing)

**Panel 12:**
Alien 1: "HUMANS ARE SO ADORABLE."
Alien 2: "INDEED."

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the human's enthusiasm about a tattoo that is actually meaningless, having tricked the aliens into providing a seemingly impressive translation ("subatomic energy particles") for what amounts to random alien symbols—playing on the common real-world trend of getting tattoos in foreign languages without understanding them.

# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure comic in black ink on white background. A large figure with a halo (God) stands with a group of smaller stick figures (angels or disciples). The setting is heaven. The art style is minimalist line drawing typical of webcomics.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"...in heaven"
"LORD, WE'RE READY TO START CREATING THE UNIVERSE."

**Panel 2:**
"BUT FIRST WE NEED TO CREATE A SYSTEM OF MATHEMATICS ON WHICH TO BASE IT."

**Panel 3:**
"HERE ARE THE AXIOMS THAT WE'RE PROPOSING."

**Panel 4:**
"OKAY, FINE. BUT JUST MAKE SURE THAT YOU INCLUDE SOME TRUE PROPOSITIONS THAT CAN NEVER BE PROVED WITHIN THE SYSTEM."

**Panel 5:**
"AND MAKE SURE THAT YOU CAN'T PROVE THE CONSISTENCY OF THE SYSTEM UNLESS THE SYSTEM IS INCONSISTENT."

**Panel 6:**
"GET TO WORK, CHOP CHOP."
"YES, LORD."

**Panel 7:**
"MUAHAHAHA"

**Panel 8:**
"HE'S SO SUBTLE."
"AND YET SO MALICIOUS."

# Visual Jokes
This comic references **Gödel's Incompleteness Theorems**, which state that any consistent formal system capable of describing arithmetic contains true statements that cannot be proved within the system, and that no consistent system can prove its own consistency. God is portrayed as deliberately building these limitations into mathematics as a cosmic prank.

# Scene Description
A nine-panel webcomic in black and white sketch style depicting a dark parody of "The Little Engine That Could." The comic shows a small train climbing a mountain, with a silhouetted figure (initially positive, progressively deteriorating in posture) aboard. The art style uses simple line drawings with hatching for shading. The final panel shifts to show a woman in bed speaking to a child.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
- Caption: "Puff, puff, chug, chug went the little engine."
- Speech bubble: "I THINK I CAN, I THINK I CAN, I THINK I CAN."

**Panel 2:**
- Caption: "And she began to climb the mountain."
- Speech bubble: "I THINK I CAN."

**Panel 3:**
- Caption: "Faster and faster she climbed with aaaall her might."
- Speech bubble: "I THINK I CAN."

**Panel 4:**
- Caption: "But then her boyfriend left her for a younger slutty whore."
- Speech bubble: "WAIT, WHAT?"

**Panel 5:**
- Caption: "...leaving her all alone in the cold cruel city to raise her kid all by herself."
- Speech bubble: "SON OF A..."

**Panel 6:**
- Caption: "To make ends meet, she waited tables all day to put herself through law school at night."
- Speech bubble: "FML!"

**Panel 7:**
- Caption: "Under so much pressure, she succumbed to the temptations of drugs and alcohol."
- Speech bubble: "I HATE MY LIFE."

**Panel 8:**
- Caption: "This was followed by depression, regret, and all-around general self-loathing."
- Speech bubble: "I WISH I NEVER HAD THIS KID."

**Panel 9:**
- Caption: "THE END."
- Caption (bottom): "It's best to let them know the harsh realities of life early."

# Visual Jokes
The primary joke is the subversion of the inspirational children's story through increasingly dark and cynical life circumstances, ending with a bitter twist where the storyteller is depicted as justifying the dark narrative to a child in bed—suggesting they're telling this deliberately pessimistic version as a bedtime story.

# Scene Description
A six-panel stick figure comic depicting a situation that escalates from complimenting a baby to mob violence. The art style is minimalist black and white line drawings with simple circular heads and basic body shapes. Characters are distinguished by simple features like hair or head shape.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 2:**
- "AWWW... WHAT A CUTE BABY."
- "THANKS."

**Panel 3:**
- "AWWW... WHAT A CUTE BABY."

**Panel 4:**
- "AAAIUAHH!"
- "HUH? WHAT?"

**Panel 5:**
- "GET AWAY!"
- "NO, WAIT!"
- "HELP!"

**Panel 6:**
- "STOP RIGHT THERE, CREEP!"
- "TAZE HIM!"
- *Caption at bottom: "Stop the discrimination."*

# Visual Joke
The comic is a commentary on gender bias and discrimination. When a woman compliments the baby, it's received warmly ("Thanks"). When a man gives the identical compliment, he's immediately perceived as a threat, triggering a violent mob response. The irony is amplified by the identical dialogue and situation, exposing the unfair assumption that men showing interest in babies are inherently suspicious or dangerous.

# Scene Description
A stick-figure comic in simple black and white line art depicting a family in cars during traffic. The progression shows escalating chaos as a minor traffic incident becomes an increasingly absurd family outing, culminating in a dark humor final panel.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- "HONK HONK"
- "HEY, JACKASS, YOU'RE HOLDING UP TRAFFIC!"

**Panel 2:**
- "THERE'S A CAR ACCIDENT OVER THERE."
- "REALLY? WHERE?"
- "ON THE OTHER SIDE OF THE STREET."

**Panel 3:**
- "HONEY, GET THE KIDS AND BRING THE POPCORN!"

**Panel 4:**
- "LOOK! I THINK I SEE A SMASHED BRAIN!"
- "OOOH."
- "YAY"

**Panel 5:**
- "Some people deserve to burn in a very special level of hell."

**Visual Elements:**
The final panel shows a hellscape with flames and multiple stick figures, serving as the punchline to the family's disturbing enthusiasm about viewing an accident.

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the contrast between the mundane traffic complaint and the family's increasingly morbid excitement about witnessing the accident, treating it like entertainment. The final caption's dark implication about deserving hell serves as meta-commentary on the family's behavior.

- Long-haired character: "ONE MIIIILYUN DOLLARS!"

**Panel 5:**
- Blue cap character: "OF COURSE IT'LL ONLY BE WORTH $287,380 IN TODAY'S DOLLARS, BUT IT'S STILL A MIILYUN DOLLARS."

**Panel 7:**
- (Silent panel showing character walking away)

## Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the irony that after decades of sacrifice, a million dollars in the future will have significantly less purchasing power due to inflation—deflating the excitement of the achievement.

# Scene Description
A humorous webcomic in stick-figure style depicting a satirical "learn programming in 21 days" guide. The comic is divided into nine panels arranged in a 3x3 grid, showing the progression of a programmer's journey over thousands of days. The art style is simple black-and-white stick figures with minimal detail. A single character (wearing glasses) appears throughout, sometimes alone and sometimes with other figures.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Days 1 - 10**
"Teach yourself variables, constants, arrays, strings, expressions, statements, functions,...."

**Days 11 - 21**
"Teach yourself program flow, pointers, references, classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism, ...."

**Days 22 - 697**
"Do a lot of recreational programming. Have fun hacking but remember to learn from your mistakes."

**Days 698 - 3648**
"Interact with other programmers. Work on programming projects together. Learn from them."

**Days 3649 - 7781**
"Teach yourself advanced theoretical physics and formulate a consistent theory of quantum gravity."

**Days 7782 - 14611**
"Teach yourself biochemistry, molecular biology, genetics...."

**Day 14611**
"Use knowledge of biology to make an age-reversing potion."

**Day 14611**
"Use knowledge of physics to build flux capacitor and go back in time to day 21."

**Day 21**
"Replace younger self."

**Bottom right caption**
"As far as I know, this is the easiest way to 'Teach Yourself C++ in 21 Days'."

# Visual Jokes
The central joke is a time-travel paradox: the programmer spends thousands of days learning advanced physics and biology to build a time machine, traveling back to Day 21 to replace their younger self—implying that "learning programming in 21 days" is impossible without decades of prerequisite knowledge obtained through time travel.

# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure style webcomic set at a retail store counter. Two characters interact: a store employee (behind the counter with a register) and a customer. The art style is minimalist black and white line drawing typical of XKCD comics.

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
Employee: "WE JACK UP THE PRICES OF ALL THE ITEMS IN THE STORE."

**Panel 2:**
Employee: "BUT IF YOU APPLY FOR ONE OF OUR LOYALTY CARDS, YOU CAN BUY AT THE REGULAR PRICES."

**Panel 3:**
Employee: "WE JUST NEED YOUR NAME, ADDRESS, EMAIL, PHONE #, AGE, SEX, MARITAL STATUS, NUMBER IN HOUSEHOLD, AND NUMBER OF CARS."

**Panel 4:**
Employee: "THEN, EVERY TIME YOU BUY SOMETHING, YOUR PURCHASES ARE ENTERED INTO OUR DATABASE."

**Panel 5:**
Employee: "THE CARD ALSO HAS AN EMBEDDED RFID TAG FOR UH... QUALITY ASSURANCE."

**Panel 6:**
Employee: "AND WE NEVER SHARE YOUR INFORMATION WITH THIRD PARTIES UNLESS THEY ASK."

**Panel 7:**
(Silent panel - customer appears to be processing)

**Panel 8:**
Customer: "WOW! YOU MEAN I GET A DISCOUNT? WHERE DO I SIGN UP?"

**Panel 9:**
Employee: "SUCKERS." [with a smirk]

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the employee's progressively absurd and invasive data collection practices being presented matter-of-factly, with the final punchline revealing the employee's contempt for customers who fall for this scheme despite its obvious predatory nature.

A stylized image rendered in the aesthetic of the "Matrix" film franchise, featuring bright green monospaced characters and code cascading down a black background. A silhouetted human figure is prominently displayed in the center, composed entirely of the same green matrix code. The figure appears to be in a contemplative or distressed pose.

**Caption (bottom of image):**
"Porn just hasn't been the same for me since I became the ONE."

# Visual Jokes

The humor relies on the juxtaposition of the iconic "The One" character concept from *The Matrix* with an absurdist, crude joke about adult content. The reference to "the One" (Neo's prophesied role) is subverted into a crude double entendre, suggesting the protagonist's newfound power or status has affected their ability to enjoy pornography. The matrix code composition of the figure reinforces the *Matrix* reference while adding to the surreal, darkly comedic tone.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A six-panel webcomic in stick-figure art style featuring a wizard character (wearing a pointed hat) interacting with a person with black hair. The first panel shows mathematical equations on a blackboard. The setting is minimal, suggesting a magical study or classroom environment.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (Blackboard):**
- H⁴(X/X'';ℝ) × H²(y/y'';ℝ)
- H⁴(x;ℝ) × H¹(y;ℝ) ———→ H⁴''
- H⁴(X;ℝ) × H²(y;ℝ) ———→ H⁴''(X
- H⁴(X';ℝ)

**Panel 2 (Blackboard):**
- X'';ℝ) × H²(y/y'';ℝ)
- ·H⁴(y;ℝ) ———→ H⁴''
- '(y;ℝ) ———→ H⁴''(X

**Panel 2 (Dialogue):**
Wizard: "WHAT MANNER OF BLACK ART IS THIS?"
Other character: "?"

**Panel 3 (Dialogue):**
Other character: "IT'S CALLED TOPOLOGY."

**Panel 4 (Dialogue):**
Wizard: "T'WOULD APPEAR YOU PRACTICE MAGIC STRAIGHT OUT OF THE DARK LAND OF MORDOR."
Other character: (silent, pointing at equations)

**Panel 5 (Dialogue):**
Wizard: "TURN TURN FROM YOUR EVIL ROAD LEST YOUR REMAINING DAYS AS A SPOTTED TOAD."
Other character: (silent)

**Panel 6 (Dialogue):**
Other character: "HUH? MY SPELL HAD NO EFFECT. HOW..."
Wizard: "IT'S CALLED TOPOLOGY."

**Panel 7 (Dialogue):**
Wizard: "A TOAD IS TOPOLOGICALLY EQUIVALENT TO A HUMAN. TO A TOPOLOGIST, THERE'S NO DIFFERENCE."

**Panel 8 (Dialogue):**
Other character: "BTW, YOUR HAT IS TOPOLOGICALLY EQUIVALENT TO A THIMBLE."
Wizard: "WHAT?"

**Panel 9 (Caption):**
"Wizards, do not meddle in the affairs of mathematicians, for they are subtle and quick to anger."

# Visual Jokes
1. The wizard's curse to turn someone into a toad fails because topologically, a toad and human are equivalent (both have one hole—the mouth/esophagus).
2. The final punchline: the retort that the wizard's pointed hat is topologically equivalent to a thimble (both are simply-connected surfaces with one hole) insults the wizard's iconic pointed hat by reducing it to an everyday object.
3. The caption is a parody of Tolkien's famous warning about meddling with wizards, applied to mathematicians instead.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A stick-figure comic in nine panels featuring two characters: a wizard-like figure (wearing a pointed hat) and a bespectacled person. The wizard is explaining an invisible ring while the other character asks increasingly skeptical questions. The art style is simple black line drawings on white background.

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
Wizard: "A MORTAL WHO OFTEN WEARS THE RING TO BECOME INVISIBLE WILL SOON WALK IN THE TWILIGHT UNDER THE EYE."

**Panel 2:**
Other character: "WAIT, DID YOU SAY INVISIBLE? THIS RING MAKES YOU INVISIBLE?"
Wizard: "WHY, YES..."

**Panel 3:**
Other character: "HOW DOES IT WORK?"
Wizard: "SUCH MATTERS ARE BEST LEFT UNTIL DAYLIGHT."

**Panel 4:**
Other character: "WHY DOES IT MATTER WHEN WE TALK ABOUT TECHNOLOGY?"

**Panel 5:**
Wizard: "IT'S NOT TECHNOLOGY! 'TIS MAGIC, BOY!"
Other character: "OH... OKAY."

**Panel 6:**
Other character: "BUT SURELY THIS 'MAGIC' IS BASED ON SCIENTIFIC PRINCIPLES, EVEN IF ONLY WEAKLY ROOTED IN FACT."

**Panel 7:**
Other character: "FOR EXAMPLE, I'M ASSUMING THAT THE RING SOMEHOW PREVENTS THE ABSORPTION AND SCATTERING OF INCIDENT ELECTROMAGNETIC WAVES."
Wizard: "ELECTRO-WHAT?"

**Panel 8:**
Other character: "IS IT EFFECTIVE FOR ALL WAVELENGTHS OR ONLY FOR THE VISIBLE PART OF THE SPECTRUM?"
Wizard: "FORGET IT."

**Panel 9:**
Other character: "DOES THE RING ALSO MAGICALLY SUSPEND YOUR DISBELIEF?"

**Footer Caption:**
"Dear Book Stores,
Please stop putting fantasy and science fiction books into the same category."

# Visual Jokes
The comic's central joke is the tension between magical and scientific explanations: the wizard insists the ring operates through magic and becomes increasingly frustrated when the other character tries to rationalize it with electromagnetic theory. The footer punchline suggests this conflict reflects how bookstores categorize incompatible genres together.

# Scene Description
A nine-panel comic featuring two simple stick figures (one with long hair, one bald) sitting at a table with a laptop and papers. The art style is minimalist line drawing. The couple discusses their financial situation and life choices across the panels.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"OUR NET WORTH IS NOW SMACK DAB IN THE MIDDLE OF THE PARETO DISTRIBUTION."

**Panel 2:**
"AND WITH YOUR $5/HR RAISE, OUR COMBINED IN-COME TAKES US ABOVE THE POVERTY LINE."

**Panel 3:**
"THIS MEANS WE CAN FINALLY MAKE OUR MINIMUM CREDIT CARD PAYMENTS WITHOUT MISSING A MORTGAGE PAYMENT."

**Panel 4:**
"NOW, IF WE SKIMP ON MEALS AND APPLY FOR MORE CREDIT CARDS,..."

**Panel 5:**
"...WE CAN BUY A FUCK-THE-JONESES LUXURY ITEM EVERY COUPLE OF YEARS."

**Panel 6:**
"KINDA MAKES LIVING UNDER THE HEEL OF CRUSHING DEBT SEEM SOMEWHAT INNOCUOUS, HUH?"

**Panel 7:**
(Silent panel - both figures sit)

**Panel 8:**
"WE DID IT."

**Panel 9:**
"WE'RE OFFICIALLY AMERICAN MIDDLE CLASS, BABY!"

# Visual Jokes
The escalating irony of the couple's "accomplishments"—moving from barely above poverty to affording luxury items through debt accumulation—is presented as genuine celebration, critiquing the precarious nature of middle-class financial stability built on credit and consumption.

# Scene Description

A single-panel comic formatted as a complaint letter. The art style is simple black-and-white line drawings. The letter contains two small diagrams of khaki pants at the top, followed by a larger illustration depicting three stick figures on public transportation (appears to be a bus or train). One figure sits wearing the pants in question, with a blue head, while two other figures stand nearby looking uncomfortable.

# Text Transcription

**Letter Header:**
Dear Nameless Pants Company,

**Body:**
I am writing in regard to your K-1 line of khaki pants (Style #5318008). Having just purchased a pair, I feel that I must point out a design flaw in this item. When the pants are bent in a certain way, the zipper has a tendency to bulge into an awkward shape.

**Caption below diagrams:**
It makes me look like I'm popping a huge boner every time I try to sit down. I just felt compelled to inform you that...

**Final line:**
...these pants are AWESOME! Thanks!

**Closing:**
Sincerely,
A Fan

# Visual Joke

The humor is entirely dependent on the image: the diagram shows how the zipper bulges outward when the pants are bent/sat in, creating an appearance of sexual arousal. The comic plays on the contrast between framing this as a serious design complaint and then praising the pants anyway, as if the wearer finds this "flaw" to be a desirable feature.

comics.title: Pascal's Wager
comics.transcript:
# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A simple stick-figure webcomic in three panels. Two adult figures in helmets are having a conversation with two children in a valley-like setting with explosions, a tree, and sun in the background. The art style is minimalist black ink on white.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (large, top):**
"ALL I'M SAYING IS THAT THE EXISTENCE OF A GOD PLAYS NO ROLE IN ANY SCIENTIFIC EXPLANATIONS OF NATURAL PHENOMENA. THERE ARE NO MODELS OF THE PHYSICAL UNIVERSE IN WHICH GOD'S EXISTENCE IS REQUIRED OR USEFUL. LOOK, I'M NOT DENYING THE POSSIBILITY OF THE EXISTENCE OF ANY FORMS OF HIGHER INTELLIGENCE BEYOND THE SCOPE OF OUR COMPREHENSION. WHAT I'M TRYING TO SAY IS THAT YOUR PARTICULAR EXPLANATIONS ARE UNINTELLIGIBLE FROM A SCIENTIFIC POINT OF VIEW. FURTHERMORE..."

**Panel 2 (bottom left):**
"OKAY, THAT'S ENOUGH FOR ME."
"HUH? WHAT?..."

**Panel 3 (bottom right):**
"MYTH BUSTED!"
"YOU GUYS."

## Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the contrast between the adult's lengthy, careful scientific argument about God and burden of proof, and the children's immediate response to "myth busted" the entire philosophical discussion—treating complex theology as if it were a simple fact-checking exercise.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A diary entry comic in simple black and white line art style featuring a young student narrator sharing various incidents from a typical school day. The art style is minimalist with stick-figure-like characters.

# Text Transcription

**Header:**
Dear Diary,

Today was just another typical day at school. Let me just say for the record that middle school is the dumbest idea ever invented. You get gorillas like me who need to shave twice a day mixed in with little pipsqueaks whose testicles haven't dropped yet.

**First scene (labeled "ME"):**
Character speech bubble: "OUTTA MY WAY, N00BS!"

**Caption:**
Before homeroom, I snuck into the janitor's closet with Kelly Forkes and Jen Fallon where we played a good game called Seven Minutes in Heaven.

**Second scene:**
Door labeled "JANITOR" with three figures standing outside.

**Caption:**
Sure, I'm only in middle school, but I still get alotta nookie for my age. I guess it comes with the territory – me being captain of the wrestling team and all. God, I love being a jock.

**Third scene:**
Character saying: "SHIP-SEKI!"

**Caption:**
During third period, we had a math test so I made sure to sit behind this Korean kid. I heard that those people are good at math and stuff.

**Fourth scene (labeled with two side-by-side figures):**
Left figure: "Greg"
Right figure: "dorky fat kid"

**Caption:**
During lunch period, I ran into this loser kid named Greg or something. I can't stand him cuz he's always hanging out with this dorky fat kid like they wuz in love or something.

Character speech bubble: "LOL FAG."

**Caption:**
Anyway, I used the lunch money to buy a six-pack and I got drunk in the woods behind the school with a bunch of high school kids.

**Final scene:**
Three figures standing together.

**Closing caption:**
All in all, it was a pretty good day at school.

**Footer:**
I'd like to see this book written and the pink one banned from libraries.

# Visual Jokes/Puns

- The narrator's self-description as needing to "shave twice a day" is played for crude humor about masculinity.
- "Seven Minutes in Heaven" is presented as an innocent-sounding game name for an obviously sexual activity.
- The "SHIP-SEKI" joke references racial stereotyping about Asian students and math ability.
- The final footer comment suggests this is satire mocking immature, problematic teenage behavior and attitudes.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A multi-panel educational webcomic with a minimalist stick-figure art style set against white backgrounds with black text and binary code sequences. The comic progresses from philosophical discussion to cosmic revelation, featuring simple line drawings of stick figures, a Turing machine diagram, a photograph of a person, Earth in space, and binary code overlays.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"Today I want to talk to you about a long, but finite, sequence of random ones and zeroes. This sequence is thousands and thousands of digits long. Part of this particular sequence looks like this:"

[Binary code block displayed]

"It certainly seems random, and so it is. One can almost imagine that this sequence was produced by repeatedly flipping a fair coin. Now you can't touch or feel this sequence of numbers like it was a real object, but it is definitely real."

**Panel 2:**
"It lives as if life in the Platonic ether, floating freely and happily along with an infinite number of other sequences and other mathematical constructs - like space-filling curves, Klein bottles, and infinite dimensional manifolds. It has an absolute existence independent of any human mind able to conceive or contemplate it. They all come from 'The Book'."

[Turing machine diagram displayed with mathematical notation]

"In fact, if you look far enough into the binary representation of pi, you would most probably find this exact sequence embedded somewhere in there.

π = 11.001001000011111..."

**Panel 3:**
"This random sequence of numbers existed before you were born. It existed before the storming of the Bastille. It existed before Caesar crossed the Rubicon. It existed before the Egyptians built the great pyramids. And before the dinosaurs roamed the Earth."

[Binary code background with silhouettes of a bird and dinosaur]

**Panel 4:**
"And because its existence is abstract - transcending space and time - it most likely existed at the beginning of the universe."

**Panel 5:**
"Now an amazing thing about this sequence is that you can turn it into almost anything by feeding it into a machine. You could build a machine that would turn it into the third movement of Beethoven's 10th. You could build a machine that would turn it into a moving series of pictures."

[Simple box diagram with arrow pointing to photograph of a person]

"Or you could build a machine that would turn it into a general-purpose operating system - and someone has."

**Panel 6:**
[Binary code overlay with image of Earth against black space background]

"Now the single 'smartest' (and most brazen) thing that any person has ever done..."

"...was to convince the world that this abstract sequence of numbers was his property."

**Final Panel:**
"C:\GENIUS!"

# Visual Jokes/Puns Dependent on Image

1. The binary code sequences are presented as actual visual representations of abstract mathematical objects existing in the "Platonic ether"
2. The progression from abstract binary → machine diagram → photograph → Earth creates a visual metaphor for information encoding reality
3. The final "C:\GENIUS!" mimics a computer file path, completing the metaphor that the Earth's operating system (life itself) is property—a darkly comedic reference to claiming copyright on code that encodes reality
4. The silhouettes of dinosaur and bird in the binary background create a visual pun on the concept of information existing before physical forms

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description

A retro-styled computer terminal interface rendered in green monospace text on a black background, mimicking 1980s-90s hacker aesthetic. The image shows a login screen for a fictional "Imperial Computer Network" with ASCII art lettering. No characters are present; this is purely a text-based interface screen.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

Trying xxx.imperialnet.xxx.xxx...
Connected to xxx.imperialnet.xxx.xxx.
Escape character is '^]'.

Welcome to the entire Imperial Computer Network.
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

[ASCII art logo of "IMPERIAL"]

Before attempting to login, please ensure you have
read the Galactic Empire Imperial Computing Regulations.

ATTENTION!
This shell is intended for QUALIFIED PERSONNEL ONLY.
If you are not an authorised user of this system,
DO NOT ATTEMPT TO LOGIN.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

If you are here for the first time, type 'new' to create a new account.
Or if you want to login as a Guest, just ENTER and type your
name at the login prompt and type 'guest' as your password.

login: R2D2
passwd: guest

Welcome, R2D2.
You now have root access to the entire Imperial Computer Network.

Enter 'help' for a list of built-in commands.

$ find / -name "tractorbeam"

Am I the only one who thought that computer security
seemed woefully lax on the Death Star?

# Visual Jokes/Puns

The humor depends entirely on the image: a Star Wars reference showing R2D2 logging into the Death Star's computer system with trivially easy credentials ("guest" password), followed by a search command for "tractorbeam." The joke is the commentary that the Death Star's security was absurdly poor—R2D2 gains root access instantly and can presumably find and disable critical systems. This is a meta-joke about both the fictional universe's security flaws and the actual ease with which R2D2 infiltrated the Death Star in *A New Hope*.

# Scene Description

A simple stick-figure comic strip with minimalist art style. Two businessmen in fedoras carrying briefcases encounter a demon character with horns. The setting progresses from a street meeting to an office/contract scenario.

---

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 2:**
- "WHY, HELLO, WALL STREET."

**Panel 3:**
- "TIME TO SETTLE YOUR DEBTS. I PROMISED YOU RICHES BEYOND YOUR WILDEST DREAMS OF AVARICE."

**Panel 4:**
- "AND NOW YOUR SOULS BELONG TO ME."

**Panel 5:**
- "UH... YOU DIDN'T READ THE FINE PRINT OF OUR CONTRACT, DID YOU?"
- "WHAT?"

**Panel 6:**
- "HERE ARE THE TERMS TO WHICH WE AGREED."

**Panel 7:**
- "YOU SEE, WE POOLED OUR SOULS TOGETHER AND CREATED A PORTFOLIO OF SECURITIZED SOUL OBLIGATIONS WHICH WE SOLD RETAIL TO MAIN STREET. NOW, UNDER THE TERMS OF THE CONTRACT, YOU ARE REQUIRED TO SELL TO US SOUL DEFAULT SWAPS ON THIS UNDERLYING REFERENCE PORTFOLIO. SO IF YOU CLAIM OUR SOULS NOW, WE WOULD DEFAULT ON OUR SOUL OBLIGATIONS AND YOU WOULD HAVE TO PAY US OUR SOULS BACK."
- "?"

**Panel 8:**
- "ON THE BRIGHT SIDE, WE BOTH GET TO SCREW THE PEASANTS AGAIN."

**Panel 9:**
- "SO..."

**Panel 10:**
- "THE LEARNER BECOMES THE MASTER, HUH?"
- "ONLY A MASTER OF AWESOMENESS."

---

# Visual Jokes/Puns

The primary joke is a financial satire: the demon's traditional soul-selling contract is subverted by Wall Street's use of complex financial instruments (securitized obligations, derivatives, and default swaps)—turning the tables on the devil himself through the very mechanisms that caused the 2008 financial crisis. The final panel's ironic camaraderie between the demon and the bankers suggests they've become kindred spirits in exploiting the masses.

# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure webcomic with nine panels arranged in a 3x3 grid. Two stick figures—one wearing what appears to be a shirt/torso and one in profile—converse throughout. The art style is minimalist black line drawings on white background. The final panel shows one figure flying away with a jet pack.

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- Figure 1: "I'M ALREADY BORED WITH MY iPAD."
- Figure 2: (listening)

**Panel 2:**
- Figure 2: "NO FLASH SUPPORT OR MULTITASKING. AND THE WIFI ISSUES ARE.... ARE...."

**Panel 3:**
- Figure 1: "UH... WHAT IS THAT ON YOUR BACK?"
- Figure 2: "PERSONAL JET PACK."

**Panel 4:**
- Figure 1: "HOW WERE YOU ABLE TO AFFORD A JET PACK?"

**Panel 5:**
- Figure 2: "WELL, INSTEAD OF BUYING AN iPAD, I USED THE MONEY TO HELP LEVERAGE THE PURCHASE OF APPLE JULY 27th CALLS."

(Silent panel—figures standing)

(Silent panel—Figure 2 floating upward with jet pack)

(Figure 1 standing alone holding an iPad as Figure 2 flies away in the jet pack)

# Visual Jokes
The primary joke is situational irony: instead of buying the disappointing iPad, Figure 2 invested in Apple stock options and profited enough to buy a jet pack—an absurdly impractical but infinitely more exciting alternative. The final panel wordlessly emphasizes this contrast as Figure 1 remains earthbound with their iPad while Figure 2 literally rises above the situation.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A humorous comic comparing two romantic relationship scenarios through economic and mathematical analysis. The top half is a detailed whiteboard/notebook with equations and charts; the bottom half shows a silhouetted couple, with one person proposing. The art style is minimalist with hand-drawn lettering and mathematical notation.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Top Section - Character Analysis**

CHAD
- ASSETS: Sort of a bad-boy, Owns Harley, Great in bed
- LIABILITIES: Sort of a bad-boy, Poor credit, Bad conversation skills

WALTER
- ASSETS: Kinda geeky, Good sense of humor, High earning potential, Great with kids
- LIABILITIES: Kinda geeky, Workaholic, Likes nickelback

**Equations and Formulas**

Happiness with Chad = X₄
Happiness with Walter = Y₄

X₄ = Pₓ + (5 Eₓ) + (3 Hₓ)
Y₄ = Pᵧ + (5 Eᵧ) + (3 Hᵧ)

n(x) = (1/√(2π)) e^(-x²/2)

μ₀(Cₓ, X₀) + μ₁(Cᵧ, Xᵧ) = (C₀^(1-α) X₀^(1-α))/(1-α) + ρ (C₁^(1-α) X₁^(1-α))/(1-α)

n ≈ log∈(x) [complex mathematical notation]

p↓ᵢ(sⱼ, k) = Sⱼ N(zₖ + σⱼ) - κe^(-B) [additional notation]

zₖ = (ln(Sⱼ/K) + rᵧ)/σⱼ - σⱼ/2

FV = PV(1+r)^t

E(R) = Σ pₑRₑ from e=1 to m

**Chart Section**

LONG-TERM PROJECTIONS

[Graph showing two lines over TIME vs EXPECTED HAPPINESS:
- Dashed line labeled "WITH CHAD" - starts high, gradually decreases
- Solid line labeled "WITH WALTER" - starts lower, gradually increases over time]

**Bottom Section - Comic Scene**

[Person proposing to another person in silhouette]

Dialogue: "O, WALTER! OF COURSE I'LL MARRY YOU!"

# Visual Jokes/Puns

1. **The entire premise**: A romantic proposal is presented as a complex economic and mathematical analysis comparing two relationship options, with utility functions, probability distributions, and financial formulas treating love as a quantifiable variable.

2. **The chart**: Shows that while Chad provides immediate happiness ("bad-boy" appeal), Walter's happiness increases over time due to his stability, earning potential, and emotional availability—a long-term investment metaphor.

3. **The contrast**: The clinical, equation-heavy analysis is juxtaposed with a genuine romantic moment, implying that despite all the rational analysis, the emotional/romantic decision ultimately wins out.

# Scene Description
A nine-panel stick figure comic in simple black and white line art. A stick figure character with a blue circular head sits on a park bench throughout, observing animals (squirrels and dogs). The comic progresses from peaceful observation to violent action, ending with a philosophical question. The art style is minimalist webcomic format.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (caption):**
"I love sitting in the park observing the beauty of nature."

**Panel 2:**
"Aww, what a cute little squirrel."
"I like watching animals frolicking happy and free."

**Panel 3:**
"Hey there, lil' fella."
"Animals are like our brothers and sisters on this Earth."

**Panel 4:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 5:**
"Uh oh, should I warn the squirrel?"

**Panel 6:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 7:**
"Rip. Kill. Slassh."

**Panel 8:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 9 (caption):**
"But who am I to violate the Prime Directive?"

# Visual Jokes
The humor depends on the visual progression: the character's peaceful philosophy about nature's harmony is immediately tested when dogs attack the squirrel. The final panel's reference to Star Trek's "Prime Directive" (non-interference) is darkly comedic given the violent outcome, suggesting the character justified inaction through moral philosophy rather than genuine principle.

A six-panel stick figure webcomic with minimalist art style. The main character wears a blue cap and red clothing. A second character with a round head and glasses appears in most panels. The final panel shows them in bed together. The comic is titled "VICTORIA'S SECRET" at the top and "BUYING LINGERIE - Sooooooo NOT worth it." at the bottom.

**Panel 2:**
- Title: "VICTORIA'S SECRET"

# Scene Description
A simple black-and-white cartoon illustration showing a round-headed figure with two dot eyes holding open a large book titled "STRING THEORY." The figure appears confused or overwhelmed. To the right sits a dictionary labeled "WITTEN TO ENGLISH DICTIONARY." The art style is minimalist and humorous, drawn in a basic line-drawing style typical of instructional or educational comics.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)
- Title: "String Theory for Dummies"
- Book title (held by figure): "STRING THEORY"
- Dictionary label: "WITTEN TO ENGLISH DICTIONARY"
- Caption at bottom: "STEP 1: begin"

# Visual Joke
The humor relies on the contrast between the figure attempting to learn string theory using a regular book and the need for a specialized "Witten to English Dictionary" to translate it into understandable language. This is a reference to physicist Edward Witten, a prominent string theorist known for complex mathematical formulations. The implication is that string theory is so difficult that even a "dummies" guide requires translation from Witten's notoriously dense theoretical work.

# Scene Description
A three-panel comic strip (with multiple panels per row) featuring two simple stick figures on either side of a Mondrian-style abstract painting featuring a wooden frame, red background, blue and yellow rectangles, and white lines. The art style is minimalist black-and-white line drawing with the painting as the colorful focal point.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (top left):** [No dialogue]

**Panel 2 (top center):** 
- Left figure: "INTERESTING. I SEE THE HEAVY INFLUENCE OF THE NON-REPRESENTATIONAL FORMS OF NEO-PLASTICISM."
- Right figure: "WHAT?"

**Panel 3 (top right):**
- Right figure: "O, RIGHT. SURE, I CAN SEE THAT."

**Panel 4 (middle left):** [No dialogue]

**Panel 5 (middle center):**
- Left figure: "NOTICE THE MINIMALIST USE OF COLOR TO EXPRESS THE UNIVERSAL ABSOLUTES THAT UNDERLIE REALITY."

**Panel 6 (middle right):** [No dialogue]

**Panel 7 (bottom left):**
- Left figure: "CLEARLY, THIS REPRESENTS A DISGUISED FULFILLMENT OF A LATENT DESIRE TO SEXUALLY POSSESS ONE'S OWN SISTER."
- Right figure: "HUH?"

**Panel 8 (bottom center):**
- Left figure: "WTF?"

**Panel 9 (bottom right):**
- Right figure: "THAT'S... UH... DISTURBING."
- Left figure: "YEAH, RIGHT?"

**Caption (bottom right):**
"FACT: Criticism always tells you more about the critic than it does about the object of criticism."

# Visual Jokes
The humor lies in the escalating absurdity of art criticism interpretations of the same simple painting—from legitimate modernist analysis to increasingly unhinged pseudo-psychological readings, culminating in a bizarre Freudian interpretation that has nothing to do with the artwork itself, perfectly demonstrating the caption's point.

# Scene Description
A nine-panel stick figure comic with a simple black-and-white art style. Two characters are shown in conversation across most panels, with one character speaking from the left side and another responding from the right. The final panel includes a caption at the bottom.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"SO EINSTEIN'S THEORY COMPLETELY OVERTURNED OUR CONVENTIONAL NOTIONS OF SPACE AND TIME."

**Panel 2:**
"SOUNDS REALLY 'PROGRESSIVE'."
"INDEED, IT WAS."

"WOW. HOW 'EQUITABLE'. WHAT ARE YOU, SOME KINDA SOCIALIST?"

**Panel 6:**
(Silent panel)

**Panel 7:**
"WHAT?"

**Panel 8:**
(Silent panel)

**Panel 9:**
"ANYWAY,... WHY DO YOU HATE OUR TROOPS?"

**Bottom caption:**
"This is what comes to mind when I hear the words 'Political Science'."

# Visual Joke
The humor relies on the absurd non-sequitur where the left character's neutral explanation of Einstein's physics is misinterpreted through an increasingly politicized lens, culminating in an accusation of hating troops—a satirical comment on how scientific or academic discourse gets distorted through ideological filters.

# Scene Description
A two-panel comic in simple black-and-white line art. The top panel shows a serene pastoral scene with a rabbit in a field, trees, rolling hills, sun, and clouds. The bottom panel depicts the same scene overlaid with numerous scientific equations, diagrams, and annotations covering the landscape and sky. The art style is minimalist and sketch-like.

# Text Transcription

**Top Panel:**
(No text)

**Bottom Panel:**
(Reading left to right, top to bottom)

Left side (sky):
- Circular diagram with mathematical notation
- ∇·E = ¼ ρ
- ∇·B = 0
- ∇×E + ∂B/∂t = 0
- F = Gm₁m₂/r²
- Rₐᵥᵧ - ⅛gᵣₐᵦRᵣₐᵦ = 8πGTᵣₐᵦ

Center (over landscape):
- Various mathematical expressions and partial differential equations
- P + ½ρv² + ρgh = C
- C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O
- [−ℏ²/2m ∇² + V]ψ = iℏ ∂ψ/∂t

Right side (over trees):
- Diagram showing atomic/molecular structure with radiating lines labeled with π and N notations
- nCO₂ + nH₂O → (CH₂O)ₙ + nO₂
- hν
- Q ⟷ CO₂

**Bottom right caption:**
"This is how scientists see the world."

# Visual Jokes
The primary visual joke is the contrast between the peaceful, simple natural scene in the top panel and the same scene completely obscured by scientific equations and formulas in the bottom panel—illustrating how scientists perceive the world through complex mathematical and physical principles rather than simple observation.

# Scene Description
A four-panel black and white comic strip with simple line drawings showing the relationship between the intellect and senses. Each panel contains dialogue and diagrams illustrating a philosophical progression.

---

# Text Transcription

**Panel 1:**
- Caption: "The intellect receives information about the world from the senses."
- Left box label: "INTELLECT"
- Right box label: "SENSES"
- Arrows pointing between the boxes
- Sensory descriptors around SENSES: "HOT / COLD / LOUD / SOFT / RED / BLUE / BIG / SMALL / BITTER / SWEET"

**Panel 2:**
- Thought bubble containing equations: "F = ma / W_TOT = ΔK / E(r) = q/(4πε₀) / ∫(r') λ dr' / r³ / T²/a³ = 4π²/GM"
- Left box label: "INTELLECT"
- Right box label: "SENSES"
- Caption: "The intellect processes the information to build models of the physical world."

**Panel 3:**
- Thought bubble containing equations: "L = L₀√(1 - v²/c²) / iℏ ∂/∂t |ψ⟩ = H |ψ⟩ / G_μν = 8πGT_αβ"
- Left box label: "INTELLECT"
- Right box label: "SENSES"
- Caption: "The intellect then concludes that the world is counterintuitive..."

**Panel 4:**
- Speech bubble from SENSES to INTELLECT: "YOU SUCK."
- Left box label: "INTELLECT"
- Right box label: "SENSES"
- Caption: "O, bitter irony."

---

# Visual Jokes
The comic's humor relies on the visual contrast between increasingly complex mathematical formulas (from classical mechanics through quantum mechanics and general relativity) used by the intellect to contradict sensory experience, ultimately resulting in the senses dismissing the intellect with a crude insult. The joke culminates in irony: the intellect's conclusion that reality contradicts the senses is itself contradicted when the senses literally reject it.

# Scene Description

A hand-drawn map in the style of a fantasy island illustration depicts an island of knowledge surrounded by concentric circles representing a "sea of ignorance." The island is divided into labeled regions representing different academic disciplines and fields of study, each marked with small tree symbols and terrain features. The illustration uses simple line work in black ink.

# Text Transcription

**Map Labels (by region, reading left to right, top to bottom):**

- PHYSICAL SCIENCES
- PHYSICS
- BIOCHEMISTRY
- GENETICS
- ELECTRICAL ENGINEERING
- ENGINEERING
- MOLECULAR BIOLOGY
- CHEMISTRY
- PHILOSOPHY (BUILDING BLOCKS)
- ANALYSIS
- SOCIAL SCIENCES
- ANTHROPOLOGY
- LINGUISTICS
- MATH
- THE BAY OF UNKNOWABLE TRUTHS
- CLIMATOLOGY
- EARTH SCIENCES
- GEOLOGY

**Left sidebar (vertical text):**
THE SEA OF IGNORANCE

**Bottom text (italicized quote):**
We live on an island surrounded by a sea of ignorance. As our island of knowledge grows, so does the shore of our ignorance.
— John Archibald Wheeler

# Visual Jokes/Puns

The central conceit is a visual metaphor: as the "island of knowledge" expands, its coastline (the "shore of ignorance") grows longer, illustrating the paradox that increased knowledge reveals more unknowns. "The Bay of Unknowable Truths" serves as an ironic inland sea on the island itself.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic in black ink with blue watercolor-style hat fills. Two characters meet at a bus stop: one wearing a blue hat (male-presenting) and one with long straight hair (female-presenting). The comic progresses through their increasingly awkward conversation as the male character asks increasingly inappropriate and bizarre questions, escalating from small talk to crude personal inquiries.

---

## Dialogue and Text (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
- Label: "BUS STOP"
- Character 1: "HI."
- Character 2: "OH...UH...HI."

**Panel 4:**
- Character 1: "SOME WEATHER, HUH?"
- Character 2: "YEAH, LOOKS LIKE A HEAT WAVE."

**Panel 6:**
- Character 1: "EXCUSE ME, BUT HOW MANY MILLISECOND PULSARS HAVE BEEN DETECTED SO FAR IN THE GLOBULAR CLUSTER 47 TUCANAE?"
- Character 2: "LIGHT?"

**Panel 7:**
- Character 2: "HOW THE HELL AM I SUPPOSED TO KNOW?"

**Panel 8:**
- Character 1: "FREAK."

**Panel 11:**
- Character 1: "HEY, Y'KNOW THAT PART OF THE MALE ANATOMY LOCATED BETWEEN THE BALLS AND THE ANUS? YEAH, WHAT'S THAT CALLED?"
- Character 2: "LIGHT?"

**Panel 12:**
- Character 1: "LOOK, MISTER, I DON'T KNOW WHAT THE HELL YOUR PROBLEM IS, BUT PLEASE STOP TALKING TO ME."

**Panel 15:**
- Character 1: "SO HOW OFTEN DOES ONE HAVE TO MASTURBATE BEFORE IT'S CONSIDERED EXCESSIVE?"

**Panel 16:**
- Sound effect: "*SPRAY*"
- Character 1: "AAAA!!!GH"

**Panel 17:**
- Character 2: "OOOH... STINGS..."

**Bottom section (separate scene):**
- Caption: "Questions I (probably) wouldn't ask a total stranger..."
- Caption: "...yet I wouldn't didn't hesitate to ask Google."
- Character 1 (with tablet/phone): "WHY ARE HUMAN FEMALES THE ONLY PRIMATES WITH BIG BOOBS?"

---

## Visual Jokes
- The male character's escalation from normal small talk → obtuse science question → crude anatomical question → masturbation frequency question creates absurdist humor
- The female character's deadpan "LIGHT?" responses (non-sequiturs) to inappropriate questions
- The punchline: she pepper-sprays him, then the final panel reveals he's now Googling equally inappropriate questions on his device
- The contrast between "wouldn't ask a stranger" yet immediately consulting Google shows the absurdity of information-seeking behavior

# Scene Description
A six-panel webcomic in simple black stick-figure style. The first five panels show a conversation between two stick figures (one appearing to represent a person, one appearing to represent their partner). The final panel shows a highway scene with two cars. The art style is minimalist line drawing.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"I WILL ALWAYS LOVE YOU."

**Panel 2:**
"I WANT YOU NEAR ME FOREVER."

**Panel 3:**
"I HAVE NO INTERNAL MEASURE AGAINST WHICH TO GAUGE MY PROGRESS TOWARDS SELF-ACTUALIZATION."
[Response:] "UGH"

**Panel 4:**
"MY ENTIRE SELF-WORTH IS BASED ON YOUR OPINION OF ME AND ON EXTERNAL VALIDATIONS."

**Panel 5:**
"VALIDATE ME! VALIDATE ME! FOR THE LOVE OF ALL THAT'S HOLY!"

**Panel 6:**
"Every time someone tailgates me on the highway, that is how I imagine the driver to be."

# Visual Jokes
The comic's humor derives from escalating emotional dependence: romantic declarations evolve into increasingly desperate pleas for external validation, culminating in the punchline that tailgating behavior is interpreted as pathological neediness for validation.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A nine-panel comic in black and white and blue tones depicting a man's increasingly elaborate romantic fantasy that progressively derails. The art style shifts from silhouettes to simple circle-faced characters in the later panels. The comic uses a mix of detailed prose and simple dialogue to create comedic contrast.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"So tonight I'm taking Jen to Le Bec Fin, the finest restaurant in all of Philadelphia."

**Panel 2:**
"This could be THE night. I think this relationship is ready to move to the next level."

**Panel 3:**
"After dinner, I'm going to invite her home and we will uncork that bottle of 1947 Château Cheval Blanc."

**Panel 4:**
"Then I'll put on some Marvin Gaye and make my move."

**Panel 5:**
"Our trembling bodies will be drawn to each other with a mindless hunger as if in response to an unspoken summons."

**Panel 6:**
"I will tear her clothes off and we will, at long last, surrender to the violent impulses of unconfessed desire."

**Panel 7:**
"As I feel the smooth silkiness of her skin against mine, her lost in a blur with no distinction between her being and mine, we will be lifted up in sweet convulsion on a cloud of orgasmic delight, shaping our desires into a single sensation of such intensity and joy that..."

**Panel 8:**
"Well, you get the picture. So if you see a necktie on the door knob, don't come a-knockin'."
Character 2: "CUZ IT WOULD MEAN THAT WE BE MAKIN' SOME SWEET SWEET LOVE."

**Panel 9:**
Character 1: "HOWEVER, IF THERE'S NO NECKTIE, THEN I PROBABLY CHICKENED OUT AND DIDN'T INVITE HER BACK."

Character 2: "IN WHICH CASE I'LL PROLY JUST DOWNLOAD SOME PORN, PUNISH THE PIGMAN, AND CRY MYSELF TO SLEEP."

**Caption (bottom right):**
"A single necktie can convey quite a 'bit' of information."

## Visual Jokes/Puns
- The dramatic shift from flowery, pretentious language to crude slang mirrors the tonal whiplash of the fantasy collapsing
- The "bit" in quotation marks at the end plays on the necktie as a signal/indicator
- The contrast between the silhouetted romantic silhouettes in early panels and the simple sad circle-faces in later panels emphasizes the emotional deflation of the fantasy

comics.transcript:
# FUCKING MAGNETS: How do they work?

## Scene Description
This is an educational infographic with a white background, featuring black text, diagrams, and illustrations. The style is informal and comedic, using simple line drawings to explain magnetic principles. Characters include simple magnet illustrations, wire coils, atoms, and electrons. The overall layout flows top-to-bottom with explanatory text blocks interspersed with scientific diagrams.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

The phenomenon of magnetism was first observed at least 2500 years ago in small fragments of magnetized iron ore. The earliest known surviving descriptions of magnets show that such phenomena were known to the people of ancient Greece, India, China, and parts of Asia Minor. These strange stones attracted to and repelled only objects like iron and nickel. It was observed that rubbing a magnet with another magnet would sometimes cause the previously non-magnetized object to become magnetized. It was also observed that when magnets were placed near certain other objects that previously exhibited no magnetic properties, the target object would also become magnetized.

Today we know that a magnet produces an invisible magnetic field in the space around it and a second magnet responds to that field. This magnetic field can be visualized as invisible magnetic field lines radiating from the north pole.

However, it should be kept in mind that the direction of the field lines is just a convention and does not imply that the magnetic field is actually moving.

Magnets always have a north and south pole. In other words, you can't break a magnet in half to obtain a magnet with only one pole; left as is you might wish just a south pole. The two smaller pieces would, in fact, become separate magnets, each with its own north and south poles.

[DIAGRAM: Two magnets labeled N and S combining]

Similarly, if you join the north pole of one magnet with the south pole of another magnet, the two magnets would act as a single magnet with a single north pole and a single south pole.

[DIAGRAM: Two S N magnets combining to form S N]

A magnet's magnetic field can be described by a vector quantity called the magnetic dipole moment. The strength of the magnetic field at any given point is proportional to the magnitude of the magnetic dipole moment and decreases with the distance from the magnet. You can visualize the magnetic field as a torque which tends to align the magnetic moment along the lines of the field. In the case of a bar magnet, you visualize the magnetic moment by imagining the vector pointing from its south pole to its north pole.

Magnetic fields are created by moving electric charge (such as moving electrons or protons). For example, when a current moves through a wire, it produces a magnetic field around the wire as illustrated below.

[DIAGRAM: Wire with magnetic field lines around it]

If you wind the wire into a coil as illustrated below, the magnetic field will reinforce itself.

[DIAGRAM: Coiled wire with magnetic field arrows]

As you can see, a coil with a current running through it becomes sort of like a bar magnet (called a solenoid) with a north and south pole. The more times you wind the coil, the stronger the magnetic field produced. You can make the magnet even stronger by winding the coil around a core of magnetic material (Yay, congratulations! You just built a kind of magnet called an electromagnet).

Every electron has its own magnetic moment due to an intrinsic property of the electron called spin. Spin is a quantum mechanical property of the electron but you can think of it as being similar to a classically spinning sphere.

[DIAGRAM: Electron with spin indicated]

Electrons not only spin; they also revolve around the nucleus of an atom. It was once thought that electrons orbited around the nucleus the same way that planets orbit around the sun but today we know that the picture is far more complex (involving concepts from quantum mechanics that won't concern us here). Nevertheless, this orbital motion of the electron also gives rise to a magnetic moment.

The atoms of certain elements have acquired electrons in their outer orbitals which can cause the atom to become an unpaired orbital. This unpaired electron's magnetic moment tends to line up all aligned. Such materials are called ferromagnetic. Under normal conditions (i.e. no externally applied magnetic field) the magnetization of the domains are aligned randomly and hence, the material has no net magnetic field. However, when an external magnetic field is applied, the domains tend to align themselves parallel to the field, thus magnetizing the material.

[DIAGRAM: Two boxes showing random vs. aligned magnetic field domains]

To make a long story short,

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A two-panel black and white webcomic in minimalist line art style. The top panel shows a group of people huddled in a wooden boat during heavy snow and rain, with icebergs visible. The bottom panel depicts the same group of people in a framed painting/artwork being viewed by three silhouetted figures in what appears to be a museum or gallery setting.

## Text Transcription

**Top Panel (Speech Bubble):**
"Cold, hungry, wet... I don't know how much longer we can take this. Eating worm-ridden biscuits and rotten meat. Dysentery, small pox, fever... Half of us are bleeding from our feet from walking miles in the snow with no shoes. Many of us have grown despondent and think we should just give up. Man alive, the sleet is really pounding down now.

Should we be victorious, I wonder how history will remember us. Will future generations remember all the hardships we've endured and the sacrifices we've made? How will our great great great great great great grand children remember this moment?"

## Visual Jokes
The primary joke is a contrast between the speaker's existential concern about how history will remember their hardship and suffering, and the bottom panel's reveal: they are indeed remembered—but only as a romanticized, heroic painting viewed in a gallery, stripped of all the actual suffering, disease, and despair they're currently describing.

# Scene Description
A six-panel black and white stick-figure comic set in prehistoric times (30,002 BC). Two early humans observe a group of primates. The art style is minimalist with simple line drawings. Below the comic is an illustration showing the concept of "humans" with drawings of running cats being hunted by arrows, captioned "The geeks of the animal kingdom."

# Dialogue and Text Transcription

**Panel 1 (top left):**
"30,002 BC"

**Panel 2 (top center):**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 3 (top right):**
"HEY, HUMANS!
PUNY HUMANS."

**Panel 4 (middle left):**
"LOOK AT ME. I'M A HUMAN WITH MY UNUSUALLY LARGE INCISOR TEETH AND MY SLOW BIPEDAL LOCOMOTION.
HAHAHA."

**Panel 5 (middle center):**
"LOOK OUT. THEY MIGHT SMITE US WITH THEIR UNUSUALLY LARGE CRANIAL CAPACITIES.
HAHAHA."

**Panel 6 (middle right):**
"HEY, WHA'CHA MAKING THERE? ANOTHER ONE OF YOUR LITTLE ERGOT TOYS?"

**Bottom Section:**
"HUMANS
The geeks of the animal kingdom"

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the ironic contrast between the primates mocking humans for their "useless" traits (large brains, bipedalism, tool-making) that will ultimately make humans the dominant species. The final caption "The geeks of the animal kingdom" reinforces this—geeks being socially dismissed but ultimately successful, mirroring humanity's evolutionary trajectory.

# Webcomic Transcription: "Mitch"

## Scene Description
A simple stick-figure style comic in 9 panels arranged in a 3x3 grid. The drawings are minimalist black line art with occasional color (red for the beer cup). The narrative follows the misadventures of a college friend named Mitch through various chaotic situations.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"I had this friend in college named 'Mitch'."
[Speech bubble from stick figure: "I'M MITCH"]

**Panel 2:**
"Mitch really liked to party. Sometimes too much."

**Panel 3:**
"One time, he drank too much and threw up a little in his beer cup."

**Panel 4:**
"And then he just continued to party - drinking out of that same cup."

**Panel 5:**
"During our junior year, he got wasted, stole his girlfriend's car, and crashed it into a tree.
(Thank God no one was hurt.)"
[Image shows stick figure and crashed car]

**Panel 6:**
"When we bailed him out of jail, he had cuts and bruises all over his face... but not from the crash."

**Panel 7:**
"When he was getting arrested, he tried to run from the police while still wearing handcuffs.
He tripped and fell face first into some thorn bushes."

**Panel 8:**
"I haven't seen Mitch since graduation. The last I heard, he was going to medical school."

**Panel 9:**
"MORAL OF THE STORY:
NURSE, SCALPEL!
The less you know about your doctor, the better."

## Visual Jokes
The primary joke is the ironic juxtaposition of Mitch's reckless, chaotic behavior throughout college with him becoming a doctor. The final panel's punchline relies on the reader understanding that someone this accident-prone and poor at decision-making should not be in a medical profession.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A multi-panel black and white webcomic with some colored elements, depicting a philosophical narrative about dreams and their importance. The art style is simple stick-figure and minimalist illustration. The comic progresses from a daydream sequence to a space scene to a final architectural landscape.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Top section (daydream cloud):**
- No dialogue, but labels in Asian characters appear throughout

**Bottom space section:**
"Some people claim that they never remember any of their dreams.

Those people are missing out on some of the best moments of their lives."

**Bottom architectural section:**
No text

## Visual Jokes/Elements Dependent on Image

1. **Daydream structure**: The entire middle section is contained within a thought cloud/speech bubble, visually representing that these are imagined scenarios
2. **Contrast**: The stark black space scene with colorful celestial objects contrasts with the simple white space of the daydream sequence
3. **Repetition**: The repeated panels of a figure in a car window create a comedic "looping" effect, suggesting obsessive dreaming or the cyclical nature of dreams
4. **Progression**: Visual movement from mundane life (bottom) → imagination (middle) → cosmic dreams (upper space) → architectural surrealism (final), suggesting dreams elevate ordinary existence

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description

A vertical evolutionary timeline in simple black-and-white line art style. The comic depicts the evolution of life from single-celled organisms 3.5 billion years ago through to modern humans, with a poignant narrative woven through the middle sections. Early life forms are shown as simple circles and bacteria; the timeline branches into increasingly complex organisms (fish, amphibians, reptiles, mammals, primates) before reaching humans at the bottom. The middle section contains three comic panels with dialogue between two single-celled organisms.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Caption at top:**
"3.5 BILLION YEARS AGO"

**Panel 1 (early in the timeline):**
"GO FORTH, MY SISTER, AND EXPLORE THIS VAST UNIVERSE."

**Panel 2:**
"AND SHOULD OUR PATHS EVER CROSS AGAIN, OH THE STORIES WE'LL TELL."

**Panel 3:**
"GOOD-BYE, SIS."
"PARTING IS SUCH SWEET SORROW."

# Visual Jokes

The primary joke is a poignant subversion of the evolutionary timeline: two single-celled organisms share an emotional farewell, with one lineage evolving into insects and the left branch eventually leading to humans, while the other evolves into fish and complex vertebrates. The implication is that humans and insects are distant "siblings" who parted 3.5 billion years ago, making the sentimental dialogue darkly ironic given that humans spend their existence swatting insects—an unknowing reunion with their evolutionary cousins.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description

A two-panel black and white webcomic in simple line-art style. Both panels show a figure with glasses at a desk surrounded by bookshelves. The top panel depicts an organized, pristine study; the bottom panel shows the same space in complete disarray with papers and books scattered everywhere.

# Text Transcription

**Top Panel (Header):**
"Mathematics... possesses not only truth, but supreme beauty - a beauty cold and austere", ~ like that of a pure crystal, sublimely poetic in total perfection.

**Top Panel (Side text box):**
Writing an elegant proof of a beautiful theorem is the act of merging patterns of ideas in perfect harmony, teasing order out of chaos, like creating an abstract system of zero entropy.

**Top Panel (Footer):**
~ Bertrand Russell

**Bottom Panel (Footer):**
But of course it's not a closed system.

# Visual Jokes

The primary humor is the stark contrast between the idealized, orderly depiction of mathematical work in the top panel versus the chaotic reality shown in the bottom panel. The second panel reveals crumpled papers, scattered books, overflowing trash, and visible equations on the whiteboard—visual evidence that actual mathematical work is messy and entropic, directly contradicting Russell's poetic characterization and the notion of "zero entropy" referenced above.

# Scene Description
A humorous webcomic showing a bald man in a blue shirt riding on a mathematical curve plotted in MATLAB. The man, labeled "Harold," sits on the oscillating wave holding a glowing laptop. The background is deep purple with a grid, mimicking MATLAB's plotting interface. The art style is cartoonish with the character superimposed on the mathematical graph.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Command line text (top left):**
```
>> x = 0 : pi/20 : 6 * pi;
>> plot(x, exp(-0.2*x) .* sin(x), 'm' )
```

**Title (bottom center):**
HAROLD DISCOVERS MATLAB

# Visual Joke
The humor derives from the literal visualization of a person "riding" on a plotted mathematical function. Harold is physically positioned on the curve created by the MATLAB command, as if he's surfing or riding the dampened sine wave. This plays on the idea of becoming immersed in or discovering the joy of MATLAB programming—here taken literally as Harold rides the mathematical output itself.

# Comic Transcription

## Scene Description
A minimalist line-drawn webcomic featuring a cat on a brick wall. The comic uses a 9-panel grid format (3x3) with the final panel below showing a separate scene. The art style is simple black and white with selective use of blue to indicate sky and night. The cat is drawn in a basic outline style, and the wall is rendered with simple brick patterns.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (top left):** [No dialogue]

**Panel 2 (top center):** 
"SO HUMID. BAD FUR DAY."

**Panel 3 (top right):** [No dialogue]

**Panel 4 (middle left):**
"I HOPE I DON'T RUN INTO THAT DOG TODAY. HE'S SO MEAN."

**Panel 5 (middle center):** [No dialogue]

**Panel 6 (middle right):**
"WHY CAN'T HUMANS JUST STRUCTURE A STABLE AND LASTING PEACE IN THE MIDDLE EAST?"

**Panel 7 (bottom left):** [No dialogue]

**Panel 8 (bottom center):** [No dialogue]

**Panel 9 (bottom right):** [No dialogue - shows nighttime]

**Final panel (separate, below grid):**
"EXACTLY FUCKS WERE GIVEN THIS DAY"
[A rat is shown writing/posting this sign]

## Visual Jokes/Puns

1. The cat's "bad fur day" complaint due to humidity becomes increasingly absurd when contrasted with its shift to pondering geopolitical stability, then complete existential indifference by nightfall.

2. The final punchline juxtaposes the cat's earlier concerns with the rat's sarcastic sign-posting, implying zero concern was given to any of the cat's worries—the rat is literally posting this message, perhaps as a taunt.

# Scene Description
A six-panel stick figure webcomic in black ink on white background. A simple stick figure character (drawn in blue in some panels) progresses through life stages, from childhood to adulthood, sharing their evolving dreams with family members. The final panel shows the character in a wheelchair. A quote by Thomas La Mance appears at the bottom.

# Text Transcription

**Panel 1:**
"WHEN I GROW UP, IMMA BE AN ASTRONAUT."

**Panel 2:**
"GOTTA STUDY HARD IF I WANNA BE AN ASTRONAUT."

**Panel 3:**
"SOME DAY I'LL QUIT MY JOB AND PURSUE MY ASTRONAUT DREAMS."

**Panel 4:**
"I'LL BE THE FIRST PERSON TO WALK ON MARS. YOU'LL SEE."

**Panel 5:**
"ONCE MY KIDS ARE GROWN, I'LL APPLY TO NASA."

**Panel 6:**
"MAYBE I COULD JUST HAVE MY ASHES SCATTERED IN SPACE WHEN I DIE."

**Bottom caption:**
"Life is what happens to us while we are making other plans.
---Thomas La Mance"

# Visual Jokes
The comic's humor lies in the visual progression showing the character's astronaut dreams being gradually diminished by life responsibilities (education, work, parenthood) until the final panel reveals physical disability, making literal space travel impossible. The darkly comedic endpoint—scattering ashes in space as a final compromise—visualizes the quote about plans versus reality.
