# Comic Transcription

## Scene Description
A nine-panel stick figure comic in black and white. Two main characters: Otto (a round character with black fill) and a woman with long hair wearing a hat (introduced later). The art style is minimalist with simple line drawings. The narrative follows a workplace confrontation that escalates into physical comedy.

## Dialogue and Text (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
Woman: "I'M SORRY, OTTO, BUT I MET SOMEONE ELSE."

**Panel 2:**
Otto: "I DIDN'T PLAN IT. IT JUST HAPPENED."

**Panel 3:**
Woman: "CAN WE STILL BE FRIENDS?"

**Panel 4:**
[Sound effect] "SLAP"

**Panel 5:**
Man in hat: "I'M SORRY, OTTO, BUT OUR COMPANY IS DOWNSIZING AND YOU JUST DON'T HAVE ENOUGH SENIORITY."

**Panel 6:**
[Sound effect] "POW"

**Panel 7:**
Woman: "GODDAMMIT, OTTO! YOU HAVE LUPUS!"

**Panel 8:**
[Sound effect] "CRACK"

**Panel 9:**
Caption: "Goddammit, Otto! Hit back!"

## Visual Jokes
The comic's humor relies on Otto being repeatedly struck by different people for different reasons (romantic rejection, job loss, medical condition). The final panel's encouragement to "hit back" is ironic given Otto's apparent passivity throughout the sequence.

# Scene Description
A humorous webcomic styled as a Ouija board. The illustration shows a classic Ouija board layout with decorative corners featuring a sun (top left), moon and star (top right), and a heart planchette (right side). The board uses ornate gothic-style artwork typical of Ouija board designs.

# Text Transcription (reading order)
- "BINARY" (top center)
- "OUIJA" (top center, below "BINARY")
- "0" (left side of board)
- "1" (right side of board)
- "LOG OFF" (bottom center)
- Caption below image: "My attempt to access the (long-dead) first computer I ever owned."

# Visual Jokes
The primary joke is a pun on "Ouija board" terminology: instead of the traditional letters and numbers found on a Ouija board, this one displays only "0" and "1" (binary code), with "LOG OFF" replacing the typical "GOODBYE." This creates a tech-themed parody where the user is attempting to spiritually communicate with a deceased computer using binary language and computing terminology.

# Webcomic Transcription: "The Wedding"

## Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic depicting a wedding ceremony that takes an unexpected and surreal turn. The art style is minimalist black-and-white line drawings. Characters include a bride, groom (Dex), wedding guests, and a priest/officiant. The narrative progresses from a traditional wedding interruption to an elaborate romantic escape and ends with an absurdist twist.

## Dialogue and Text (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"...if any person can show just cause why they may not be joined together, let them speak now or forever hold their peace."

**Panel 2:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 3:**
"NOOOOOOO!!! DON'T DO IT!!!"
"What?!!"

**Panel 4:**
"I love you, Rachel! I've always loved you - more than life itself. I've always wanted to tell you that!"

**Panel 5:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 6:**
"I...I love you, too, Dex!"
"What?!"

**Panel 7:**
"Get him! Quick, let's get outta here and start our lives together. We have to make up for all our lost time."
"Stop!"

**Panel 8:**
"Get back! Get away! We love each other!"

**Panel 9:**
"We did it."
"I love you."

**Panel 10:**
"I know a place where no one can find us."

**Panel 11:**
"Oh, Dex, I never thought I could be so happy."

**Panel 12:**
"Not even the Bolivian army can stop the awesomeness of our love."

**Panel 13:**
"I love the way you look.
I love the way you feel.
I love the way you taste."

**Panel 14:**
"Oh, Dex, isn't it perfect?"
"Too perfect."

**Panel 15:**
"I now pronounce you husband and wife."

**Panel 16:**
(No dialogue; shows figures falling through clouds)

## Visual Jokes/Puns
- The climactic final panel reveals that the entire romantic escape sequence was imagined—the couple ascends to heaven and is literally pronounced "husband and wife" by a heavenly officiant, suggesting they died in their "perfect" moment. The joke is the dark, absurdist subversion of the romantic narrative: their "too perfect" love story ends in death.

A nine-panel comic strip in simple black-and-white stick figure style. A wizard character wearing a pointed hat stands repeatedly at a "Moria Entrance" sign, attempting different magical incantations to open a door. The comic is titled "Middle-earth dictionary attack" at the bottom.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
- Label: "MORIA ENTRANCE"

**Panel 2:**
- Label: "MORIA ENTRANCE"
- Dialogue: "OPEN SESAME!"

**Panel 3:**
- Label: "MORIA ENTRANCE"

**Panel 5:**
- Label: "MORIA ENTRANCE"

- Label: "MORIA ENTRANCE"

**Panel 7:**
- Label: "MORIA ENTRANCE"

**Panel 8:**
- Label: "MORIA ENTRANCE"

**Panel 9:**
- Label: "MORIA ENTRANCE"
- Dialogue: "A, AARDVARK, AB, ABACK, ABACUS, ABAFT, ABALONE"

**Bottom caption:**
"Middle-earth dictionary attack"

# Visual Joke
The comic references the Lord of the Rings scene where the wizard must speak "friend and enter" in Elvish to open Moria's doors. Instead, the wizard tries common magic words, then exhaustively recites dictionary entries alphabetically—a "dictionary attack," a cybersecurity term for trying passwords systematically. The humor combines fantasy lore with internet security terminology.

# Petite Girl Takes on Two Monster Chicks at Once

## Scene Description
A simple line-drawn webcomic in black and white featuring stick-figure style characters. The story depicts a superhero rescue scenario where "Petite Girl" defeats two large monster roosters attacking a city. The comic uses minimalist art with basic circular heads, simple line bodies, and a small town cityscape backdrop.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (Top scene):**
- "RAWR"
- "HELP!"

**Panel 2:**
- "OH NO! WHO WILL SAVE US?!"

**Panel 3:**
- "WAIT, LOOK! UP IN THE SKY!"
- "IT'S SUPERGIRL!"
- "IT'S MARVEL GIRL!"
- "NO, IT'S..."

**Panel 4:**
- "IT'S PETITE GIRL!"

**Panel 5:**
- "HAVE NO FEAR. PETITE GIRL IS HERE TO SAVE THE DAY."
- "GO GO" "YAY"

**Panel 6:**
- "TAKE THAT, YOU BIRD OF UNUSUAL SIZE!"
- "POW"
- "AND MY HEAT VISION WILL TAKE CARE OF THE OTHER ONE."
- "EXTRA CRISPY."

**Panel 7 (Bottom):**
- "THANK U, PETITE GIRL"
- "YESHHH DOOBIES"

**Caption (blue text):**
- "petite girl takes on two monster chicks at once"

**Final element:**
- "CLICK"
- "clicked link was disappointed"

## Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the contrast between the grandiose superhero trope (expecting famous heroes like Supergirl or Marvel Girl) and the absurdly diminutive "Petite Girl" who nonetheless defeats giant roosters ("chicks"). The final panel's clickable link subverts expectations—despite saving the day, the rescued citizens' enthusiastic response is met with disappointment, creating an anti-climactic joke about internet links.

# Scene Description
A scientific chart displaying seven different graphs (labeled A through G) showing intensity vs. time curves. Each graph illustrates a different type of response cycle, accompanied by humorous self-descriptive labels. The art style is simple black-and-white line drawings on a white background, presented in a grid format typical of educational or technical documents.

# Text Transcription

**Title/Header:**
"I have taken to quantifying, recording, and meticulously classifying the various types of sexual response cycles I've experienced."

**Graph Labels (in reading order):**

A. normal

B. constipated

C. blew delta
   ("I don't wanna talk about it.")

D. I sneezed halfway through

E. I was drunk

F. (no label visible)

G. buyer's remorse

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies entirely on the mismatch between scientific presentation and absurd, mundane explanations. Graph C's sharp spike represents an interruption, Graph D shows an interruption mid-cycle, Graph E displays an irregular/chaotic pattern, and Graph G depicts a dramatic reversal into negative territory—all of which are used as literal visual representations of awkward or comedic sexual situations.

# Scene Description
A stick figure comic in simple black and white line art. The first panels show a stick figure woman sitting on a couch encountering a large round object (a person in a hat) emerging from behind the couch. The middle panels depict an escalating insult exchange between the two characters. The final panels show the round character sitting dejectedly surrounded by boxes of beauty/cosmetic products, with a concluding caption about supply and demand.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1-2:**
- "HOWDY, STRANGER!"
- "POP"

**Panel 3:**
- "?"

**Panel 4:**
- "MY GOD, YOU ARE MORBIDLY OBESE."
- "WHAT?"

**Panel 5:**
- "WHAT ARE YOU, A SIZE 8? SIZE 6. SIZE HIPPO."

**Panel 6:**
- "AND YOU CALL THOSE BREASTS? LOOKS MORE LIKE MOSQUITO BUMPS. HA HA."

**Panel 7:**
- "AND LOOK AT THOSE CROW'S FEET, GRANNY. YOU LOOK LIKE A 40-YEAR-OLD."
- "GASP"

**Panel 8:**
- "NOBODY COULD EVER BE ATTRACTED TO SUCH A GOD-AWFUL HOMELY FACE LIKE YOURS."

**Panel 9:**
- "ANYWAYZ, BE SURE TO CALL MY TOLL-FREE NUMBER IF YOU DON'T WANT TO DIE ALONE."

**Bottom panels - Product boxes (left to right):**
- CLOWN LIPSTICK
- SUPER BRA
- WONDER BURT
- DIY BOTOX
- DIY COLLAGEN
- MAGIC SHAMPOO

**Right stack:**
- FAKE BOOBS
- FAKE EYELASHES
- FAKE NAILS
- FAKE HAIR
- FAKE EYEBROW PENCIL THINGIES

**Final caption:**
"First, create the supply. Then manufacture the demand."

# Visual Jokes
The irony of the insult-hurling character selling beauty products—they literally create insecurity to profit from solutions. The round character's transformation from confident to devastated, sitting between boxes of "fake" products, illustrates how manufactured demand exploits body image anxieties.

# Comic Transcription

## Scene Description
A six-panel stick figure webcomic in black ink on white background. The comic shows a character with a blue hat/head experiencing sleep deprivation and the consequences of interrupting someone's leisure time. The art style is simple line drawings with minimal detail. The final panel is labeled "then and now" and shows a time progression.

## Dialogue and Text (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"NOT SLEEPY. WHY DO I HAVE TO GO TO BED?"
"WAH AH"

**Panel 2:**
"DAMMIT! I DON'T WANNA READ 'MOBY DICK.' THIS IS STUPID."

**Panel 3:**
"NO GIRLS IN THE SANDBOX, COOTIES! COOTIES!"

**Panel 4:**
"AHHH... I DON'T HAVE TO GET UP FOR ANOTHER TEN HOURS. OH, SWEET, SWEET SLEEP, HOW DO I LOVE THEE?"

**Panel 5:**
"OH, GLORIOUS DAY. I GET TWO FULL HOURS OF LEISURE READING TIME TODAY."
"INTERRUPT MY LEISURE READING TIME, AND I'LL RIP YOUR EYEBALLS OUT THE SOCKETS AND SKULL-F*** YOU TO DEATH."
"DAT ASS."

**Panel 6:**
[Two figures standing, no dialogue]
"then and now"

## Visual Jokes
The comic's humor derives from the escalating contrast between childish complaints about bedtime and sleep in early panels versus the adult's obsessive protection of sleep and leisure time in later panels, demonstrating how priorities invert with age.

# Scene Description
A simple line-drawn comic in black ink with minimal detail. A stick figure character wearing a blue cap stands on the left, holding what appears to be a clipboard or notepad. A cat sits on a table or surface to the right, depicted in a simple line style. The setting is minimal and sparse.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Top section (checklist with checkboxes):**
- ☑ FAILURE TO CONFORM TO SOCIAL NORMS
- ☑ DECEITFULNESS
- ☑ IMPULSIVITY OR FAILURE TO PLAN AHEAD
- ☑ IRRITABILITY AND AGGRESSIVENESS
- ☑ RECKLESS DISREGARD FOR THE SAFETY OF OTHERS
- ☑ CONSISTENT IRRESPONSIBILITY
- ☑ LACK OF REMORSE

**Bottom caption:**
"I have concluded that all cats are psychopathic."

**Citation:**
American Psychiatric Association (2000), Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders

# Visual Joke
The humor depends on the image: the character appears to be clinically evaluating the cat (holding a clipboard with a checklist), and the cat's typical behavioral traits—independence, lack of obvious affection, disregard for human safety and property—precisely match the clinical criteria for psychopathy when applied literally to animal behavior.

# Scene Description
A stick-figure comic in black ink on white background. The scene depicts multiple stick figures debating the meaning of "respect," which appears on a sign/tombstone-like object. The comic escalates from philosophical discussion to physical violence in the final panel.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 2:** 
- "WHAT DOES IT MEAN?"

**Panel 3:**
- "CLEARLY, IT MEANS TO 'RESPECT YOURSELF'."

**Panel 4:**
- "NO, I THINK IT MEANS TO 'RESPECT OTHERS'."

**Panel 5:**
- "NO, I'M SURE IT MEANS TO 'RESPECT YOUR FAMILY'."

**Panel 6:**
- "NO, IT'S SAYING TO 'RESPECT YOUR SPOUSE', 'RESPECT YOUR COUNTRY', 'RESPECT ALL LIFE'."

**Panel 7:**
- "THAT'S NOT WHAT IT MEANS."
- "HEY, QUIT SHOVIN'."
- "I WAS HERE FIRST!"

**Panel 8:** (No dialogue; shows figures attacking each other with weapons and violence)

# Visual Jokes
The primary joke is ironic: the figures are arguing about the definition of "respect" while simultaneously showing complete disrespect through physical violence and aggression, culminating in a chaotic battle scene. The escalation from peaceful philosophical debate to brutal conflict undercuts the meaning of their discussion.

comics.title: In the Neutral Zone
comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A three-panel stick figure comic in black and white. The art style is minimalist line drawing. Panel 1 shows a figure at a ship's wheel; Panel 2 shows two figures looking at a computer screen; Panel 3 shows a single figure with head in hand.

# Text Transcription

**Panel 1:**
- Caption (top left): "CAPTAIN, ROMULAN WARBIRD DECLOAKING TO STARBOARD!"
- Caption (bottom left): "ON SCREEN!"

**Panel 2:**
- Dialog box (center): "Adobe Flash Player Update
This content requires Adobe Flash Player 258.1. Would you like to install it now?"
- Two buttons: "Yes" and "No"

**Panel 3:**
- No text

# Visual Joke
The humor relies on the contrast between the dramatic Star Trek scenario (a Romulan warbird decloaking in battle) and the mundane reality of an Adobe Flash Player update prompt interrupting the moment. The third panel shows the captain's defeated reaction to this anti-climactic interruption, subverting the expected space combat scenario.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A simple line-drawn comic in black and white featuring two characters: a small round creature wearing a pink party hat lying in bed on the left, and a tall figure with raised arms on the right. A small table with a candle sits between them. The art style is minimalist and humorous.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Title/Caption (top):**
"So some monkey that evolved on some mud ball orbiting an average main sequence G2 star in the Orion arm of some minor galaxy in the backwash of the Virgo Supercluster has done nothing but eat, sleep, and poop for the duration of about 2.9010 x 10^17 periods of the radiation corresponding to the transition between the two hyperfine levels of the ground state of the cesium-133 atom at rest at a temperature of 0 K."

**Dialogue:**
The small creature (left) says: "WTF ?"

**Caption (bottom):**
"Apparently, it's a big deal."

**Credit (bottom right):**
"Lucy V. Rocks"

# Visual Jokes
The humor derives from the absurdist contrast between the extraordinarily technical, pedantic description of a monkey's birthday (defining time through cesium-133 atomic oscillations—the basis of the second) and the creature's bewildered response ("WTF?"), with the other character celebrating as if this is obviously momentous.

comics.title: The Biggest Loser

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A three-panel stick figure comic in black and white with minimal detail. The first panel shows a group reunion scene with seven simple stick figures standing in a line. The second panel depicts the same figures boarding a rocket ship. The third panel shows a single stick figure standing alone, watching a rocket launch into space, with a banner reading "CLASS REUNION WELCOME CLASS OF'" visible in the background.

# Text Transcription

**Panel 1 (top):**
"AND GILBERT HERE JUST HAD HIS THIRD CHILD, A BEAUTIFUL DAUGHTER. AND JOANNA HERE JUST BECAME CEO OF HER COMPANY, CHRISTINE IS RUNNING FOR SENATE IN DELAWARE, AND GOOGLE JUST ACQUIRED MY STARTUP FOR $900,000,000.

ANYWAY, IT WAS GOOD TO SEE YOU AGAIN."

**Panel 2 (middle):**
"C'MON, LET'S GO, GUYS."

**Panel 3 (bottom):**
"CLASS REUNION WELCOME CLASS OF '"

**Caption below all panels:**
"Sometimes I feel like this."

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies entirely on the image: despite the speaker's accomplishments being so extraordinary that their friends literally abandon them to board a rocket and escape Earth, the speaker is left alone at the class reunion. The visual metaphor of everyone literally rocketing away emphasizes their isolation despite (or because of) their exceptional success.

# Webcomic Transcription: Cellular and Molecular Biology Infographic

## Scene Description
A comprehensive educational infographic with a minimalist, scientific illustration style featuring diagrams of cellular structures, molecular processes, and biological mechanisms. The artwork uses bright greens, yellows, oranges, and grays with simple line drawings and labeled anatomical diagrams. Multiple circular magnified views zoom into progressively smaller biological structures, from cells to molecules.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Top Section - Cell Membrane:**
- "Поперечний переріз клітинної мембрани" (Cross-section of cell membrane)
- "Білок-канал для іонів" (Channel protein for ions)

**Second Section - DNA Structure:**
- "Локальна деструкція структури ДНК" (Local destruction of DNA structure)

**Third Section - Neuron Diagram:**
- "Синапс нейрона" (Neural synapse)
- "Обогащение рецепторов на постсинаптической мембране" (Enrichment of receptors on postsynaptic membrane)

**Fourth Section - Cell Division/Nucleus:**
- Multiple labels pointing to nuclear structures and chromosomes
- "Хромосома" (Chromosome)
- "Структура хромосомы и расположение генов" (Chromosome structure and gene location)

**Fifth Section - Organelle Details:**
- Circular magnified views showing progressively detailed cellular components
- "ДНК" (DNA)

**Sixth Section - Brain and Nervous System:**
- "Мозок" (Brain)
- "Серце" (Heart)
- "Механизм нейрохимической передачи" (Mechanism of neurochemical transmission)

**Seventh Section - Signal Transduction:**
- "Сигнальная коллеция" (Signal collection)
- Block diagram showing signal cascade with yellow highlighted boxes
- Text describing signal transmission mechanisms

**Bottom Section - Energy Production:**
- Diagram of mitochondria
- "АТП синтаза" (ATP synthase)
- "Протонна помпа" (Proton pump)
- Text describing oxidative phosphorylation and ATP synthesis
- "Чисельність молекул АТП" (Number of ATP molecules)

**Circuit Diagram Elements:**
- Flow charts showing biochemical pathways
- Arrows indicating processes and transformations
- Yellow boxes highlighting key steps

## Visual Jokes/Puns Dependent on Image

No explicit visual puns are present. However, the infographic uses progressive magnification (zoom-in technique) as a visual metaphor for "drilling down" into increasingly complex biological structures, which creates visual coherence and conceptual clarity about scale and complexity in biology.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description

A dark-themed technical artwork combining computer graphics, assembly code, and physics. The left side shows a black background with white-outlined geometric shapes (polygons and polyhedra) of varying sizes scattered across the space. The right side displays overlaid computer code (assembly language with hexadecimal values), logic gate diagrams, and mathematical equations. The overall composition merges 3D graphics programming with low-level computing concepts.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Left side labels:**
- "gainful employment of Maxwell's equations"

**Top right assembly code block:**
- "abfXORLd"
- "OXFF <<"
- "OXFF <<" "abx"
- "OXFF <<" "edx"
- "OXFF <<" "[ecx]"

**Code sections with hex values:**
- "[ecx]" "0011111"
- "edx" "1000100"
- Various binary and hexadecimal strings throughout

**Assembly instructions visible:**
- "mov bl, [ecx]"
- "mov bl, [ecx+2]"
- "shr abx, db [ecx]"
- "FF.dr8sig"
- "FF.dr@sig"
- "break;"

**Mathematical equations (bottom right):**
- "∮ E · dA = Q_enc/ε₀"
- "∮ B · dA = 0"
- "∮ E · dl = -dΦ_B/dt"

**Binary/hex data blocks:**
Multiple rows of binary (0011, 0101, etc.) and hexadecimal values

# Visual Jokes/Puns

The central visual pun is the title itself: "gainful employment of Maxwell's equations" juxtaposed with Maxwell's equations displayed alongside computer graphics code. This plays on the dual meaning of "employment"—both "use/application" (in physics) and "gainful employment" (having a job). The image suggests that Maxwell's equations, fundamental to electromagnetism and light propagation, are literally *employed* as tools in computer graphics rendering and 3D polygon generation.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description

The comic consists of two sections. The top section shows a simple stick-figure style conversation between two characters (one appears to be presenting/explaining, the other listening) across four panels. The bottom section depicts a night sky filled with colorful stars above a blue ocean, with a sailing ship in the center and three labeled islands or phenomena below the water's surface.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (Top Left):**
"in the future..."
"WELL, IT'S FINISHED."

**Panel 2 (Top Center):**
"WE'VE DISCOVERED ALL OF THE FUNDAMENTAL LAWS OF NATURE. THE REST IS JUST DETAILS."

**Panel 3 (Top Right):**
"WHAT IS THERE LEFT FOR US SCIENTISTS TO DO NOW?"

**Panel 4 (Bottom Left):**
"WE COULD ALWAYS JUST BECOME PHILOSOPHERS."

**Panel 5 (Bottom Center):**
(Silent panel with character reaction)

**Panel 6 (Bottom Right):**
(Character crying/distressed)

**Bottom Section Labels:**
- "dark matter"
- "?" (question mark on small island)
- "quantum gravity"

**Caption:**
"The true 'Age of Discovery' is now."

# Visual Jokes/Puns

The primary joke relies on the ironic contrast between the comic's opening premise (that all fundamental laws of nature have been discovered) and the bottom section's visual punchline. The illustrated "Age of Discovery" metaphor shows a ship sailing an ocean dotted with unsolved mysteries (dark matter, quantum gravity, and unknown phenomena represented by the question mark)—suggesting that despite claims of complete understanding, vast unknowns remain. The crying character's reaction in the final panel underscores the existential humor: even after "finishing" science, scientists discover there's still everything left to understand.

# Scene Description
A comic strip featuring anthropomorphized electrical components in a power grid setting. The art style is simple line drawing with minimal shading. Two characters—an electrical grid/system (speaking from above) and what appears to be a Megatron-like transformer character (speaking from below)—are having a conversation about power management. The bottom half shows a technical diagram of transformers with coils and electrical connections.

# Dialogue and Text (in reading order)

**Grid character:** "What's going on? I'm detecting a voltage fluctuation from you."

**Transformer character:** "I am attempting to short the regional power grid which will temporarily cause mild discomfort for some humans living on the eastern seaboard."

**Grid character:** "NO, MEGATRON! DON'T DO IT!"

**Label (bottom):** "TRANSFORMERS (the boring version)"

# Visual Jokes
The primary joke is a pun on the name "Transformers"—playing on the famous robot franchise by depicting literal electrical transformers (the utilitarian devices used in power distribution) instead, with one attempting a mundane act of sabotage. The humor stems from the contrast between the dramatic tone and the "boring" technical reality of what transformers actually do. The caption "(the boring version)" explicitly acknowledges this subversion of expectations.

# Webcomic Transcription: "Years Ago in College"

## Scene Description
A multi-panel autobiographical webcomic in simple stick-figure style with blue and black ink. The narrator (depicted as a simple figure) recounts a relationship story spanning several years, from college through a breakup. Panels vary in size and layout, with handwritten-style text and casual formatting.

## Full Text Transcription

**Panel 1 (Title):**
"Years ago in college
I was having an awkward conversation with my parents
about a girl I liked. I was too shy to ask her out, so I
happened on the next train to Princeton."

**Panel 2:**
"At this time, I had not yet met her parents and I didn't know anything about her family, but I did know that she was a girl and that was enough. When she answered the phone, I asked for her to meet me on the train."

[Dialogue in panel:]
"HI"
"WELL, I CAN'T ASKED STUPID QUESTIONS"
"WHAT ARE YOU DOING?"
"I'M ON THE TRAIN"

**Panel 3:**
"I had had that I didn't think it was a good idea for me to meet her. She said she lived just down the street from me. So I rode a call to come pick me up at the train station."

[Dialogue:]
"WANT TO MEET YOU ON THE TRAIN"
"UM... WAIT, DO YOU?"
"IF I TELL YOU THAT I'M ON THE TRAIN... IS THAT BAD?"

**Panel 4:**
"Finally, I convinced her girlfriend to leave the house and we took a walk on the beach near her place. It was nice. We walked back through the brick subway-like brakes. She was awkwardly holding all of the pieces of beach and tried to pick one up."

[Dialogue in scene:]
"LOOK AT ALL THESE INTERESTING SORTS OF LOVES"
"THAT'S... THAT'S KIND OF WEIRD"
"WELL"

**Panel 5:**
"For the next three hours she showed me all of the interesting parts of town, pointing it round little alleys, and we shared the exact the beach and places she used to go. When we got back to her place, she asked me if I wanted to come in. We ended up on the couch and just started chatting. Then, suddenly, she became very nervous and I realized that she wanted me to kiss her. So I did it. Her roommate walked in. It was awkward."

[Dialogue throughout:]
"THIS IS WHERE I HUNG OUT MOST IN HIGH SCHOOL"
"SO I JUST THOUGHT, UM... WELL... MAYBE YOU COULD KISS ME?"
"WAIT, ARE YOU—"
"OH MY GOD, I'M SO SORRY, I DIDN'T KNOW YOU TWO WERE—"
"NO, NO, IT'S FINE"

**Panel 6:**
"For some reason, she seemed really excited to show me her life.

She offered to drive me to the train station. But that trip is where things really went wrong. We were kissing between kiss idea, who then tried to open my car door while we were making out. She grabbed my arm really hard."

[Dialogue:]
"WAIT, KIND OF MUSIC DO YOU LIKE?"
"I REALLY LIKE THIS BAND"
"OH, THEY'RE PRETTY GOOD, COOL"
"YEAH, YOU SHOULD LISTEN TO THEM"

**Panel 7:**
"On the train ride home, so I pondered the day's events. Maybe I'm just a timeless robot with the heart. She, remember that episode of Star Trek: The Next Generation where Data didn't know if he was in love? When we got..."

[Dialogue:]
"THING WAS UP ON MY HEAD"
"CELL PHONE, HUH HOW ARE THOSE DAYS? MY PHONE IS POWERED BY INSECTILE QUANTUM COMPUTERS AND SO YOUR PHONE... CONTINUES TO DRIVE MY CORE PROCESSORS, THEREBY EXTENDING MY MEMORY"

**Panel 8:**
"...but something like that. I got all the episodes mixed up in my head.

But I didn't know what happened. I had a really, really weird feeling about our relationship. Like there was something fundamentally broken about it. I remembered that my previous girlfriend moved a little too fast. Why too early in our relationship. Maybe this was different?"

# Scene Description
A webcomic in simple black-and-white stick figure style depicting a monkey attempting to solve a complex mathematical equation. The comic shows the monkey's journey from confusion to a moment of apparent intellectual breakthrough, followed by a humbling reality check.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

Panel 1: "/ + | ="
Panel 1: "?"

Panel 2: (light bulb icon above monkey's head, indicating an idea)

Panel 3: "/ + | = 2"

Panel 4: (Monkey dancing/celebrating)

Panel 5: (Monkey thinking, with mathematical notation in thought bubble:)
"Z = e^(iπ) = D∂e(iπm)
= e^(iπ)u(z) = D∂e(iπ)S(x)
= e^(iπ)S(x) + J∂ / D∂e(iπ)
= (e^(iπ)u(z))^2+1 - 3π"

Panel 6: (Person (appears to be human) looking frustrated at monkey)
"?"

Panel 7: (Person writing/working on mathematical equations on paper)

Panel 8: (Person sitting, speaking to monkey)
"WHAT'DYA WANT, A FREAKIN' COOKIE?"

Panel 9: (Monkey looking sad/dejected)

# Visual Jokes/Puns
The core joke is that the monkey solved a simple arithmetic problem (1 + 1 = 2) but then overcomplicates it with advanced theoretical mathematics (complex exponentials, derivatives, etc.), only to be dismissed by the human with a sarcastic reference to the "infinite monkey theorem" and the condescending phrase about giving the monkey a cookie for basic accomplishment. The title "Even the Einstein of monkeys is still... just a monkey" reinforces this humbling conclusion.

# Scene Description
A simple stick figure comic in black and white line art. The character has a round head with blue coloring and stick limbs. The comic shows a progression across five panels depicting the character's anticipation and reaction to an Amazon package delivery.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

Panel 1: "HMMM, I WONDER IF MY AMAZON PACKAGE HAS ARRIVED YET."

Panel 5: "IT'S HERE!"

Panel 5: "Why do I do this?"

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the character's escalating physical behavior: starting by wondering about the package, then obsessively checking by climbing stairs repeatedly, and finally sprinting up the stairs with intense energy the moment it arrives. The joke is that the character has been anxiously checking multiple times before the package finally shows up, and now exhibits frantic, over-the-top excitement despite having just obsessively monitored its arrival. The final panel's self-aware question "Why do I do this?" comments on the irrational compulsive behavior of repeatedly checking for a package that hasn't arrived yet.

# Scene Description
A six-panel comic strip featuring two simple circular characters (one with a slight smile, one neutral) having a conversation. The art style is minimalist stick figures with basic expressions. The setting appears to be abstract, with wavy lines suggesting ground or a landscape at the bottom of each panel. Mathematical equations and notations are visible throughout several panels.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
Caption: "SO IF WE SIMPLY ASSUMED THAT LIGHT TRAVELED 64 ORDERS OF MAGNITUDE FASTER IN THE EARLY UNIVERSE..."
[Mathematical notation visible but illegible in image]

**Panel 2:**
Caption: "...THE HORIZON PROBLEM GOES AWAY, OBVIATING THE NEED FOR COSMOLOGICAL INFLATION."
[Mathematical equations shown]

**Panel 3:**
Caption: "FURTHERMORE..."
Character 1: "WAIT... WAIT A MINUTE..."
[Mathematical equations shown]

**Panel 4:**
Character 2: "YOU HAVE TO JUSTIFY THAT. YOU CAN'T JUST ARBITRARILY MAKE LIGHT TRAVEL FASTER THAN C."
[Mathematical equation shown]

**Panel 5:**
Character 1: "NO YOU CAN'T."
[Mathematical equation shown]

**Panel 6:**
Character 2: "UP HIGH!"

The humor depends on the mathematical absurdity presented and the final punchline: mathematicians celebrating by giving a high-five after successfully "disproving" the physicist's proposal through logical argument, resulting in their ban from physics conferences.

# Scene Description
A simple line-drawn webcomic featuring two stick figures sitting on opposite ends of a bench. The art style is minimalist black and white. The comic progresses through multiple panels showing a conversation between the two characters, with increasingly existential and nihilistic dialogue.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"Hey, there. Are you tired?"
"What?"

**Panel 2:**
"You must be tired, cuz you've been running through my mind all d..."
"My my my..."

**Panel 3:**
"Aren't we a confident one."

**Panel 4:**
"Well, sure. I believe that confidence is important for..."
"We're only stardust, y'know."

**Panel 5:**
(Silent panel)

**Panel 6:**
"What?"

**Panel 7:**
"Life is a but a fleeting dream, an insignificant sliver of worldline twisting through spacetime."

**Panel 8:**
(Silent panel)

**Panel 9:**
"Okaay..."

**Panel 10:**
"All you touch and all you see is all your life will ever be."

**Panel 11:**
"All those... memories will be lost in time, like tears... in rain."

**Panel 12:**
"We're just arbitrary subunits of a quivering biomass fighting the ravages of increasing entropy in a harsh unforgiving environment, trying desperately to evade the inevitable deterioration to thermodynamic equilibrium long enough to propagate our genetic information."
(Figure lying down exhausted)

**Panel 13:**
"You are not special. You are not a beautiful or unique snowflake. You're the same decaying organic matter as everyone else."

**Panel 14:**
"And some day, man will be gone. There will be nothing to show that we were ever here... but stardust."

**Panel 15:**
(Silent panel)

**Panel 16:**
"So tell me again, what reason do you have for being so confident?"

**Panel 17:**
"none ?..."

**Panel 18:**
"And don't you forget that."

# Visual Jokes/Observations
The humor derives from the contrast between the initial casual pickup line attempt and the increasingly nihilistic, existentially crushing responses that utterly obliterate any romantic or confident sentiment. The exhausted prone figure in one panel emphasizes the emotional weight of the monologue.

# Maxwell's Demon - Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A simple black-and-white stick figure comic depicting a person in bed being visited by a horned demon creature. The art style is minimalist line drawing with expressive character faces. The comic is divided into 9 panels in a 3x3 grid, progressing through a humorous conversation between the human and the demon.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- "Z Z Z Z" (sleep sounds)

**Panel 2:**
- "Z Z Z Z" (sleep sounds)

**Panel 3:**
- "AHEM."
- "?"

**Panel 4:**
- "I CAN HAZ SOUL?!!"
- "AAIGH! DEMON!"

**Panel 5:**
- "HEH HEH. RELAX, DUDE. I'M JUST PLAYIN'."

**Panel 6:**
- "I WAS JUST WONDERING IF I COULD BORROW THIS HAMMER."
- "UH... SURE."

**Panel 7:**
- "WHAT DO YOU NEED THIS HAMMER FOR?"
- "JUST BUILD-ING A WALL IN THE MIDDLE OF YOUR ROOM. NO BIGGIE."

**Panel 8:**
- "IT'S JUST WHAT I DO. OK, THANKS A LOT, BUDDY."

**Panel 9:**
- "NOBODY'S GONNA BELIEVE THIS."
- "BTW, IT MIGHT GET A LITTLE WARM ON THIS SIDE OF THE ROOM."

**Title/Credit:**
- "Maxwell's Demon"
- "only the least scariest demon... EVAH"

## Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the subversion of demonic expectations: instead of threatening the soul, the demon asks to borrow a hammer to build a wall—a reference to Maxwell's Demon, a thought experiment in thermodynamics. The title reveals this is intentionally "the least scariest demon," playing on the anti-climactic resolution.

# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure webcomic with a red circular character (appearing to be a superhero or speedster based on the lightning bolt design) interacting with plain stick figures. The art style is minimalist black and white with one character colored red with yellow accents.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"HMMM. I HAVE TO GET BACK HOME FOR MY WIFE'S 30TH BIRTHDAY PARTY."

**Panel 2:**
"BUT IT'S NOT FOR ANOTHER COUPLE OF HOURS. MAYBE I CAN GO FOR A QUICK JOG FIRST."

**Panel 3:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 4:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 5:**
"HONEY, I'M HOME!"

**Panel 6:**
(No dialogue)

# Visual Jokes
The primary joke is that the red character (a speedster, indicated by the lightning bolt) decides to go for a "quick jog," which he intends to last only a couple of hours. However, due to his superhuman speed, he loses track of time completely, missing his wife's birthday party entirely. The final panel shows him arriving home to find the party already finished, with only the cake remaining—a classic superhero consequence of not accounting for how fast he actually moves.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A simple line-drawn comic featuring two characters: Dr. Carroll (drawn with a striped/hatched head) and another person (drawn as a simple circle). The art style is minimalist black and white. The comic progresses from a conversation between the two characters to a newspaper headline, ending with Dr. Carroll reading the newspaper.

## Dialogue and Text (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
- "DR. CARROLL, CAN YOU EXPLAIN SPECIAL RELATIVITY TO LAYMAN?"
- "SURE."

**Panel 2:**
- "IT'S BEST TO ILLUSTRATE THIS WITH A THOUGHT EXPERIMENT."

**Panel 3:**
- "SUPPOSE AN ALIEN IS APPROACHING EARTH IN A SPACESHIP AND HE'S FIRING A LASER DIRECTLY AT EARTH."

**Panel 4 (labeled "10 MINUTES LATER..."):**
- "SO WE CAN NO LONGER THINK OF SPACE AND TIME AS BEING SEPARATE."

**Panel 5:**
- "WE HAVE TO THINK OF THE ALIEN SPACESHIP AS EXISTING IN A 4-DIMENSIONAL SIGNAL ENTITY CALLED SPACETIME."

**Panel 6:**
- "OMG. THAT'S AMAZING."

**Panel 7:**
- "CAN WE QUOTE YOU ON THAT?"
- "CERTAINLY."

**Panel 8:**
- "GREAT, THANKS."

**Panel 9:**
[Empty panel showing Dr. Carroll alone]

**Newspaper Headline:**
- "RESPECTED SCIENTIST MAKES EXTRAORDINARY CLAIM"
- "TIME-TRAVELING INTERDIMENSIONAL ALIENS THREATEN EARTH WITH DEADLY LASERS"

**Final Panel:**
Dr. Carroll reading the newspaper with a frustrated expression.

## Visual Jokes
The humor depends on the contrast between Dr. Carroll's careful scientific explanation of spacetime and how it's sensationalized by the media into a false alien invasion story. The reader's enthusiastic "OMG. THAT'S AMAZING" is what triggers the misquote, turning legitimate physics into tabloid hysteria.

# XKCD Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A multi-panel stick figure comic in black and white. The comic shows conversations between simple stick figures illustrating various scenarios related to scientific pursuits, curiosity, and rationalization. The art style is minimalist with basic line drawings.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Top Panel (Large):**
"So the beta decay from the cesium-137 is monitored by a detector interfaced to the computer. The software then converts the time intervals between pairs of decay events into random bits. If a certain sequence of 1s and 0s is produced, it activates this laser which, in turn, sweeps across the Casimir cavitation. When the arc reaches the top, it switches on this Van de Graaff generator which levitates this mylar origami unicorn!"

Character 1: "Wait... what?"
Character 2: "Unicorn?"

**Six-Panel Grid Below:**

Panel 1 - Character 1: "What the hell did you build this device for again?"
Panel 2 - Character 2: "What?"
Panel 3 - Character 1: "Does this thing have any useful applications?" / Character 2: "It... levitates unicorns. cooler toys"

Panels 4-6: Silent panels showing character confusion.

**Second Section - Three-Row Grid (Thanksgiving Scene):**

Panel 1 - Character 2: "Wanna help me finish my thanksgiving leftovers?"
Panel 2 - Character 1: "Nah. I'm trying to watch my weights."
Panel 3 - (Silent)

Panel 4 - Character 2: "Do you believe that the Standard Model of particle physics is correct?"
Panel 5 - Character 1: "Um... sure. I guess so. Why?"
Panel 6 - Character 2: "Then mass is probably just a mirage..."

Panel 7 - Character 2: "...the illusory consequence of our swimming in an ocean of Higgs field permeating the universe."
Panels 8-9: Character 1 eating, getting larger.

Label at bottom: "greater rationalization skills"

**Third Section - Three-Row Grid (Computer/Article Scene):**

Panel 1 - Character 2: "Hey, have you read this article? Researchers at CERN successfully trapped antihydrogen."
Panel 2 - Character 1: "Holy cow! Those bastards finally did it. This is amazing. This is—"
Panel 3 - (Silent, character looking at computer)

Panel 4 - (Silent, character at computer)
Panel 5 - Character 1: "OMG OMG OMG OMG."
Panel 6 - Character 2: "What's the matter with you?"

Panel 7 - Character 1: "Nothing."
Panel 8 - (Silent)
Panel 9 - Character 2: "I'll be in my bunk." / Character 1: "eww"

Label at bottom: "frequent nerdgasms"

**Fourth Section - Three-Row Grid (Battery Scene):**

Panel 1 - Character 2: "Honey, do we have any D batteries for the flashlight?"
Panel 2 - Character 1: "Strictly speaking, they're not 'batteries.' They're cells."
Panel 3 - (Silent, character gesturing)

Panel 4 - (Silent, character looking annoyed)
Panel 5 - Character 1: (Silent)
Panel 6 - Character 1 with explosion/action lines around them

Label at bottom: "more opportunities to be an elitist dick"

**Bottom Panel (Large):**
Title: "Reasons for getting a science/engineering education."

## Visual Jokes/Puns Dependent on Image

1. **The Unicorn Device**: The elaborate, pointless contraption that levitates origami unicorns - a commentary on how scientists often build things simply because they can, not because they have practical applications.

2. **Higgs Field Mirage**: The character uses quantum physics reasoning to dismiss the concept of weight/mass, allowing them to rationalize eating more food - a humorous misapplication of legitimate physics.

3. **Nerdgasm Escalation**: The character's increasing excitement and eventual "bunk" reference is a joke about intense intellectual arousal over scientific achievements.

# Scene Description
A six-panel webcomic in minimalist stick-figure style featuring two characters—one round-headed and one with a black helmet/head—in conversation. The final panel shows the helmeted character reading a book. The art style is simple black lines on white background.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- Lieutenant: "WHAT SEEMS TO BE THE PROBLEM, LIEUTENANT?"

**Panel 2:**
- Helmeted character: "WHY ISN'T THE WARP ENGINE ONLINE?"

**Panel 3:**
- Lieutenant: "WELL, IT SEEMS THAT THE ORIGINAL DESIGN ENGINEERS USED A LOT OF OFF-THE-SHELF COMPONENTS."

**Panel 4:**
- Lieutenant: "SOME OF THE SUBSYSTEMS RUN ON LEGACY CODE THAT ISN'T COMPATIBLE WITH OUR ISOLINEAR CHIPS AND..."

**Panel 5:**
- Helmeted character: "I DON'T WANT EXCUSES, MR LA FORGE."
- Lieutenant: "I WANT MY WARP ENGINES."
- Helmeted character: "YES, CAPTAIN."

**Panel 6:**
- Caption: "New technologies will come and go but COBOL is forever."
- Book title: "TEACH YOURSELF COBOL IN 24 HRS"

# Visual Jokes
The humor derives from the juxtaposition of futuristic sci-fi technology (warp engines, isolinear chips) with COBOL, an obsolete 1960s programming language still used in legacy systems today. The final panel's ironic statement about COBOL's permanence, combined with the captain's desperate crash-course solution, satirizes real-world technical debt in software systems.

A six-panel stick figure comic in black and white with blue accent color. The first three panels show a group of four stick figures in conversation. The bottom three panels depict one figure interacting with a washing machine, with the final panel showing the figure knocked back by the machine door.

**Panel 4:**
(No text)

**Panel 5:**
"ZAP"

The comic is a subversion of epic battle speeches from films like *Gladiator*. The dramatic buildup and warrior mentality is undercut by the reveal that the warriors are actually fighting a washing machine—a mundane household appliance. The final panel shows the blue stick figure knocked backward by the washing machine door, and the caption suggests frustration with the static electricity (electrons) that result from the laundry.

# Scene Description
A black and white comic in a sketchy, minimalist style depicting a post-apocalyptic narrative. The comic features three main illustrations: a ruined cityscape with tall buildings and dark smoke, a figure in revealing clothing standing amid destruction, and a final image of a partially buried Statue of Liberty in a barren landscape. The overall tone is dystopian and satirical.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

"For years I tried to warn them but they wouldn't listen.

And then the terrible times came.

At first, the people of Earth simply squabbled over the dwindling resources.

Then came war, and famine,... and pestilence.

Now anarchy reigns and all of the social progress and technological achievements of humanity have collapsed into a ruinous heap.

Savage bandits now roam the slum-ridden streets of formerly magnificent cities, scavenging and murdering for the few remaining scraps in order to survive.

I am writing this in the hopes that civilization will once again rise from the ashes; in the hopes that humanity will start again and rebuild itself.

To any future civilization that may read this, please do not repeat our mistakes. Please heed my warning.

Please use 128-bit IP addresses from the beginning."

# Visual Jokes
The final punchline is a technical joke: the comic's apocalyptic warning ends with advice to use IPv6 (128-bit IP addresses) instead of IPv4 (32-bit), suggesting that IPv4 address exhaustion contributed to humanity's downfall. This is a humorous commentary on a real technical limitation that has affected internet infrastructure.

# Scene Description
A three-panel webcomic showing a stick figure attempting to decorate a Christmas tree with ornaments and tinsel. The art style is simple line drawings with a green Christmas tree, colored ornaments (gold/yellow, silver/white, and red), a yellow star on top, and a stick figure with a blue hat. The comic illustrates an NP-hard computational problem using a humorous Christmas decoration scenario.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
- "Gold, silver, and red Christmas tree ornaments."
- "Hang ornaments so that no two adjacent ornaments share the same color."

**Panel 2:**
- "30 feet of tinsel."
- "Find tinsel-hanging pattern that touches every branch exactly once, loops back, and uses at most 30 feet."
- Numbered labels on tree: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8

**Panel 3:**
- "Nailed it."
- "Christmas is (NP-)hard. □"

# Visual Jokes
The comic is a pun on computational complexity theory. The final panel's caption "Christmas is (NP-)hard" is a play on words: "hard" refers both to the difficulty of Christmas preparation and the computer science term "NP-hard" (a class of computationally difficult problems). The stick figure's solution, shown with tangled red tinsel looping around the tree in a chaotic pattern, humorously depicts the struggle of solving these mathematically complex problems in practice.

# Webcomic Transcription: "...on Mars"

## Scene Description
A multi-panel comic in simple black-and-white stick figure style. The narrative follows aliens on Mars discussing a plan to send an agent to Earth, then shifts to Earth in 1995 where the agent attempts to spread scientific misinformation. The final panels show the agent's growing frustration as their applause-seeking efforts fail and they question the meaning of their mission.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (Top):**
- Yellow label: "...on Mars"
- Circular seal: "FREE MARTIAN COUNCIL" (text around border)
- Alien speech bubble: "My fellow Martians of the Supreme Council..."

**Panel 2:**
- Alien speech bubble: "...we have a problem."

**Panel 3:**
- Alien speech bubble: "The people of Earth have become too scientifically advanced."

**Panel 4:**
- Alien speech bubble: "If they continue to progress at this rate, they will soon threaten our Martian civilization."

**Panel 5:**
- Alien speech bubble: "We have decided to send Agent KRLLL to Earth disguised as a human."

**Panel 6:**
- Alien speech bubble: "He will then infiltrate the ranks of Earth's scientific community to propagate erroneous scientific thinking."

**Panel 7:**
- Alien speech bubble: "Good luck, Agent KRLLL, and godspeed."

**Panel 8:**
- Yellow label: "Earth 1995"
- Text box: "M-THEORY
  Type I  Type IIA  Type IIB
  Heterotic-O  Heterotic-E"

**Panel 9:**
- Sound effects: "APPLAUSE APPLAUSE APPLAUSE"

**Panel 10:**
- Speech bubble: "BUT, DR. WITTEN, WHAT DOES THE M STAND FOR?"

**Panel 11:**
- (Agent's face shown, no dialogue)

**Panel 12:**
- (Agent's face shown, smiling, no dialogue)

**Panel 13:**
- (Large close-up of Agent's frustrated face with wide eyes)

## Visual Jokes
1. The irony that the alien agent's plan to spread scientific misinformation by presenting M-Theory (legitimate cutting-edge physics) backfires when someone asks a reasonable critical question.
2. The agent's growing realization that even spreading legitimate complex science causes skepticism—their mission to promote "erroneous thinking" is inadvertently promoting good scientific questioning.
3. The final panel's exaggerated face suggests the agent's existential crisis about what they're actually supposed to be doing.

# Scene Description
A single-panel webcomic in black and white line art featuring a stick figure character progressively attempting to destroy a barcode from an Amazon package box. The figure goes through increasingly elaborate and paranoid schemes to prevent the barcode from being scanned, culminating in the character appearing distressed. The art style is minimalist stick-figure drawing with simple geometric shapes.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

"Carefully peel off barcode from package box."

"Shred."

"No wait. That's stupid. A barcode scanner only needs a thin slice of the whole block to interpret it."

"Anyone with a smartphone would still be able to read my UPS tracking number."

"Maybe I should shred it vertically."

"But that black line at the top would make it easy for anyone to determine the orientation of the strips."

"I should cut out the top line first."

"And then shred it vertically."

"But if the top is cut out at an angle, then it would be easy to line up the strips in the correct order by height."

"Then any Joe Shmoe with a scanner could read my tracking number, social engineer their way to my Amazon account, and find out that I read a lot of click lit. Unacceptable."

"It's best to cut off the ends of the strips at random angles and heights."

"Or maybe I should just burn it. It's the only way to be sure."

"I have a debilitatingly irrational fear of dumpster divers."

# Visual Jokes

1. **Progressive paranoia escalation**: The visual humor stems from the increasingly elaborate destruction methods, with each barcode iteration becoming more chaotic and fragmented.

2. **Orientation diagrams**: The small instructional-style diagrams showing how to rotate the barcode strips create humorous "helpful instructions" for something absurd.

3. **Stick figure with lighter**: The final image of the stick figure holding what appears to be a lighter/torch conveys manic desperation through simple visual language.

4. **Barcode degradation**: The visual progression from intact barcode → horizontally shredded → vertically shredded → randomly fragmented creates a visual narrative of escalating destruction.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A nine-panel minimalist stick-figure comic in black, white, and blue. The narrative follows a character wearing a blue cap through increasingly surreal and darkly humorous scenarios, progressing from a social gathering to space exploration. The art style uses simple line drawings with selective color (the cap remains consistently blue throughout).

## Text Content
No dialogue, captions, labels, or equations appear in this comic.

## Visual Narrative & Jokes

The comic relies entirely on visual storytelling and escalating absurdist humor:

1. **Panel 1**: Character with blue cap among friends (establishing the protagonist)
2. **Panel 2**: The blue cap becomes popular—everyone copies it
3. **Panel 3**: Mass adoption spreads to a lunar/alien landscape setting
4. **Panel 4**: Character alone, reflecting on the trend
5. **Panel 5-6**: Character driving away; the cap's popularity causes them to flee in a car (twice shown)
6. **Panel 7**: Character escapes to a cliff/edge as a last resort
7. **Panel 8**: Character launches themselves off the cliff in desperation
8. **Panel 9**: Character floating in space on a distant planet—the ultimate escape

**Visual Joke**: A commentary on trend cycles and social pressure. The protagonist's distinctive blue cap becomes so popular that it loses its individuality, driving them to increasingly extreme measures of escape—ultimately fleeing to another planet. The humor escalates from relatable social anxiety to cosmic absurdism.

# Scene Description
A minimalist stick-figure webcomic in black ink depicting a humorous alternate history scenario. The setting shifts from a tree branch with a hanging apple to an open field. The main character is a simple stick figure; a squirrel is drawn as a small furry creature. The comic is followed by a book cover mockup titled "Funny Squirrel Illustrations" by Isaac Newton.

# Dialogue and Text (in reading order)

**Comic panels:**
- "SLIP"
- "AWW... WHAT A CUTE LITTLE SQUIRREL."
- "WHO'S A CUTE LITTLE SQUIRREL? YOU ARE. YES YOU ARE."
- (Light bulb appears above stick figure's head)
- (Apple falls from tree)
- (Stick figure sits alone)

**Book cover:**
- "FUNNY SQUIRREL ILLUSTRATIONS"
- "By Isaac Newton"

**Caption below comic:**
- "History could have been a lot different."

# Visual Jokes
The primary joke is a subversion of the famous Isaac Newton apple-and-gravity story: instead of an apple falling on Newton's head and inspiring his theory of gravity, a squirrel distracts him on the tree branch. The squirrel causes Newton to slip and fall, preventing the historical moment. The "book cover" punchline suggests that without this gravitational inspiration, Newton instead wrote a book of humorous squirrel illustrations—a comedic inversion of his actual scientific legacy.

# Scene Description

A humorous evolutionary tree diagram drawn in simple black-and-white line art style against a white background. The diagram shows the evolutionary path from a BLT sandwich at the bottom, tracing upward through various organisms (labeled with "MMM" at one stage), mayo, fungi, plants, animals, bacteria, and archaea. Small illustrated icons represent each stage: the sandwich, a simple face, mayo jar, mushrooms, vegetables, various animals (including a pig), and branching organisms at the top. The overall style is deliberately crude and comedic.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Labels (top to bottom, left to right):**
- BACTERIA
- ARCHAEA
- EUKARYOTA
- PLANTS
- ANIMALS
- FUNGI

**Other text:**
- MAYO (labeling a jar)
- MMM (labeling a simple face)
- BLT (centered below the sandwich)
- "the convergence of awesome" (subtitle)

# Visual Jokes

The primary joke is that the diagram presents a BLT sandwich as the "endpoint" of evolution, with all of Earth's organisms (bacteria, plants, animals, fungi) converging to create it. The "MMM" face expresses satisfaction at this culinary endpoint. This inverts standard evolutionary trees by suggesting that evolution's ultimate purpose is producing a sandwich.

# Scene Description

The image shows a mathematical solution displayed on a white background with a black border. The solution involves solving a linear differential equation with initial conditions. Cat images are used as placeholder variables in the latter part of the solution, creating a visual substitution puzzle.

# Text Transcription

**Solve:**
y''' + 2y" - 5y' - 6y = 0,    y(0) = y'(0) = 0,    y"(0) = 1

---

m³ + 2m² - 5m - 6 = 0

m = -1, m = 2, m = -3

---

y = [cat₁]e⁻ˣ + [cat₂]e²ˣ + [cat₃]e⁻³ˣ

[cat₁] + [cat₂] + [cat₃] = 0

-[cat₁] + 2[cat₂] - 3[cat₃] = 0

[cat₁] + 4[cat₂] + 9[cat₃] = 1

---

[cat₁] = -1/6

[cat₂] = 1/15

[cat₃] = 1/10

---

y = -1/6 e⁻ˣ + 1/15 e²ˣ + 1/10 e⁻³ˣ

# Visual Joke

Different cat images replace the constants in the equation system, creating a humorous visual substitution where solving for the cats literally means finding their coefficient values.

comics.transcript:
# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A multi-panel webcomic in simple black-and-white line art style featuring a stick figure character with a distinctive blue/filled-in head. The comic spans three sections separated by dotted lines, progressing from the character's various adventures and social interactions to a final commentary on internet culture and commercialism. The art style is minimalist with basic geometric shapes and simple line drawings.

---

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (Top Row):**
- "TELNET" (label on boat)
- "PORT 23" (sign on dock)

**Panel 2 (Second Row, Left):**
- "GUEST LOGIN" (speech bubble)
- "# ls -tle" (command text)
- "GUEST DENIED" (speech bubble)

**Panel 3 (Second Row, Right):**
- "REC.ARTS.STARTREK" (sign)

**Panel 4 (Third Row, Left):**
- "FIN" (label on vehicle)

**Panel 5 (Third Row, Right):**
- "COMMPROGRAMMING" (sign)

**Panel 6 (Fourth Row, Right):**
- "HMMM, WHERE ARE ALL THESE PEOPLE COMING FROM ?" (speech bubble)

**Panel 7 (Fifth Row, Left):**
- "GETTIN' AWFUL CROWDED IN MY SKY." (speech bubble)

**Panel 8 (Fifth Row, Right):**
- "HEY, YOU KIDS !..." (speech bubble)

**Panel 9 (Bottom Section):**
- "MICROSOFT" (building label)
- "EBAY" (building label)
- "GOOGLE" (building label)
- "YAHOO" (building label)
- "50% OFF WATCHES" (sign)
- "GIRLS GIRLS GIRLS" (sign)
- "FACEBOOK" (building label)
- "MALE ENHANCEMENT" (sign)
- "...GET OFFA MY LAWN !!!" (speech bubble from character)

---

## Visual Jokes/Puns

1. **"TELNET" / "PORT 23"** – Technical reference to early internet telnet protocol and port 23; depicts outdated internet technology as a literal "port" with a boat.

2. **"# ls -tle"** – Unix/Linux command line reference; represents the character attempting to access guest login on early internet systems.

3. **"FIN"** – The car is labeled "FIN," a visual pun on "THE END" pronounced as "fin."

4. **Crowded sky/lawn** – The character's personal space becomes progressively more commercialized and crowded as the internet matures, culminating in the final panel.

5. **"Get offa my lawn!!!"** – Classic "grumpy old man" phrase applied to the early internet adopter watching the internet become commercialized with corporate websites and advertisements, representing the shift from early internet culture to modern commercial internet.

# Scene Description
A six-panel webcomic in simple stick-figure style. The first five panels show a group of stick figures discussing a banking scandal, with one figure (colored blue) speaking while others listen. The final panel shows the blue figure at a computer, with a caption at the bottom.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:** "ABC Bank is perpetrating a massive fraud against the people."

**Panel 2:** "They're getting rich on the backs of ordinary people like us."

**Panel 3:** "They're getting rich by break-ing the law; by treating us like chattel; by disregarding basic human dignity."

**Panel 4:** "And they continue to be supported by the Federal Reserve and protected by the gov't."

**Panel 5:** "The question is 'What are we gonna do about it?'."

**Panel 6:** "BUY 1000 SHARES ABC" with "CLICK" sound effect

**Bottom caption:** "I know a winner when I hear it."

# Visual Jokes
The humor is ironic: after passionately denouncing corporate fraud and corruption, the character's solution is to invest in the very company they criticized—profiting from the same system they condemned. The joke critiques how outrage can be redirected into complicity through financial gain.

# Transcription: "Math Doesn't Suck" Webcomic

## Scene Description
A humorous educational webcomic in simple line-drawn style featuring a stick figure person in bed experiencing seasonal changes and their effects on sleep patterns and temperature. The comic uses diagrams, equations, and illustrations to explain scientific concepts related to the Earth's axial tilt and its impact on daily life.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (top left):** Person sleeping in bed with "z z z z" (sleep symbols)

**Panel 2 (top center):** Person waking up in bed

**Panel 3 (top right):** Person at computer with yellow arrow pointing at screen

**Panel 4 (large circular diagram):**
- "23.5°" (at top)
- "MARCH EQUINOX" (label with blue circle at top)
- "SPRING" (arrow pointing to left blue circle)
- "WINTER" (arrow pointing to right blue circle)
- "SUMMER" (arrow pointing to bottom blue circle)
- "AUTUMN" (arrow pointing to right blue circle)
- Yellow circle in center (representing Sun)

**Panel 5 (bottom of main section):**
- "37° 26' 19.7292" N    12° 2' 2.3208" W" (coordinates)
- "θ₁" (angle label)
- "SIN θ₁ = cosh cosδ cosφ + sinδ sinφ" (equation)
- Yellow circle (Sun) with arrow pointing from blue circle (Earth)

**Panel 6 (middle left):** Person in bed thinking, speech bubble: "8 AM-1SM. ○ ○ ○ ○ ○"

**Panel 7 (distance diagram):**
- "MY HOUSE" (small house illustration)
- "2.1 MILES" (distance label)
- Stick figures and mountains
- "MOUNTAINS" (label)

**Panel 8 (graph):**
- Title: "TYPICAL WEEKDAY"
- Y-axis: "HAPPINESS / PRODUCTIVITY" with upward arrow
- X-axis: "TIME OF DAY" with marks at "12AM, 3, 6, 9, 12PM, 3, 6, 9"
- Wavy line showing energy fluctuation throughout the day

**Panel 9 (diagram with equations):**
- "SOUND PROPAGATION" (arrow pointing right)
- "TEMPERATURE INVERSION" (arrow pointing down)
- House, stick figures, mountains
- Equations: "y = 331m/s + (0.6m/s/C°)·T"
- "1 =B(402)g/m³" (partial/unclear)

**Panel 10 (bottom three panels showing person in bed):**
- Left panel: Multiple curved lines representing sound waves
- Center panel: "WARM FRONT MOVING IN. TODAY'S HIGH: 64°F!" (speech bubble)
- Right panel: Person in bed

**Footer text:** "Math Doesn't Suck"

## Visual Jokes/Puns
1. The comic title "Math Doesn't Suck" is ironic given the person's apparent frustration with equations and environmental factors affecting their sleep
2. The progression shows how mathematical/physical concepts (Earth's tilt, temperature inversions, sound propagation) directly impact the character's everyday comfort and sleep schedule—illustrating how abstract math has concrete real-world consequences
3. The use of actual scientific equations mixed with simple stick figures creates a humorous contrast between complexity and simplicity

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A stick figure comic in simple black line art with red accent coloring. The scene is divided into two panels: the upper panel shows a figure standing on clouds pointing a red laser pointer upward toward the sky with a speech bubble; the lower panel shows several small stick figures on the ground being hit by the red laser beam. The art style is minimalist and humorous.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Title:** "Do you believe in God?"

**Upper panel - Speech bubble:** "HA HA."

**Lower panel - Speech bubble:** "Well, *someone's* gotta be on the other end of that laser pointer."

**Lower panel - Label on small figures:** "atheists"

## Visual Joke
The humor relies on a visual pun: the figure in the clouds is using a laser pointer (typically used to play with cats or pets), while the people below are labeled "atheists." The joke suggests that God is playing with atheists like a cat owner plays with a cat using a laser pointer—the atheists don't realize they're being "toyed with" by a higher power. The setup subverts the typical theological debate by reframing it as a playful game rather than a serious philosophical question.

# Transcription: "How to Study Math"

## Scene Description
A black and white line drawing in a humorous, cartoon style depicts a person wielding a sword and shield, actively fighting against a large dragon. The dragon's wings and body are completely covered with mathematical equations and formulas. The art style is simple and sketch-like, emphasizing the comedic contrast between the serious subject matter (mathematics) and the violent, combative imagery.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)
- **Title:** HOW TO STUDY MATH
- **Caption:** Don't just read it; fight it!
- **Attribution:** -- Paul R. Halmos

## Visual Jokes
The primary visual joke is the literal personification of "fighting" math—rather than passively reading mathematical formulas, the person is depicted as literally battling a dragon that embodies mathematics itself. This visual metaphor humorously captures the struggle many students experience when learning math, transforming an abstract intellectual challenge into a physical, combat-based scenario.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A nine-panel comic in stick figure style with black backgrounds, featuring two stick figures inside a cave and a character with a red/orange hat outside. The final panel shows a watercolor illustration of a solitary figure on a beach facing the ocean.

## Dialogue and Text (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:** (No text)

**Panel 2:** 
- "Hey, guys! You have to see this"

**Panel 3:** 
- "There's an entire world outside these cave walls!"

**Panel 4:** 
- "I found this magnificent beach where every pebble and shell is smoother and prettier than the next!"

**Panel 5:** 
- "And this great ocean of beauty lay all undiscovered before me!"

**Panel 6:** 
- "C'mon, you gotta see this!!!"

**Panel 7:** (No text)

**Panel 8:** 
- "Guys?"

**Panel 9:** (No text)

**Final Panel:** (No text - watercolor beach scene)

## Visual Joke
The humor relies on the contrast between the enthusiastic explorer's attempts to convince their cave-dwelling companions to come see the outside world, and their complete indifference. The final watercolor panel reveals the explorer alone on the beach—their friends never left the cave, leaving them solitary despite discovering this beautiful place.

# Scene Description
A two-panel comic in simple line-art style. The top panel shows a stick figure with a blue head walking down a city street lined with tall buildings. The bottom panel shows a magnified circular zoom of the same scene, revealing multiple stick figures engaged in various activities (some appearing to argue or interact dramatically) around the same street. The contrast illustrates the disparity between the figure's perception and reality.

# Text Transcription
**Bottom caption:** "This is what my neighborhood looks like to me now."

# Visual Jokes
The primary joke is a visual comparison between perception and reality: the top panel depicts the neighborhood as the figure perceives it—relatively calm and empty—while the magnified detail reveals it's actually full of activity and conflict that the figure is unaware of or ignoring. The blue coloring on the figure's head in the top panel may suggest obliviousness or willful ignorance.

# Scene Description
A 3x3 grid comic in simple black and white stick figure style with minimal detail. The comic presents a satirical argument about human nature, progressing from evolutionary biology to modern lifestyle desires.

---

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (Top Left):**
"Millions of years of primate evolution."
[Two simple stick figure primates in climbing/jumping poses]

**Panel 2 (Top Center):**
"Animal instincts are embedded deep in our DNA."
[Three DNA double helix strands labeled "AGCT"]

**Panel 3 (Top Right):**
"We are explorers; we are hunters; we are natural born killers."
[Images of a mosquito and a beetle]

**Panel 4 (Middle Left):**
"We were not designed to sit in quiescent passivity in a cubicle."
[Stick figure sitting in a cubicle with minimal shading]

**Panel 5 (Middle Center):**
"We were not designed to live in concrete jungles infused with our insidious technologies."
[Stick figure standing before a cityscape of gray buildings]

**Panel 6 (Middle Right):**
"I want to live free; to live off of the wilderness."
[Stick figure in action pose with a beetle]

**Panel 7 (Bottom Left):**
"I don't want to suppress my animal instincts; I want to release my inner killer."
[Stick figure with "TWANG" sound effect, appearing aggressive]

**Panel 8 (Bottom Center):**
"I want to hunt my own food."
[Stick figure lunging at stacked books with "BITE" text and red coloring on top book]

**Panel 9 (Bottom Right):**
"I want to taste the fresh kill in my mouth."
[Stick figure appearing to bite or eat a stack of red books]

---

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the disconnect between the grandiose evolutionary/primal rhetoric and the absurd reality: the "hunters" are attacking books, not actual prey. The progression shows someone justifying their desire to escape modern life using primal instinct language, only to reveal they're actually trying to escape work and responsibility by biting books—a commentary on performative primitivism and the gap between romantic idealization of "natural living" and actual human behavior.

# Webcomic Transcription: "Term Paper Procrastination"

## Scene Description
A stick-figure comic in black ink depicting a student's desperate attempt to meet a term paper deadline. The comic shows the character at a laptop in various states of panic, with two full pages of the actual "paper" displayed. The art style is simple line drawings with minimal detail, typical of rage comics/meme format.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"OH NO. MY TERM PAPER IS DUE TOMORROW AND I HAVEN'T EVEN STARTED YET."

**Panel 2:**
"I HAVE TO FINISH 30 PAGES IN 8 HOURS."

**Panel 3:**
[No dialogue]

**Document Page 1 (displayed):**
"Blah Blah Blah
By Blahrabidiah

Blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah

Page 1"

**Panel 4:**
"RATS. ONLY 3 PAGES. MAYBE I SHOULD USE A HUGE FONT."

**Panel 5:**
"STILL ONLY 4 PAGES. LET'S USE 16 PT FONT, DOUBLE SPACE, AND INCREASE MARGINS TO 1.5."

**Panel 6:**
"HMM. STILL ONLY 9 PAGES. TIME TO USE SUPERFLUOUS WORDS, FLOWERY LANGUAGE, AND LOTS OF REDUNDANCIES."

**Panel 7:**
"AND MAYBE I CAN GET AWAY WITH 20 PT FONT, 3.0 SPACING, AND 2.4 MARGINS."

**Panel 8:**
"29 PAGES, BABY!"

**Document Page 2 (displayed):**
"Blah Blah Blah
By Blahrabidiah

blah blah blah blah blah blah

blah blah blah blah blah blah

blah blah blah blah blah blah

blah blah blah blah blah blah

blah blah blah blah blah blah

blah blah blah blah blah blah

blah blah blah blah blah blah

Page 1"

**News Flash Caption:**
"NEWS FLASH: When you do this, your teacher shows it to all of her teacher friends and they all laugh and laugh."

**Final Panel:**
[Stick figure standing outside "TEACHER'S LOUNGE" door]
"HA HA HA HA"

## Visual Jokes/Puns

1. **Absurdist humor**: The paper literally contains only the word "blah" repeated, becoming increasingly sparse as font size increases, yet still reaches 29 pages through formatting manipulation.

2. **Double irony**: The final "News Flash" reveals that while the student thinks they've succeeded in fooling their teacher, teachers actually find this obvious padding technique hilarious and share it as a joke among colleagues.

3. **Document display**: The actual rendered pages show how ridiculously transparent the formatting tricks are—the second page literally just has lines of "blah" with massive spacing, making the joke visually concrete.

# Webcomic Transcription: "Corporate Finance"

## Scene Description
A stick-figure comic with 9 panels arranged in a 3x3 grid. The art style is minimalist line drawings. Multiple characters (drawn as simple circles with stick limbs) have conversations about post-apocalyptic civilization rebuilding. The tone shifts from practical survival needs to corporate finance concerns.

## Dialogue and Text (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
- Character 1: "Are there any doctors here? We need doctors and nurses to take care of the wounded."

**Panel 2:**
- Character 1: "Move it! OK, now we need the materials scientists and chemists to produce building materials."

**Panel 3:**
- Character 1: "And we need the mechanical engineers to build the tools so we can hunt and farm."

**Panel 4:**
- Character 1: "I'm not sure this is the best way to maximize corporate value."

**Panel 5:**
- Character 1: "We need the civil engineers and construction workers to build buildings, bridges, and dams. GO!"

**Panel 6:**
- Character 1: "Has anyone determined the proper capital structure for this? And what is the net present value for these projects?"

**Panel 7:**
- Character 1: "Electrical engineers and physicists. Get working on electricity generation and telecommunications infrastructure."

**Panel 8:**
- Character 1: "Let's go people! CHOP CHOP!"

**Panel 9:**
- Character 1: "Maybe we should discuss executive compensation and stock options now."

**Title/Question at bottom:**
"QUESTION: What is the least useful college major if you need to rebuild civilization after the apocalypse?"

**Answer header:** "ANSWER: corporate finance"

## Visual Jokes
The primary joke is the contrast between panels 1-7 (prioritizing essential survival and infrastructure roles) and panels 4, 6, and 9 (inserting inappropriate corporate finance concerns). Panel 4's "corporate value" comment is absurd when civilization is collapsing. Panel 9's suggestion of executive compensation during apocalyptic reconstruction is the peak absurdity, making corporate finance the punchline as the least useful field.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A simple, minimalist comic strip in a 3x3 grid format featuring a stick figure with a round head wearing a blue cap/helmet, holding a banana. The character has a simple smiling face. The art style is basic line drawing. The setting appears to be a blank white background in each panel.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:** (no text)

**Panel 2:** "BANANA."

**Panel 3:** (no text)

**Panel 4:** 
"BANANA.
BANANA.
BANANA."

**Panel 5:** "BANAAANA."

**Panel 6:**
"BANAAANA.
BAAANAANAANAA.
BANAAANAANAANAAA."

**Panel 7:**
"BANÁNANA
BANÁNANANÁ
BANÁNANÁNANÁNANÁ.
BANÁNANÁNANÁNANÁNANÁ."

**Panel 8:** (no text)

**Panel 9:**
"BANANA.
I swear to God,
'banana' is NOT
a real word."

## Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the repetition and increasingly absurd elongation of the word "banana," which creates semantic satiation—the psychological phenomenon where a word loses meaning through repetition. By the final panel, the character's frustration that "banana" no longer seems like a real word mirrors the reader's experience of the word becoming meaningless through excessive repetition.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A six-panel comic strip with simple stick figures of varying sizes against white backgrounds. The art style is minimalist line drawings. Each panel shows different characters represented as circles with dot eyes and simple expressions (happy, sad, or neutral faces).

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"You may be alone now, but there is hope."

**Panel 2:**
"There is a theory that says that, for each one of you, there is a partner for you somewhere out there."

**Panel 3:**
"Your partner simply hasn't been found yet."

**Panel 4:**
"It doesn't matter what you look like; it doesn't matter whether you're attractive or not."

**Panel 5:**
"It doesn't matter how much you weigh; whether you're big or small."

**Panel 6:**
"It doesn't matter what your personality is like; whether you're charming or strange."

**Panel 7:**
"According to this theory, there is a partner out there for each and every one of you."

**Panel 8:**
"Unfortunately, there is no compelling evidence to support this theory yet."

**Panel 9:**
"So SUSY is probably wrong and you're all SOL."

## Visual Jokes
The comic's humor relies on the stark contrast between the encouraging message (panels 1-7) and the deflating conclusion (panels 8-9). The final panel shows all characters with sad/distressed faces, emphasizing the pessimistic punchline. "SOL" is an acronym for "Sh*t Out of Luck."

(Korean text, approximate: "I'll kill all of you!")

**Panel 5:**
"OH...UH...SORRY, DUDE. I DIDN'T KNOW YOU WUZ...UH... PLEASE ACCEPT MY HUMBLE RESIGNATION."

**Panel 6:**
"THAT COULDA BEEN UGLY."

**Bottom caption:**
"If there's one thing that I know, it's never to mess with Mother Nature, mother-in-laws, or motherfucking Koreans playing StarCraft."

---

# Visual Jokes
- The soldier's cocky attitude toward the "Zerg" (StarCraft reference) immediately reverses when they realize their opponent is a Korean player
- The switch to Korean dialogue and the soldier's instant capitulation plays on the stereotype of Korean dominance in competitive StarCraft
- The final caption ties together mothers, mothers-in-law, and "motherfucking Koreans" as equally formidable forces not to be challenged

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A nine-panel stick figure comic in black ink with minimalist style. The narrative follows a student's progression from sleeping through math class to commiserating with a friend about struggling with the subject. The art style is deliberately crude stick figures with simple line work.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (top left):** "zzzzz" (sleeping sounds)

**Panel 2 (top right):** [No text]

**Panel 3 (middle left):** [No text]

**Panel 4 (middle right):** [No text]

**Panel 5 (center left):** [No text]

**Panel 6 (center right):** [No text]

**Panel 7 (bottom left):** [Red boxing gloves visible on two figures]

**Panel 8 (bottom right):** [No text]

**Panel 9 (bottom, large panel):**
- Left figure: "MAN, I REALLY SUCK AT MATH."
- Right figure: "YEAH, WHAT'S THAT FEEL LIKE? BEING STUPID, I MEAN."

## Visual Jokes
The primary visual joke is the progression from the student sleeping in class → being hit in the head with a basketball → hitting a trash can in frustration → two friends boxing with red gloves, which sets up the ironic punchline where one friend asks what being "stupid" feels like as if it's a novel experience—implying they too struggle with math.
