## Scene Description
A stick-figure comic in black ink on white background depicting a character's increasingly absurd plan to commit murder. The character is drawn as a simple circle head with stick limbs, wearing a mask and sunglasses in later panels. The final panel shows the character in bed, rendered in blue, with a thought bubble above.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"HMMM. WHAT SHOULD I DO TODAY?"

**Panel 2:**
"I KNOW! I THINK I'LL MURDER SOME RANDOM GUY."

**Panel 3:**
"FIRST I NEED A MURDERING MASK."

**Panel 4:**
"NOW I NEED TO PUT ON MY MURDERING SHOES."

**Panel 5:**
"AND, FINALLY, I NEED A MURDERING KNIFE."

**Panel 6:**
"THEN PICK SOME RANDOM HOUSE IN A LOW-CRIME NEIGHBORHOOD."

**Panel 7:**
"...AND BREAK IN WITHOUT LEAVING ANY SIGNS OF INTRUSION."

**Panel 8:**
"NOW I'M GONNA WAIT BEHIND THIS SHOWER CURTAIN FOR HOURS."

**Panel 9:**
"THEN, WHEN THE OWNER COMES HOME AND GOES TO BED, IMMA MURDER THE HELL OUTTA HIM."

**Panel 10:**
"When things go bump in the night, I have an amazing capacity for irrationality."

## Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the contrast between the character's elaborate, detailed planning for murder and the final punchline revealing it's all an anxious bedtime fantasy, with the caption's deadpan acknowledgment of nighttime irrationality.

# Scene Description
A minimalist stick figure comic in the style of xkcd. The figure sits at a computer desk wearing a blue beanie/hat, surrounded by a massive cloud of website screenshots and browser windows floating above the desk. Below, a magnified circular detail shows an Ethernet cable connector. The art uses simple black line drawings against a white background.

# Text Transcription
**Caption (bottom):**
"How can so much of my life flow through this tiny thing?"

# Visual Jokes
The humor is visual and conceptual: the comic contrasts the enormous volume of internet content and digital life (represented by the towering mass of website screenshots) with the tiny physical conduit (the Ethernet cable) through which all that data actually flows. It's a commentary on how much of modern life and information depends on an almost impossibly small piece of hardware.

# Scene Description
A six-panel black and white webcomic featuring two simple cartoon characters (stick figures with round heads) sitting at a computer. The left character wears a cap and the right character has longer hair. The art style is minimalist sketch-based. The comic depicts a conversation about creating a video game player character.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (top left):**
[Computer screen text visible:]
"connecting to 192.168.x.x SSH..."
"Attempting to exploit SSHv1 (RC32..."
"Resetting root password to '210N0103'"
"System open! Access level 49"
"$ ssh 192.2.2.2 1 root"
"root@192.2.2.2's password:"

**Panel 2 (top center):**
Character 1: "I'M IN. HOW DO YOU WANT TO DO THIS?"

**Panel 3 (top right):**
Character 2: "HOW ABOUT THIS? UNDRAFTED HARVARD GRAD. WHITE DUDE. CUT FROM 2 TEAMS. NOW THE BIGGEST STAR IN THE NBA. OVERNIGHT."

**Panel 4 (bottom left):**

Character 2: "YEAH!"

**Panel 5 (bottom center):**
Character 2: "THIS IS GONNA BE AWESOME."
Character 1: "HEE HEE."

**Panel 6 (bottom right):**
Character 1: "TRINITY! NEO! QUIT FUCKING WITH THE MATRIX!"

# Visual Jokes
The final panel reveals the characters are not actually hacking into a game system—they are literally manipulating "the Matrix," breaking the fourth wall and the comic's own reality. The joke subverts expectations by revealing the hacking scenario was metaphorical manipulation of actual reality.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A screenshot of Gmail showing an email composition window with a popup dialog box overlaid. The interface displays a red "SEND" button at the top, along with "Save Now" and "Discard" options. The email is addressed to "jenny" with a subject line "<3 <3 <3". A warning dialog from Google has appeared, interrupting the send action.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Top toolbar:**
- SEND
- Save Now
- Discard

**Email header fields:**
- To: jenny [REDACTED]@g...
- Add Cc | Add Bcc
- Subject: <3 <3 <3
- Attach a file

**Formatting toolbar:**
- B (Bold) | I (Italic) | U (Underline) | T (Font) | ⊢T⊣ (Strikethrough)
- Plain Text

**Email body text:**
"Dearest Jenny,

I can't stop thinking about you. I had that dream about you again last night. Y'know that dream where I am Captain Picard and you are the Borg? I miss you so much. Why didn't you return my calls last night? I tried calling you several times last night but I kept getting your voicemail. I know you were home last night because of your facebook and twitter posts. Are you upset with me or something? I hope I didn't do anything to upset you. Please write back soon. I think you are the one. I think we should get married. What do you think?"

**Popup dialog box (Google warning):**
- Header: "The page at https://mail.google.com says:"
- Message: "You have only been dating Jenny for a week.

You wrote a message that makes you sound needy and a little bit crazy. Send anyway?"
- Buttons: OK | Cancel

# Visual Jokes
The comic's humor derives from the contrast between the sender's obsessive, desperate message (mentioning dreams, repeated calls, marriage proposals after one week) and Gmail's algorithm detecting the needy tone and warning the user before sending. The juxtaposition of romantic subject hearts "<3 <3 <3" with the increasingly unhinged message content emphasizes the disconnect between intent and reality.

# Scene Description

This is a black-and-white stick-figure webcomic in the style of xkcd. The story follows a stick figure character who is mysteriously transported into a mathematical singularity after being hit by an object labeled "3". The character encounters various mathematicians and philosophers who explain increasingly abstract concepts (Euclidean geometry, pi, palindromes, the Koch snowflake, and the metric system) before revealing they are game developers. The final panels show tension over Half-Life 3.

---

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1-3:** (Silent panels showing object labeled "3" approaching, then hitting the character)

**Panel 4:**
- Sound effect: "BLAT!"

**Panel 5:**
- Character: "HEY, WHERE AM I?"

**Panel 6:**
- Character in suit: "WELCOME TO THE CHRONOSYNCASTIC INFUNDIBULUM, A PLACE WHERE INFINITY ENDS."
- Other character: "WHAT?"

**Panel 7:**
- Character in suit: "YOU ARE NOW INSIDE A MATHEMATICAL SINGULARITY WHERE INFINITY ENDS."
- Other character: "WOW REALLY?"

**Panel 8:**
- Character: "LOOK, SEE? TWO PARALLEL LINES MEETING IN EUCLIDEAN SPACE."
- Other character: "COOL!"

**Panel 9:**
- Character in suit: "AND THESE ARE THE LAST SEVERAL DIGITS OF PI."
- Text displayed: "33462648323397985353629514135"
- Other character: "WOW! IT'S A PALINDROME!"

**Panel 10:**
- Character in suit: "AND, NOW, FLYING CARS ARE THE NORM."

**Panel 11:**
- Character in suit: "HERE, THE KOCH SNOWFLAKE IS EVERYWHERE DIFFERENTIABLE. THERE IS NO HALTING PROBLEM, AND AMERICANS HAVE ADOPTED THE METRIC SYSTEM."
- Other character: "YOU'RE KIDDING!"

**Panel 12:**
- Character in suit: "THIS PLACE IS AMAZING."

**Panel 13:**
- Character: "BUT WHO ARE THOSE GUYS?"
- Character in suit: "THEM? OH... UH... GAME DEVELOPERS."

**Panel 14:**
- Character in suit: "WE DON'T LIKE TO TALK ABOUT IT."
- Other character: "PERFECT. HALF-LIFE 3 MUST BE PERFECT!"

---

# Visual Jokes

1. **Panel 9:** The "palindrome" joke is visual—the digits shown (33462648323397985353629514135) actually read the same forwards and backwards, making it a true palindrome in this mathematical paradise.

2. **Panel 10:** The flying cars are drawn as simple rockets/space vehicles, a humorous reference to sci-fi tropes about "the future."

3. **Overall meta-joke:** The webcomic satirizes the gaming community's obsession with Half-Life 3, using an absurdist mathematical setting where even impossible concepts exist—yet game developers still can't deliver the game.

# Scene Description
A three-panel educational comic followed by a larger scene. The first three panels show a character with glasses explaining rainbow formation to another character, using simple stick-figure style drawings. The final panel depicts a stick figure standing in rain beneath a complete rainbow, drawn in color with visible color bands (red, orange, yellow, green, blue, purple).

---

# Text Transcription

**Panel 1:**
"... SO SHORTER WAVELENGTH LIGHT EMERGES FROM THE RAINDROP AT A SMALLER ANGLE TO THE INCIDENT LIGHT THAN LONGER WAVELENGTH LIGHT."

**Panel 2:**
"IN THIS WAY, THE COMBINED EFFECTS OF DISPERSION, REFRACTION, AND REFLECTION PRODUCE A RAINBOW."

**Panel 3:**
"WOW, NATURE IS AWESOME!"

**Panel 4:**
"I NEVER GET ANY CREDIT ANYMORE.
* SIGH *"

**Label (bottom):**
"ROYGBIV"

---

# Visual Jokes
The humor derives from the contrast between the two characters' reactions: while one marvels at nature's awesome complexity, the other (presumably representing "ROYGBIV"—the traditional mnemonic for rainbow colors—or the rainbow itself) sighs in frustration at receiving no credit or acknowledgment for the phenomenon they create. The final panel's larger, colorful rainbow emphasizes its visual splendor while the stick figure complains about being unappreciated.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A six-panel comic in simple stick figure style. The setting shows a character at a computer in various panels. The art is minimalist black and white line drawing typical of xkcd-style webcomics.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (top left):** 
[No text]

**Panel 2 (top center):**
[No text]

**Panel 3 (top right):**
[Question mark above character's head]

**Panel 4 (middle left):**
"HEY, RANDY. FORGOT TO LOG OUT OF FACEBOOK."

**Panel 5 (middle center):**
"I HAVE COMPLETE ACCESS TO HIS ACCOUNT."

**Panel 6 (middle right):**
"THIS MEANS THAT I CAN MAKE ANY CHANGES I WANT TO HIS PROFILE."

**Panel 7 (bottom, large):**
"I'M A HACKER, BITCH!"

**Text at very bottom:**
"1337 h4x0r skillz!"

## Visual Jokes
The humor stems from the dramatic overstatement of "hacking skills"—simply having someone forget to log out of Facebook and gaining access to their account is presented with the same grandiose language ("I'M A HACKER, BITCH!") and leetspeak ("1337 h4x0r skillz") typically associated with serious cybercriminals, creating an ironic contrast between minor social engineering and legitimate hacking.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A six-panel stick figure comic in black and white depicting a programmer at a desk with a laptop. The art style is minimalist XKCD-style stick figures. The character wears glasses and sits at a desk throughout most panels, with increasing agitation progressing through the comic.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 3 (top right):**
- ';' expected...............
- Invalid expression statement
- Invalid method declaration
- ............

**Final panel (bottom right):**
"because nobody likes a Grammar Nazi"

## Visual Joke
The comic is a visual pun on "Grammar Nazi" — the programmer's compiler errors about grammar and syntax are so frustrating that they metaphorically "attack" the character (depicted with violent motion lines in panel 5), visualizing the abstract concept of being harassed by grammar rules as literal violence. The joke culminates in the character appearing destroyed in the final panel, with the caption explaining they've become violent *because* of grammar enforcement, inverting the typical meaning of "Grammar Nazi" into a commentary on how frustrating strict syntax rules can be.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A multi-panel comic in a minimalist black and white style with rust-red Martian soil. It depicts an astronaut on Mars experiencing poetic wonder, contrasted with mission control's pragmatic expectations. The art uses simple geometric shapes for the figure and landscape, with the emotional tone shifting from romantic to absurdist humor.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"So beautiful."

**Panel 2:**
"This very day, Great Mars, I traverse thy desolate plains,"

**Panel 3:**
"And wander lonely as a cloud, bereft of darkened rains."

**Panel 4:**
"How many weary steps upon rusted soil o'ergone. How far the travels from Man's first dawn."

**Panel 5:**
"If sight could sing, o what composeth the eyes, Both rejoice the splendor whence songs arise."

**Panel 6:**
"Great Mars, what majestic secrets unrevealed still, What mysteries doth thou shroud under hill?"

**Panel 7:**
"O, spark of life that struggles to be free,"

**Panel 8:**
"On Earthen fertile crescent, why should this a desert be?"

**Panel 9:**
"To see the Universe in a grain of sand,"
[Label: "MISSION CONTROL"]

**Panel 10:**
"WHAT THE HELL IS HE DOING NOW?"
"SHOULDN'T HE BE DOING A SOIL ANALYSIS?"

**Bottom caption:**
"Never send a poet to do a scientist's job."

## Visual Jokes
- The contrast between the astronaut's increasingly flowery, poetic internal monologue and mission control's exasperated realization he's quoting Blake instead of conducting experiments
- The absurdity of romantic poetry recitation on Mars, undermined by the mundane requirement of soil analysis

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A stick-figure comic in simple black line art depicting the lifecycle of a baby being delivered by a stork and subsequently swept up into increasingly powerful water currents, culminating in a whirlpool. The progression moves from domestic delivery to natural disaster through a series of sequential panels showing the character's journey downstream.

## Text Transcription
**Panel 1 (top left):** "SPLASH"

All other panels contain no dialogue or text.

## Visual Jokes and Narrative Elements

1. **The Stork Delivery**: The comic opens with the classic image of a stork delivering a baby bundle, referencing the traditional "stork delivery" myth.

2. **Escalation Through Water Flow**: The visual humor builds through escalation—the baby begins in calm water with gentle current arrows, which progressively increase in number, intensity, and complexity.

3. **The Whirlpool**: The final panel reveals the baby has been swept into a massive whirlpool, with concentric circular arrows showing the vortex's pull. Multiple stick figures can be seen at various positions around the maelstrom.

4. **Dark Humor**: The comic uses the contrast between the innocent beginning (stork delivery) and the increasingly catastrophic natural disaster as the joke—an ironic commentary on fate or life's chaotic trajectory.

# Scene Description
A nine-panel webcomic in simple stick-figure style featuring a character with a blue cap playing poker alone. The comic uses minimalist black line drawings against white backgrounds to tell a story about superstition and bad luck.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"One night, I was having trouble falling asleep."

**Panel 2:**
"So I got up and decided to play a couple of hands of self poker."

**Panel 3:**
"Almost immediately, I dealt myself a royal flush."
"WOO - HOO !"

**Panel 4:**
"ODDS AGAINST 649,739:1"
"TODAY MUST BE MY LUCKY DAY !"

**Panel 5:**
"But then it occurred to me."

**Panel 6:**
"What if I just wasted my entire lifetime's supply of good luck on one useless poker hand? ...alone in my apartment."

**Panel 7:**
"SON OF A..."

**Panel 8:**
[Silent panel showing character in contemplative pose]

**Panel 9:**
"My life has sucked ever since."
"NOOOOOOOOO"
"True story."

# Visual Jokes
- The punchline relies on the character's paranoid superstition that one lucky poker hand exhausted all their lifetime luck
- The progression from celebration to existential dread is conveyed entirely through the character's changing expressions and exclamations

# Scene Description
A simple black and white stick figure comic set outdoors at night with stars visible and a green ground. Two characters lie on their backs looking up at the sky, having a conversation about distant civilizations and space phenomena.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"JUST THINK HOW MANY CIVILIZA-TIONS MUST EXIST OUT THERE IN THE UNIVERSE. THERE MUST BE MILLIONS."

**Panel 2:**
"LOOK, THERE COULD BE A CIVILIZA-TION ON A PLANET ORBITING THAT STAR RIGHT THERE."

**Panel 3:**
"IN FACT, THERE'S PROBABLY AN ALIEN ON THAT PLANET LOOK-ING UP AT THE STARS RIGHT NOW."

**Panel 4:**
"...AND HE'S PROBABLY WONDERING ACTUALLY, THAT'S SUPERNOVA 1987A."
"IF..."
"WHAT?"

**Panel 5:**
"IT'S THE REMNANT OF A HUGE STELLAR EXPLOSION IN THE LARGE MAGELLANIC CLOUD."

**Panel 6:**
"ANY CIVILIZATION IN THAT STAR SYSTEM MUST LIKELY WOULD HAVE BEEN VIOLENTLY OBLITERATED 168,000 YEARS AGO."

**Panel 7:**
(Silent panel with stars)

**Panel 8:**
"COOL!"

**Panel 9 (footer):**
"A little knowledge makes everything a little cooler."

# Visual Jokes
The humor stems from the contrast between the wonder of contemplating distant life and the sudden deflation when one character realizes the star in question is actually the remnant of a supernova that would have destroyed any civilization there 168,000 years ago—yet the other character finds this *more* cool rather than less.

# Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic in simple black-and-white line art showing two characters engaged in an absurdist battle against legislation. The first character wields a broom as a weapon while the second character watches. The final panel features a large wave approaching, rendered with more detailed cross-hatching.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"BRACE YOURSELF.
EVIL IS COMIN."

**Panel 2:**
"YOU SHALL NOT PASS!"

**Panel 3:**
"HEY, WHAT'S GOING ON?"
"I'M FIGHTING EVIL."
"WITH A BROOM?"

**Panel 4:**
"THERE'S A GROWING TIDE OF IMPENDING LEGISLATION THAT UTILIZES OVERLY BROAD LANGUAGE THAT THREATENS TO ERADICATE OUR PRIVACY AND ERODE OUR CIVIL LIBERTIES."

**Panel 5:**
"IT MUST BE FOUGHT.
WITH A BROOM?"

**Panel 6:**
"HERE IT COMES!"

**Panel 7:**
"BACK! BACK! BACK! BAD LEGISLATION!"
(Sound effect: "SWEEP! SWEEP!")

**Panel 8:**
(Silent panel showing characters standing)

**Panel 9:**
"WOO-HOO! VICTORY!"

**Panel 10:**
"SEE? WE MUST ALWAYS FIGHT THE TIDE."

**Panel 11:**
"WITH A BROOM?"
"YOU KEEP SAYING THAT."

**Panel 12:**
(A massive wave approaches)

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the absurd literal interpretation of fighting a "tide" of legislation with a broom—combining the metaphorical language of political resistance with physical action against an actual oncoming wave, which proves futile.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A simple line-drawn webcomic featuring a character with a blue head sitting in bed reading. The art style is minimalist stick-figure based. The sequence shows the character progressing from bed to discovering a dictionary on the floor, with increasingly frustrated expressions. The final panel includes a meta-caption about vocabulary.

## Dialogue and Text (in reading order)

**Panel 1:** (no text)

**Panel 2:** 
"'AMANUENSIS'? WHAT THE HELL DOES THAT MEAN?"

**Panel 3:** 
"THIS ENTIRE PARAGRAPH MAKES NO SENSE UNLESS I KNOW THE DEFINITION OF THAT WORD."

**Panel 4:** 
"MAYBE I CAN FIGURE IT OUT FROM CONTEXT."

**Panel 5:** (no text)

**Panel 6:** 
"NOPE. STILL MAKES NO SENSE."

**Panel 7:** (no text)

**Panel 8:** 
"* SIGH * TOO FAR."

**Panel 9:** (no text)

**Caption (bottom right):** 
"my vocabulary hasn't increased since I was 17 years old"

**Label (on book):** 
"DICTIONARY"

## Visual Jokes
The humor depends on the visual progression: the character's reluctance to consult the dictionary (too far away to reach from bed) is played against the self-aware admission that they haven't bothered learning new words in years, suggesting laziness and stagnation.

# Scene Description
A stick-figure comic set in heaven. A large figure (God) with a halo stands before three smaller figures (angels) in blue. The art style is simple black line drawings on white background with minimal detail.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (Header):** "...in heaven"

**Panel 1:** 
- Caption: "HERE ARE THE FINAL HARDWARE SPECS FOR THE UNIVERSE."

**Panel 2:**
- God: "GET STARTED PROGRAMMING THE LAWS OF PHYSICS IMMEDIATELY."
- Angel: "YES, LORD. WE'LL GET ST... WAIT."

**Panel 3:**
- Angel: "THIS CAN'T BE RIGHT. THERE'S ONLY 64OK OF MEMORY?"

**Panel 4:**
- God: "YEAH, THAT'S RIGHT. THAT OUGHT TO BE ENOUGH FOR ANYTHING."

**Panel 5:**
- God: "BUT THIS WILL SERIOUSLY LIMIT THE ACCURACY WITH WHICH THE PROGRAM CAN SIMULTANEOUSLY CALCULATE THE POSITION AND MOMENTUM OF ANY PARTICLE."

**Panel 6:**
- Angel: "YEAH, SO? YOU GOT A PROBLEM WITH THAT?"
- God: "UH... NO."

**Panel 7:**
- God: "GREAT. GET TO WORK CHOP CHOP!"
- Angel: "YES, LORD."

**Panel 8:**
- Angel: "THIS IS GONNA CONFUSE THE HUMANS."

# Visual Jokes
The comic is a humorous origin story explaining Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle as a result of the universe being programmed with insufficient memory (64K). The joke relies on the absurdist premise that physical laws are constraints caused by hardware limitations rather than fundamental truths.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic depicting a man's journey from conspiracy theorist to would-be villain to disillusioned operative. The art style is simple black-and-white stick figures with minimalist settings (desks, pyramids, corporate buildings). The narrative progresses through multiple panels showing the character's internal monologue as he plans and executes an evil scheme, only to have it thwarted by the revelation that there is no actual conspiracy.

---

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"As I grew older, it became increasingly clear to me that the world was run by evil cabal of elites."
"THEY'RE COLLAPSING OUR ECONOMY AND DESTROYING OUR FUTURE. NOBODY COULD BE THAT INCOMPETENT. IT MUST BE AN EVIL PLAN."
Label on figure: "WALL ST TIMES"

**Panel 2:**
"They lie to us and manipulate us through the mainstream media; they continually dumb us down with TV shows devoid of intellectual content."
"ONLY AN EVIL CONSPIRACY COULD PRODUCE SUCH VAPID CRAPPY TV."

**Panel 3:**
"I concluded that we were all just slaves of the Illuminati."
"ALEX JONES WAS RIGHT."

**Panel 4:**
"I decided that I needed to fight back."
"BUT, I SHALL... BE..."

**Panel 5:**
"FREE!"
[Character jumping with arms raised, "SNAP"]

**Panel 6:**
"But the Illuminati can only be defeated from the inside. I needed to infiltrate their organization."
[Pyramid with eye symbol]

**Panel 7:**
"I got my foot in the door as a lowly mail clerk in one of their evil illuminational corporations."

**Panel 8:**
"I worked my way up the ranks with promotion after promotion..."

**Panel 9:**
"I worked hard and made the right connections."

**Panel 10:**
"I studied their evil ways and learned their techniques."
[Figure reading book titled "THE ART OF THE DEAL"]

**Panel 11:**
"Until... FINALLY... I made it to the top."
[Figure sitting at desk labeled "MA BOSSYPANTS"]

**Panel 12:**
"I WAS the Illuminati."
[Figure standing on pyramid with eye symbol]

**Panel 13:**
"I decided not to waste any time."
"SMITHERS!"
"YES, SIR?"

**Panel 14:**
"I put my plan into effect immediately."
"IT'S TIME TO END THIS."

**Panel 15:**
"IT'S TIME TO END THE CONSPIRACY OF DESTROYING THE ECONOMY AND DUMBING DOWN THE POPULATION."
"THE LINE MUST BE DRAWN HERE!"

**Panel 16:**
"THIS FAR, NO FURTHER!"

**Panel 17:**
[No dialogue, two figures facing each other]

**Panel 18:**
"BUT, SIR, THERE IS NO CONSPIRACY."

**Panel 19:**
"OH... OKAY."

**Panel 20:**
"THAT WILL BE ALL, SMITHERS."
"YES, SIR."

**Panel 21:**
[Two figures in silent panel]

**Panel 22:**
[Two figures in silent panel]

**Panel 23:**
"I decided to weep for humanity."

---

# Visual Jokes/Puns Dependent on Image

1. **The pyramid with eye**: Reference to the "all-seeing eye" conspiracy theory symbol associated with Illuminati mythology.

2. **Silent panels (21-22)**: The absence of dialogue creates an awkward pause that emphasizes the anticlimactic deflation of the character's grand conspiracy-fighting plans.

3. **"MA BOSSYPANTS" desk nameplate**: A play on words suggesting a stereotypical villainous boss character, undermining the serious tone of the infiltration narrative.

4. **Character's progression**: Visual escalation from small stick figure to standing atop the pyramid represents his rise in power, which is immediately rendered meaningless by the revelation.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A nine-panel black and white stick figure comic depicting two characters discussing parallel universes and time travel paradoxes. The art style is minimalist line drawings with simple circular heads and stick bodies. The setting appears to be abstract/non-specific, with a box-like structure visible in the first panel.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"IT WORKED! MY INVENTION WORKS!"

**Panel 2:**
"I HAVE TRAVELED TO A PARALLEL UNIVERSE."

**Panel 3:**
"HEY, IT'S ME!"
"HUH?"

**Panel 4:**
"I'M YOU FROM A PARALLEL UNIVERSE. I'VE INVENTED A PARALLEL UNIVERSE MACHINE."
"WOW, REALLY?"

**Panel 5:**
"THAT'S AMAZING. BUT IT'S TOO BAD YOU'RE STUCK HERE NOW."
"WHAT?"

**Panel 6:**
"Y'SEE, IN THIS UNIVERSE, THE MANY-WORLDS INTERPRETATION OF QUANTUM MECHANICS IS FALSE."

**Panel 7:**
"IN THIS UNIVERSE, YOUR UNIVERSE DOESN'T EXIST."

**Panel 8:**
"OH NO... THAT MEANS THAT... WAIT. WAIT A MINUTE."

**Panel 9:**
"THAT DOESN'T EVEN MAKE SENSE. NEITHER DOES RUSSELL'S PARADOX, YET IT EXISTS."

# Visual Jokes
The humor derives from the logical paradox: if the alternate universe doesn't exist in this universe's physics, then the traveling character shouldn't exist either—creating a self-referential contradiction that mirrors Russell's Paradox, which the character notes at the end despite its apparent irrelevance.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A nine-panel stick figure comic depicting a conversation between three characters. The art style is minimalist black line drawings on white background. The same three figures appear in each panel: one simple circle face on the left, one with horizontal lines (representing stress/exhaustion) in the middle, and one with a circular head on the right. The dialogue progresses as a conversation between them, with speech bubbles indicating who is speaking.

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- Left figure: "THE WIFE AND KIDS ARE DRIVING ME CRAZY."

**Panel 2:**
- Middle figure: "I CAN'T FIND A MOMENT'S PEACE TO MYSELF. IS THAT TOO MUCH TO ASK?"
- Right figure: "I HEAR YA, BRAH."

**Panel 3:**
- Right figure: "AND LITTLE BILLY HAS BEEN ACTING UP IN SCHOOL AGAIN. THAT KID IS NOTHING BUT TROUBLE."

**Panel 4:**
- Left figure: "I WISH I HAD A WIFE AND FAMILY."

**Panel 5:**
- Right figure: "AND MY BOSS IS KILLING ME AT WORK."

**Panel 6:**
- Middle figure: "HE'S ALWAYS ALL UP MY ASS ABOUT THOSE DAMN TPS REPORTS."

**Panel 7:**
- Middle figure: "I WISH I HAD A STEADY JOB."
- Right figure: "AND WHAT'S WITH THIS HOUSING MARKET?"

**Panel 8:**
- Right figure: "I HAVE A $400,000 MORTGAGE ON A HOUSE THAT JUST APPRAISED AT $226,000. THAT'S MESSED UP, BRAH."
- Left figure: "I WISH I COULD AFFORD TO OWN A HOUSE."

**Panel 9:**
- Middle figure: "I DON'T KNOW HOW MUCH MORE OF THIS I CAN TAKE."
- Left figure (looking at middle figure with arms raised): "WHEN YOU'RE YOUNG, NOBODY TELLS YOU HOW MUCH LIFE IS GONNA SUCK."
- Right figure: "I WISH I HAD A LIFE WORTH COMPLAINING ABOUT."

# Visual Jokes
The primary joke is situational irony: each character complains about their circumstances while the character to their left expresses envy for those exact same circumstances. This creates a cyclical commentary on human dissatisfaction—people tend to want what others have rather than appreciating their own situations. The final panel emphasizes this by showing the "grass is always greener" mentality taken to its logical extreme, where even having nothing is preferable to the burden of having responsibilities to complain about.

# Scene Description
A six-panel stick figure comic depicting a conversation between two characters. The first character has a round head, the second wears a hat and carries what appears to be a gas can. The art style is simple black line drawings on white background, typical of webcomic format.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- Character 1: "OH MY GOD ! MY HOUSE ! MY HOUSE IS ON FIRE !"
- [Visual: House icon with flames]

**Panel 2:**
- Character 2: "I'M RUINED."
- Character 1: "HEY, IS THAT YOUR HOUSE ?"

**Panel 3:**
- Character 2: "IT'S ALL GONE."
- Character 1: "IT'S A GOOD THING I JUST TOOK OUT A HOME INSURANCE POLICY ON YOUR HOUSE."

**Panel 4:**
- Character 1: "WHAT ? ON MY HOUSE ?"
- Character 2: "YEAH, JUST LAST NIGHT !"

**Panel 5:**
- Character 2: "YOU BOUGHT INSURANCE ON MY HOUSE ?"
- Character 1: "YEAH, IT'S AN AMAZING COINCIDENCE."

**Panel 6:**
- Character 2: "HOW IS THAT POSSIBLY LEGAL ?!!"
- Character 1: "HEY, IS THAT GREECE ?"

# Visual Joke
The humor relies on the absurdity and criminality of the situation: Character 2 appears to have set Character 1's house on fire, then conveniently took out insurance on it the night before. When questioned about the legality, Character 1 deflects with a non-sequitur question, suggesting guilty knowledge of insurance fraud.

A three-panel webcomic in simple stick-figure style. The setting appears to be a laboratory or workspace with various equipment and containers on a table. Three characters are present: one with a simple round face, one with black hair, and one wearing glasses. The art style is minimalist line drawing typical of meme comics.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

- Character 1: "ANY LUCK TODAY?..."
- Character 2: "NO? OKAY, THANKS."

**Panel 2:**
- Character 2: "NOPE. SET! DIDN'T FIND ANYTHING AGAIN TODAY."

**Panel 3:**
(No dialogue)

**Bottom caption:**
"ASTROBIOLOGIST
the greatest profession"

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the visual progression: the character asking "any luck" is gradually revealed to be leaving/exiting in each panel, eventually leaving the astrobiologist alone. The joke is that astrobiologists search for extraterrestrial life with minimal success, making the profession one of perpetual disappointment—yet the caption ironically calls it "the greatest profession," suggesting the character finds meaning in the futile search itself.

# Scene Description
A simple line-drawn webcomic showing a single character with a blue circular head and black body seated at a desk with a blank canvas/paper. The art style is minimalist with black outlines on white background. The comic uses a 3x3 panel grid format.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"HMMM. WHAT SHOULD I DRAW FOR MY NEXT COMIC?"

**Panel 2:**
"I HAVE BEFORE ME A BLANK CANVAS ON WHICH I CAN CREATE ANY WORLD I WANT."

**Panel 3:**
"MAYBE I'LL JUST DRAW A COMIC ABOUT WHAT I'M DOING AT THIS VERY MOMENT."

**Panel 4:**
"HEH, A COMIC ABOUT NOTHING . . . WITH NO PUNCHLINE. HEHEHER."

**Panel 5:**
"IT'S STRANGE TO THINK THAT I'M DRAWING THIS COMIC IN MY TINY LITTLE ROOM RIGHT NOW. . ."

**Panel 6:**
"AND IN JUST A FEW MINUTES, THOUSANDS OF PEOPLE FROM ACROSS THE PLANET WILL BE READING THIS."

**Panel 7:**
"IT'S LIKE I CAN FEEL THEIR EYES ON THE BACK OF MY HEAD RIGHT NOW."

**Panel 8:**
(Empty panel with no dialogue)

**Panel 9:**
"THIS IS KINDA FREAKIN' ME OUT, MAAAN."

# Visual Jokes
The primary humor is meta-textual: the character becomes increasingly unsettled realizing that readers are literally watching him draw the very comic they're reading, creating a recursive loop of self-aware observation.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A multi-panel webcomic in simple stick-figure style depicting a conversation between a father figure (wearing glasses) and son about academic work and the Riemann Hypothesis. The comic progresses from a home office setting through various confrontational scenes to an ending about why mathematicians aren't popular subjects for movies.

---

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
Father: "WORD ON THE STREET IS YOU'RE WORKING ON THE RIEMANN HYPOTHESIS."

**Panel 2:**
Father: "WHAT YOU SHOULD BE DOING IS WORKING ON YOUR THESIS!"

**Panel 3:**
Father: "SON, YOUR EGO IS WRITING CHECKS YOUR BRAIN CAN'T CASH."

**Panel 4:**
Son: "THE NAMES OF EVERY FIELD'S MEDAL WINNER ARE UP ON THAT PLAQUE."

**Panel 5:**
Father: "DO YOU THINK YOUR NAME WILL BE ON THAT PLAQUE?"
Son: "YES, SIR."

**Panel 6:**
Father: "THAT'S PRETTY ARROGANT CONSIDERING THE COMPANY YOU'RE IN."
Son: "I LIKE THAT IN A MATHEMATICIAN."

**Panel 7:**
[Mathematical equation on blackboard, illegible in image]
Son working intently

**Panel 8:**
Father: "YOU REALLY ARE A COWBOY."

**Panel 9:**
Father: "WHAT'S YOUR PROBLEM, KAZANSKI?"

**Panel 10:**
Father: "YOU'RE EVERYONE'S PROBLEM. DON'T LIKE YOU BECAUSE YOUR PROOFS LACK RIGOR."

**Panel 11:**
Son: "THAT'S RIGHT! ICE. MAN. I AM DANGEROUS."

**Panel 12:**
Father: "THAT'S ACTUALLY NOT WHAT I SAID."

**Panel 13:**
[Multiple figures congratulating]
Crowd: "CONGRATULATIONS ON SOLVING RIEMANN, YOU CAN FLY WITH THE 'ET AL' ANY TIME."

**Panel 14:**
Son: "BULLSHIT! YOU CAN BE MINE."

**Panel 15:**
[Son celebrating]
Multiple voices: "YAY YAY YAY"
[Text on right side: "ETERNAL SALVATION"]

**Final panel:**
"THE END"
Subtitle: "There's a reason why they don't make a lot of movies about mathematicians."

---

# Visual Jokes

1. **Top Gun parody**: The entire comic mimics the "Maverick vs. Iceman" dynamic from *Top Gun*, with the father playing Stinger (the commanding officer), the son as the arrogant pilot, and dialogue referencing "dangerous" flying and "Et al" replacing "Top Gun."

2. **Rigor/Rigorous pun**: The father criticizes proofs lacking "rigor," which sounds like it could relate to "rigor mortis," while the son misinterprets this as a personal attack leading to a fighter-pilot ego response.

3. **Mathematical self-delusion**: The son believes his own hype about solving an unsolved problem (Riemann Hypothesis), leading to a movie-style celebration that parodies inspirational film endings.

4. **The final caption**: The joke is that mathematicians lack the dramatic interpersonal conflict and emotional arcs that make compelling films—they're too focused on abstract problems to create cinematic drama.

comics.title: The Happy Programmer

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A four-panel webcomic featuring a brown-headed character sitting at a desk with a laptop. The art style is simple, minimalist line drawings in black and brown. The comic is titled "The Joy of Programming with Bob Ross" at the bottom right.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"NOW VERY GENTLY PASS THE POINTER TO THE FUNCTION AS AN ARGUMENT."
"VEEERY GENTLY."

**Panel 2:**
"DON'T TRY TO COPY MY CODE EXACTLY.
JUST LET YOUR IMAGINATION GO."

**Panel 3:**
"MAKE LOVE TO THE CODE.
CARESS IT."

**Panel 4:**
"NOW LET'S ADD SOME HAPPY LITTLE BINARY TREES HERE.
JUST FOR FUN."

# Visual Jokes
The comic parodies Bob Ross's calm, encouraging painting instruction style by applying it absurdly to programming. The humor stems from treating code and programming concepts (pointers, functions, binary trees) with the same gentle, artistic reverence Ross used for painting techniques. Panel 3's instruction to "caress" the code while the character appears distressed creates additional comedic contrast.

# Scene Description
A six-panel stick figure webcomic. Two characters are shown throughout: one with a round head holding a sword, and another with a knit cap. The art style is minimalist black line drawings on white background. The setting appears to be domestic (implied couch in final panel).

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- Character 1: "UH, HONEY? WHY ARE YOU HOLDING A SWORD?"

**Panel 2:**
- Character 2: "I AM DEFENDING THE SANCTITY OF OUR MARRIAGE FROM THE GAYS."
- Character 1: "WHAT?!!"

**Panel 3:**
- Character 1: "BELIEVE YOU ME, THEY'RE OUT THERE... PLANNING THE DEMISE OF OUR MARRIAGE."

**Panel 4:**
- Character 2: "LIKE ROVING BANDS OF VISIGOTHS AT THE PERIPHERY OF THE EMPIRE..."

**Panel 5:**
- Character 2: "SOON THE BARBARIANS WILL BE MARCHING OVER THE SEVENTH HILL."

**Panel 6:**
- Character 1: "HAVE YOU LOST YOUR MIND?"

**Panel 7:**
- Character 2: "YOU'LL BE SINGING A DIFFERENT TUNE WHEN OUR MARRIAGE STARTS TO CRUMBLE AFTER THE INVASION."

**Panel 8:**
- Character 1: "THAT'S IT! I CAN'T TAKE THIS ANGUISH ANYMORE. YOU ARE ABSOLUTELY CRAZY!!!"

**Panel 9:**
- Character 2: "I'M SLEEPING ON THE COUCH TONIGHT."
- Character 1: "IT'S BEGUN!"

# Visual Jokes
The joke is the absurdist escalation: the character with the sword conflates same-sex marriage equality with literal military invasion, comparing it to historical barbarian invasions (Visigoths), while his spouse reacts with increasing exasperation. The final panel's punchline is that by sleeping on the couch, he has accidentally created the very marital breakdown he claimed to be preventing—proving his apocalyptic prediction "correct" through his own behavior.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A three-panel comic strip in simple line art style depicting a pilot in a cockpit. The pilot has a round blue head and is seated at an aircraft control panel filled with buttons, switches, and instruments. The comic illustrates the frustration of switching from one complex system (Vim text editor) to another (Emacs).

# Dialogue Transcription

**Panel 1:**
"WHY WON'T THIS STEER LEFT? OH, RIGHT. I PRESSED THE WRONG BUTTONS."

**Panel 2:**
"THIS THING IS A NIGHTMARE TO FLY. THESE AREN'T MY CONTROLS."

**Panel 3:**
"AARGH! I KEEP HITTING ESC BY ACCIDENT. DAMN MUSCLE MEMORY."

**Caption (bottom right):**
"switching from Vim to Emacs
DAY 1"

# Visual Joke
The comic is a metaphor comparing the difficulty of switching between Vim and Emacs text editors to the disorientation of flying an unfamiliar aircraft. The pilot's repeated accidental keypresses and muscle memory issues mirror the learning curve developers face when transitioning between these notoriously complex editors. The cockpit setting emphasizes how overwhelming and foreign the new system feels.

# Scene Description
A comic strip showing two people in bed having a conversation. The art style is simple black and white line drawings with a minimalist aesthetic. The comic is presented in a 3x3 grid format with the final panel showing a wider scene.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"A HEALTHY HUMAN MALE PRODUCES ABOUT 3.2 ml OF EJACULATE PER SEXUAL ENCOUNTER."

**Panel 2:**
"AND THERE ARE ABOUT 79 MILLION SPERM PER ml IN A TYPICAL EJACULATION."

**Panel 3:**
"THAT GIVES US ABOUT 250 MILLION SPERM PER EJACULATION."

**Panel 4:**
"A SINGLE SPERM CONTAINS ROUGHLY 3.2 BILLION DNA BASE PAIRS...EACH BASE PAIR REPRESENTING 2 BITS OF DATA."

**Panel 5:**
"EVEN IF WE SUBTRACT NON-CODING REGIONS AND ASSUME LOSSLESS COMPRESSION BASED ON A COMMON REFERENCE GENOME..."

**Panel 6:**
"...THAT'S STILL A HELLUVALOTTA DATA I'M WASTING EACH TIME WE HAVE SEX."

**Panel 7:**
"YOU'RE NOT SACRIFICING AS MUCH AS I AM FOR THIS RELATIONSHIP."

**Panel 8:**
(Silent panel)

**Panel 9:**
"WELL, THIS DOES SOLVE THE PROBLEM."

# Visual Jokes
The final panel shows one person alone on the couch, implying the relationship has ended—a dark humor resolution to the "sacrificing for the relationship" argument.

# Scene Description
A stick figure comic with five panels arranged vertically. Each panel features a large brown mountain on the left side and stick figures on the right. The art style is simple black line drawings with minimal detail. The comic presents a humorous misinterpretation of a Biblical passage about faith moving mountains.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"VERILY I SAY UNTO YOU, IF YE HAVE FAITH AS A GRAIN OF MUSTARD SEED, YE SHALL SAY UNTO THIS MOUNTAIN, REMOVE HENCE TO YONDER PLACE; AND IT SHALL REMOVE..."

**Panel 2:**
"MOVE THIS MOUNTAIN WITH FAITH? I'M CALLING BULLSHIT. PROVE IT."
"*SIGH* O YE OF LITTLE FAITH."

**Panel 3:**
"COME, MY DISCIPLES. LET US SHOW HIM WHAT TRUE FAITH LOOKS LIKE."

**Panel 4:**
"OH, OKAY."

**Panel 5:**
"APPARENTLY I MISUNDERSTOOD THE WORD 'FAITH'."

**Bottom caption:**
"JESUS - the most misunderstood historical figure of all time"

# Visual Joke
The final panel reveals the "disciples" have literally moved the mountain using heavy machinery (depicted as silhouettes of bulldozers and trucks), defeating the spiritual purpose of the original lesson about faith through a literal, mechanical interpretation.

# Scene Description

This is a two-panel webcomic in simple black and white line art style showing the OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model layers as a metaphorical comparison between two people communicating. The left panel shows a woman, and the right panel shows a man. Below each stack of seven labeled layers are illustrations depicting progressively more abstract representations of communication, ending with the two figures exchanging a heart symbol at the bottom.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Left Column (top to bottom):**
- LAYER 1 PHYSICAL
- LAYER 2 DATA LINK
- LAYER 3 NETWORK
- LAYER 4 TRANSPORT
- LAYER 5 SESSION
- LAYER 6 PRESENTATION
- LAYER 7 APPLICATION

**Right Column (top to bottom):**
- LAYER 1 PHYSICAL
- LAYER 2 DATA LINK
- LAYER 3 NETWORK
- LAYER 4 TRANSPORT
- LAYER 5 SESSION
- LAYER 6 PRESENTATION
- LAYER 7 APPLICATION

**Bottom panel labels:**
- LAYER 3
- LAYER 7
- LAYER 4
- LAYER 1

# Visual Jokes/Puns

The comic illustrates how human romantic communication mirrors the OSI model: complex digital/software interactions (layers 5-7) are encoded down through physical transmission (layers 1-2) to reach another person, who must decode them back up through the layers. The final result—two people in love—shows that despite all the technical "layers" of communication (words, tone, body language, physical proximity), the ultimate message is simple: a heart.

comics.comment: I have also intentionally included fatal mistakes in the equations to make it more realistic. Please see the errata page on our website.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A humorous webcomic contrasting a simple, child-friendly explanation of basic arithmetic with an absurdly complex mathematical derivation. The top half features simple line-drawn rabbits and plain language. The bottom half abruptly shifts to advanced mathematical notation and multi-line equations. The visual joke is the extreme disconnect between the promised "simple easy-to-understand example" and the highly technical content that follows.

---

# Text Transcription

**Top Section:**

Suppose you have one rabbit.

Now suppose someone gives you one more rabbit.

Now, if you count your rabbits, you have two rabbits. So one rabbit plus one rabbit equals two rabbits. So one plus one equals two.

1 + 1 = 2

And that is how arithmetic is done.

Now that you understand the basic idea behind arithmetic, let's take a look at a simple easy-to-understand example that puts into practice what we just learned.

**"Try It Out Example 1.7"** [in box]

**Bottom Section (Mathematical Equations):**

log Π(N) = (N + 1/2) log N − N + A − ∫[N to ∞] B₁(x)dx/x,     A = 1 + ∫[1 to ∞] B₁(x)dx/x

log Π(s) = (s + 1/2) log s − s + A − ∫[0 to ∞] B₁(t)dt/(t + s)

log Π(s) = lim[n→∞] [s log(N + 1) + Σ[n=1 to N] log n − Σ[n=1 to N] log(s + n)]

= lim[n→∞] [s log(N + 1) + ∫[1 to N] log x dx − 1/2 log N + ∫[N to 1] B₁(x)dx/x

− ∫[1 to N] log(s + x)dx − 1/2[log(s + 1) + log(s + N)]

− ∫[1 to N] B₁(x)dx/(s + x)]

= lim[n→∞] [s log(N + 1) + N log N − N + 1 + 1/2 log N + ∫[1 to N] B₁(x)dx/x

− (s + N)log(s + N) + (s + N) + (s + 1)log(s + 1)

− (s + 1) − 1/2 log(s + 1) − 1/2 log(s + N) − ∫[1 to N] B₁(x)dx/(s + x)]

= (s + 1/2) log(s + 1) + ∫[1 to ∞] B₁(x)dx/x − ∫[1 to ∞] B₁(x)dx/(s + x)

+ lim[n→∞] [s log(N + 1) + (N + 1/2) log N

− (s + N + 1/2) log(s + N)]

= (s + 1/2) log(s + 1) + (A − 1) − ∫[1 to ∞] B₁(x)dx/(s + x)

+ lim[n→∞] [s log(N + 1)/(1 + s/N) + (N + 1/2) log(1 + s/N)]

---

# Visual Jokes
The primary visual joke is the caption at the bottom: **"If the authors of computer programming books wrote arithmetic textbooks..."** This punchline reveals that the webcomic is satirizing technical computer science and mathematics texts that promise simple explanations but immediately launch into incomprehensibly advanced material.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A six-panel comic strip in pixel art style featuring a personified moon character being hunted. The panels show urban nighttime settings with buildings, silhouettes of hunters, and the moon character hiding and fleeing. The art uses a limited color palette of blues, grays, browns, blacks, and whites.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"I am the hunted.
Such is my fate in life.
And my only crime?
... that I exist."

**Panel 2:**
"I grow weary of this game..."

**Panel 3:**
"Hiding like a criminal
in the dark of night."

**Panel 4:**
"My hunters are
fearsome predators."

**Panel 5:**
"And they're getting close.
... too close."
[Label on door: "125 GeV"]

**Panel 6:**
"...clever humans."

## Visual Jokes
The "125 GeV" label on the door is a reference to the Higgs boson discovery at CERN (125 GeV is the measured mass). The comic depicts scientists hunting the moon like a celestial particle, playing on the absurdist comparison between particle physics experiments and literal hunting.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description

This is a webcomic adaptation of Walt Whitman's poem "Sea-Drift" (1871), presented as a series of illustrated panels with a minimalist art style. The comic features simple line drawings of a figure in green (presumably representing the poet or narrator) in various maritime settings—on ships, at sea, reading, and contemplating the ocean. The panels alternate between colored illustrations and black backgrounds with white text. The visual style is stark and contemplative, with an emphasis on vast seascapes and the solitary figure against expansive horizons.

---

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"In cabin'd ships at sea,"

**Panel 2:**
"The boundless blue on every side expanding,"

**Panel 3:**
"With whistling winds and music of the waves, the large imperious waves,"

**Large Panel (black background):**
"Or some lone bark buoy'd on the dense marine,
Where joyous full of faith, spreading white sails,

She cleaves the ether mid the sparkle and the foam of day, or
under many a star at night,"

**Panel 5:**
"By sailors young and old haply will I, a reminiscence of the land, be read,"

**Panel 6:**
"In full rapport at last."

**Large text section (italicized):**
"Here are our thoughts, voyagers' thoughts,
Here not the land, from land, alone appears, may then by them be said,
The sky o'eraches here, we feel the undulating deck beneath our feet,
We feel the long pulsation, ebb and flow of endless motion,
The tones of unseen mystery, the vague and vast suggestions of the briny world, the liquid-flowing syllables,
The perfume, the faint creaking of the cordage, the melancholy rhythm,
The boundless vista and the horizon far and dim are all here,
And this is ocean's poem."

**Large Panel (black background with earth/satellite image):**
"Then falter not O book, fulfil your destiny,"

"You not a reminiscence of the land alone,"

**Panel (grey background with ship's wheel):**
"You too as a lone bark cleaving the ether, purpos'd I know not whether, yet ever full of faith,"

**Black panel:**
"Consort to every ship that sails, sail you!
Bear forth to them fold'd my love, (dear mariners, for you I fold it here in every leaf)"

**Panel with close-up of book:**
"Speed on my book! spread your white sails my little bark athwart the imperious waves,"

**Final panels:**
"Chant on, sail on,
bear o'er the boundless blue from me to every sea,"

"This song for mariners and all their ships.
---Walt Whitman
1871"

---

# Visual Jokes/Details Dependent on Image

1. **The figure in green** appears consistently throughout as a visual anchor, representing the poet/narrator and creating continuity across the maritime scenes.

2. **Scale contrast**: The tiny figure is frequently shown against vast seascapes and celestial bodies (satellites, moon, sun), emphasizing the sublime smallness of humanity against nature.

3. **The book itself becomes a "ship"**: In the lower panels, the book is compared to a vessel, with visual representation of it as an object in the vastness of space/ocean, making the metaphor literal and visual.

4. **Circular/spiral imagery** in the ship's wheel panel mirrors the cyclical nature of voyages and reading.

5. **The satellite imagery** modernizes Whitman's 19th-century poem by showing contemporary maritime/space exploration, subtly updating the work's relevance.

# Scene Description
A three-panel webcomic in simple stick-figure style. The first two panels show an interview setting with a recruiter behind a desk speaking to a job candidate. The third panel shifts to a humorous illustration of an astronaut floating in space above Earth.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"WE'VE REVIEWED YOUR CV. UNFORTUNATELY, YOU'RE JUST NOT GOOGLE MATERIAL."

**Panel 2:**
"PERHAPS EMPLOYMENT AT GOOGLE IS JUST A BIT... UM ...AMBITIOUS" FOR YOU."

**Panel 3:**
"DAMMIT."

# Visual Joke
The humor relies on the phrase "Google material" being reinterpreted literally in the final panel. The rejected candidate is shown as an astronaut floating in space, suggesting they've taken the criticism to heart and are now aiming for employment at actual Google—as in the search engine's name origin, implying they need to work at a level as distant and unreachable as space itself. It's a play on the impossibly high standards suggested by the recruiter.

# Scene Description
A six-panel webcomic in simple stick-figure style with a blue character (distinguished by a filled blue circle for a head) and white stick figures. The comic contrasts the blue character's violent intrusive thoughts about harming others with the reality of their restrained behavior. Settings include a crowded street, an office with a desk, and a car interior.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"LOOK HOW EASILY I COULD JUST PUSH THIS GUY ONTO THE STREET INTO TRAFFIC."

**Panel 2:**
"SO BLINDLY TRUSTING OF STRANGERS."

**Panel 3:**
"IT'S AMAZING HOW THE DAILY WORKINGS OF SOCIETY SO FRAGILY DEPEND UPON THE ASSUMPTION OF THE NONCRAZINESS OF OTHERS."

**Panel 4:**
"I COULD SO EASILY SWERVE ONTO THE OTHER SIDE OF THE STREET INTO THE ONCOMING CARS."

**Panel 5:**
"JUST LOOK AT ED SITTING AT HIS DESK ALL INNOCENTLY."

**Panel 6:**
"I COULD PROBABLY HURY THAI KICK HIM IN THE HEAD AND THROW HIM OUT THE WINDOW RIGHT NOW."

**Bottom caption:**
"By the beneficence of others, I was not murdered 512 times today."

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the stark contrast between the blue character's graphic violent intrusive thoughts and their complete inaction—emphasizing how society functions despite people constantly having impulses they don't act on.

# Cyanide & Happiness Comic Transcription

## Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic in black, blue, and red ink showing an office worker's elaborate plan to leave work early without encountering their boss. The comic is divided into three main sections: three introductory panels explaining the situation, two floor plan diagrams (1st and 4th floors), and a detailed "Operation Jailbreak" sequence showing the worker's escape attempt in 8 sequential panels.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Top panels:**
1. "I've finished my work and it's not 5 o'clock yet."
2. "If I want to leave early without running into my boss, I have to plan carefully."
3. "I know that my boss is giving a presentation in room 103 until 4:45."

**Floor plan labels (1st floor):**
- "room 103"
- "stairwell 1"
- "Afterwards, she will most likely visit Dr. Erickson's lab for a couple of minutes."
- "Dr. Erickson's lab"
- "Then she'll come back up to the 4th floor via stairwell 1."

**Floor plan labels (4th floor):**
- "stairwell 2"
- "So I'll have a brief window during which I'll have to sprint down the hallway."
- "And make my way to stairwell 2."
- "my desk"
- "boss' office"

**Operation Jailbreak sequence panels:**
- Panel 1: "Operation Jailbreak is in effect."
- Panel 2: (no dialogue)
- Panel 3: (no dialogue)
- Panel 4: (numbered "4," no dialogue)
- Panel 5: "HEY, WHERE ARE YOU GOING?" / "OH, uh...I WAS JUST...uh...Y'know..."
- Panel 6: (no dialogue)
- Panel 7: "OKAY, HAVE A GREAT WEEKEND."
- Panel 8: (numbered "2," no dialogue)
- Final panel: "My boss is an evil wizard."

## Visual Jokes
- The elaborate planning sequence contrasts darkly with the anticlimactic discovery that the boss was simply heading in the opposite direction
- The numbered panels (4, 3, 2) suggest a countdown structure that subverts expectation
- "My boss is an evil wizard" is a humorous exaggeration of the boss's seemingly supernatural ability to appear at exactly the wrong moment, despite the worker's careful planning

A three-panel minimalist stick-figure webcomic. The art style features simple line drawings with a stick figure wearing a blue cap and a large, rounded creature labeled "INTERNET." The setting is plain white background with black outlines.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

- Label: "INTERNET"

**Panel 2:**
- Label: "INTERNET"

**Panel 3:**
- Dialogue: "HEY, AMUSE ME."
- Label: "INTERNET"
- Caption (bottom): "Sometimes it's just not in the mood."

# Visual Jokes/Puns
The humor relies on the visual contrast between the stick figure's demanding posture (hands on hips, commanding tone) and the Internet's inert, blob-like appearance across all three panels. The joke is that despite being asked to "amuse," the Internet remains completely unchanged and unresponsive—a commentary on internet reliability or the user's dependence on it for entertainment.

# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure comic with a programmer character (depicted with basic circles and lines) sitting at a computer. The comic uses a classic XKCD-style minimalist art style with black lines on white background. The scene progresses through 9 panels showing an interaction between a programmer and another character.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"SMART POINTERS ARE FOR WIMPS. REAL PROGRAMMERS GO RAW."

**Panel 2:**
"REAL PROGRAMMERS ALWAYS KEEP TOTAL CONTROL OF MEMORY."

**Panel 3:**
"IN FACT, REAL PROGRAMMERS DON'T EVEN HAVE TO CHECK FOR BAD MEMORY ALLOCATIONS."

**Panel 4:**
"THAT'S RIGHT. I AM THE BAD BOY OF PROGRAMMING. I'M GONNA LIVE FAST, DIE EARLY, AND CODE CLOSE TO THE METAL."

**Panel 5:**
"YOU!!"
[Question mark from other character]

**Panel 6:**
"THE CODING STANDARDS MANUAL. READ IT! LEARN IT! LOVE IT!"

**Panel 7:**
"WE'RE TIRED OF CORRECTING YOUR MISTAKES. YOU'RE SLOWING DOWN THE ENTIRE TEAM."

**Panel 8:**
"GET YOUR ACT TOGETHER."

**Panel 9:**
"WE BAD BOYS ARE OFTEN MISUNDERSTOOD."
[Text on right: "SIGH" and "*SMASH*"]

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the programmer's self-aggrandizing attitude about "raw" memory management being immediately deflated by workplace reality—he's not a rebel coder, just someone making mistakes that burden his team.

# Webcomic Transcription: "An Adventure in the Wilderness"

## Scene Description
A simple stick-figure comic in black ink with blue-colored protagonist. The art style is minimalist line drawings with occasional detailed backgrounds. The narrative follows a character's childhood adventure into the wilderness and their reflective journey home, ending with them contemplating their experience in bed at night.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"One day, when I was 4 years old, I decided that I wanted to go on an adventure in the wilderness."

**Panel 2:**
"The first order of business was to procure a supply of food."

**Panel 3:**
"One can never go wrong with cheese curls."

**Panel 4:**
"With a sandwich bag full of life-sustaining cheese curls in hand, I set out into the unfamiliar."

**Panel 5:**
"Immediately, I chanced upon what appeared to be an abandoned trail, possibly built by an ancient race long forgotten."

**Panel 6:**
"Just imagine the adventures that occurred along this trail... lost in the sands of time."

**Panel 7:**
"It wasn't long before the elements mercilessly tried to force my return home."

**Panel 8:**
"Perhaps the gods have been angered by my brazen attempt to explore forbidden lands."

**Panel 9:**
"I soldiered on. Many perils were encountered and overcome. Eventually, I reached the end of the known world."

**Panel 10 (Map):**
- "SCARY FOREST"
- "END OF THE KNOWN WORLD"
- "MY HOUSE"
- "START"

**Panel 11:**
"Beyond this place be mysteries begging to be explored."

**Panel 12:**
"But first I needed rest."

**Panel 13:**
"I found a dry patch of ground under an old board."

**Panel 14-16:**
"And I feasted on the good food." [Repeated panels showing character eating with "CHOMP" sound effects]

**Panel 17:**
"There were still many uncharted lands awaiting discovery..."

**Panel 18:**
"...but the sun was sinking fast."

**Panel 19:**
"And the wilderness can be an unforgiving place at night." [Dark panel with vertical lines suggesting night/danger]

**Panel 20:**
"Reluctantly, I started my long trek back home."

**Panel 21:**
[Silhouette panels]

**Panel 22:**
"Safe at home again. Cold and tired."

**Panel 23:**
"Sleep did not come easy that night."

**Panel 24:**
"For I had briefly tasted the sweet nectar of independence."

**Panel 25:**
"And it lingered."

## Visual Jokes/Elements Dependent on Image

1. **The "end of the known world"** - The map shows the character only walked to the edge of their suburban neighborhood/backyard, which is humorous in contrast to their epic language describing it.

2. **Scale contrast** - The character's perception of a small neighborhood trail as an "ancient forgotten trail" is emphasized by childlike wonder against mundane reality.

3. **Silhouette panels** - The transition home is shown in increasingly dark silhouettes, emphasizing the ominous nature of the darkening wilderness.

4. **Final panel** - A moon visible through a window suggests the adventure continues in the character's mind despite being safely home.

# Comic Transcription

## Scene Description
A three-panel stick figure comic drawn in simple black lines. The first two panels show two groups of stick figures facing each other across a border, apparently in a tense standoff. The final panel depicts both groups engaged in an elaborate, chaotic battle scene with musical notes, set against a background suggesting rain or chaos. The art style is minimalist XKCD-style stick figures.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
- Left figure: "REMOVE YOUR ARMY FROM MY LAND."
- Right figure: "I LIKE YOUR LAND. I THINK WE'LL STAY."

**Panel 2:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 3:**
- Left figure: "HMM, IF ONLY THERE WAS A WAY TO SATIATE THE BLOODLUST INHERENT IN OUR HUMAN NATURE IN A MANNER THAT WILL EXORCISE OUR PETTY PAROCHIAL PREJUDICES WHILE MINIMIZING CASUALTIES."

**Bottom panel (battle scene):**
- Text at bottom: "Are you ready for some proxy tribal warfare?!!"
- Musical notes (♪) appear throughout the scene

## Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the contrast between the first panel's diplomatic/serious territorial dispute and the final panel's absurd, celebratory depiction of both armies dancing together in what appears to be a joyful musical battle, complete with rain/chaos symbols and upbeat musical notes—suggesting they've resolved their conflict through spontaneous synchronized dancing rather than actual combat.

# Scene Description
A classroom discussion webcomic in simple black and white stick-figure style. A female student with shoulder-length hair sits among rows of other students (shown as simple circles). The comic follows an escalating debate about a poem's meaning, progressing from literary interpretation to increasingly absurd conspiracy theories, ending with the student lying in bed at night, overwhelmed.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"CAN ANYONE TELL ME WHAT THIS POEM MEANS?"

**Panel 2:**
(Silent)

**Panel 3:**
"YES, SEAN?"

**Panel 4:**
"I BELIEVE THAT THE FLOWERS IN THIS POEM REPRESENT THE BLOSSOMING OF INNOCENCE IN THE GROWING FACE OF THE DICHOTOMOUS RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN MAN AND NATURE."

**Panel 5:**
"THAT IS THE STUPIDEST THING I'VE EVER HEARD."

**Panel 6:**
"IT'S LIKE HE SIMPLY BLURTED OUT THE FIRST SEMI-SEMANTICALLY-CORRECT SENTENCE THAT POPPED INTO HIS HEAD, COMPLETELY IGNORING THE FACT THAT IT CONVEYED ABSOLUTELY NOTHING MEANINGFUL."

**Panel 7:**
"HOW MANY COCKTAIL PARTIES HAVE I HAD TO SUFFER THROUGH BY GETTING CAUGHT HAVING TO TALK TO BLOWHARD LIKE HIM?"

**Panel 8:**
"IN FACT, HOW MANY INTERNATIONAL INCIDENTS THROUGHOUT HISTORY HAVE BEEN PRECIPITATED BY CARELESS CHATTERBOXES LIKE HIM?"

**Panel 9:**
"MANY POWERFUL 'INSTITUTIONALIZED ORGANIZATIONS' OFTEN EMPLOY NON-THINKING MOUTHPIECES LIKE HIM TO PROPAGATE THEIR MESSAGES OF HATE AND INTOLERANCE."

**Panel 10:**
"FOR CENTURIES, GOVERNMENTS AND TYRANTS HAVE MANIPULATED USEFUL IDIOTS LIKE HIM TO HELP JUSTIFY TERRORISM, WAR, AND GENOCIDE."

**Panel 11:**
"I MUST PUT A STOP TO THIS NOW BEFORE THIS MONSTER IS RELEASED INTO THE WILD."

**Panel 12:**
"BUT HE'S THE ONLY STUDENT WILLING TO CONTRIBUTE ANYTHING TO THIS DISCUSSION. IF I QUASH HIS ENTHUSIASM NOW, THIS IS GONNA BE ONE HELL OF A BORING CLASS FOR THE REST OF THE SEMESTER."

**Panel 13:**
"HORNS OF A DILEMMA."

**Panel 14:**
(Silent - teacher asking follow-up)
"THAT'S A VERY INTERESTING INSIGHT, SEAN. COULD YOU PLEASE ELABORATE?"

**Panel 15:**
(Silent - student looking concerned)

**Panel 16:**
"I AM ONE SMALL COG IN THE VAST MACHINERY OF EVERYTHING WRONG WITH THIS WORLD."

# Visual Jokes
- The final panels show the student lying in bed at night in darkness, having spiraled from a simple classroom discussion into existential despair and self-awareness about complicity in societal problems.
- The contrast between the escalating internal monologue (which the classroom cannot hear) and the teacher's polite, encouraging responses creates situational comedy.

# Comic Transcription

## Scene Description
A minimalist stick-figure webcomic with 9 panels arranged in a 3x3 grid, plus one additional panel below. The art style features simple circle faces with minimal details against white backgrounds. A single character (with a slightly more expressive face) argues with three blank-faced listeners in most panels. The final panel shows physical action with multiple figures and "Scientists baffled" caption.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"I HAVEN'T EATEN RED MEAT IN 1697 DAYS!"

**Panel 2:**
"I HAVEN'T EATEN POULTRY IN 1588 DAYS AND...
HEY, NOBODY CARES!"

**Panel 3:**
"WHY DO YOU KEEP REMINDING US EVERYDAY THAT YOU'RE VEGETARIAN?"

**Panel 4:**
"NOBODY CARES WHAT YOU ATE LAST NIGHT!"

**Panel 5:**
"NOBODY CARES THAT YOU DON'T EAT STEAK, NOBODY CARES THAT YOU DON'T EAT CHICKEN!"

**Panel 6:**
"AND NOBODY CARES THAT YOU DON'T EAT FISH OR...
FISH?"

**Panel 7:**
"I HAVEN'T EATEN FISH IN 1027 DAYS."

**Panel 8:**
[Silent panel - no text]

**Panel 9 (Inset box):**
"STUDY: On average, meat-eaters live five years longer than vegetarians."

**Final Panel (Below):**
[Shows figures beating the speaker]
"Scientists baffled."

## Visual Jokes
The humor depends on the irony shown in the image: the vegetarian's smug bragging about not eating various meats gets increasingly ignored, until a scientific study suggesting meat-eaters live longer is revealed—prompting the other characters to physically attack him, leaving scientists puzzled by the outcome.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A six-panel comic in simple black and white line art featuring a character with glasses and a round head (appearing to be a student or academic) standing at a podium or lectern, addressing an audience of simple circular heads shown from behind. The style is minimalist and humorous, with the visual comedy relying on the character's changing expressions and the implied reactions of the silent audience.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

"THUS, WE CAN EASILY CALCULATE THE ENERGY EIGENVALUES FOR THIS PROBLEM."

"LET'S PAUSE TO PONDER JUST HOW REMARKABLE AND MIND-BOGGLINGLY BIZARRE QUANTUM PHYSICS REALLY IS."

**Panel 4:**
[Silent panel - character and audience shown with no dialogue]

**Panel 5:**
[Silent panel - character and audience shown with no dialogue]

## Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the extended silence in panels 4-5, suggesting the audience's lack of response or engagement, followed by the character enthusiastically answering their own rhetorical question. The contrast between the professor's attempt at inspiring awe and the implied indifference of the students creates the comic effect.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A four-panel comic strip in black and white line art showing two parallel scenarios. Each scenario begins with a speaker at a podium addressing a crowd of identical round-headed figures. The art style is simple, minimalist stick figures. The first column shows one speaker and scenario, the second column shows an identical setup with a different speaker. The final panels show newspaper headlines. The overall tone is satirical.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Top Left Panel:**
"... SO, AS WE DEAL WITH THESE LONG-TERM OPPORTUNITIES TO PROVIDE A MORE ENGAGING GAMING EXPERIENCE, WE ARE ACUTELY AWARE OF THE FRUSTRATION EXPERIENCED BY OUR CUSTOMERS AND ..."

[Crowd member interjects:] "HEY! GABEN JUST TOUCHED HIS BEARD 3 TIMES!"

**Top Right Panel:**
"... SO, AS WE ASSESS THE BENEFITS AND COSTS OF ALTERNATIVE POLICY APPROACHES, WE MUST NOT LOSE SIGHT OF THE DAUNTING ECONOMIC CHALLENGES THAT CONFRONT OUR NATION AND ..."

[Crowd member interjects:] "HEY! BERNANKE JUST TOUCHED HIS BEARD 3 TIMES!"

**Middle Left Panel:**
[Speaker at podium with crowd]
"IT'S A SIGN!"
"YEAH"
"THREE! THREE! THREE!"

**Middle Right Panel:**
[Identical scene with different speaker and crowd]
"IT'S A SIGN!"
"YEAH"
"THREE! THREE! THREE!"

**Bottom Left Panel:**
Newspaper headline: "NEWS - HL3 CONFIRMED"

**Bottom Right Panel:**
Newspaper headline: "NEWS - QE3 CONFIRMED"

**Caption Below Comic:**
"The parallels are uncanny."

## Visual Jokes
The comic's humor relies on parallel structure: both panels depict speakers (Gabe Newell of Valve and Federal Reserve Chair Ben Bernanke) inadvertently "confirming" information through coincidental beard touches. The left side references the gaming community's obsession with Half-Life 3 (HL3), while the right side parodies economic policy discussion (QE3 = Quantitative Easing 3). The joke is that crowds will find significance in meaningless gestures, with each side interpreting the same action through their own cultural/professional lens.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A minimalist stick-figure comic in black ink showing a progression narrative. The top panels depict a character repeatedly attempting to climb a rope with a hammer. The middle panels show two characters together, then one alone at the edge of a cliff with hearts floating above their head. The final large panel shows the character who fell at the bottom of the cliff surrounded by friends holding a "FRIEND ZONE" sign, with a cactus and desert landscape visible. The art style is simple stick figures with minimal detail.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)
- Bottom caption: "...but it can be done."
- Sign in final panel: "FRIEND ZONE"

## Visual Jokes
The primary visual joke is the ironic juxtaposition of the caption "...but it can be done" with the scene showing the character has successfully climbed the cliff/rope to reach their love interest, only to end up in the "friend zone" at the bottom of a vast cliff—suggesting that while the physical task was accomplished, the romantic goal was not. The metaphorical use of the "friend zone" as an actual geographic location at the bottom of a cliff is the core visual pun.

comics.transcript:
# Webcomic Transcription: "Lucky > Good"

## Scene Description
A stick-figure comic depicting a race between a person and a rabbit/hare, illustrated in simple black ink line drawings. The narrative progresses through multiple panels showing the competitor's journey across various terrains and obstacles, culminating in three alternate endings determined by different chance events. The art style is minimalist stick-figure format with occasional shading for emphasis.

## Text Transcription

**Panel 1 (top-left):** "BANG"

**Panel 2 (top-right):** "START"

**Panel 7 (middle-left, dialogue):** ""

**Panel 14 (lower section, left panel):** "ZAP"

**Panel 16 (bottom-right, final panel):** "FINISH"

**Caption (very bottom of comic):** "lucky > good"

## Visual Jokes and Image-Dependent Humor

1. **The Race Setup:** A person fires a starting gun while a turtle slowly moves forward and a rabbit dashes away—establishing an absurd race dynamic.

2. **Environmental Obstacles:** The rabbit encounters increasingly surreal challenges (flying away, being struck by lightning, caught in rain), while the turtle steadily progresses.

3. **Luck vs. Skill:** The three final panels show different outcomes based on random events (lightning strike, rain) rather than the competitors' abilities, with the rabbit being consistently unlucky despite superior speed.

4. **The Punchline:** The turtle wins not through persistence (the original fable's moral) but through pure luck while the faster rabbit suffers misfortune—inverting the traditional Tortoise and Hare narrative to suggest luck matters more than inherent ability.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A stick-figure comic in black and white, featuring two human characters and a boxy robot character with an antenna. The art style is minimalist line drawings. The setting is undefined/abstract across 10 panels arranged in a 2x5 grid.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- "Hey, is that a robot?"

**Panel 2:**
- "Good boy."
- "CAT"

**Panel 3:**
- "Not just any robot. THIS is the current state-of-the-art in artificial intelligence."

**Panel 4:**
- "We trained a 9-layered locally connected sparse autoencoder with pooling and local contrast normalization on a dataset of 10 million images. It was trained for 3 days on a cluster of 1000 machines comprising 16,000 cores."

**Panel 5:**
- "It can now recognize 22,000 different object categories with 15.8% accuracy."

**Panel 6:**
- "15.8%?"
- "Impressive, nuh?"
- "CAT"

**Panel 7:**
- "Impressive? Didn't you AI guys program a machine that defeated a world chess champion way back in 1997?"

**Panel 8:**
- "And didn't you AI guys build a computer system that kicked the respective asses of the two best Jeopardy! champions of all time?"

**Panel 9:**
- "What happened to 'HAL 9000 by 2001'? Where are our Series 800 Terminator robots?"

**Panel 10:**
- "You call this progress?!!"

**Panel 11:**
- (Silent panel)

**Panel 12:**
- "Sorry we annoyed you with our awesomeness."
- "CAT"

## Visual Jokes
- The robot consistently identifies a cat as "CAT" with 100% accuracy, despite only achieving 15.8% accuracy on 22,000 object categories—a humorous commentary on AI's narrow capabilities versus human expectations of broad intelligence.
- The human's frustration escalates as he contrasts the robot's mediocre performance with AI's historical achievements (Deep Blue defeating Kasparov, IBM Watson winning Jeopardy!), highlighting the gap between specialized AI success and general intelligence.

# Scene Description
A nine-panel webcomic in black and white stick-figure style showing two characters: one with dark hair at a laptop and one with glasses. The humor centers on the disconnect between theoretical cryptographic success and practical computational reality.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"Hey, check it out. I think I figured out how to crack 2048-bit RSA encryption."

**Panel 2:**
"I can do it using the general number field sieve."

**Panel 3:**
"On that crappy laptop? It would literally take you quadrillions of years!"

**Panel 4:**
"Ahh, but y'see, I'm pretty sure that I'll perfect my time machine within 20 years."

**Panel 5:**
"Then I can just add a function that sends the result back in time after the calculations finish."

**Panel 6:**
"From our point of view, it'll be like we receive the answer as soon as the calculations start. Observe."
*[Shows "START" label pointing to laptop]*

**Panel 7:**
[Dark-haired character waits at laptop]

**Panel 8:**
"Hmmm. Nothing."

**Panel 9:**
[Empty panel with waiting character]

**Panel 10:**
"I was sure that that was going to work."

**Panel 11:**
"I'll bet the universe ended before the calculations finished."

**Panel 12:**
"Y'know, if it wasn't for real life, you'd be like a genius or something."
*[Other character responds]*
"Damn universe!"

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the visual passage of time through repetitive panels showing no results, emphasizing the absurdity of the theoretical solution and the character's increasingly desperate rationalizations for its failure.

# Scene Description
Three identical panels showing a simple stick figure character at a desk or table, surrounded by equations and formulas written on the board/wall behind them. The art style is minimalist line-drawn, typical of webcomic format. The character appears increasingly frustrated or overwhelmed across the three panels, with the final panel showing them appearing to give up or collapse.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1, 2, and 3 (equations written on board):**
- E = mc²
- E = m²c =
- E = m·c
- E = m·lose = Emkd
- E = c/m = Emcml
- E = √mc = E = mc²
- E = m%c

**Caption (bottom right, below all three panels):**
"The best way to have a good idea is to have a lot of ideas.
--- Linus Pauling"

# Visual Joke
The humor depends on the progression: the character generates increasingly absurd and nonsensical variations of Einstein's famous equation E=mc², eventually degrading into "E = m·lose" and other gibberish. Combined with the quote about having many ideas to get good ones, the visual joke is that most of the character's "ideas" are completely wrong—illustrating the quote literally through failed scientific equations.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A webcomic parody of Nietzsche's "Thus Spoke Zarathustra" rendered in simple stick-figure and minimalist art style. The comic depicts a bearded philosopher descending a mountain to meet a young woman, attempting to teach her about "The Superman" philosophy, but being repeatedly interrupted and distracted by modern technology and social media.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"When Zarathustra was thirty years old, he left his home and the lake of his home, and went into the mountains."

**Panel 2:**
"There he enjoyed his spirit and solitude, and for ten years did not weary of it."

**Panel 3:**
"But at last his heart changed, and rising one morning with the rosy dawn, he went before the sun, and spake thus unto it:"

**Panel 4:**
"Lol! I am weary of my wisdom, like the bee that hath gathered too much honey; I need hands outstretched to take it."

**Panel 5:**
"Zarathustra went down the mountain alone and there suddenly stood before him a young woman."

**Panel 6:**
"And to the young woman Zarathustra spake thus:
I TEACH YOU THE SUPERMAN."
Woman: "What?"

**Panel 7:**
Man: "Man is something that is to be surpassed. What have ye done to surpass man?"
Woman: "ok, glass, record a video of this crazy man."

**Panel 8:**
Man: "The Superman is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Superman SHALL BE the meaning of the earth!"
Woman: "ok, glass, upload video to YouTube."

**Panel 9:**
Man: "Are you talking to your glasses?"
Woman: "Yeah. It just took a video of you and uploaded it to the internet. See?"
Man: "Wh-what kind of phone is that?"

**Panel 10:**
Man: "What is all this crazy technology? How did you..."
Woman: "shhh, I'm video chatting with my friends."

**Panel 11:**
(Silent panel - man appears to be waiting)

**Panel 12:**
Man: "How long was I on that freakin' mountain?"

**Caption at bottom:**
"Thus spake Zarathustra."

# Visual Jokes
- The philosophical monologue about the Superman is undercut by the woman immediately dismissing him and treating him as content for social media
- The irony of Zarathustra's profound spiritual teaching being interrupted by mundane video chatting with friends
- The final question suggests he's been out of touch so long that modern technology seems absurd to him
- The comic subverts the gravitas of the original philosophical text with millennial/modern internet culture references

# Scene Description
A six-panel webcomic in black and white (with one blue panel) depicting a silhouetted figure in various situations. The art style is minimalist with simple line drawings. The comic contrasts the "good news" about an undiscovered genius with the harsh reality of their circumstances.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (top left):**
"GOOD NEWS!"
"The next 'Einstein' is alive and living on planet Earth right now."

**Panel 2 (top middle):**
"She lives in a country nobody ever talks about."
"...in a village nobody cares about."

**Panel 3 (top right):**
"She has never read a textbook."
"...and has never seen the inside of a school."

**Panel 4 (middle left):**
"Every morning she makes the long trek to the market to sell firewood."

**Panel 5 (middle, blue background):**
"...and every night she makes the long trek back to feed her children."

**Panel 6 (bottom right, blue background):**
"You've never heard of her."
"...and you never will."

# Visual Jokes/Observations
The comic's irony lies in the contrast between the optimistic "good news" opening and the progressively bleaker reality presented. The silhouetted figure remains consistent throughout, emphasizing anonymity and invisibility despite potential genius.
