# Scene Description
A stick figure comic strip with a minimalist art style. The main character (stick figure with a round head) is shown learning programming languages across multiple panels. A second character appears periodically to offer advice or commentary. The setting is minimal, with a book/laptop representing the learning material.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (top left):**
Book cover: "THE BLOOP PROGRAMMING LANGUAGE FRAMEWORK"

**Panel 2:**
Character 1: "Dude, why are you learning BLooP? That's so 2012."
Character 2: "What?"

**Panel 3:**
Character 1: "Never forget to check the hourglass. See? BLooP has expired."

**Panel 4:**
Character 1: "Here. Start learning FlooP. All of the hottest startups use FlooP."
Character 2: "oh, okay."

**Panel 5:**
Character 1: "Good luck."
Character 2: "Thanks."

**Panels 6-7:**
(Silent panels showing character studying)

**Panel 8:**
Character 1: "FlooP is so 5 minutes ago."
Character 2: "Yes. LITERALLY 5 minutes ago."

**Panel 9:**
Character 1: "Here. You want to learn GlooP now."

**Panel 10:**
Character 1: "And now you have a fresh hourglass again."

**Panel 11:**
(Silent panel showing character studying)

**Panel 12:**
Character 2: "Why don't you just tell me what's going to be hot in 6 months?"

**Panel 13:**
Character 1: "hahah. You're funny."
Character 2: "Dude, why are you learning GlooP?"

# Visual Joke
The comic satirizes the rapid obsolescence and constant churn in tech culture, where programming frameworks and languages become outdated almost immediately. The hourglass metaphor represents expiration dates on trendy technologies, and the character keeps introducing new languages (BLooP → FlooP → GlooP) in an endless cycle of learning something obsolete before it's mastered.

# Scene Description
A black and white stick figure comic divided into 9 panels arranged in a 3x3 grid. The main character (a simple stick figure with black hair) interacts with a round-faced companion character across various scenes. The final panel shows the character riding a rainbow-colored unicorn. The art style is minimalist line drawing with occasional text emphasis and visual effects.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"So I arbitrarily defined an arbitrary mathematical structure and arbitrarily gave it some arbitrary features."

**Panel 2:**
"Then I arbitrarily formulated some arbitrary theorems about it."

**Panel 3:**
"What practical applications could this possibly have?"

**Panel 4:**
"There are no practical applications. That's the beauty of pure mathematics."

**Panel 5:**
"YOU !!!" [with radiating emphasis lines]
[Mathematical notation: "K₍₋ε₎ω05₍₎"]

**Panel 6:**
"I'm from the future. Come with me if you want to live!"
"What?"

**Panel 7:**
"In the future, your mathematical theories make possible an inter-dimensional rift in the space-time-consciousness continuum which allows Xur and the Ko-Dan armada to conquer the galaxy."
"I don't understand"

**Panel 8:**
"No time to explain. GET ON THE DAMN UNICORN!"

**Panel 9:**
[Character riding rainbow-colored unicorn]

**Bottom caption:**
"All math is applied math... eventually."

# Visual Jokes
- The absurd escalation from pure mathematics to intergalactic conquest via an unexplained time-traveling visitor
- The mathematical notation in Panel 5 appears nonsensical/garbled, emphasizing chaos
- The final panel's rainbow unicorn is the absurd visual payoff to "GET ON THE DAMN UNICORN"
- The meta-joke that abstract mathematics always finds applications, no matter how ridiculous

comics.transcript:
# Transcription: "The Parable of the Computers"

## Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic presented as a biblical parable, adapting Matthew 25:14-30 (The Parable of the Talents). The art style uses simple line drawings with minimal detail. The master character is distinguished by a blue cap/head. The comic uses multiple panels arranged in a grid format, progressing through the narrative with both dialogue and action sequences.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"For the kingdom of heaven is as a man travelling into a far country, who called his three servants, and delivered unto them his goods."

**Panel 2:**
"And unto his three servants he gave one computer each."
"And straightaway took his journey."

**Panel 3:**
"After a long time the lord of those servants cometh, and reckoneth with them."

**Panel 4:**
"And the lord of the servants said unto the first servant:"
"What have you done with the computer in my absence?"

**Panel 5:**
"Lord, thou deliveredst unto me a computer: behold, I have created a simple and easy-to-use graphical operating system that now runs 90% of the world's personal computers."

**Panel 6:**
"And his lord said unto him: Well done, thou good and faithful servant: thou hast been faithful over a few things, I will make thee ruler over many things."

**Panel 7:**
"Enter thou into the joy of thy lord."
"WOO-HOO"

**Panel 8:**
"And the lord of the servants said unto the second servant:"
"What have you done with the computer in my absence?"

**Panel 9:**
"Lord, thou deliveredst unto me a computer: behold, I have created a social networking site that now has over a billion users worldwide."

**Panel 10:**
"And his lord said unto him: Well done, thou good and faithful servant: thou hast been faithful over a few things, I will make thee ruler over many things."

**Panel 11:**
"Enter thou into the joy of thy lord."
"yee-ha"

**Panel 12:**
"And the lord of the servants said unto the third servant:"
"And what have you done with the computer in my absence?"

**Panels 13-14:**
[Silent panels showing the third servant with no response]

**Panel 15:**
[Final panel showing the third servant sitting at a desk with a laptop, presumably having done nothing productive]

## Visual Jokes/Puns

1. **The parable adaptation**: The biblical "Parable of the Talents" is reframed using modern computers and technology instead of money/talents, creating humor through the anachronistic juxtaposition of ancient religious text with contemporary tech innovations.

2. **Character identification**: The master (lord) is consistently marked by his blue cap, making him immediately identifiable across panels.

3. **Emotional escalation**: The first two servants show increasingly exuberant celebrations ("WOO-HOO," "yee-ha") as they're rewarded.

4. **Silent panel buildup**: Panels 13-14 use silence to create comedic tension before the reveal of what the third servant did (or didn't do).

5. **The punchline setup**: The third servant's apparent inaction with the computer at the end implicitly suggests he hid or wasted it, setting up the parable's moral about stewardship and accountability in a tech context.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description

A stick-figure webcomic with a self-deprecating, humorous tone. The art style is minimalist line drawings on white backgrounds. The comic depicts a lone stick figure labeled "ME" in various scenarios contrasted with "glorious knights" or other figures, illustrating the speaker's role as support staff in academic research. Laboratory equipment (flasks, beakers, test tubes, burners) appears throughout. The final panel shows a published academic paper with the narrator highlighted in the author list.

---

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (top):**
"You may not always see us but we are always there."

[Arrow pointing to stick figure]: "ME"

---

**Panel 2:**
"We are the beleaguered squires toiling in the backdrop while the glorious knights in burnished armor take to the calamitous battlefield and pierce the dark forces of ignorance with bitter spears of reason."

[Arrow]: "ME" [pointing to small stick figure beside two larger figures with weapons]

---

**Panel 3:**
"Brandishing elegant tools of empirical illumination, they drive back the wavering darkness of superstition."

[Left side: stick figure crouching with laboratory equipment]

"...bringing to bear the full might of logic and deductive reasoning... And we are there."

[Right side: two stick figures, one pointing at equations on a board]

---

**Panel 4:**
"Many battles have been fought and many battles have been won as they engird their foes in an 'ever-receding pocket of scientific ignorance'* - and we are there."

[Bottom left]: "ME" [tiny stick figure]

[Right side: castle with flag, stick figures approaching with weapons, labeled]: "*NJT"

---

**Panel 5:**
"And when tales of their victories are put to paper and added to the esteemed archives of academia, our names you will see amongst those of the scholarly knights of lore."

[Mock academic paper layout]:
**"Some Great Scientific Discovery"**

Authors: "Polly Postdoc, Gary Graduatedstud, Obligatory Asian Dude, Larry Labtech, **[ME]**, Pricilla Principlesinvestigator"

Abstract: "We made some great scientific discovery."

Introduction: "Blah blah blah [repeated placeholder text]"

[Arrow pointing to "ME" in author list]

---

**Panel 6 (bottom sections):**
"You may not always see us but we are always there.

We are indispensable journeymen in the grand adventure of scientific discovery.

We are the squires of the academic knights."

[Stick figure at laboratory bench with equipment]

"We are the test tube washers"

---

# Visual Jokes & Puns

1. **Scale contrast**: The narrator ("ME") is drawn progressively smaller compared to the "glorious knights," visually emphasizing their diminished status and invisibility.

2. **Laboratory equipment as "toil"**: The repetition of the same laboratory setup throughout suggests mundane, repetitive work contrasted with the knights' "glorious" battles.

3. **Author list visibility**: The narrator's name appears in the published paper but is still visually minimized and requires an arrow to locate, reinforcing the theme of being "always there" but unseen.

4. **"Test tube washers" finale**: The comic concludes by reducing the researcher's role to its most menial task, a self-aware joke about the hierarchy in academia.

5. **Medieval metaphor**: The juxtaposition of knights with swords fighting "ignorance" against modern scientists using lab equipment creates absurdist humor about academic work being framed as epic heroism.

comics.title: The Story of the Cheese Maze

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A simple, whimsical black-and-white comic strip featuring four characters (two rats named Avelli and Machi, and two humanoid creatures named Hill and Napoleon) in a maze. The art style is minimal line drawings with simple stick figures. The narrative follows the characters' discovery of cheese and their subsequent journey of building a machine and learning. The comic uses simple rectangular panels with occasional larger illustrated scenes.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

"Once, long ago in a land far away, there lived four little characters who ran through a maze looking for cheese to make them happy."

"Two were rats named 'Avelli' and 'Machi' and two were littlepeople - beings who were as small as rats but who looked and acted a lot like people today. Their names were 'Hill' and 'Napoleon'."

"Every day the four of them spent like little mice searching for their own special cheese."

"Then one day, they each found their own kind of cheese at the end of one of the corridors."

"And every morning after that, the rats and the littlepeople headed directly to the same corridor to find the good good cheese."

"Until one day, the rats approached the littlepeople, pounded their tiny little bitch asses into the ground, and stole their cheese."

"The rats used their newfound cheese wealth to build a machine that could create cheese out of thin air."

"The machine churned out so much good good cheese, so much cheese: glorious mountains of Swiss cheese, soaring towers of Cheddar cheese, gushing rivers of delicious cheese Whiz."

"And the rats built a huge wall around their cheese to keep out the littlepeople."

"Sometimes the rats threw pieces of cheese over the wall. And they laughed and laughed while voraciously sucking their cheese Callahes as the littlepeople fought and clawed for every tiny morsel."

"So the littlepeople learned to read - books that explained how anyone can get all the cheese they wanted if they worked hard and had a positive attitude."

"The littlepeople loved those books."

"THE END"

# Visual Jokes/Puns Dependent on Image

1. The contrast between the cute, simple character designs and the brutal description "pounded their tiny little bitch asses into the ground" creates dark comedy through juxtaposition.

2. The enormous cheese mountains/towers in the illustration emphasize the grotesque excess described in the text.

3. The littlepeople reading books shown as small figures conveys the irony that self-help literature offers no practical solution to the rats' monopoly.

4. The wall illustration shows the scale disparity and the hopelessness of the littlepeople's situation, making the "positive attitude" conclusion absurdly inadequate.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A black and white webcomic with a minimalist art style featuring a young person with short dark hair. The comic alternates between simple line drawings and more detailed sketches. Settings include a bedroom, abstract cosmic imagery, mathematical equations, and surreal landscapes. The overall tone is contemplative and whimsical, blending mundane reality with cosmic imagination.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (top):**
"Alone in my room.
scribbling equations."

**Panel 2:**
"I've... seen things.
...things you wouldn't believe."

**Panel 3:**
"...terrible, beautiful
things quivering on the
horizon of my mind's eye."

**Panel 4:**
"I have seen the cold
and austere universe
of randomness dance
in rhythm with the
music of the primes
at the edge of
infinity."

**Panel 5:**
"I have run barefoot along
the sinewy embankments of
countless Calabi-Tau
manifolds."

**Panel 6:**
"I have soared naked through the magnificent solitude of infinite
dimensional Hilbert space and through exotic realms that have no
business existing in the mundane abstractions of mortal men."

**Panel 7:**
"With surgical precision,
I have weft, stitched, and
knotted together the
obscene contortions that
would make angels weep."

**Panel 8:**
"I have collapsed stars into
monstrous vortices from which
nothing can escape and I have
blithely skated along its
pristine event horizon."

**Panel 9:**
"I have witnessed the birth
and death of this and many
other universes many times.
So it goes."

**Panel 10:**
"Alone in my room."

**Panel 11:**
"With nothing but
the formidable lens
of mathematics and
a fecundity of
imagination...."

**Panel 12 (bottom):**
"I've seen some shit."

[Mathematical equations are visible on the final panel but are not clearly legible enough to transcribe with accuracy]

## Visual Jokes/Puns
The central joke is the contrast between the grandiose, cosmic claims made throughout the comic and the mundane reality revealed at the end—the character has experienced all these incredible things purely through imagination and mathematics while alone in their room, culminating in the casual, crude understatement "I've seen some shit."

# Scene Description

A stick-figure webcomic with a humorous, irreverent tone. The comic uses simple black line drawings on white background, with occasional panels showing indoor scenes and a final scene depicting a nighttime cityscape. The narrative follows a first-person character recounting their extraordinary abilities and mysterious nature.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

"Yes, it's true that I once forged a sword of awesomeness out of chunks of meteorites."

"And yes, I once killed a ferocious bear with my bare hands to save a basket of kittens."

"And every year on April 20, I make sweet, sweet love to a beautiful mermaid on a beach while wearing a dead bear's skin."

"I didn't have to use my bare hands to kill that bear because I, of course, shoot deadly lasers from my ass if I want to."

"But I'm a private person. I live alone in my lair of solitude atop my own personal Mount Olympus."

"And in my lair, I bide my time by wisely monitoring the progress of humanity."

"On occasion, when I have found that humanity was stuck in a stalemate or suffering from a stale status quo bias, I have descended from my hiding place."

"...and imparted my Promethean gift of disruptive innovation to the masses."

"And then, as quickly as I came, I would disappear into the dark of night like a ghost in an Audi 5000."

"Out of sight from the attention of civilisation once more, I am again free to monitor the progress of humanity."

"And if the people of earth should ever require my services again..."

[Final panel shows a nighttime cityscape with a spotlight beam]

"I, Satoshi Nakamoto, shall return."

# Visual Jokes

- The stick figure shooting lasers from their posterior (ass lasers)
- The specific mention of "Audi 5000" paired with the image of a car, creating an absurdist reference
- The final reveal identifying the narrator as Satoshi Nakamoto (the pseudonymous creator of Bitcoin), juxtaposing the grandiose fictional persona with a real cryptocurrency figure
- The Bitcoin symbol appearing in the spotlight beam in the final panel

# Scene Description
A stick-figure comic in black and white showing a character with wild, spiky hair (suggesting they're having an epiphany or excited moment) standing before three seated audience members. The character's emotional state becomes increasingly frantic across the panels.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- Equation label: "F = G(m₁m₂)/r²"

**Panel 2:**
- Character: "I CALL IT THE LAW OF UNIVERSAL GRAVITATION."

**Panel 3:**
- Character: "Y'KNOW, IT WOULD BE REALLY COOL IF, LIKE, SOMEONE COULD FIND A PRECISE VALUE FOR..."

**Panel 4:**
- (Silent panel)

**Panel 5:**
- Character: "DID YOU HEAR WHAT I SAID?"

**Panel 6:**
- Audience member: "YES, M'LORD."

**Panel 7:**
- Character: "THEN I MEAN NOW! MOVE!"

**Panel 8:**
- (Silent panel showing character alone)

# Visual Jokes

The final panel's dismissal of experimentalists suggests frustration with those who must do the tedious practical work to validate theoretical predictions.

# Scene Description
A multi-panel webcomic in simple black and white stick figure style. The first section shows a classroom scene with a professor teaching quantum mechanics to students. The final panel shows the professor redirected to teaching Philosophy 101 to a larger class instead.

---

# Full Text Transcription

**Panel 1 (Header):**
"So these are the basic postulates of quantum mechanics that we'll be working with this semester."

[Handwritten notes on board: "ℏ∂ψ/∂t=-ℏ²/2m∇²ψ + V(x)ψ" and additional quantum mechanics notation]

**Panel 2:**
"Are there any questions so far?"

**Panel 3:**
"Yes, Sean?"

**Panel 4:**
"So are you saying that quantum phenomena don't exist without the presence of a conscious observer?"

**Panel 5:**
"Uhh... no, that's not exactly what..."

"And you seem to be implying that the mathematical abstractions used to describe the processes are more real than the objects themselves."

**Panel 6:**
"I think you misunderstood what I..."

"And isn't mathematics just a man-made construct constrained by the physiological limitations of human cognitive abilities?"

**Panel 7:**
"Well, mathematics is just a tool that..."

"What if an alien from the Andromeda galaxy with a vastly different brain structure, and hence system of mathematics, were to develop a quantum theory based on their own mathematics? Couldn't their conception of reality possibly be completely different from our own? Who's to say that their version of reality isn't more real than ours? In fact, what is real? Maybe we should come up with a new definition of reality."

**Panel 8:**
[Silent panel - professor and students]

**Panel 9:**
"I can see that there's nothing more that I can teach you."

**Panel 10:**
"I believe that you're ready for our advanced class."

**Panel 11:**
[Large empty space - transition]

**Panel 12 (Final scene):**
[Classroom sign reads: "PHILOSOPHY 101"]

[Professor stands at lectern facing a much larger classroom full of students]

---

# Visual Jokes

1. **The escalation**: Sean's increasingly absurd philosophical questions about quantum mechanics become progressively longer and more ridiculous across three consecutive panels, building comedic momentum.

2. **The ironic punishment**: The professor, having finally found a student who questions everything, is rewarded by being moved from teaching a small quantum mechanics class (assumed advanced/graduate level) to teaching Philosophy 101 to a large introductory lecture hall—suggesting philosophical debate is far more chaotic and populated than rigorous physics.

3. **Silent acknowledgment**: The empty middle panel serves as a beat where both professor and students realize the magnitude of what just happened, adding to the comedic timing.

A nine-panel stick-figure webcomic showing a character at a desk experiencing escalating financial delusions and power fantasy, which is abruptly deflated by a humbling reality check. The art style is minimalist black-and-white line drawings with simple circular faces.

**Panel 1:**
"BUY 5000 MARCH 50 CALLS, START BUYING 10,000 SHARE BLOCKS AND TAKE IT UP TO 50 DOLLARS."

**Panel 2:**
"ANOTHER PHONE CALL. ANOTHER MILLION DOLLARS."

**Panel 4:**
"WITH A CLICK OF A MOUSE, I CAN INSTALL WORLD LEADERS OR COLLAPSE SMALL GOVERNMENTS."

"I HOLD THE FATE OF MILLIONS OF PEASANTS IN MY HAND."

"I AM A LORD OF FINANCE. I AM THE WIZARD OF WALL STREET."

"I AM MASTER OF THE UNIVERSE!"

## Scene Description
A stick-figure comic in simple black and white art style showing a progression of social events. The setting is outdoors near a house with windows and a door. The narrative follows Mike (identified by a blue hat) as word about his party spreads through increasingly larger groups of people. The comic uses minimalist stick figures to illustrate the exponential growth of party attendees.

## Dialogue and Text (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"HEY, WHATCHA LOOKIN' AT?"

**Panel 2:**
"MIKE, MY NEIGHBOR, IS THROWING A PARTY... AGAIN."

**Panel 3:**
"SOME GUYS HAVE ALL THE FUN."

**Panel 4:**
"GOT ANY BEER?"
"IN THE FRIDGE."

**Panel 5:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 6:**
"WHA'SUP, KEMO SABE?"
"MIKE IS THROWING ANOTHER PARTY."

**Panel 7:**
"LOOKS FUN."
"YEAH."

**Panel 8:**
"WHAT'S GOING ON?"
"MIKE IS HAVING A PARTY."

**Panel 9:**
"WHAT'S HAPPENING, GUYS?"
"MIKE IS HAVING A PARTY."
"DUDE'S A PARTY ANIMAL."

**Panel 10:**
"LUCKY BASTARD."

## Visual Jokes
The primary humor derives from the exponential growth of the party—the comic visually demonstrates how information spreads through social networks, with each panel showing progressively more people arriving, despite no one actually receiving a direct invitation from Mike. The final panel shows the party has become massive while Mike remains isolated inside, unaware of the chaos his gathering has created.

# Scene Description
A nine-panel webcomic in simple black and white stick figure style featuring two characters: a person with long dark hair and a round-headed figure in blue. The comic takes place in a bedroom and depicts an increasingly absurd conversation about "spicing up" their intimate life. The final panel shows a letter addressed to a magazine.

# Text Transcription

**Panel 1:**
"Hey, what's with all this equipment in the bedroom?"

**Panel 2:**
"I thought I'd spice up our sex life."

**Panel 3:**
"Okaaay... but why do you have a paintball gun? It's filled with Sriracha sauce."

**Panel 4:**
"I thought it would be nice to shoot the sauce up your nose right before you climax."
"WHAT?"

**Panel 5:**
"Why? Why would you do that?! Why? Why?"
"To enhance the pleasure."

**Panel 6:**
"Whose pleasure?"

**Panel 7:**
"And then I'm gonna snap this mousetrap on your balls"
"Are you crazy?"

**Panel 8:**
"But first I'm gonna twist your penis like a pretzel around this hot curling iron."

**Panel 9:**
"C'mere, you. Time to be pleasured."
"Dear, God! Make it all stop."

**Letter Panel:**
"Dear Cosmo,
Yeah, if you could stop giving sex tips to our girlfriends, that'd be great.

Sincerely,
MEN"

# Visual Jokes
The humor depends entirely on the escalating absurdity and graphic nature of the proposed "enhancements," contrasted with the innocent stick figure art style. The final letter to Cosmo magazine (a real publication known for sex advice) suggests this is a parody of unrealistic or painful sex tips commonly found in magazines.

# Scene Description
A simple black and white comic strip featuring two characters: a monkey and a blue sphere character (likely representing a human or observer). The art style is minimalist stick-figure-like drawing. The setting progresses from what appears to be a zoo or enclosed space. The comic uses a grid layout with 8 panels arranged in 4 rows of 2 columns.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 2:**
Monkey: "Hey, buddy, why don't you take a picture? It'll last longer."
Blue sphere: "What?"

**Panel 3:**
Blue sphere: "You think you're pretty hot stuff, don't you? How noble in reason, how infinite in faculty!"
Blue sphere: "?"

**Panel 4:**
Monkey: "Don't you look down on me, you *Homo sapiens* sumbabitch. Genetically, we're practically identical."

**Panel 5:**
Monkey: "What if certain random genetic mutations and millions of years of cumulative natural selection turned out just a little bit differently?"

**Panel 6:**
Monkey: "It could have been you with the measly 400 cc cranial capacity sitting here in this cramped prison masturbating in public and throwing poo. But nooooooooo..."

**Panel 7:**
Monkey: "Look at you, with your 1400 cc brain living in your big, fancy house in the suburbs listening to Mozart and reading Shakespeare getting all fat off of the fruits of your magnificent technological innovations."

**Panel 8:**
Monkey: "You think I'm an evolutionary failure, don't you?"
Blue sphere: "DON'T YOU?!!"
Blue sphere: "I just wanted to see some monkeys."

**Title caption (bottom):**
"zoos make me sad"

# Visual Jokes
The primary humor derives from the contrast between the blue sphere character's innocent desire to view animals at a zoo and the monkey's increasingly elaborate philosophical tirade about human superiority and evolutionary contingency. The irony is that the monkey delivers sophisticated arguments about evolution and human arrogance while the observer simply wanted casual entertainment. The final punchline's humor relies on the absurdity of the monkey's passionate speech being triggered by a simple tourist taking pictures.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A six-panel stick figure comic depicting a conversation between two people sitting at a dining table. The art style is minimalist black line drawings on white background. The humor builds through a series of increasingly absurd misunderstandings about relationship partners.

## Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- Woman: "I don't think I'm getting enough mathematical stimulation from his relationship."
- Man: "What are you saying?"

**Panel 2:**
- Woman: "I'm having an affair with your brother Johann."
- Man: "What?! But I'm Johann."

**Panel 3:**
- Woman: "You are? Oh, then I guess I meant to say Jacob. When did you start sleeping with Jacob?!!"

**Panel 4:**
- Man: "Right after I broke it off with Daniel."
- Woman: "You slept with my brother Daniel too!!"

**Panel 5:**
- Woman: "Brother? No, I slept with your *uncle Daniel*."
- Man: "Wait... What?"

**Panel 6:**
- Woman: "Sorry. I keep getting all you Bernoullis mixed up."
- Man: (silent, frustrated reaction)

## Visual Joke
The humor relies on the escalating absurdity of the misunderstandings, culminating in the revelation that the woman has been confusing multiple men from the "Bernoulli" family (referencing the famous Swiss mathematical family, tying back to the opening mention of "mathematical stimulation").

# Scene Description
A six-panel stick figure comic depicting a meeting or committee discussion. The first five panels show three stick figures seated at a table in what appears to be a meeting room. The final panel shows the same three figures at a formal "Fields Medal Rules Committee" meeting table. The art style is minimalist black line drawings typical of webcomics.

# Dialogue and Text (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"Okay, so now we need to come up with an arbitrary prejudice with which we can discriminate against people."

**Panel 2:**
"How about 'nobody over six feet tall'?"
"Nah, not arbitrary enough."

**Panel 3:**
"How about 'nobody over 200 pounds'?"

**Panel 4:**
"NO, NO, NO! That makes too much sense!"

**Panel 5:**
"How about 'nobody over 40 years old'?"

**Panel 6:**
Label: "FIELDS MEDAL RULES COMMITTEE"

# Visual Jokes
The comic's humor relies on the reveal in the final panel that this arbitrary and absurd discrimination standard (age-based eligibility) is actually the real criterion for the Fields Medal, a prestigious mathematics award given only to mathematicians under 40 years old. The earlier rejected criteria were more logical, making the actual rule seem absurdly arbitrary by comparison.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A six-panel comic strip featuring two characters: one wearing a blue cap (the astrologer) and one with black hair (the client). The art style is simple black-and-white line drawings. The setting appears to be an astrologer's reading room with a crystal ball visible in most panels. The final panel includes a dictionary definition and caption below the comic itself.

## Dialogue and Text (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
- Astrologer: "Yes, yes, I can predict your future from the positions of the stars."
- Client: "What's going to happen to me in the future?"

**Panel 2:**
- Astrologer: "When yond star that centers at our system's heart,"
- Client: "You mean the sun?"

**Panel 3:**
- Astrologer: "Yes, when yond same star does advance his burning eye, and smatters the peaceful eastern sky, causing the night's dark dew to dry;"

**Panel 4:**
- Astrologer: "It portends that you shall from your home depart."
- Client: "You mean the morning? yeahyeahyeah... whatever..."

**Panel 5:**
- Astrologer: "Well of course I'll leave my house in the morning. I go to work in the morning."

**Panel 6:**
- Client: "Sometimes it's scary how accurate these readings are."

**Below comic:**
- Dictionary entry: "astrology n: the divination of the supposed influences of the stars and planets on human affairs and terrestrial events by their positions and aspects" — Merriam-Webster Dictionary
- Caption: "Strictly speaking, I practice astrology everyday."

## Visual Jokes
The humor relies on the astrologer using flowery, archaic language (seemingly from Shakespeare) to describe mundane, obvious events—specifically, the sun rising in the morning and causing people to go to work. The client's deadpan realization that this "accurate" prediction is simply describing a daily routine is the joke, compounded by the final caption's pun that everyone "practices astrology" by simply observing that the sun's rising means it's time to leave for work.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A stick figure comic in simple black line art with blue-filled heads/caps. The narrative progresses across six panels showing a character with a blue cap in various situations, depicting a humorous commentary on social dynamics and consequences.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

Panel 4 (large bottom left): "NUDGE"

Panel 5 (right side, vertical text): "INTERNET"

# Visual Narrative and Jokes

The comic's humor is entirely visual:

1. **Panel 1**: A solitary figure with a blue cap, standing alone
2. **Panel 2-3**: The figure encounters one, then two stick figures without caps, appearing to nudge/push them
3. **Panel 4**: A cascading domino effect—dozens of stick figures tumble in a long chain reaction, with the blue-capped character standing at the end, labeled "NUDGE"
4. **Panel 5-6**: The consequences unfold—stick figures fall off a cliff into the "INTERNET" below, while others (including the original blue-capped character) are also falling

**The visual pun**: A single small "nudge" in real life creates an exponential chain reaction that sends crowds into the internet/online world—commentary on how minor actions can have massive, unintended consequences when amplified through social networks.

# Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic in simple black and white line art depicting two prehistoric characters (Thag and another) around a campfire, progressing through various scenarios involving glass and its applications. The comic uses minimal, crude drawings characteristic of "SMBC" or similar webcomics.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1 (Top Left):**
- Label: "30,003 BC"

**Panel 2:**
- Character 1: "Thag notice that sometimes when sand get hot, make glass."
- Character 2: "What?"

**Panel 3:**
- Character 1: "But when Thag see glass, Thag see not just glass. Thag see future."
- Character 2: "What?"

**Panel 4:**
- Caption: "When Thag imagine time reversed, glass look like artifact from future."
- Character 2: "What?"

**Lower Section (Left Side):**
- Caption: "Some day, people make use glass see outside from cave."
- Caption: "Some day, make use glass see small things."
- Caption: "Learn secret of life."

**Lower Section (Right Side):**
- Caption: "And some day, people make huge caves covered with glass reach sky. This Thag know."

**Bottom Section (Left to Right):**
- Caption: "Some day, use glass see far away things."
- Caption: "In fact, some day, put big glass in sky. See far away heavens."
- Label on image: "Learn secret of heavens."

**Bottom Row:**
- Caption: "Some day, glass help learn us secret light."
- Caption: "One day, glass make use for store information." [with CD image]
- Caption: "Can make use glass for carry information." [showing fiber optic cable]
- Caption: "And make use glass for see information." [showing computer monitor]

**Final Row:**
- Character 1: "This Thag know."
- Character 1: "That all Thag want to say."

# Visual Jokes/Puns
- The humor relies on Thag's philosophical realization that glass—discovered by accident—will become the foundation for countless future technologies (telescopes, microscopes, windows, fiber optics, computer screens, CDs, etc.)
- Thag's broken English ("Thag notice," "make glass," "Thag see") adds comedic simplicity to profound technological foresight
- The progression from a chance discovery to modern technology emphasizes how transformative glass has been to human civilization

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A simple black-and-white stick figure comic depicting two characters in bed at night. One character (in white) is trying to sleep while the other (in blue) is awake and increasingly agitated. The comic progresses from late-night conversation to escalating frustration as one character becomes obsessed with historical genealogy research.

---

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"Hey, are you sleeping?"
"Trying to."

**Panel 2:**
"I have a question."

**Panel 3:**
"Did the Yorks really have a better claim to the throne than the Lancasters?"
"Don't care."

**Panel 4:**
"Sure you do. Look, I mentally constructed a family tree to figure it out."
"Don't care."

**Panel 5:**
"Now, if we ignore the usual laws of primogeniture and stuff and we naively restrict our analysis to autosomal DNA inheritance (which really doesn't make any practical sense except as a mathematical exercise but who cares?), the Duke of York shared about 15.625% of his genes with Edward III while Henry VI only shared about 6.25%. But of course that huge difference isn't surprising since, after all, York was a generation closer to Edward *and* he descended from two of Edward's sons.

Dooooonnnntttt caaaarrrreeeeee.

But it gets really interesting if we trace the lineage back even further. Henry VI's great grandmother, Blanche of Lancaster, was the great great granddaughter of Henry III so, according to my calculations, Henry VI shared about 1.56% of his genes with Henry III. York shared about 1.95% with Henry III. But York was killed at the Battle of Wakefield before he could take the throne. Remember? And then his son, Edward IV, became king while Henry VI was still alive. Now, Edward IV's mother was the great granddaughter of Edward III so Edward IV shared about 1.76% of his genes with Henry III. But the difference between Edward IV and Henry VI means nothing when you consider the increasing variability of autosomal DNA transmission with each successive generation. So between the years 1460 and 1471, it's a freakin' stalemate!

Pretty interesting, huh?"

**Panel 6:**
"I need you to get up and check my math on a calculator and confirm my facts on Wikipedia."

**Panel 7:**
"C'mon, get up. It's killing me. Get up!"

**Panel 8:**
(No dialogue)

**Panel 9:**
"Get up! Get up! Get up! Get up! Get up! Get up! Get up! Get up! Get up!"

**Panel 10:**
"GO THE F*CK TO SLEEP, BRAIN!!!"

**Panel 11:**
"How can anyone sleep with so many important unresolved questions in the world?"

---

## Visual Jokes

1. **The contrast between the characters' states**: The blue character is increasingly agitated while the white character remains in bed, illustrating the classic scenario of one person's obsession preventing another from sleeping.

2. **Physical escalation**: As the comic progresses, the blue character goes from sitting calmly to standing up, bouncing, and eventually shouting in frustration—visually representing mounting desperation.

3. **The absurdity of the research focus**: The detailed, academic discussion of medieval genealogy and autosomal DNA inheritance percentages is humorously incongruous for a late-night bedroom conversation and completely unimportant to actual sleep.

4. **The ironic conclusion**: The blue character's final complaint about unresolved questions being an obstacle to sleep is itself the source of the problem—their own obsessive questioning is what prevents sleep in both characters.

# Scene Description

This webcomic presents a comedic adaptation of Romeo and Juliet's famous balcony scene. The art style is minimalist, featuring simple stick figures and a woman in a window/balcony (represented by a rectangular box). A man in a blue hat reads from a book while the woman leans out from above. The comic is structured in five panels showing the progression of their interaction, with the man's romantic declarations becoming increasingly awkward and the woman's responses becoming more exasperated.

---

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**

"...I'll prove more true
Than those that have more cunning to be strange.
I should have been more strange, I must confess,
But that thou overheard'st, ere I was ware,
My true love's passion; therefore pardon me;
And not impute this yielding to light love,
Which the dark night hath so discovered."

"Yeeeah boollii...
You're as good as in, Romeo."

**Panel 2:**

"...This bud of love, by summer's ripening breath,
May prove a beauteous flower when next we meet.
Good night, good night! As sweet repose and rest
Come to thy heart as that within my breast."

"Okay, Romeo. That's your cue to leave.
You're doing well but you need to get
out of there now before you say
something awkward."

**Panel 3:**

"O, wilt thou leave
me so unsatisfied?"

"What? No, shut
up, Romeo!"

"What satisfaction canst
thou have to-night?"

"Stop it! You were as good
as in. Now she gon think
you're a creeper."

"Th'exchange of thy love's
faithful vow for mine.
I gave thee mine before
thou didst request it,
And yet I would it were
to give again.

blah blah
blah blah
blah blah"

"You're gonna ruin your
chances. Stop talking!
Stop talking! Stop talking!"

**Panel 4 (Left side):**

"I hear some noise within. Dear love, adieu.—
—Sweet Montague, be true.
Stay but a little. I will come again.

[Exit above.]"

**Panel 4 (Right side):**

(Silent panel showing both characters separated)

**Panel 5 (Left side):**

"C'mon, Romeo, get outta
there. Go home. And don't
call her for at least three
days so she doesn't think
you're a loser. GO HOME!"

**Panel 5 (Right side):**

"O blessed,
blessed
night!"

"AMATEUR!!!"

---

# Visual Jokes

1. **The "Yeeeah boollii" moment** - The visual of Juliet enthusiastically celebrating Romeo's approach (like a sports commentator or hype person) undermines the romantic poetry.

2. **"blah blah blah"** - Romeo's continued romantic monologue is reduced to meaningless sounds, emphasizing how he's overshooting his romantic moment and becoming annoying rather than charming.

3. **The director/coach role** - The stick figure functions as an unseen prompter or director trying to manage Romeo's performance, adding modern social/dating advice to a classical text.

4. **The final "AMATEUR!!!"** - After being coached to leave gracefully, Romeo's ecstatic response ("O blessed, blessed night!") as he finally exits shows he's still making it awkward, prompting exasperated criticism.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description

This is a webcomic in simple black-and-white stick figure style. It begins with a physicist explaining a particle physics diagram to a colleague, which is then humorously misinterpreted as an alien invasion warning. The comic escalates through scenes of an alien attack destroying civilization, followed by an underwater sequence, and ending with a paleolithic cave painting discovery that recontextualizes the entire story.

---

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:** (No text)

**Panel 2:** "< So this diagram represents the production of a quark-antiquark pair by the annihilation of an electron and a positron into a Z boson. >"

**Panel 3:** "< Wait. I'm receiving an emergency alert via my neural link. >"

**Panel 4:** "< O my gods! The Anunnaki are attacking earth!!! >"

**Panel 5:** "< Look! Anunnaki warships! >"
(Label on building: "CERN CAUX INSTITUTE PARTICLE PHYSICS")

**Panel 6:** "< PEOPLE OF EARTH, PREPARE FOR THE COMPLETE AND UTTER DESTRUCTION OF YOUR CIVILIZATION. >"
Response: "< We're doomed. >"

**Panel 7:** (Visual: UFOs firing beams at city)

**Panel 8:** (Visual: Underwater scene, mostly empty)

**Panels 9-11:** (Visual: Progression showing figure alone underwater)

**Panel 12:** (Visual: Black panel with UFO and figure)

**Panel 13:** "UPPER PALEOLITHIC HUMANS CAPABLE OF PRIMITIVE RAT WORSHIP AND WORSHIPPED ANIMALS"
(Visual: Cave painting-style drawing)

---

# Visual Jokes

1. The particle physics diagram in Panel 1 superficially resembles UFOs/flying objects due to simple stick-figure representation, creating the misinterpretation in Panel 3.

2. The escalation from a mundane scientific explanation to apocalyptic alien invasion is absurdist comedy based on miscommunication.

3. The final reveal suggests the entire alien invasion was actually ancient humans creating a cave painting of the particle physics diagram, implying either cyclical history or that primitive humans were somehow aware of advanced physics—a science fiction paradox played for laughs.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A simple stick-figure comic depicting two time travelers (one with black hair, one with white/blonde hair) arriving in Elizabethan England via a TARDIS-style time machine. The art style is minimalist with simple line drawings, circular heads, and basic features. The comic progresses through multiple panels showing their encounter with two Elizabethan gentlemen.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
"We did it! We have travelled to Elizabethan England.
Now we can visit some of our heroes."

**Panel 2:**
"Look. A couple of Elizabethan gentlemen, like they've jumped straight out of the history books."

**Panel 3:**
"Greetings, good sirs. I was wondering if you could direct me to the residence of one Will Shakespeare."

**Panel 4:**
"Good Lord. A chinaman.
What?"

**Panel 5:**
"Looks like the invasion has begun.
no no no... wait. I'm Korean-American."

**Panel 6:**
"See? Here's my U.S. passport.
Of course that means nothing to you yet."

**Panel 7:**
"Maybe we should have him drawn and quartered.
Maybe we should run.
Hold on.
Nah, let's just hang him."

**Panel 8:**
"Who the hell do you guys think you are talking to my friend like that?"

**Panel 9:**
"Such impudence in a commoner woman. Must be a witch.
What?"

**Panel 10:**
[Empty panel with just the two characters]

**Panel 11:**
"Definitely a witch.
Okay. NOW we run!"

**Panel 12:**
[Bottom panel with time machine illustration]
"TIME MACHINE

AWESOME
(for white guys)"

## Visual Jokes
- The punchline relies on the final caption "(for white guys)" - commenting on how time travel adventures are framed as exciting for white male protagonists while the diverse travelers face immediate discrimination and danger in the past.
- The progression from friendly greeting to witch-hunting accusation happens rapidly when the travelers assert themselves.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A two-panel comic in simple black and white stick figure style. The top panel shows a figure at a laptop displaying a customer review. The bottom panel shows the same figure clicking "Add to Cart," with a caption below criticizing the review.

## Text Transcription

**Top Panel (Customer Review):**
- Star rating: ★☆☆☆☆
- Review text: "This book sucks. I wanted a book about physics but this book has calculus everywhere. If I wanted equations I would have bought a math book. If your looking for a book about physics their are plenty of better books around."

**Bottom Panel:**
- Button label: "Add to Cart"
- Sound effect: "CLICK"

**Caption below comic:**
"stupid reviewer, bad review

DOUBLE NEGATIVE"

## Visual Joke
The humor is in the ironic double negative: The reviewer's own text contains multiple grammatical errors (misused "their/there," "your/you're"), making the reviewer appear unintelligent while criticizing the book. The comic suggests the reviewer is adding the poorly-written book to their cart *because* of these errors in their own review—a self-aware joke about the reviewer being hypocritical.

# Scene Description
A single-panel webcomic in a simple line-drawing style. The upper portion shows a smartphone permission request screen with a white background and black text. Below that is a cartoon character—a simple stick figure with a round head wearing a blue baseball cap—looking distressed and speaking in a speech bubble. The overall tone is humorous commentary on app permissions.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Permission Request Screen:**
This app needs access to:

**Storage**
Modify or delete the contents of your USB storage

**Your location**
Precise (GPS) location

**Phone calls**
Read phone status and identity

**Network communication**
Full network access

**Hardware controls**
Record audio, take pictures and video

**Your personal information**
Read your contacts, read call log, read your credit report, read your personal journal that you hide under the dresser, read your deepest private thoughts and peer into the dark twisted recesses of your psyche and compile a profile of you that will allow us to predict with 95% accuracy your future behavior including but not limited to your dining habits, weekend activities, and your masturbation schedule

*as we silently judge you*

[ACCEPT button]

**Character's Speech Bubble:**
Well, I just wanted a flashlight app, but okay.

**Caption (bottom right):**
The greedy apps always take it one permission too far.

# Visual Jokes
The humor depends entirely on the escalating absurdity of the permission requests, which start reasonable (storage, location) and become increasingly invasive and ridiculous, culminating in the app claiming to predict masturbation schedules "as we silently judge you"—presented in the same formal language as legitimate permissions. The contrast between wanting a simple flashlight app and the dystopian surveillance demands creates the comedic effect.

# Scene Description

A simple line drawing shows a bookshelf viewed from the side with books standing upright. The books are arranged in groups labeled by category along the bottom axis. A small figure stands to the right, observing the collection. The art style is minimalist black-and-white line drawing.

# Text Transcription

**Shelf labels (left to right):**
- FOREVER
- CENTURIES
- DECADES
- YEARS
- 2-18 MONTHS
- PERMANENT EXPIRY

**Individual book spines (left to right):**
GRILLIAS, CLASSICS RESOURCE, ARCHIVED RESOURCE, COMPLETE SINGLE-ISSUE, LOST, BLACK DEATH, RESTORATION PROJECT, RESTORATION PROJECT, GAME SYSTEM, GAME SYSTEM, GAMING, STORAGE, STORAGE, BENCHMARK, BENCHMARK, ENCODING, FEATURE, FEATURE, PSYCHOLOGY, BLUEPRINT, POLITICS, TACTICS, TACTICS, TOPOLOGY, TECHNOLOGY, PSYCHOLOGY, BLUEPRINT, POLITICS, TOPOLOGY, PSYCHOLOGY, TOPOLOGY, TRASH CAN, TRASH CAN, MISCELLANEOUS, ENVIRONMENT, ENVIRONMENT, PERMAMENT, PERMANENT, EXPIRY

**Caption at bottom:**
"I like to arrange my books in order of decreasing time till obsolete/outdated/needs revision."

# Visual Joke

The humor derives from the systematic categorization of books by their anticipated obsolescence rather than by traditional organizing methods (author, genre, title). The progression from "FOREVER" to "PERMANENT EXPIRY" creates an ironic personal classification system that reflects the owner's pessimistic view of how quickly information becomes outdated.

# Scene Description
A four-panel stick figure comic in black and white. The first three panels show an indoor scene with two figures at a computer workstation. The bottom panel shows an outdoor scene with two figures facing each other, one holding a gun.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- "Isolate the murderer's face. Zoom in and enhance."
- "TAP TAP TAP" (sound effect)

**Panel 2:**
- "I think I got it."

**Panel 3:**
- "That's our boy, Johnny. Bring him in."

**Panel 4:**
- "FREEZE!"
- "!"

# Visual Joke
The humor relies on the contrast between the pixelated image enhancement. When the computer "enhances" and isolates the murderer's face from surveillance footage, it produces a heavily pixelated/censored block that is completely unintelligible. The police then "identify" this pixelated blur as a specific suspect ("Johnny") and proceed to arrest the matching pixelated figure in the final panel—making a joke about how enhancement technology doesn't actually work the way TV crime dramas depict it.

# Webcomic Transcription: "Gloria"

## Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic rendered in minimalist black and white line art with one character distinguished by a blue circular head. The narrative follows a philosophical journey triggered by a chance encounter with an earworm. Scenes alternate between everyday settings (bus, train station, office, street) and abstract conceptual representations.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"So I was talking to the old R3 line to work one morning."

**Panel 2:**
"And for some reason, I started singing a song in my head."
[Musical notes] "GLORIA GLORIA I THINK THEY'VE GOT A YOUR NUMBER" ♪

**Panel 3:**
"I couldn't get that song out of my head."
"WHAT THE HELL MAN. I DON'T EVEN LIKE THIS SONG."

**Panel 4:**
"Just then, the jolly ticket-collecting guy entered our singing car hit out our heart out."
[Musical notes] "GLORIA GLORIA I THINK THAT'S GOT A TICKET PLEASE." ♪

**Panel 5:**
[Musical notes] "GLORIA" ♪
"HOLY CRAP! WE READ EACH OTHER'S MINDS."

**Panel 6:**
"I was struck with wonder as I tried to wrap my mind around the cosmic significance of this synchronicity."

**Panel 7:**
"Had I somehow tapped into that vast reservoir of the collective unconscious?"

**Panel 8:**
"Had somehow tuned in to the universal asthetic records?"

**Panel 9:**
"Did the neural tripping patterns in my brain somehow achieve some kind of quantum resonance with the neurons of that jolly ticket-collecting guy's brain right at that instant?"

**Panel 10:**
"I couldn't find a reasonable explanation for what had just happened."

**Panel 11:**
"All I knew was that life was suddenly full of exciting new possibilities!"

**Panel 12:**
"The universe took on a whole new meaning for me that morning. No longer was it the universe just a random jumble of trivial events full of meaningless probabilities."

"Somehow, some way... we were all interconnected at a fundamental level in an intricate web of consciousness."

**Panel 13 (Large network diagram):**
[Visual of interconnected circles with various stick figures]

**Panel 14:**
"I no longer thought of consciousness as merely an emergent property arising out of the complexity of the universe, but as the infinite substrate upon which all sentence and thought is built."

**Panel 15:**
"Due to my newly acquired perception of the inexpressible nature of undifferentiated reality, I spent the rest of the day at work affected with a great sense of euphoria."

**Panel 16:**
"On my way home from work, I was still feeling that mystical connection with the universe. And then... I saw it."

**Panel 17:**
"I saw it as I got off the train at Market East Station."

**Panel 18:**
"There it was. An advertisement for some fashion thing or clothing hanging on the wall."
[Advertisement showing] "GLORIA"

**Panel 19:**
"It became immediately clear what had happened. I had seen that poster before getting on the train. In some obscure corner of human memory, my brain had inadvertently started running a new Gloria loop in my mind."

**Panel 20 (Large tangled neural network visualization):**
[Complex web of interconnected lines with code-like text overlaid]
"InternalMediaLayer.setGlobalVolume('neural/gloria.ogg');
InternalMediaLayer.setCurrentSong('gloria');
InternalMediaLayer.getVolume(true);
InternalMediaLayer.play();"

**Panel 21:**
"And I'm pretty sure that the jolly ticket-collecting guy must have seen it through the window as the train stopped at Market East Station. And his brain must have started the same thread too."

**Panel 22:**
"Suddenly, the world was reasonable and boring again."

**Panel 23:**
"My entire day had been a lie."

**Panel 24:**
"I then marveled at the utter stupidity of my own experience. And at that moment, I was alive again."

## Visual Jokes/Puns

1. **The Gloria Loop**: The entire premise is a play on how earworms work and pattern recognition—what feels like cosmic synchronicity is

# Scene Description
A three-panel webcomic in simple black-and-white stick figure style. The scene depicts someone playing a video game, with an in-app purchase dialog box appearing in the middle panel.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
- Caption: "CAPTAIN, ROMULAN WARBIRD DECLOAKING TO STARBOARD!"
- Caption: "ON SCREEN!"

**Panel 2:**
- Dialog box title: "Confirm Your In App Purchase"
- Dialog box text: "See Romulan Warbird for one bar of gold-pressed latinum."
- Button labels: "Cancel" | "Buy"

**Panel 3:**
- (No text)

# Visual Joke
The humor relies on the contrast between an immersive sci-fi gaming scenario (Star Trek reference with Romulan Warbirds) being interrupted by a mundane microtransaction dialog box demanding payment to see the promised in-game content. The absurdity of having to pay real money just to view an enemy that should be part of normal gameplay is the joke, made funnier by the currency reference to "latinum" (Star Trek's fictional money).

# Webcomic Transcription: "Tony Montana"

## Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic in black and white (with blue highlights for one character named Chucky) depicting a humorous recollection of a night when the narrator and their friend Chucky climbed a pyramid. The art style is minimalist stick figures with simple line drawings. The narrative progresses through multiple panels showing the climb up, the disastrous descent, and the aftermath, culminating in two final full-page panels with dark backgrounds.

## Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (Top left):**
"One night, Chucky and I were just hanging out in my apartment playing Soulsaifer."

Character 1: "WHOA! WHAT?"
Character 2: "I WANNA CLIMB A MOUNTAIN."

**Panel 2 (Top right):**
"There was nothing particularly significant about the said date, but it was August 4 of that year."

Character 2: "WHAT?"
Character 1: "I WANNA CLIMB A MOUNTAIN LIKE RIGHT NOW."

**Panel 3 (Middle left):**
"And just like that, I dragged Chucky outside to join me on my adventure."

Character 1: "WHERE THE HELL ARE YOU TAKING ME?"
Character 2: "ALMOST THERE."

**Panel 4 (Middle, dark background):**
"Somewhere in the middle of the university campus stands a pyramid. I had always wanted to climb it but it was a way too huge to do it by myself."

Text: "THERE SHE IS. THE SUMMIT BECKONS."

**Panel 5 (Below dark panel, left):**
"Chucky, always a good sport, immediately started racing up the side of the pyramid."

**Panel 6 (Below dark panel, center):**
"I quickly followed after."

**Panel 7 (Below dark panel, right):**
"We stood on the peak triumphant and proud."

**Panel 8 (Full width, below):**
"And we both let out a primal scream in brazen defiance of God, the universe, and the winds of Fate."

Character 2: "WHO YOU THINK YOU F**KING WITH?!? I TONY MONTANA!!!"

Character 1: "Because that's what you do when you conquer a mountain. IT'S THE LAW!"

Character 2: "YOU F**K WITH ME, YOU F**KING WITH BEST!!!"

Caption: "But getting to the top was easy. Getting down? Not so much. On the way down, the pyramid somehow magically transformed into a freakin' frictionless surface."

**Panel 9 (Left side, three-panel sequence):**

Panel 9a: "As I tried to make my way down, I slipped and started accelerating at a frightening rate."

Panel 9b: "My fall was finally broken by the tremendous impact of my feet against the hard concrete step at the bottom."

Panel 9c: "The pain was excruciating. To be honest, I probably suffered a minor bone fracture in my left foot that night."

**Panel 10 (Left side, below previous):**
"I turned around to witness Chucky succumbing to the same fate."

**Panel 11 (Right side):**
"In a sinister display of schadenfreude, I couldn't help but laugh as I watched Chucky falling around like a helpless turtle on its back sliding towards certain doom."

**Panel 12 (Bottom left):**
"My reaction oscillated between genuine concern for my friend and pure amusement at the funny scene."

Character 2: "HA HA... YOU OKAY...? HA...? ...HA HA..."

Character 1: "LAUNCH"

**Panel 13 (Bottom right):**
"As Chucky and I limped away, we passed by a couple sitting on a bench nearby."

Caption: "BOY THAT WAS REALLY SMART."

Character 1: "LIMP LIMP"
Character 2: "LIMP LIMP"

**Final Panel (Full page, dark background):**
"Now I'll bet you're thinking that climbing a pyramid, screaming "I Tony Montana", and breaking my foot was a pretty stupid way to spend the night."

[Image shows the pyramid with two small figures at bottom]

"But I'll also bet that you don't remember what

# Scene Description
A six-panel webcomic in black and white stick-figure style. The character (a person with long hair at a computer with a lightbulb icon) interacts with a round-faced character wearing glasses across the panels. The setting is minimalist, focusing on dialogue.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
Person at computer: "WHAT THE...?"

**Panel 2:**
Glasses character: "HEY, YOUR COMPUTER IS ACTING FUNNY."

**Panel 3:**
Person at computer: "YEAH, I GOT TIRED OF BEING SNOOPED ON BY GOVERNMENT AGENCIES."

**Panel 4:**
Person at computer: "SO I CREATED A NEW SUPER-SECURE FIREWALL."

**Panel 5:**
Person at computer: "IT EMPLOYS A SOPHISTICATED ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORK TO LEARN THE CHARACTERISTICS OF OUTGOING PACKETS THAT HAVE A HIGH PROBABILITY OF BEING VULNERABLE TO SURVEILLANCE, AND IT DYNAMICALLY UPDATES THE FILTERING RULES ACCORDINGLY."

**Panel 6:**
Person at computer: "IT'S 100% EFFECTIVE."

**Panel 7:**
Glasses character: "AIN'T NOBODY SNOOPIN' ON THIS GUY."

**Panel 8:**
Person at computer: "YEAH, BUT THERE'S LIKE NO INTERNET CONNECTION AT ALL. UNFORTUNATE SIDE EFFECT."

# Visual Jokes
The humor stems from the ironic trade-off: the character successfully blocks all surveillance by making the computer completely unusable, defeating the purpose of having internet security.

# Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic in simple black-and-white line art depicting a conversation between two characters about vaccine safety, progressing from skepticism to acceptance through an "Achievement Unlocked" moment.

---

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
- Character 1: "THERE'S NO WAY THAT I'M GETTING MY KIDS VACCINATED. VACCINES ARE DANGEROUS."

**Panel 2:**
- Character 2: "NAW, MAN, YOU TOTALLY SHOULD GET THEM VACCINATED. YOU'RE BEING IRRESPONSIBLE."
- Character 1: "BUT WHY? HOW DO VACCINATIONS EVEN WORK?"

**Panel 3:**
- Character 2: "VACCINATIONS ARE LIKE, Y'KNOW, TOTALLY GUD FOR YOU. YOU SHOULD TOTALLY VACCINATE YOUR KIDS CUZ OF THE VACCINATION-Y GUDNESS AND STUFF."

**Panel 4 (Achievement Screen):**
- "ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED"
- Text burst: "ANTI-VAXXER BULLSHIT DEBUNKED BECAUSE OF THE UNDENIABLE FORCE OF LOGIC"

**Panel 5:**
- Character 1: "KIDS, GET IN THE CAR! WE GON GET VACCINATED UP IN THIS BITCH!"
- Character 2: "I HELPED."

**Bottom Caption:**
"If you want to be a debunker, you should probably know a little something about what you're debunking."

---

# Visual Jokes
The "Achievement Unlocked" screen parodies video game achievement notifications, ironically treating the character's emotional outburst as a "victory" despite the preceding explanation being equally vague and unhelpful as the anti-vaccine rhetoric. The final caption serves as the actual punchline, critiquing both characters' inability to explain the science.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A webcomic in stick figure style featuring two characters in conversation. The character on the left lies in bed or on a couch, while the character on the right sits beside them. Digital clock timestamps appear in the bottom right of each panel, progressing from 1:10 to 10:51. The progression shows increasing concern and philosophical debate as the conversation continues.

# Dialogue Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
- Left character: "Doc, I can't seem to stop playing Grand Theft Auto V. It's like I'm addicted."
- Right character: [listening, no dialogue]
- Clock: 1:10

**Panel 2:**
- Left character: "Say what?"
- Right character: "Hmmm, interesting. Have you considered the possibility you're still playing GTA right now?"
- Clock: 2:43

**Panel 3:**
- Right character: "Think about it. GTA V is so realistic that your brain could be confused."
- Clock: 3:22

**Panel 4:**
- Right character: "Maybe you're sitting in your living room right now with Doritos stains on your shirt playing a video game."
- Clock: 4:09

**Panel 5:**
- Right character: "And maybe your brain is actively creating this fantasy right now based on elements from GTA."
- Clock: 5:19

**Panel 6:**
- Right character: "It's possible that when you last turned off your console, you only fantasized that you did, and that you're still stuck in the video game."
- Clock: 6:11

**Panel 7:**
- [Visual: A small drawing of a Doritos bag appears on the left side]
- Clock: 7:15

**Panel 8:**
- Right character: "If that is true, then your subconscious might subtly try to introduce inconsistencies into your fantasy to try to get you to wake up."
- Clock: 8:09

**Panel 9:**
- Right character: "For example, your fantasy might include a message from the internet to try to get you to wake up."
- Clock: 10:10

**Panel 10:**
- Left character: "Your subconscious could be trying to get you to please wake up right now."
- Clock: 10:49

**Panel 11:**
- [Visual: A small star outline appears on the left side]
- Clock: 10:50

**Panel 12:**
- Left character: "Naw, that's just crazy talk."
- Right character: "Maybe you're right."
- Clock: 10:51

# Visual Jokes
The primary visual joke is the progression of clock timestamps showing only 1 minute and 41 seconds of real time passing while the conversation becomes increasingly existential and recursive. The appearance of the Doritos bag and star are subtle visual cues suggesting the "subconscious inconsistencies" the doctor describes, which the viewer may notice but the left character dismisses—reinforcing the joke about being unable to distinguish reality from simulation.

# Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic with four panels showing a conversation between three characters and one character with a blue hat. The art style is minimalist with simple line drawings. The setting progresses from abstract concepts to a street with a parked car.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1 (top left):**
"Ever since we discovered the power of flight, we have been limited only by our imagination."

**Panel 2 (top right):**
"We can travel anywhere in the world any time we want."

**Panel 3 (top right, speech bubble):**
"WE COULD LEAVE THIS CRAPPY TOWN RIGHT NOW AND GO TO THE BAHAMAS, OR HAWAII, OR PARIS. ANYWHERE!"

**Panel 4 (bottom left, speech bubble):**
"OR... WE COULD STAY HERE AND POOP ON THAT GUY'S CAR EVERYDAY."

**Panel 5 (bottom right):**
(No dialogue, shows the three figures have chosen to stay)

**Caption (bottom center):**
"I will never understand the birds in my town."

# Visual Jokes
The comic's humor relies on the contrast between the philosophical opening about unlimited freedom through flight, and the reveal that the characters are birds choosing to stay in town specifically to harass someone by defecating on their car. The punchline subverts the grandiose setup with petty, vindictive behavior.

# Scene Description
A nine-panel minimalist webcomic featuring a simple square character with two dots for eyes. The art style uses basic line drawings on white backgrounds. The narrative follows the character's progression through increasingly higher dimensions as it interprets insults literally.

# Dialogue Transcript (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
"HAHA. YOU'RE SO STUPID AND NARROW-MINDED. YOUR THINKING IS SO ONE-DIMENSIONAL."

**Panel 2:**
"IT'S AS IF YOUR BRAIN IS PHYSICALLY INCAPABLE OF COMPREHENDING THE GREATER PERSPECTIVE."

**Panel 3:**
"Yeah, well, y'know that's just like your opinion, man."

**Panel 4:**
(No dialogue - character shown with motion lines suggesting movement)

**Panel 5:**
(No dialogue - character now appears as a 3D cube)

**Panel 6:**
(No dialogue - character shown as a 4D hypercube/tesseract)

**Panel 7:**
(No dialogue - character as a 3D structure pointing upward)

**Panel 8:**
"Good-bye."

**Panel 9:**
(No dialogue - character returns to 2D square form alone)

# Visual Jokes
The comic's central joke: The character literally transcends dimensions in response to being called "one-dimensional," then "narrow-minded" (2D), progressively becoming 3D, 4D, and beyond—visually representing an ascension beyond the insulter's comprehension.

# Scene Description
A simple stick-figure comic in black and white with nine panels arranged in a 3x3 grid. Two stick figures interact with a large rectangular machine (depicted as a tall box). The art style is minimalist line drawing.

# Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
Caption: "STEP INSIDE THE MACHINE AND PREPARE TO BE AMAZED."

**Panel 2:**
Figure inside machine: "WHAT AM I DOING IN HERE?"

**Panel 3:**
Figure outside machine: "OK, COME ON OUT."

**Panel 4:**
Figure outside machine: "WHAT JUST HAPPENED?"
Figure outside machine: "YOU JUST TRAVELED 15 SECONDS INTO THE FUTURE."

**Panel 5:**
Figure inside machine: "NOT JUST A TIME MACHINE."
Figure outside machine: "YOU... YOU BUILT A TIME MACHINE?"

**Panel 6:**
Figure outside machine: "THIS BADBOY HAS ITS OWN TRACTOR BEAM AND IT IS CURRENTLY PULLING THE EARTH TOWARDS ITSELF."
Figure inside machine: "WOW, REALLY?"

**Panel 7:**
Figure inside machine: "WOAH."
Figure outside machine: "IT ALSO PRODUCES ITS OWN FORCE FIELD WHICH PREVENTS IT FROM GOING THROUGH THE GROUND."

**Panel 8:**
Figure outside machine: "AND RIGHT NOW, IT IS TRANSMITTING EM WAVES DIRECTLY ONTO YOUR RETINAS, PRODUCING A 3D IMAGE IN YOUR BRAIN."

**Panel 9:**
Figure inside machine: "YES, THE UNIVERSE IS VERY AMAZING."
Figure outside machine: "THIS TECHNOLOGY IS AMAZING!!!"

# Visual Jokes
The humor relies on escalating absurdity: the "time machine" that only moves time forward 15 seconds becomes increasingly implausible with added features (tractor beam, force field, neural imaging), with the final punchline that all of these "amazing" features are simply describing normal physics/perception.

# Scene Description
A nighttime illustration with a dark blue starry sky and a crescent moon in the upper right corner. The lower portion shows a silhouetted forest treeline. Three waveform diagrams are displayed in the middle section, each labeled with different nocturnal creatures. At the bottom left is a small illustration of a bar with a lit window. A final waveform appears at the very bottom. The overall aesthetic is contemplative and educational, with a focus on the visual representation of sound.

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

"Have you ever listened to the sounds of nature at night?"

"Those are the sounds of the myriad creatures participating in the great chain of life. Each creature must heed its inner primal urge to scream desperately for the attention of a potential mate – an urge which was molded by millions of years of evolutionary pressure."

"If you listen carefully, you might be able to make out the specific songs of the different creatures."

"The beautiful melody of the birds."

"The hypnotic chirping of the crickets."

"The massed chorus of the cicadas."

"If you listen at the right time of night, these sounds can reach a deafening crescendo."

"So the next time you're out at night, listen carefully to the beautiful symphony emanating from the darkness."

"Those are the sounds of the myriad creatures of the night seeking love."

# Visual Jokes
The waveform diagrams humorously juxtapose the romantic description of nature's "beautiful symphony" with clinical, densely-packed audio visualizations—the most chaotic-looking waveform represents the cicadas' "massed chorus," emphasizing the overwhelming cacophony rather than beauty. The final waveform at the bottom (presumably from inside the lit bar) suggests humans also participate in this primal nighttime ritual, creating a humorous parallel.

comics.transcript:
# Scene Description
A stick-figure webcomic depicting an interview or conversation between two people at what appears to be a desk or table. The art style is minimalist black line drawings. The comic uses a hierarchical layout with dialogue panels at top and bottom, connected by larger explanatory text blocks and scientific illustrations in the middle (mathematical equations, molecular structures, DNA helix, atomic diagrams, and chemical reaction pathways).

---

# Text Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:**
- "SO YOUR RÉSUMÉ SAYS THAT YOU MAJORED IN MATHEMATICS."
- "YEAH, THAT'S RIGHT."

**Panel 2:**
- "WELL, ACTUALLY I MAJORED IN PHYSICS. BUT PHYSICS IS JUST A SUBFIELD OF MATHEMATICS."
- "WHAT?"

**Panel 3 (Caption box):**
"When you look at our universe at its most fundamental level, everything is defined purely and completely through equations and numbers."

**Displayed equations:**
- R_μν - 1/2 R g_μν = 8πG T_μν
- [−ℏ²/2m ∇² + V]ψ = iℏ ∂ψ/∂t
- |↑↓| = 2

**Character grid (periodic table-style):**
U, E, I, E, G, H
J, S, U, Y
E, H, T, Z
X, Y, Z, W

**Panel 4 (Caption box):**
"One can literally conceive of an infinite number of mathematical universes, but our universe just happens to be a particular solution to one."

**Panel 5:**
- "BUT... THAT'S ... JUST LIKE MY OPINION."
- "OKAY, SO YOU WERE A PHYSICS MAJOR."
- "YEP."
- "WELL, NO. ACTUALLY I WAS A CHEMISTRY MAJOR."

**Panel 6 (Caption box):**
"But when you think about it, chemistry is all about the physics of atoms and the interactions of molecules. It's really just a subfield of physics and hence a subfield of math."

**Displayed images:**
- Atomic model (nucleus with electron orbits)
- Two molecular structure diagrams

**Panel 7 (Caption box):**
"If you want to be really technical, I suppose I was a biology major. Since biology is really just the study of the chemical reactions and processes of living systems, I suppose you could say that it is a subfield of chemistry and hence a subfield of physics and hence a subfield of math."

**Displayed images:**
- Cell diagram
- DNA double helix structure
- Complex biochemical pathway diagram with ATP, molecular structures, and reaction arrows

**Panel 8:**
- "IN PARTICULAR, I MAJORED IN NEUROSCIENCE."
- "LET'S JUST SKIP TO THE END, SHALL WE?"
- "WHAT DOES IT SAY ON YOUR DIPLOMA?"
- "COMPARATIVE LITERATURE."

---

# Visual Jokes

1. **The recursive argument**: Each panel reveals a progressively broader field of study, with the character systematically reframing their major as a subfield of a more fundamental discipline. The mathematical "proof" that everything reduces to mathematics creates an absurd logical chain.

2. **The periodic table grid**: The character grid in Panel 3 mimics a periodic table layout but contains letters spelling various words/sounds, visualizing the reduction of complex concepts to their most basic components.

3. **The final punchline subversion**: After laboriously working backward through physics → chemistry → biology → neuroscience, the diploma reveals "Comparative Literature"—completely outside the STEM hierarchy, undermining the entire nested argument about disciplinary reduction to mathematics.

4. **Visual escalation**: The scientific diagrams and equations progressively increase in complexity (from basic equations to molecular structures to biochemical pathways), mirroring the ironic "building up" of justification for a fundamentally flawed argument.

# Scene Description
A minimalist stick figure webcomic with two characters: one wearing a blue hemisphere cap and one wearing an orange hemisphere cap. The characters are drawn as simple circles with stick limbs. The comic takes place across 9 panels in a 3x3 grid, showing a conversation between these two "opposite" characters.

# Dialogue Transcription (in reading order)

**Panel 1:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 2:**
- Blue character: "HUH? WH- WHO ARE YOU?"
- Orange character: "I AM BIZARRO-YOU."

**Panel 3:**
- Blue character: "WOW, REALLY? SO YOU'RE, LIKE, THE OPPOSITE OF ME?"
- Orange character: "YEP."

**Panel 4:** (No dialogue)

**Panel 5:**
- Blue character: "SHOULDN'T YOU BE PLOTTING THE EVIL DESTRUCTION OF EARTH OR SOMETHING?"

**Panel 6:**
- Blue character: "THAT WOULD IMPLY THAT YOU ARE EXCEPTIONALLY GOOD."

**Panel 7:**
- Blue character: "UNFORTUNATELY, YOU'RE SO UNREMARKABLE AT EVERYTHING IN LIFE THAT WE'RE PRACTICALLY IDENTICAL."
- Orange character: "OH, OKAY."

**Panel 8:**
- Blue character: "THAT'S A LITTLE DEPRESSING."
- Orange character: "I'M A LITTLE DELIGHTED."

# Visual Jokes
The primary visual joke is that the two characters are literally identical except for their cap colors, reinforcing the punchline that they're so unremarkable they're practically the same person despite being "opposites."

# Scene Description
A three-panel stick figure comic in simple black and white line art with blue coloring on the characters' heads. The first two panels show a stick figure narrating childhood memories, while the third panel depicts a dramatic role reversal with an alarmed, demonic-looking rabbit chasing the now-tiny stick figure.

# Text Transcription

**Panel 1 (top left):**
"When I was little, I always wanted to catch a cute bunny and make it my pet."

*Handwritten dialogue from stick figure:*
"I WILL CATCH HIM, AND I WILL HUG HIM AND PET HIM AND SQUEEZE HIM..."

**Panel 2 (top right):**
"But, alas, the bunnies always escaped."

*Stick figure continues:*
"However, that's not the real tragedy of this story."

**Panel 3 (bottom, full width):**
Large text: "BAAAWAGLHAR"

*The bunny, now depicted with horns, wild eyes, and an aggressive expression, is chasing the tiny stick figure in terror.*

**Bottom caption:**
"Always try to see things from the other person's perspective."

# Visual Jokes
The primary joke is the perspective reversal: the innocent childhood fantasy of catching and hugging a bunny is reframed as a terrifying predator chase from the bunny's point of view. The bunny's demonic appearance and the onomatopoeia "BAAAWAGLHAR" emphasize the horror of being hunted by a much larger creature.

# Webcomic Transcription

## Scene Description
A stick-figure comic in black and white depicting a traffic stop. A police officer pulls over a driver, leading to an increasingly absurd conversation about physics and energy calculations. The final panels show the driver's internal monologue as they drive away, with a transition to an exterior shot of the car.

## Text Transcription (Reading Order)

**Panel 1:**
Officer: "DO YOU KNOW WHY I PULLED YOU OVER?"
Driver: "NO, OFFICER."

**Panel 2:**
Officer: "YOU DISRESPECTED THE RULES OF PHYSICS. YOU SWITCHED INTO THE PASSING LANE IN FRONT OF THAT GUY AND MADE HIM DECELERATE BY 8 M/S TO MATCH YOUR SPEED."

**Panel 3:**
Officer: "SINCE THERE WERE NO CARS BEHIND HIM, CLEARLY, YOU SHOULD HAVE JUST LET HIM PASS YOU BEFORE YOU SWITCHED LANES."

**Panel 4:**
Officer: "YOU WERE DIRECTLY RESPONSIBLE FOR MAKING HIM LOSE 300,000 JOULES OF KINETIC ENERGY."

**Panel 5:**
Officer: "YOU OWE HIM ABOUT $0.0006 OF GAS MONEY."
Driver: "OKAY."

**Panel 6:**
Officer: "PLUS PSYCHOLOGICAL PAIN & SUFFERING, PUNK!"
[Empty panel with officer's thought bubble]

**Panel 7:**
Thought bubble text: "SONUVABITCH."
[Exterior shot of car driving away]

**Panel 8:**
[Car continues driving]

## Visual Jokes
- The comic's humor relies on the officer's pedantic application of physics equations to a minor traffic violation, treating it as a serious crime
- The final panels show the officer subsequently experiencing road rage ("SONUVABITCH"), immediately becoming guilty of the same offense they just cited, creating ironic comeuppance
